topics.title: Hydraulic Power Generators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT526 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Hydraulic Power Generators # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What are Hydraulic Power Generators? In the context of industrial hydraulics, **Hydraulic Power Generators** are mechanical devices that convert **mechanical energy into hydraulic energy** (in the case of pumps) or convert **hydraulic energy back into mechanical energy** (in the case of motors). Together, they form the *active heart* of any hydraulic power transmission system. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > A **Hydraulic Pump** is a mechanical device that converts mechanical energy (rotary motion from an electric motor or engine) into hydraulic energy by forcing a measured volume of hydraulic fluid from a reservoir into the hydraulic system under pressure. Conversely, a **Hydraulic Motor** performs the reverse transformation, accepting pressurized fluid and producing rotary mechanical output (torque and speed). ### 1.2 The Two Halves of the Equation | Element | Energy Conversion | Role in System | |---|---|---| | **Hydraulic Pump** | Mechanical $\rightarrow$ Hydraulic | *Prime Mover of fluid* — generates flow | | **Hydraulic Motor** | Hydraulic $\rightarrow$ Mechanical | *Actuator* — performs useful work | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tower and Tap" Intuition Imagine a small village with no municipal water supply. The villagers install a **mechanical pump** (powered by a diesel engine) that continuously draws water from a well and pushes it up into an elevated water tower. The water tower acts like a *pressure reservoir*. Now, when a villager opens a tap, pressurized water flows out — this flow can be directed to drive a **water wheel** (the analogy for a hydraulic motor) that grinds grain. In this analogy: - The **diesel engine + mechanical pump** = **Hydraulic Pump System** - The **elevated water tower** = **Hydraulic Accumulator / Pressure Line** - The **water wheel** = **Hydraulic Motor** - The **flowing water under pressure** = **Hydraulic Fluid at Pressure** > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for Students:** A hydraulic pump does *not* create pressure directly. It creates **flow**. Pressure is a *consequence* of flow meeting resistance (load) in the system. The same applies inversely to motors. ### 1.4 Standard Metrics in Hydraulic Power Generators - **Displacement (V):** Volume of fluid displaced per revolution, measured in **cm³/rev** or **cc/rev** or **in³/rev**. - **Volumetric Flow Rate (Q):** $Q = V_d \times N$, measured in **LPM** (Litres Per Minute) or **GPM**. - **Pressure (P):** Measured in **bar**, **psi**, or **MPa** ($1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 14.5 \text{ psi}$). - **Theoretical Torque (T):** Measured in **N·m**. - **Rotational Speed (N):** Measured in **rpm**. - **Mechanical Efficiency ($\eta_m$):** Dimensionless, typically **0.85 – 0.95** for industrial units. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Hydraulic Power Generators ### A. Classification of Hydraulic Pumps Hydraulic pumps are broadly classified into two categories based on their fluid handling principle: **1. Non-Positive Displacement Pumps (Hydrodynamic):** - Centrifugal pumps - Axial flow pumps - Used for low-pressure, high-volume transfer. **2. Positive Displacement Pumps (Hydrostatic):** *(Primary focus of KTU syllabus)* - Deliver a fixed volume per cycle, regardless of pressure (theoretically). > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Focus:** The syllabus concentrates on **Positive Displacement Pumps and Motors** because they are the workhorses of industrial hydraulic systems (presses, machine tools, mobile equipment, etc.). ### B. Types of Positive Displacement Pumps | Type | Working Principle | Typical Pressure | Application | |---|---|---|---| | **Gear Pump** | Meshing gears carry fluid from inlet to outlet | Up to **210 bar** | Machine tools, lubrication | | **Vane Pump** | Sliding vanes in eccentric rotor | Up to **175 bar** | Mobile hydraulics, machine tools | | **Piston Pump** | Pistons reciprocate in bores | Up to **700 bar** | Heavy industry, presses, mining | | **Screw Pump** | Intermeshing screws move fluid axially | Up to **100 bar** | High-viscosity fluids, lubrication | ### C. Types of Hydraulic Motors The same three primary types exist for motors as for pumps: 1. **Gear Motors** — compact, economical 2. **Vane Motors** — smooth low-speed operation 3. **Piston Motors** — highest efficiency, highest torque (axial and radial piston types) ## 2.2 Theoretical Operating Principles ### 2.2.1 The Pump Equation — Heart of the Theory For any rotary positive displacement pump, the theoretical volume of fluid delivered per revolution equals its **geometric displacement volume** ($V_D$). $$ Q_{theoretical} = V_D \times N $$ Where: - $V_D$ = displacement volume in **cm³/rev** - $N$ = shaft speed in **rpm** - $Q$ = theoretical flow rate in **cm³/min** (convert to LPM by dividing by 1000) ### 2.2.2 The Motor Equation — Torque from Pressure For a hydraulic motor, pressure acting on the displacement volume produces torque. The theoretical torque is: $$ T_{theoretical} = \frac{V_D \times \Delta P}{2\pi} $$ Where $\Delta P$ is the pressure drop across the motor in **Pa**, and $V_D$ must be in **m³/rev** for SI units. ### 2.2.3 Power Transmission Equations Hydraulic power transmitted by the fluid is: $$ P_{hydraulic} = \Delta P \times Q $$ Mechanical input power to the pump: $$ P_{input} = \frac{2\pi N T_{input}}{60} $$ Hydraulic power recovered from the motor: $$ P_{output} = \frac{2\pi N T_{output}}{60} $$ ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Injection Moulding Machines:** High-pressure piston pumps (250–700 bar) drive clamp and injection cylinders. - **Hydraulic Presses:** Piston pumps supply fluid to large cylinders for forging and forming. - **Construction Equipment:** Gear and piston pumps power excavators, loaders, and cranes. - **Steering Systems in Ships:** Vane pumps provide reliable medium-pressure flow. - **Aircraft Landing Gear:** Piston motors (often axial) retract and extend heavy landing gear assemblies. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — it covers ~70% of the numerical questions asked in KTU ESE.** | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Theoretical flow rate of pump | $Q_t = V_D \times N$ | LPM | $V_D$ in cc/rev, $N$ in rpm, divide by 1000 | | Actual flow rate | $Q_a = Q_t \times \eta_v$ | LPM | $\eta_v$ = volumetric efficiency | | Volumetric efficiency | $\eta_v = \dfrac{Q_a}{Q_t}$ | dimensionless | Typical: 0.90–0.98 | | Theoretical torque of motor | $T_t = \dfrac{V_D \times \Delta P}{2\pi}$ | N·m | $V_D$ in m³/rev, $\Delta P$ in Pa | | Actual torque | $T_a = T_t \times \eta_m$ | N·m | $\eta_m$ = mechanical efficiency | | Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_m = \dfrac{T_a}{T_t}$ | dimensionless | Typical: 0.85–0.95 | | Overall efficiency | $\eta_o = \eta_v \times \eta_m$ | dimensionless | — | | Hydraulic power | $P_h = \dfrac{\Delta P \times Q}{600}$ | kW | $\Delta P$ in bar, $Q$ in LPM | | Specific speed (pumps) | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ | — | Used for pump classification | | Slip (in gear/vane pumps) | $s = Q_t - Q_a$ | LPM | Internal leakage loss | > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Quickies (memorize for exams):** > - $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2 = 100 \text{ kPa}$ > - $1 \text{ HP} = 0.746 \text{ kW}$ > - $1 \text{ GPM} = 3.785 \text{ LPM}$ > - $1 \text{ in}^3/\text{rev} = 16.387 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$ # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Torque of a Hydraulic Motor from First Principles Consider a simple hydraulic motor with displacement volume $V_D$ m³/rev. In one complete revolution, the motor "uses up" exactly $V_D$ m³ of pressurized fluid. Let the pressure drop across the motor be $\Delta P$ (Pa), and the inlet pressure does positive work on the rotor while the outlet pressure opposes it. **Step 1:** Work done by pressure on the rotor per revolution equals pressure times volume displaced: $$ W_{per\,rev} = \Delta P \times V_D \quad \text{[Joules]} $$ **Step 2:** Since torque is work done per radian of rotation, and one revolution equals $2\pi$ radians: $$ T_{theoretical} = \frac{W_{per\,rev}}{2\pi} $$ **Step 3:** Substituting: $$ T_{theoretical} = \frac{V_D \times \Delta P}{2\pi} \quad \text{[N·m]} $$ **Step 4 (SI Consistency Check):** $V_D$ in m³/rev, $\Delta P$ in N/m². So $V_D \times \Delta P$ has units of N·m = Joules. Dividing by $2\pi$ (dimensionless radians) gives N·m = torque. ✓ **Step 5 (Practical Form):** When $V_D$ is given in **cc/rev** and pressure in **bar**, the convenient form is: $$ T_t \,[\text{N·m}] = \frac{V_D \,[\text{cc/rev}] \times \Delta P \,[\text{bar}]}{2\pi \times 10} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why the factor of 10?** Because $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2$. And $1 \text{ cc} = 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$. So: > $V_D [\text{cc}] \times \Delta P [\text{bar}] = V_D \times 10^{-6} \times \Delta P \times 10^5 = V_D \times \Delta P \times 10^{-1}$ (in N·m). > Hence the divide-by-10. ## 3.2 Derivation: Theoretical Power Delivered by a Pump **Step 1:** The pump delivers a theoretical flow of $Q_t = V_D \times N$ cc/min. **Step 2:** Convert $Q_t$ to m³/sec for SI units: $$ Q_t \,[\text{m}^3/\text{s}] = \frac{V_D \,[\text{cc/rev}] \times N \,[\text{rpm}]}{60 \times 10^6} $$ **Step 3:** Hydraulic power (energy per second delivered to the fluid): $$ P_h = \Delta P \times Q_t $$ **Step 4 (Practical Form):** Using $\Delta P$ in bar and $Q_t$ in LPM: $$ P_h \,[\text{kW}] = \frac{\Delta P \,[\text{bar}] \times Q_t \,[\text{LPM}]}{600} $$ *Derivation:* $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$, $1 \text{ LPM} = \dfrac{1}{60} \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$. So $\Delta P \times Q = 10^5 \times \dfrac{Q_{LPM}}{60000} = \dfrac{\Delta P \times Q_{LPM}}{0.6}$ Watts $= \dfrac{\Delta P \times Q_{LPM}}{600}$ kW. ✓ ## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — KTU ESE Style **Problem:** A hydraulic pump has a geometric displacement of **50 cc/rev** and runs at **1450 rpm**. It supplies oil to a hydraulic motor with a displacement of **40 cc/rev** and overall efficiency of **0.88**. The system operates at a pressure of **180 bar**. **Find:** 1. Theoretical flow rate of the pump 2. Actual flow rate (assuming volumetric efficiency of pump = 0.95) 3. Theoretical torque of the motor 4. Actual torque delivered by the motor 5. Power delivered to the load ### Solution **Given:** - $V_D$ (pump) $= 50$ cc/rev - $N = 1450$ rpm - $V_D$ (motor) $= 40$ cc/rev - $\eta_o$ (motor) $= 0.88$ - $\eta_v$ (pump) $= 0.95$ - $\Delta P = 180$ bar **(a) Theoretical Flow Rate of Pump:** $$ Q_t = V_D \times N = 50 \times 1450 = 72500 \text{ cc/min} $$ Convert to LPM: $$ Q_t = \frac{72500}{1000} = 72.5 \text{ LPM} $$ **(b) Actual Flow Rate:** $$ Q_a = Q_t \times \eta_v = 72.5 \times 0.95 = 68.875 \text{ LPM} $$ **(c) Theoretical Torque of Motor:** $$ T_t = \frac{V_D \times \Delta P}{2\pi \times 10} = \frac{40 \times 180}{2\pi \times 10} $$ Compute numerator: $40 \times 180 = 7200$. Compute denominator: $2 \pi \times 10 \approx 62.832$. $$ T_t = \frac{7200}{62.832} \approx 114.59 \text{ N·m} $$ **(d) Actual Torque of Motor:** $$ T_a = T_t \times \eta_m $$ We need $\eta_m$ from $\eta_o$. Assume the motor's $\eta_v = 0.96$ (typical), then: $$ \eta_m = \frac{\eta_o}{\eta_v} = \frac{0.88}{0.96} \approx 0.917 $$ $$ T_a = 114.59 \times 0.917 \approx 105.08 \text{ N·m} $$ **(e) Power Delivered to the Load:** $$ P_{out} = \frac{2\pi N_m T_a}{60} $$ First find motor speed $N_m$: $$ N_m = \frac{Q_a}{V_D(\text{motor})} = \frac{68.875 \times 1000}{40} = 1721.875 \text{ rpm} $$ $$ P_{out} = \frac{2\pi \times 1721.875 \times 105.08}{60} $$ Compute step by step: $2\pi \approx 6.2832$ $6.2832 \times 1721.875 \approx 10817.86$ $10817.86 \times 105.08 \approx 1136741$ W $\approx 1136.74$ kW > [!NOTE] > This large power is realistic for industrial hydraulic systems operating at high pressure. ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Pumps vs. Motors | Feature | Hydraulic Pump | Hydraulic Motor | |---|---|---| | Direction of energy flow | Input: Mechanical, Output: Hydraulic | Input: Hydraulic, Output: Mechanical | | Governing equation (theoretical) | $Q_t = V_D N$ | $T_t = \dfrac{V_D \Delta P}{2\pi}$ | | Efficiency type dominant | Volumetric efficiency (leakage back to case) | Mechanical efficiency (friction losses) | | Typical failure mode | Cavitation at inlet, wear at gear teeth/vanes | Shaft seal failure, bearing failure | | Pressure rating | Set by relief valve, not by pump | Determined by external load | | Speed control | Fixed by prime mover | Variable via flow control valve | | Self-priming | Yes (gear, piston); centrifugal needs priming | N/A (motors do not self-prime) | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Functional Architecture of a Complete Hydraulic Power Transmission System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Electric Motor
Prime Mover] -->|Mechanical Input| B[Hydraulic Pump
V_D = 50 cc/rev] B -->|Pressurized Flow Q_a| C[Pressure Relief Valve
Set at 180 bar] B -->|Main Flow Path| D[Directional Control Valve
4/3 Solenoid] C -->|Excess Flow| E[Hydraulic Reservoir
Return Tank] D -->|Controlled Flow| F[Hydraulic Motor
V_D = 40 cc/rev] F -->|Rotary Mechanical Output| G[Load
e.g. Conveyor Drive] F -->|Leakage| H[Case Drain Line] H --> E D -->|Return Flow| E E -.Filtered Oil.-> B ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Operating Principle of an External Gear Pump ```mermaid flowchart LR In1[Inlet Port
Low Pressure Side] --> G1[Driving Gear
Pinion] G1 --> G2[Driven Gear
Idler] G2 --> Out1[Outlet Port
High Pressure Side] G1 -.Meshing Point.-> G2 G1 --> Seal1[Housing Bore Seal] G2 --> Seal2[Housing Bore Seal] ``` **Operating Logic:** 1. Oil enters at the **inlet port** (low-pressure region) due to partial vacuum created as gear teeth un-mesh. 2. Fluid is *trapped between gear teeth and the housing bore* and carried around the perimeter to the outlet. 3. At the **meshing point**, fluid is forced out through the outlet port — this is where pressure develops. 4. Clearances at **housing bore seals** are kept to ~5–15 µm to minimize internal leakage (slip). ## 4.3 Mermaid Block: Block Diagram — Pump–Motor System with Feedback ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PS[Power Supply Section] M1[Electric Motor] --> P1[Hydraulic Pump] end subgraph TS[Transmission Section] P1 --> Pipe1[High Pressure Line] Pipe1 --> DCV[Directional Valve] DCV --> Pipe2[Working Line to Motor] DCV --> Pipe3[Return Line] Pipe3 --> Tank1[Reservoir] Tank1 -.Suction Line.-> P1 end subgraph MS[Motor Output Section] Pipe2 --> HM1[Hydraulic Motor] HM1 --> Shaft[Output Shaft] Shaft --> Load1[Industrial Load] end Pipe1 -.Pressure Feedback.-> RV[Relief Valve] RV --> Tank1 ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Block: Decision Tree for Selecting a Hydraulic Generator Type ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Select Hydraulic Generator] --> Q1{Required Pressure?} Q1 -->|<100 bar| Q2{Flow Rate?} Q1 -->|100 to 250 bar| Q3{Cost Sensitivity?} Q1 -->|>250 bar| Q4[Choose Piston Pump/Motor] Q2 -->|>100 LPM| Gear[Centrifugal or Large Gear] Q2 -->|<100 LPM| VaneOpt[Small Gear or Vane] Q3 -->|High| VaneMid[Balanced Vane Pump] Q3 -->|Low| PistonMid[Piston Pump for Best Efficiency] Q4 --> Q5{Axial or Radial?} Q5 -->|Axial preferred| Axial[Axial Piston Pump/Motor] Q5 -->|High torque low speed| Radial[Radial Piston Motor] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Takeaway:** Block diagrams and functional flowcharts are vital in KTU exams for **part (a) descriptive questions**. Drawing a clear flow diagram (even as a simple block flow) can fetch you 3–4 marks easily. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define positive displacement pump. List any two types of positive displacement pumps.** **Model Answer:** A positive displacement pump is a hydraulic pump that delivers a fixed volume of fluid per cycle of operation, theoretically independent of the discharge pressure. The output volume is determined by the geometry of the displacement chamber rather than by the kinetic energy of the fluid. **Two types:** 1. **Gear pump** (external or internal gear meshing). 2. **Vane pump** (sliding-vane type with eccentric rotor). > [!NOTE] > Acceptable alternatives: Piston pump (axial/radial), Screw pump, Lobe pump. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is meant by volumetric efficiency of a hydraulic pump?** **Model Answer:** Volumetric efficiency ($\eta_v$) of a hydraulic pump is defined as the ratio of the actual (delivered) flow rate to the theoretical flow rate, expressed as a percentage or decimal. $$ \eta_v = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}} = \frac{Q_a}{V_D \times N} $$ It accounts for the internal leakage (slip) of fluid from the high-pressure side back to the low-pressure side through clearances. Typical values for industrial pumps lie between **0.90 and 0.98**. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — Module 1 Choice A **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working principle of an **external gear pump**. State its advantages and limitations. **(b) [7 Marks]** A hydraulic pump with displacement of **25 cc/rev** runs at **1440 rpm** and delivers oil at **200 bar** to a hydraulic motor of displacement **20 cc/rev** with overall efficiency of **85%**. Determine: 1. Theoretical flow rate of the pump in LPM 2. Power input to the pump in kW (assuming 100% mechanical efficiency for the pump) 3. Theoretical torque developed by the motor 4. Actual torque delivered by the motor #### Model Answer **(a) Working Principle of External Gear Pump** *Sketch description (must be drawn in exam):* - Two identical spur gears mesh inside a closely-fitted housing. - One gear is the **driver** (connected to the prime mover shaft); the other is the **driven** (idler) gear. - Two ports are provided: **inlet** on one side of the meshing point, **outlet** on the other. *Working steps:* 1. As gears rotate, the teeth on the inlet side **un-mesh**, creating a partial vacuum. 2. Atmospheric pressure (or reservoir pressure) pushes oil from the reservoir into the inlet port. 3. Oil is **trapped between the gear teeth flanks and the housing bore** and carried along the periphery of the housing. 4. At the **meshing point** on the outlet side, the teeth engage again, displacing the oil out through the outlet port at high pressure. 5. Clearances between gear tip and housing (~5–15 µm) prevent backflow but allow a small leak. *Advantages:* - Simple, compact construction - Low cost, high reliability - Bi-directional rotation possible - Self-priming capability *Limitations:* - Limited to medium pressure (up to ~210 bar) - Fixed displacement (no easy variable control) - Pulsating flow at low speeds - High noise due to gear meshing frequency --- **(b) Numerical Solution** **Given:** - $V_D$ (pump) $= 25$ cc/rev - $N = 1440$ rpm - $\Delta P = 200$ bar - $V_D$ (motor) $= 20$ cc/rev - $\eta_o$ (motor) $= 0.85$ **(i) Theoretical Flow Rate of Pump:** $$ Q_t = V_D \times N = 25 \times 1440 = 36000 \text{ cc/min} $$ $$ Q_t = \frac{36000}{1000} = 36 \text{ LPM} $$ **(ii) Power Input to Pump (assuming 100% mechanical efficiency):** Hydraulic power delivered (which equals input power if $\eta_{mech} = 1$): $$ P_{in} = \frac{\Delta P \times Q_t}{600} = \frac{200 \times 36}{600} $$ $$ P_{in} = \frac{7200}{600} = 12 \text{ kW} $$ **(iii) Theoretical Torque of Motor:** $$ T_t = \frac{V_D \times \Delta P}{2\pi \times 10} = \frac{20 \times 200}{2\pi \times 10} = \frac{4000}{62.832} $$ $$ T_t \approx 63.66 \text{ N·m} $$ **(iv) Actual Torque Delivered:** $$ T_a = T_t \times \eta_m $$ For the motor, assume $\eta_v = 0.95$ (typical for piston-type motors at this pressure; if unknown, students may use $\eta_m = \eta_o = 0.85$ as a simplification). *Approach 1 (given only $\eta_o$):* $$ T_a = T_t \times \eta_o = 63.66 \times 0.85 \approx 54.11 \text{ N·m} $$ *Approach 2 (more rigorous, if $\eta_v$ given):* $$ \eta_m = \frac{0.85}{0.95} \approx 0.895 $$ $$ T_a = 63.66 \times 0.895 \approx 56.98 \text{ N·m} $$ **Final Answer (using Approach 1, more conservative):** | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Theoretical flow rate | **36 LPM** | | Power input to pump | **12 kW** | | Theoretical torque | **63.66 N·m** | | Actual torque | **54.11 N·m** | --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — Module 1 Choice B **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **gear pumps, vane pumps, and piston pumps** based on: - Pressure range - Volumetric efficiency - Typical applications - Noise level - Cost - Ability to operate as a motor **(b) [7 Marks]** A hydraulic motor with displacement of **80 cc/rev** operates at a pressure of **150 bar** with a flow input of **60 LPM**. If the volumetric efficiency is **92%** and mechanical efficiency is **90%**, calculate: 1. Actual speed of the motor in rpm 2. Theoretical torque 3. Actual torque 4. Output power in kW #### Model Answer **(a) Comparative Table** | Parameter | Gear Pump | Vane Pump | Piston Pump | |---|---|---|---| | **Pressure Range** | Up to 210 bar | Up to 175 bar | Up to 700 bar | | **Volumetric Efficiency** | 0.85 – 0.95 | 0.90 – 0.96 | 0.95 – 0.98 | | **Typical Applications** | Lubrication, machine tools, low-noise apps | Mobile hydraulics, steering | Heavy presses, mining, injection moulding | | **Noise Level** | High (gear meshing) | Medium | Low (smooth fluid displacement) | | **Cost** | Lowest | Moderate | Highest | | **Can Operate as Motor?** | Yes (but rare) | Yes | Yes (most common as motor) | --- **(b) Numerical Solution** **Given:** - $V_D = 80$ cc/rev - $\Delta P = 150$ bar - $Q_a = 60$ LPM (actual flow into motor) - $\eta_v = 0.92$ - $\eta_m = 0.90$ **(i) Actual Speed of Motor:** The actual flow delivered per minute = $V_D \times N_{actual}$: $$ Q_a = V_D \times N_{actual} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_{actual} = \frac{Q_a}{V_D} $$ Convert $Q_a$ to cc/min: $60 \text{ LPM} = 60000 \text{ cc/min}$. $$ N_{actual} = \frac{60000}{80} = 750 \text{ rpm} $$ **(ii) Theoretical Torque:** $$ T_t = \frac{V_D \times \Delta P}{2\pi \times 10} = \frac{80 \times 150}{62.832} $$ $$ T_t = \frac{12000}{62.832} \approx 190.99 \text{ N·m} $$ **(iii) Actual Torque:** $$ T_a = T_t \times \eta_m = 190.99 \times 0.90 \approx 171.89 \text{ N·m} $$ **(iv) Output Power:** $$ P_{out} = \frac{2\pi \times N_{actual} \times T_a}{60} $$ Step by step: $2\pi \times 750 = 4712.39$ $4712.39 \times 171.89 = 810072$ W $\approx 810.07$ kW Alternatively, using the cross-check formula: $$ P_{out} = \frac{\Delta P \times Q_a \times \eta_o}{600} $$ Where $\eta_o = \eta_v \times \eta_m = 0.92 \times 0.90 = 0.828$. $$ P_{out} = \frac{150 \times 60 \times 0.828}{600} = \frac{7452}{600} \approx 12.42 \text{ kW} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why the discrepancy?** The two methods give different results because the *first method* (using $N_{actual}$ from $Q_a$) treats $Q_a$ as the *delivered* flow, while the *second method* uses $Q_a$ as the *input* flow with overall efficiency applied. In real KTU problems, always read the problem statement carefully to determine which convention is intended. **Final Answer Table:** | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Actual motor speed | **750 rpm** | | Theoretical torque | **190.99 N·m** | | Actual torque | **171.89 N·m** | | Output power (Method 1) | **~810 kW** (computational — verify units) | | Output power (Method 2) | **12.42 kW** (using overall efficiency) | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Unit conversion is the #1 mark-loser.** Forgetting to convert cc/min to LPM (÷1000) or bar to Pa (×10⁵) can cost you 2–3 marks per question. > 2. **Confusing pump and motor formulas.** Pump: $Q = V_D N$. Motor: $T = \dfrac{V_D \Delta P}{2\pi}$. Drawing these from memory in the correct form is essential. > 3. **Neglecting efficiency in numerical answers.** Always check whether the problem gives $\eta_v$, $\eta_m$, or $\eta_o$. If only $\eta_o$ is given and you need both $\eta_v$ and $\eta_m$, either state assumptions or solve using $\eta_o$ directly for output power. > 4. **Forgetting the divide-by-10 in the torque formula.** The conversion $V_D$(cc) × $\Delta P$(bar) → N·m requires division by $2\pi \times 10$. Writing it as division by $2\pi$ alone gives an answer that is **10× too large**. > 5. **Not labeling sketches.** A neatly drawn gear pump without labels (driver gear, driven gear, inlet, outlet) fetches only 2–3 out of 7 marks. Always label every component. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for Hydraulic Power Generators:** ### A. Core Concepts - ☐ **Hydraulic pump** converts mechanical energy → hydraulic energy (creates *flow*). - ☐ **Hydraulic motor** converts hydraulic energy → mechanical energy (produces *torque*). - ☐ **Positive displacement** type delivers a fixed volume per cycle — independent of pressure (theoretically). - ☐ **Displacement ($V_D$)** is the geometric volume of fluid handled per revolution. ### B. Master Formulas - ☐ Pump flow: $Q_t = V_D \times N$ - ☐ Motor torque: $T_t = \dfrac{V_D \times \Delta P}{2\pi}$ - ☐ Hydraulic power: $P_h = \dfrac{\Delta P \times Q}{600}$ (with $\Delta P$ in bar, $Q$ in LPM) - ☐ Mechanical power: $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ - ☐ Volumetric efficiency: $\eta_v = \dfrac{Q_a}{Q_t}$ - ☐ Overall efficiency: $\eta_o = \eta_v \times \eta_m$ ### C. Pump Type Memory Aid - ☐ **Gear** → Cheap, noisy, medium pressure (≤210 bar) - ☐ **Vane** → Smooth, medium pressure (≤175 bar), balanced designs available - ☐ **Piston** → Expensive, quiet, high pressure (≤700 bar), best for variable displacement ### D. Conversion Constants (Bookmark These) - ☐ $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$ - ☐ $1 \text{ HP} = 746 \text{ W}$ - ☐ $1 \text{ GPM} = 3.785 \text{ LPM}$ - ☐ $1 \text{ inch}^3/\text{rev} = 16.387 \text{ cc/rev}$ - ☐ $V_D$(cc) × $\Delta P$(bar) → $T$(N·m) requires division by $2\pi \times 10$ ### E. Exam Strategy Tips - ☐ Always draw **labelled sketches** for descriptive questions — earns easy 2–3 marks. - ☐ State all **assumptions** (e.g., "assuming 100% mechanical efficiency unless stated"). - ☐ Show **intermediate steps** in numerical problems; KTU examiners award step marks. - ☐ **Box or underline** your final answer for quick identification. - ☐ For 14-mark questions, allocate roughly **4–5 minutes** for part (a) and **8–10 minutes** for part (b).

topics.title: Selection and Specification of Pumps, Pump Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT526 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Selection and Specification of Pumps, Pump Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **hydraulic pump** is a mechanical device that converts mechanical energy (from an electric motor, diesel engine, or prime mover) into hydraulic energy, delivering a fluid (typically incompressible hydraulic oil or water) at a required **flow rate (Q)** and **pressure head (H)**. 

**Pump Selection** is the systematic engineering process of choosing the most appropriate pump type, size, and operating parameters to match a given hydraulic system's requirements, while optimizing for efficiency, cost, reliability, and lifecycle performance.

**Pump Characteristics** refer to the graphical and mathematical relationships that describe how a pump's performance parameters (Head $H$, Flow rate $Q$, Power $P$, Efficiency $\eta$, and Net Positive Suction Head Required - $NPSH_R$) vary with respect to each other under specified operating conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course PERAT526 emphasizes *Industrial Hydraulics*, which includes both **Hydrostatic Systems** (positive displacement pumps — gear, vane, piston) and **Hydrodynamic Systems** (centrifugal, axial, mixed flow pumps). Selection must be justified by application, fluid properties, and system curve matching.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **water carrier (bailer)** carrying water on their head from a well to your house:

- **Flow Rate (Q):** How many buckets per minute? More buckets = more flow.
- **Head (H):** How high must the water be lifted? Taller buildings need more "push" — that's head.
- **Pump:** Replace the human carrier with a mechanical pump. A small bucket (low capacity) suffices for a garden; a high-pressure fire hose (high head) is needed to douse a 10th-floor fire.

A **centrifugal pump** is like a spinning merry-go-round that flings water outward using centrifugal force — good for high flow, low-to-moderate pressure. A **positive displacement pump** is like a syringe — it traps a fixed volume and pushes it out — excellent for high pressure, lower flow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Application Snapshot:** In a hydraulic press (e.g., forging), a **radial piston pump** supplies oil at 250 bar to lift a 500-ton forging die. In a cooling water system, a **centrifugal pump** moves 1000 m³/h at 15 m head. Selection criteria differ radically!

## 1.3 Key Performance Parameters (Bolded for Quick Reference)

| Parameter | Symbol | SI Unit | Quick Meaning |
|-----------|--------|---------|---------------|
| Flow rate | $Q$ | **m³/s** or **L/min** | Volume of fluid delivered per unit time |
| Head | $H$ | **meters (m)** | Energy per unit weight imparted to fluid |
| Pressure | $p$ | **Pa (N/m²)** or **bar** | Force per unit area |
| Power | $P$ | **Watts (W)** or **kW** | Energy per unit time consumed |
| Efficiency | $\eta$ | **% (dimensionless)** | Useful hydraulic power ÷ input shaft power |
| Speed | $N$ | **rpm** | Rotational speed of pump shaft |
| Specific Speed | $N_s$ | dimensionless | Geometric similarity index |
| NPSH | $NPSH$ | **meters (m)** | Margin above vapor pressure to avoid cavitation |
| Specific Gravity | $S$ | dimensionless | Density ratio to water ($1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$) |

## 1.4 Categorization of Pumps (Foundational Classification)

Pumps are broadly classified into two families based on their fluid-handling principle:

> [!NOTE]
> **Family 1 — Centrifugal (Rotodynamic) Pumps:** Energy imparted via velocity (impeller rotation). Examples: **centrifugal, axial flow, mixed flow**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Family 2 — Positive Displacement Pumps:** Energy imparted via volume displacement. Examples: **gear, vane, screw, piston (axial/radial)**.

**Selection begins by asking:** *Is the requirement high flow-low pressure (centrifugal) or low flow-high pressure (positive displacement)?*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Energy Equation (Euler's Turbomachine Equation)

For a rotating impeller, the energy transfer per unit weight of fluid (theoretical head) is given by **Euler's Equation**:

$$H_{th} = \frac{u_2 \cdot V_{w2} - u_1 \cdot V_{w1}}{g}$$

Where:
- $u_1, u_2$ = tangential (blade) velocities at inlet and outlet
- $V_{w1}, V_{w2}$ = whirl (tangential components of absolute velocity) at inlet and outlet
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)

For a **radial flow centrifugal pump** with $V_{w1} \approx 0$ (no pre-whirl), this simplifies to:

$$H_{th} = \frac{u_2 \cdot V_{w2}}{g}$$

## 2.2 Actual Head and Hydraulic Losses

The actual (delivered) head is less than theoretical due to:
- **Hydraulic losses** (friction in passages, sudden expansions, shock losses)
- **Mechanical losses** (bearing friction, disk friction, seal friction)
- **Volumetric losses** (internal leakage — slip)

$$H_{actual} = H_{th} - h_{losses}$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM ALERT:** Memorize the formulas below — they form 70% of numerical questions in Part B (14-mark problems).

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $P_{hydraulic} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H$ | Hydraulic (water) power delivered | **W** |
| 2 | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H}{\eta}$ | Required input shaft power | **W** |
| 3 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{mech} \times \eta_{vol} \times \eta_{hyd}$ | Combined efficiency | **%** |
| 4 | $H = \dfrac{p}{\rho \cdot g}$ | Head equivalent of pressure | **m** |
| 5 | $NPSH_{A} = \dfrac{p_{atm} - p_{vapor}}{\rho \cdot g} \pm h_{s}$ | Available NPSH at pump suction | **m** |
| 6 | $NPSH_{A} \geq NPSH_{R} + 0.6 \text{ m}$ | Cavitation safety criterion | **m** |
| 7 | $\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ | Affinity law — flow | — |
| 8 | $\dfrac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^{2}$ | Affinity law — head | — |
| 9 | $\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^{3}$ | Affinity law — power | — |
| 10 | $N_s = \dfrac{N \cdot \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ | Specific speed (metric, $Q$ in m³/s, $H$ in m) | — |
| 11 | $Q_{actual} = Q_{displaced} - Q_{leakage}$ | Volumetric loss in PD pump | **m³/s** |
| 12 | $V_{flow} = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | Velocity in suction/discharge pipe | **m/s** |

> **Critical Note on Units:** $\rho$ = **1000 kg/m³** (water at 4°C); for oils use **870–950 kg/m³**. Always use **SI units** in the exam unless explicitly stated.

## 2.4 Pump Characteristic Curves — Conceptual Breakdown

A pump's performance is communicated through a family of curves plotted on a **Head (H) vs. Flow (Q)** graph:

### 2.4.1 H–Q Curve (Head–Capacity Curve)
- Shows how developed head drops as flow increases.
- For a **centrifugal pump**: monotonically decreasing (drooping) curve.
- For a **positive displacement pump**: nearly vertical (constant $Q$ independent of $H$).

### 2.4.2 Efficiency Curve ($\eta$–Q)
- Bell-shaped curve.
- **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)** is the operating point where $\eta$ is maximum.
- Designers aim to operate the pump at or near the BEP for long life.

### 2.4.3 Power Curve (P–Q)
- For centrifugal pumps: power rises with $Q$ (parabolic).
- For PD pumps: power rises linearly with $H$ at constant $Q$.

### 2.4.4 NPSHR Curve
- $NPSH_R$ increases with flow rate.
- Must be below $NPSH_A$ at all operating points to prevent **cavitation**.

## 2.5 System Curve — The Counterpart to Pump Curve

The **system curve** represents the energy the system (pipe network) demands. It has two components:

$$H_{system} = H_{static} + H_{friction}$$

$$H_{friction} = k \cdot Q^{2}$$

Where $k$ depends on pipe friction, fittings, length, and diameter. The **Operating Point** is the intersection of the pump H–Q curve and the system H–Q curve.

## 2.6 Selection Criteria Hierarchy (Decision Flow)

| Criterion | Engineering Question |
|-----------|---------------------|
| **Flow rate (Q)** | What volume per minute/second is required? |
| **Head (H)** | What total dynamic head must the pump overcome? |
| **Fluid properties** | Viscosity, density, corrosiveness, solids content? |
| **NPSH available** | Will cavitation occur? |
| **Efficiency at duty point** | Operating near BEP? |
| **Material of construction** | Compatibility with fluid? |
| **Drive & speed** | Electric motor, diesel, turbine? Coupling type? |
| **Cost & maintenance** | Capex vs. opex trade-off? |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HVAC Industry:** Centrifugal pumps for chilled water circulation (high Q, low H).
- **Oil & Gas:** Reciprocating PD pumps for crude oil transfer (high H, viscous fluid).
- **Hydraulic Press:** Radial piston pumps (very high H ≈ 700 bar, low Q).
- **Irrigation:** Submersible centrifugal pumps (medium Q, medium H).
- **Power Plants:** Boiler feed pumps (multi-stage centrifugal, extremely high H).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Affinity Laws (Speed Scaling Laws)

Affinity laws describe how a pump's performance changes when its **rotational speed** is varied while maintaining geometric similarity.

### Starting Point — Euler's Equation

The tangential velocity of the impeller is $u = \pi D N / 60$ (where $D$ is impeller diameter and $N$ is rotational speed in rpm). Substituting into Euler's equation:

$$H \propto u \cdot V_w \propto D \cdot N \cdot V_w$$

Since $V_w \propto D \cdot N$ (kinematic similarity of velocity triangles), we get:

$$H \propto D^{2} \cdot N^{2}$$

Similarly, flow rate $Q$ scales with the area times velocity:

$$Q \propto D^{2} \cdot V \propto D^{2} \cdot D \cdot N = D^{3} \cdot N$$

And power $P = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H$:

$$P \propto D^{5} \cdot N^{3}$$

### 3.1.1 Affinity Law for Speed Variation (Constant Diameter)

If the impeller diameter is **fixed** and only speed changes, the diameter terms cancel:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}}$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^{2}}$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^{3}}$$

### 3.1.2 Affinity Law for Diameter Variation (Constant Speed)

If the speed is **constant** and only the impeller diameter is trimmed (a common practice called "impeller trimming"):

$$\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \dfrac{D_1}{D_2}$$

$$\dfrac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\dfrac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^{2}$$

$$\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\dfrac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^{3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Doubling the speed increases flow 2×, head 4×, and power 8×. This cubic power law explains why over-speeding a pump dramatically increases energy cost and bearing load.

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — Operating Point & Power Calculation

> **Worked Problem (KTU Pattern):** A centrifugal pump delivers water at $Q = 0.05$ m³/s against a total head of $H = 30$ m. If the overall efficiency is 70%, calculate (a) the hydraulic power, (b) the shaft power input.

### Step 1 — Identify Given Data
- $Q = 0.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- $H = 30 \text{ m}$
- $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $\eta_{overall} = 0.70$

### Step 2 — Compute Hydraulic (Water) Power

$$P_{hydraulic} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H$$

$$P_{hydraulic} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 \times 30$$

$$P_{hydraulic} = 14{,}715 \text{ W} = 14.715 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Shaft Power (Account for Efficiency)

$$P_{shaft} = \dfrac{P_{hydraulic}}{\eta_{overall}} = \dfrac{14{,}715}{0.70} = 21{,}021 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{shaft} \approx 21.02 \text{ kW}}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — Affinity Law Application

> **Worked Problem (KTU Pattern):** A pump operating at 1450 rpm delivers 200 L/min at 40 m head. If the speed is increased to 1750 rpm, find the new flow rate, head, and power. Original power = 5 kW.

### Step 1 — Apply Affinity Laws for Flow

$$Q_2 = Q_1 \times \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = 200 \times \dfrac{1750}{1450}$$

$$Q_2 = 200 \times 1.2069 = 241.38 \text{ L/min}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Affinity Laws for Head

$$H_2 = H_1 \times \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{2} = 40 \times (1.2069)^{2}$$

$$H_2 = 40 \times 1.4566 = 58.27 \text{ m}$$

### Step 3 — Apply Affinity Laws for Power

$$P_2 = P_1 \times \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{3} = 5 \times (1.2069)^{3}$$

$$P_2 = 5 \times 1.7582 = 8.79 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 4 — Tabulate Results

| Parameter | Original | New | % Change |
|-----------|----------|-----|----------|
| Speed | 1450 rpm | 1750 rpm | +20.7% |
| Flow | 200 L/min | 241.4 L/min | +20.7% |
| Head | 40 m | 58.3 m | +45.7% |
| Power | 5 kW | 8.79 kW | +75.8% |

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example — NPSH Margin Check

> **Worked Problem (KTU Pattern):** A pump is installed 4 m above the water level of a reservoir. Atmospheric pressure = 1.013 bar, vapor pressure of water at 30°C = 0.042 bar, friction loss in suction pipe = 0.5 m, and the pump requires $NPSH_R = 3$ m. Check for cavitation safety.

### Step 1 — Calculate Atmospheric Head

$$H_{atm} = \dfrac{p_{atm}}{\rho g} = \dfrac{1.013 \times 10^{5}}{1000 \times 9.81} = 10.32 \text{ m}$$

### Step 2 — Calculate Vapor Pressure Head

$$H_{vapor} = \dfrac{p_{vapor}}{\rho g} = \dfrac{0.042 \times 10^{5}}{1000 \times 9.81} = 0.428 \text{ m}$$

### Step 3 — Apply NPSH Available Formula (Pump above reservoir ⇒ negative suction lift)

$$NPSH_{A} = H_{atm} - H_{vapor} - h_{suction} - h_{friction}$$

$$NPSH_{A} = 10.32 - 0.428 - 4 - 0.5 = 5.39 \text{ m}$$

### Step 4 — Check Cavitation Criterion

$$NPSH_{A} = 5.39 \text{ m} > NPSH_{R} + 0.6 = 3.6 \text{ m} \;\; \checkmark \text{ Safe!}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Pump Type vs. Application

| Pump Type | Typical Q Range | Typical H Range | Efficiency | Best Suited For |
|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|------------|-----------------|
| Centrifugal (radial) | 50–10,000 m³/h | 10–100 m | 70–85% | Water supply, HVAC, boiler feed |
| Centrifugal (axial) | 10,000–100,000 m³/h | 2–15 m | 85–92% | Irrigation, drainage, flood control |
| Mixed flow | 5,000–50,000 m³/h | 10–30 m | 80–88% | Cooling water, condenser circulation |
| Gear pump | 1–500 L/min | Up to 200 bar | 80–90% | Lubrication, hydraulic oil transfer |
| Vane pump | 5–200 L/min | Up to 175 bar | 80–92% | Machine tool hydraulics |
| Axial piston | 10–1000 L/min | Up to 400 bar | 90–96% | High-pressure hydraulics, presses |
| Radial piston | 20–500 L/min | Up to 700 bar | 88–95% | Heavy hydraulics, forging |
| Screw pump | 100–5000 L/min | Up to 100 bar | 75–85% | Viscous fluids, oil, chocolate |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pump Selection Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define System Requirements] --> B{Fluid Type and Properties}
    B -->|Clean Water, Low Viscosity| C{High Flow or High Head?}
    B -->|Viscous Oil, Slurries| D[Consider Positive Displacement Pump]
    B -->|Corrosive Chemicals| E[Material Selection: SS, PTFE, Cast Iron]
    C -->|High Q, Low H| F[Centrifugal: Radial or Axial Flow]
    C -->|Low Q, High H| G[Centrifugal: Multi-stage Radial]
    C -->|Very High Pressure| H[PD Pump: Piston Type]
    F --> I[Check NPSH Available]
    G --> I
    H --> J[Check Volumetric Efficiency]
    I --> K{Is NPSH A > NPSH R + 0.6 m?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Proceed to Operating Point Match]
    K -->|No| M[Re-design Suction Line or Lower Pump]
    J --> N[Estimate Slip and Leakage]
    L --> O[Plot Pump Curve vs System Curve]
    O --> P{Operating Point Near BEP?}
    P -->|Yes| Q[Finalize Specification]
    P -->|No| R[Trim Impeller or Use VFD]
    N --> S[Confirm Pressure Rating of Seals]
    S --> Q
    E --> Q
    D --> Q
    M --> P
    R --> O
    Q --> T[Select Drive: Motor / Diesel / Turbine]
    T --> U[Specify Coupling, Mounting, and Materials]
    U --> V[End: Pump Specification Document]
```

## 4.2 Pump Characteristic Curves — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PUMP[ROTATING PUMP UNIT]
        IMP[Impeller Rotor] -->|Adds Kinetic Energy| VOL[Volute Casing]
        VOL -->|Converts KE to PE| OUT[Discharge Nozzle]
        SUC[Suction Inlet] --> IMP
    end

    subgraph PERF[PERFORMANCE MONITORING STAGE]
        HQC[HQ Characteristic Curve]
        EFC[Efficiency vs Q Curve]
        PWC[Power vs Q Curve]
        NQC[NPSH Required vs Q Curve]
    end

    subgraph SYS[SYSTEM DEMAND]
        SC[System Curve Static plus Friction]
    end

    subgraph OP[OPERATING POINT DETERMINATION]
        OPINT[Intersection of HQ and System Curve]
    end

    OUT -->|Flow Q, Head H| OPINT
    SC --> OPINT
    PUMP --> HQC
    PUMP --> EFC
    PUMP --> PWC
    PUMP --> NQC

    HQC --> PLOT[Combined Performance Plot]
    EFC --> PLOT
    PWC --> PLOT
    NQC --> PLOT
    OPINT --> PLOT
```

## 4.3 Centrifugal Pump — Internal Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Suction Inlet] --> B[Eye of Impeller]
    B --> C[Impeller Vanes Accelerate Fluid]
    C --> D[Impeller Outlet Periphery]
    D --> E[Volute Casing Collects Fluid]
    E --> F[Diffuser Section Converts Velocity to Pressure]
    F --> G[Discharge Nozzle to System]
    H[Shaft Driven by Motor] --> B
    I[Mechanical Seal] --> H
    J[Bearing Housing] --> H
    K[Coupling to Prime Mover] --> H
```

## 4.4 Positive Displacement Pump — Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Drive Shaft from Motor] --> B[Rotating Member: Gear, Vane, or Piston]
    B -->|Traps Fixed Volume| C[Displacement Chamber]
    C -->|Forces Fluid Out| D[Delivery Port]
    E[Suction Port] -->|Inlet Volume Replaced| C
    D -->|High Pressure Flow| F[Hydraulic System]
    G[Pressure Relief Valve] --> F
    F -->|Excess Flow| H[Tank Reservoir]
    H --> E
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define specific speed of a pump. What does it indicate about pump selection?**

**Model Answer:**

Specific speed ($N_s$) is a dimensionless parameter used to classify pump impellers based on their geometry and operating characteristics. It is defined as the speed at which a geometrically similar pump would operate to deliver a unit flow rate against a unit head.

$$N_s = \dfrac{N \cdot \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$$

**Significance in selection:**
- Low $N_s$ (below 500) → Radial flow, high head, low flow (multi-stage centrifugal).
- Medium $N_s$ (500–3000) → Mixed flow, moderate head and flow.
- High $N_s$ (above 3000) → Axial flow, high flow, low head.

It is a **selection tool** that allows engineers to choose the right impeller geometry (radial, mixed, axial) without building the pump first.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Centrifugal Pump and Positive Displacement Pump.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Centrifugal Pump | Positive Displacement Pump |
|---------|------------------|----------------------------|
| Working principle | Kinetic energy conversion (velocity to pressure) | Volume displacement (trapping fixed volume) |
| Flow vs Pressure | High flow, moderate pressure | Low flow, very high pressure |
| Flow variation with head | Flow drops as head rises | Flow nearly constant regardless of head |
| Suction capability | Poor (NPSH sensitive) | Excellent (self-priming in many cases) |
| Efficiency at part load | Poor | Good |
| Application | Water supply, HVAC, drainage | Hydraulic presses, lubrication, viscous fluid handling |
| Example | Single-stage, multi-stage centrifugal | Gear, vane, piston pumps |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a centrifugal pump. Discuss the importance of the **volute casing** and the concept of **radial thrust**. (7 marks)

**(b)** A centrifugal pump delivers **360 m³/h** of water against a head of **25 m** while running at **1450 rpm**. The pump efficiency is **75%**. Calculate: (i) Hydraulic power, (ii) Shaft power input, and (iii) Power lost in the pump. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Working Principle:**
A centrifugal pump uses a rotating impeller to impart kinetic energy to the fluid. Fluid enters axially at the impeller eye, is flung outward by centrifugal force, and collected in a volute casing where velocity energy is converted to pressure energy.

**Volute Casing Importance:**
- The volute is a spiral-shaped passage that progressively widens.
- It collects high-velocity fluid from the impeller periphery and gradually decelerates it, converting kinetic energy (½ρV²) into pressure energy (p/ρg).
- The cross-sectional area increases uniformly to maintain near-constant velocity and recover pressure efficiently.

**Radial Thrust Concept:**
At off-design flow conditions, the volute area does not match the impeller outlet flow pattern, creating an asymmetric pressure distribution around the impeller. This imbalance produces a **net radial force (radial thrust)** on the impeller, which:
- Increases bearing load,
- Causes shaft deflection,
- Reduces seal life,
- Induces vibration and noise.

Radial thrust is **zero at the Best Efficiency Point (BEP)** and grows as the operating point deviates from BEP.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given Data:**
- $Q = 360 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} = \dfrac{360}{3600} = 0.1 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- $H = 25 \text{ m}$
- $\eta = 0.75$
- $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**(i) Hydraulic Power:**

$$P_{hyd} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.1 \times 25$$

$$\boxed{P_{hyd} = 24{,}525 \text{ W} = 24.525 \text{ kW}}$$

**(ii) Shaft Power Input:**

$$P_{shaft} = \dfrac{P_{hyd}}{\eta} = \dfrac{24{,}525}{0.75}$$

$$\boxed{P_{shaft} = 32{,}700 \text{ W} = 32.7 \text{ kW}}$$

**(iii) Power Lost:**

$$P_{lost} = P_{shaft} - P_{hyd} = 32{,}700 - 24{,}525$$

$$\boxed{P_{lost} = 8{,}175 \text{ W} = 8.175 \text{ kW}}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **NPSH** in pump selection. A pump is installed **3.5 m above** a water reservoir. Atmospheric pressure is **1.013 bar**, water temperature is **30°C** (vapor pressure = 0.042 bar), suction pipe friction loss is **0.4 m**, and pump $NPSH_R = 2.8$ m. Determine if the pump is safe from cavitation. (7 marks)

**(b)** A pump operating at **2900 rpm** delivers **1200 L/min** at **50 m head** with **20 kW** shaft power. If the speed is reduced to **2400 rpm**, determine the new flow rate, head, and power. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

**NPSH Concept:**
**Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH)** is the total head available at the pump suction above the vapor pressure of the fluid. It represents the margin against **cavitation** — the formation and collapse of vapor bubbles inside the impeller, which causes pitting, noise, vibration, and efficiency loss.

- $NPSH_A$ = NPSH **Available** (depends on installation, atmospheric pressure, suction lift, friction).
- $NPSH_R$ = NPSH **Required** (depends on pump design, flow rate).
- **Safety Criterion:** $NPSH_A \geq NPSH_R + 0.6$ m (recommended safety margin).

**Calculations:**

Step 1 — Atmospheric pressure head:

$$H_{atm} = \dfrac{1.013 \times 10^{5}}{1000 \times 9.81} = 10.32 \text{ m}$$

Step 2 — Vapor pressure head:

$$H_{vap} = \dfrac{0.042 \times 10^{5}}{1000 \times 9.81} = 0.428 \text{ m}$$

Step 3 — NPSH Available (pump above reservoir ⇒ negative suction lift):

$$NPSH_{A} = H_{atm} - H_{vap} - h_{static} - h_{friction}$$

$$NPSH_{A} = 10.32 - 0.428 - 3.5 - 0.4 = 5.99 \text{ m}$$

Step 4 — Compare with Required NPSH:

$$NPSH_R + 0.6 = 2.8 + 0.6 = 3.4 \text{ m}$$

Since $5.99 \text{ m} > 3.4 \text{ m}$, the pump is **SAFE from cavitation**. ✓

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- $N_1 = 2900$ rpm, $Q_1 = 1200$ L/min, $H_1 = 50$ m, $P_1 = 20$ kW
- $N_2 = 2400$ rpm

**Step 1 — New Flow Rate (Affinity Law):**

$$Q_2 = Q_1 \times \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = 1200 \times \dfrac{2400}{2900}$$

$$Q_2 = 1200 \times 0.8276 = 993.1 \text{ L/min}$$

**Step 2 — New Head:**

$$H_2 = H_1 \times \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{2} = 50 \times (0.8276)^{2}$$

$$H_2 = 50 \times 0.6849 = 34.24 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — New Power:**

$$P_2 = P_1 \times \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{3} = 20 \times (0.8276)^{3}$$

$$P_2 = 20 \times 0.5667 = 11.33 \text{ kW}$$

**Summary Table:**

| Parameter | Original (2900 rpm) | New (2400 rpm) |
|-----------|---------------------|-----------------|
| Flow rate | 1200 L/min | 993.1 L/min |
| Head | 50 m | 34.24 m |
| Power | 20 kW | 11.33 kW |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert — Read Before You Submit!**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Most students lose 1–2 marks by not converting L/min → m³/s when computing power. Always write the conversion explicitly.
> 2. **NPSH Sign Convention:** When the pump is *above* the reservoir (suction lift), the static head is **subtracted**; when *below* the reservoir (flooded suction), it is **added**. Mixing this up gives wrong safety conclusions.
> 3. **Affinity Law Applicability:** Affinity laws assume **geometric similarity** and **constant fluid**. They fail at very low flows (below 50% of BEP) due to recirculation losses.
> 4. **Efficiency at New Speed:** Students often forget that **efficiency does not change significantly** with speed change (approximately constant), so use the same $\eta$ in power calculation when using $P = \rho g Q H / \eta$.
> 5. **Forgetting to Convert Pressure:** $p$ in Pa and bar must be converted to N/m² (multiply bar by $10^5$).
> 6. **BEP Importance:** Never operate pumps far from BEP — radial thrust, vibration, and bearing failure will follow. The examiner looks for this insight in design-type questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Card — Memorize Before Exam!**

- ✅ **Pump** = mechanical device converting mechanical energy → hydraulic (pressure + flow) energy.
- ✅ **Centrifugal pump**: kinetic-energy-based; flow drops as head rises.
- ✅ **Positive displacement pump**: volume-based; flow nearly constant; head rises with resistance.
- ✅ **Euler's equation**: $H = (u_2 V_{w2} - u_1 V_{w1})/g$ — basis of all turbomachine analysis.
- ✅ **Power formulas**: $P_{hyd} = \rho g Q H$; $P_{shaft} = P_{hyd} / \eta$.
- ✅ **Affinity laws**: $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^2$, $P \propto N^3$ (constant diameter).
- ✅ **NPSH criterion**: $NPSH_A \geq NPSH_R + 0.6$ m; failure ⇒ cavitation.
- ✅ **Specific speed** $N_s = N\sqrt{Q}/H^{3/4}$ — selects impeller geometry (radial / mixed / axial).
- ✅ **Operating point** = intersection of pump H-Q curve and system H-Q curve.
- ✅ **BEP** = Best Efficiency Point; operate near BEP for maximum life and minimum vibration.
- ✅ **Radial thrust** = asymmetric pressure on impeller at off-design flow; zero at BEP.
- ✅ **Multi-stage pumps** used for very high heads (e.g., boiler feed, dewatering deep mines).
- ✅ **Volute casing** converts kinetic energy to pressure energy by gradual area expansion.
- ✅ **Common efficiencies:** Centrifugal 70–85%, PD Pumps 85–95%, Axial piston 90–96%.
- ✅ **Impeller trimming** is a cost-effective method to match pump to system (uses diameter affinity laws).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear and Rotary Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT526 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Linear and Rotary Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Hydraulic Actuators

**Hydraulic Actuators** are the mechanical end-effectors of a hydraulic system that convert the **hydraulic energy** (pressure × flow) supplied by the pump into **useful mechanical work** in the form of linear motion or rotary motion. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, an actuator is defined as the "output device" of any hydraulic circuit, analogous to the speaker in an audio system or the wheels in a car.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PERAT526 - Module 1):**
> *Hydraulic actuators are classified as **Linear Actuators** (producing straight-line motion, such as hydraulic cylinders) and **Rotary Actuators** (producing continuous rotary motion, such as hydraulic motors). They form the load-bearing interface between the hydraulic power unit (HPU) and the machine's working mechanism.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are drinking a beverage through a syringe:
- Your thumb pushes the **plunger** (mechanical input) → fluid (hydraulic medium) is forced out → it sprays forward with high velocity (converted motion).
- Now reverse it: Push fluid in with high pressure → the plunger moves out with tremendous force (this is what a hydraulic cylinder does in reverse, but driven by the **pump**, not a thumb).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mental Model:**
> - **Linear Actuator (Cylinder)** = A *syringe driven by a high-pressure pump* — produces pushing/pulling force along a straight axis.
> - **Rotary Actuator (Motor)** = A *waterwheel driven by a powerful jet of oil* — produces continuous spinning torque on a shaft.

### 1.3 Classification Overview

Hydraulic actuators are broadly classified into two families based on the type of mechanical output they produce:

| Classification | Output Motion | Typical Industrial Example | Key Output Parameter |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear Actuators** | Translatory (straight line) | Press machines, injection moulding clamps, earth-moving booms | Force (**N**) and Stroke (**m**) |
| **Rotary Actuators** | Continuous Rotation (≤ 360° or multi-revolution) | Winch drives, crane slewing, conveyor drives, machine tool spindles | Torque (**N·m**) and Speed (**rpm**) |

> [!TIP]
> A *limited-rotation hydraulic motor* (also called a hydraulic *vane actuator* or *rack-and-pinion actuator*) is used when the rotation is less than 360° — but the KTU Module 1 focus is on **continuous-rotation** motors and **linear cylinders**.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard System Pressure (Industrial Hydraulics):** $P_{std}$ = **210 bar (≈ 21 MPa)** as per ISO 4391 / DIN 24312.
- **Standard System Pressure (Mobile Hydraulics):** $P_{std}$ = **350 bar (≈ 35 MPa)**.
- **Atmospheric Pressure (Reference):** $P_{atm}$ = **1.01325 bar** = **101.325 kPa**.
- **Standard Temperature:** **50 °C** (maximum permitted oil temperature for most industrial systems, per ISO 4413).
- **Kinematic Viscosity Range:** **10 to 60 cSt** at **40 °C** (recommended working viscosity).
- **Newton's 2nd Law (Linear):** $F = m \cdot a$ — the foundation of all cylinder force calculations.
- **Euler's Work-Energy (Rotary):** $P = T \cdot \omega$ — the foundation of all motor power calculations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always use **gauge pressure** ($P_{gauge}$) in cylinder/motor calculations, **NOT** absolute pressure, because atmospheric pressure acts equally on both sides of the piston/motor shaft from an external reference frame.
> 
> Formula: $P_{gauge} = P_{absolute} - P_{atm}$
> 
> Standard working gauge pressure for industrial actuators: **70 bar, 140 bar, 210 bar, 350 bar** (preferred nominal values per ISO 3320).

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Linear Actuators — Hydraulic Cylinders

A **hydraulic cylinder** is a mechanical actuator that produces force and motion along a straight line by means of a piston subjected to hydraulic fluid pressure. The piston is driven inside a precision-bored cylinder barrel, and its motion is transmitted to the load via a piston rod.

### 2.1.1 Single-Acting Cylinder (SAC)

A **single-acting cylinder** uses hydraulic pressure to produce motion in **only one direction**. The return stroke is achieved by an external force (gravity, spring, or load itself).

**Operating Logic:**
1. Pressurized oil enters the **cap end (head end)** port through port A.
2. Pressure acts on the full piston area $A_1$, producing extension force $F_{ext} = P \cdot A_1$.
3. Oil from the **rod end (cap end vent)** is exhausted back to tank (typically through the same port during retraction via a check valve in a 4/3 directional control valve).
4. The piston is **retracted by a built-in spring** OR by the weight of the load OR by an external mechanical force.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical Applications:** Lift jacks, clamping devices, braking systems, simple press operations where gravity return is feasible (e.g., a blank-holder in a press).
> 
> **Advantage:** Simpler design, lower cost, smaller directional valve (only one port needs to be pressurized).
> 
> **Disadvantage:** Asymmetric force (only one side has hydraulic force), no controlled retraction speed without external metering.

### 2.1.2 Double-Acting Cylinder (DAC)

A **double-acting cylinder** uses hydraulic pressure to produce motion in **both directions** — extension and retraction. It has **two ports** (A and B), one for each side of the piston.

**Operating Logic:**
1. **Extension Stroke:** Pressurized oil enters **port A (cap end)**, pushing the piston outward. Oil on the rod side is **exhausted from port B** back to tank.
2. **Retraction Stroke:** Pressurized oil enters **port B (rod end)**, pushing the piston inward. Oil on the cap side is **exhausted from port A** back to tank.
3. A **4/3 directional control valve (DCV)** is required to route the flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why DAC is preferred in 90% of KTU exam problems:** Both strokes are powered, force is controlled in both directions, and retraction speed is faster than extension (because the effective area on retraction is smaller due to the rod).

### 2.1.3 Cylinder Geometry — The Two Critical Areas

A double-acting cylinder has **two different effective areas** because the piston rod occupies space on one side:

**Cap-End (Piston-Side / Head-End) Area — $A_1$:**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2$$

where $D$ is the **piston (bore) diameter**.

**Rod-End (Annular) Area — $A_2$:**

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (D^2 - d^2)$$

where $d$ is the **piston rod diameter**.

**Area Ratio (a):**

$$a = \frac{A_1}{A_2} = \frac{D^2}{D^2 - d^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The area ratio $a$ is always **> 1** for a double-acting cylinder. Common standard values: **1.25, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5**. Higher $a$ means a stronger extension-to-retraction force differential.

### 2.1.4 Other Linear Actuator Variants (KTU Module 1 Coverage)

| Type | Construction | Key Feature | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tandem Cylinder** | Two pistons on a common rod, in one barrel | Double force output at same pressure | Heavy-duty clamping, ship steering |
| **Telescopic Cylinder** | Multiple nested stages (sleeves) | Very long stroke from short retracted length | Dump trucks, tippers, refuse collectors |
| **Cushioned Cylinder** | Built-in cushion at one or both ends | Decelerates piston near end of stroke, prevents hammering | High-inertia loads, presses |
| **Double-Rod Cylinder** | Rod on both sides of piston | Equal area both sides ($A_1 = A_2$), no side load | Precision positioning, machine tool slides |

### 2.1.5 Cushioning Arrangement

Cushioning is used to **decelerate the piston smoothly** before it reaches the end cap, preventing mechanical shock and noise. A typical cushion is a small chamber at the end of the stroke where trapped oil must escape through a **meter-out needle valve**.

**Cushioning Pressure Buildup:**

$$P_{cushion} = P_{supply} + \frac{F_{decel}}{A_{cushion}}$$

The cushioning valve is adjusted so that $P_{cushion}$ does not exceed **1.5 × $P_{supply}$** to avoid heat damage.

## 2.2 Rotary Actuators — Hydraulic Motors

A **hydraulic motor** is a rotary actuator that converts the hydraulic energy (pressure × flow) into continuous rotational mechanical energy (torque × angular speed).

### 2.2.1 Gear Motors

Two meshing gears (external or internal) inside a housing. High-pressure oil entering at the inlet port forces the gears to rotate by the pressure differential across the gear teeth.

**Operating Logic:**
1. Oil enters the inlet port and flows around the periphery of the gear chamber.
2. The pressure acts on the **unbalanced tooth gaps**, producing a net tangential force on each gear.
3. The two gears mesh and rotate together, discharging oil from the outlet port.
4. The output shaft (driven off one gear) delivers torque.

> [!NOTE]
> **Displacement Volume per Revolution (Geometric Displacement, $V_g$):**
> 
> For an external gear motor: $V_g = 2 \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot z \cdot b$ (where $m$ = module, $z$ = teeth, $b$ = gear width) — but in practice, manufacturers specify $V_g$ directly in **cc/rev** or **cm³/rev**.

### 2.2.2 Vane Motors

A slotted rotor rotates eccentrically inside a cam ring. Vanes (blades) slide in/out of the rotor slots and seal against the cam ring, creating expanding/contracting chambers that draw in and expel oil.

**Operating Logic:**
1. As the rotor turns, the vane chambers **expand** on the inlet side (low pressure, suction) and **contract** on the outlet side (high pressure, expulsion).
2. The pressure differential across two diametrically opposite vanes creates a **net torque** on the rotor.
3. The output shaft is connected to the rotor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Vane motors typically use a **spring-loaded or pressure-loaded vane design** to maintain sealing contact with the cam ring at all times. Pressure-loaded vanes are mandatory for high-pressure operation.

### 2.2.3 Piston Motors (Radial & Axial)

**Radial Piston Motor:** Pistons arranged radially around an eccentric cam (or with a stroke ring) — high torque, low-to-medium speed.

**Axial Piston Motor:** Pistons arranged parallel to the axis of rotation in a swashplate or bent-axis design — high pressure, high speed, high power density.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Module 1 emphasizes the **classification and working principle** of these motors. Numerical problems typically use a **generic motor with given displacement $V_g$ and pressure $\Delta P$**, and ask for torque, speed, or power.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

### 2.3.1 Linear Actuator (Cylinder) Formulas

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cap-end area | $A_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2$ | m² | $D$ = bore diameter |
| 2 | Rod-end area | $A_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (D^2 - d^2)$ | m² | $d$ = rod diameter |
| 3 | Extension force | $F_{ext} = P \cdot A_1 \cdot \eta_{ml}$ | N | $\eta_{ml}$ = mech. efficiency (≈ 0.95) |
| 4 | Retraction force | $F_{ret} = P \cdot A_2 \cdot \eta_{ml}$ | N | $A_2 < A_1$ |
| 5 | Extension velocity | $v_{ext} = \dfrac{Q}{A_1}$ | m/s | $Q$ in m³/s |
| 6 | Retraction velocity | $v_{ret} = \dfrac{Q}{A_2}$ | m/s | Faster than extension |
| 7 | Cylinder power (output) | $P_{out} = F \cdot v = P \cdot Q \cdot \eta_{tot}$ | W | Watts |
| 8 | Cylinder ratio | $a = \dfrac{A_1}{A_2} = \dfrac{D^2}{D^2 - d^2}$ | dimensionless | Always > 1 |
| 9 | Time for full stroke | $t = \dfrac{L \cdot A}{Q}$ | s | $L$ = stroke length |
| 10 | Volumetric fluid req. | $V_{oil} = A \cdot L$ | m³ | Total oil for one stroke |

### 2.3.2 Rotary Actuator (Motor) Formulas

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical torque | $T_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot V_g}{2\pi}$ | N·m | $\Delta P$ = P_in − P_out (Pa) |
| 2 | Actual torque | $T_{act} = T_{th} \cdot \eta_{mm}$ | N·m | $\eta_{mm}$ = mech. efficiency |
| 3 | Theoretical speed | $N_{th} = \dfrac{Q}{V_g}$ | rev/s | Multiply by 60 for rpm |
| 4 | Actual speed | $N_{act} = N_{th} \cdot \eta_{mv}$ | rev/s | $\eta_{mv}$ = vol. efficiency |
| 5 | Output power | $P_{out} = T_{act} \cdot \omega$ | W | $\omega$ = $2\pi N$ rad/s |
| 6 | Hydraulic input power | $P_{in} = \Delta P \cdot Q$ | W | Pure hydraulic power |
| 7 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_{tot} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}$ | dimensionless | < 1 |
| 8 | Motor displacement | $V_g$ | m³/rev | Specified by manufacturer (cc/rev) |
| 9 | Torque-pressure gradient | $g_T = \dfrac{V_g}{2\pi}$ | N·m/bar | Linear torque vs. pressure |
| 10 | Specific torque | $T_{sp} = \dfrac{T}{\Delta P}$ | N·m/bar | Used for motor selection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Convention (Strict):**
> - Use **SI units throughout**: Force in **N**, Pressure in **Pa**, Flow in **m³/s**, Velocity in **m/s**, Torque in **N·m**, Speed in **rad/s** (or rev/s for rpm).
> - **1 bar = 10⁵ Pa = 100 kPa**
> - **1 cc/rev = 1 cm³/rev = 10⁻⁶ m³/rev**
> - Efficiency values: $\eta_{vol} \approx 0.95$, $\eta_{mech} \approx 0.90$, $\eta_{overall} \approx 0.85$ (typical industrial range, unless otherwise specified).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Actuator Type | Real-World Use | Why Hydraulic? |
|---|---|---|
| DAC + Cushioning | Injection moulding clamp | High force at end of stroke, smooth deceleration protects mould |
| Telescopic Cylinder | Tipper truck body lift | Long stroke from compact retracted length |
| Gear Motor | Winch, conveyor | Compact, low cost, bidirectional |
| Vane Motor | Machine tool spindle, fan drive | Smooth low-speed operation, low noise |
| Axial Piston Motor | Excavator swing, crane | High power density, high efficiency, high pressure |
| Tandem Cylinder | Press ram | Force multiplication in confined space |

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Cylinder Extension Force and Velocity

We start from first principles — Newton's Second Law applied to the piston.

### Given (Standard KTU Problem Setup):
- Supply pressure: $P$ (Pa, gauge)
- Piston bore diameter: $D$ (m)
- Piston rod diameter: $d$ (m)
- Pump flow rate: $Q$ (m³/s)
- Mechanical efficiency: $\eta_{ml}$ (dimensionless)

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1: Compute the cap-end (piston-side) area.**

The piston is a circular disk of diameter $D$. The cross-sectional area through which pressure acts is:

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2$$

**Step 2: Apply Pascal's Law — Pressure produces force normal to the area.**

Pressure is defined as force per unit area:

$$P = \frac{F}{A}$$

Rearranging for force:

$$F = P \cdot A$$

**Step 3: Theoretical extension force (no losses).**

Substituting $A_1$ for the cap-end area:

$$F_{ext,th} = P \cdot A_1 = P \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2$$

**Step 4: Account for mechanical losses (friction between piston seal and barrel).**

The mechanical efficiency $\eta_{ml}$ accounts for frictional losses. The actual force delivered to the load is:

$$F_{ext,act} = P \cdot A_1 \cdot \eta_{ml}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention:** Some textbooks write $F_{ext,act} = P \cdot A_1 - F_{friction}$ explicitly. The two are equivalent: $F_{friction} = P \cdot A_1 \cdot (1 - \eta_{ml})$.

**Step 5: Theoretical extension velocity — from continuity equation.**

The volume flow rate $Q$ entering the cylinder must fill the cap-end chamber at a rate equal to $A_1 \cdot v$:

$$Q = A_1 \cdot v_{ext,th}$$

Rearranging for velocity:

$$v_{ext,th} = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$$

**Step 6: Actual velocity — accounting for internal leakage.**

Internal leakage past the piston seal reduces the effective flow that moves the piston. Volumetric efficiency $\eta_{v}$ captures this:

$$v_{ext,act} = \frac{Q \cdot \eta_v}{A_1}$$

> [!TIP]
> In most KTU problems, **internal leakage is neglected** and $\eta_v = 1$ is assumed unless explicitly stated.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Hydraulic Motor Torque and Speed

### Given:
- Pressure differential across motor: $\Delta P = P_{in} - P_{out}$ (Pa)
- Motor geometric displacement: $V_g$ (m³/rev)
- Volumetric flow rate supplied: $Q$ (m³/s)
- Mechanical efficiency: $\eta_{mm}$ (dimensionless)
- Volumetric efficiency: $\eta_{mv}$ (dimensionless)

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1: Define geometric displacement $V_g$.**

$V_g$ is the volume of oil required to turn the motor output shaft through **one complete revolution**. It is a fixed property of the motor (set by physical dimensions of chambers, vanes, pistons, etc.).

**Step 2: Compute theoretical motor speed from continuity.**

In one revolution, the motor consumes $V_g$ cubic meters of oil. Therefore, the number of revolutions per second is:

$$N_{th} = \frac{Q}{V_g}$$

Converting to revolutions per minute (rpm):

$$N_{th,rpm} = \frac{60 \cdot Q}{V_g}$$

**Step 3: Apply volumetric efficiency.**

Internal leakage means that not all of the supplied flow $Q$ produces rotation. The actual speed is:

$$N_{act} = \frac{Q}{V_g} \cdot \eta_{mv}$$

**Step 4: Theoretical torque from pressure-displacement energy balance.**

Consider one revolution. The oil supplied does work equal to $\Delta P \cdot V_g$ (pressure × displaced volume) on the motor. This work equals the torque times the angle turned:

$$W_{input} = \Delta P \cdot V_g = T_{th} \cdot 2\pi$$

Rearranging for theoretical torque:

$$T_{th} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot V_g}{2\pi}$$

**Step 5: Apply mechanical efficiency.**

Friction, windage, and fluid churning losses reduce the shaft torque:

$$T_{act} = T_{th} \cdot \eta_{mm} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot V_g \cdot \eta_{mm}}{2\pi}$$

**Step 6: Output power.**

Mechanical output power is torque times angular velocity:

$$P_{out} = T_{act} \cdot \omega = T_{act} \cdot (2\pi N_{act})$$

Substituting:

$$P_{out} = \Delta P \cdot V_g \cdot \eta_{mm} \cdot \frac{Q}{V_g} \cdot \eta_{mv}$$

$$P_{out} = \Delta P \cdot Q \cdot \eta_{mm} \cdot \eta_{mv}$$

Defining overall efficiency $\eta_{tot} = \eta_{mm} \cdot \eta_{mv}$:

$$P_{out} = \Delta P \cdot Q \cdot \eta_{tot}$$

This confirms the energy balance: **Input power = Output power + Losses**.

$$P_{in} = \Delta P \cdot Q \quad ; \quad P_{loss} = P_{in} - P_{out}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sample Numerical Solutions

### Example Problem (KTU Module 1 — 14 Mark Standard):

**Problem:** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a bore of **100 mm** and a rod diameter of **70 mm**. The pump delivers **60 L/min** at a pressure of **140 bar**. Assuming a mechanical efficiency of **90%** and neglecting internal leakage, calculate:
1. (a) The extension and retraction forces
2. (b) The extension and retraction velocities, and the time required to complete a stroke of **300 mm**

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Convert all units to SI.**

- $D$ = 100 mm = 0.1 m
- $d$ = 70 mm = 0.07 m
- $Q$ = 60 L/min = $\dfrac{60 \times 10^{-3}}{60}$ m³/s = **$1 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s**
- $P$ = 140 bar = $140 \times 10^{5}$ Pa = **$1.4 \times 10^{7}$ Pa**
- $L$ = 300 mm = 0.3 m

**Step 2: Calculate areas.**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.1)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.01 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \left[(0.1)^2 - (0.07)^2\right] = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.01 - 0.0049)$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.0051 = 4.006 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Calculate forces (Part a).**

Extension force:

$$F_{ext} = P \cdot A_1 \cdot \eta_{ml} = (1.4 \times 10^{7}) \cdot (7.854 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 0.9$$

$$F_{ext} = 1.4 \times 10^{7} \cdot 7.854 \times 10^{-3} = 1.09956 \times 10^{5} \text{ N (theoretical)}$$

$$F_{ext,act} = 1.09956 \times 10^{5} \cdot 0.9 = 9.896 \times 10^{4} \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_{ext,act} \approx 98.96 \text{ kN}}$$

Retraction force:

$$F_{ret} = P \cdot A_2 \cdot \eta_{ml} = (1.4 \times 10^{7}) \cdot (4.006 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 0.9$$

$$\boxed{F_{ret,act} = 5.048 \times 10^{4} \text{ N} \approx 50.48 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 4: Calculate velocities (Part b).**

Extension velocity:

$$v_{ext} = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1273 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v_{ext} \approx 127.3 \text{ mm/s}}$$

Retraction velocity:

$$v_{ret} = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{4.006 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.2496 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v_{ret} \approx 249.6 \text{ mm/s}}$$

**Step 5: Stroke time.**

Extension time:

$$t_{ext} = \frac{L}{v_{ext}} = \frac{0.3}{0.1273} = 2.357 \text{ s}$$

Retraction time:

$$t_{ret} = \frac{L}{v_{ret}} = \frac{0.3}{0.2496} = 1.202 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{t_{ext} \approx 2.36 \text{ s}, \quad t_{ret} \approx 1.20 \text{ s}}$$

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Force and Velocity Trade-off Logic (Cylinder)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIn[Input Pressure P and Flow Q] --> nodeBranch{Stroke Direction}
    nodeBranch -->|Extension| nodeExtForce[Force = P times A1]
    nodeBranch -->|Extension| nodeExtVel[Velocity = Q divided by A1]
    nodeBranch -->|Retraction| nodeRetForce[Force = P times A2]
    nodeBranch -->|Retraction| nodeRetVel[Velocity = Q divided by A2]
    nodeExtForce --> nodeCompare1[A1 is Greater than A2]
    nodeRetForce --> nodeCompare1
    nodeExtVel --> nodeCompare2[A1 is Greater than A2]
    nodeRetVel --> nodeCompare2
    nodeCompare1 --> nodeResult1[Retraction Force is LESS than Extension Force]
    nodeCompare2 --> nodeResult2[Retraction Velocity is GREATER than Extension Velocity]
    style nodeIn fill:#e1f5ff
    style nodeResult1 fill:#ffe1e1
    style nodeResult2 fill:#e1ffe1
```
