# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define a double-acting hydraulic cylinder. Why is it preferred over a single-acting cylinder in most industrial applications?**

**Model Answer:**

A **double-acting hydraulic cylinder** is a linear hydraulic actuator in which hydraulic fluid under pressure can be applied to **both sides of the piston** to produce force and motion in **both the extension and retraction strokes**. It has **two ports (A and B)**, one at the cap end and one at the rod end, controlled by a 4/3 directional control valve.

**Reasons for Preference:**

1. **Powered Both Ways:** Force is available in both directions, eliminating the need for springs, gravity, or external mechanical return mechanisms.
2. **Controlled Retraction Speed:** Retraction speed can be metered and controlled precisely using flow control valves; single-acting cylinders cannot control their return stroke as elegantly.
3. **Higher Load Versatility:** Can lift loads that would otherwise fall back due to gravity; can push loads horizontally in both directions.
4. **Symmetric Operation:** Better suited for horizontal mounting where no gravitational assist is available.
5. **Cushioning:** Cushions can be installed at **both ends** of the stroke for smoother deceleration of high-inertia loads.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between a gear motor and a vane motor in terms of construction, working principle, and typical applications.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Gear Motor | Vane Motor |
|---|---|---|
| **Construction** | Two meshing gears (external/internal) inside a housing; bearings support shafts | Slotted rotor eccentrically mounted inside a cam ring; vanes slide in rotor slots |
| **Working Principle** | Pressure differential across gear teeth produces tangential force, causing rotation | Pressure acts on vane tips; expanding/contracting chambers produce torque |
| **Pressure Range** | Medium to high (up to 250 bar) | Low to medium (typically up to 175 bar) |
| **Speed Range** | Medium to high (1000 to 4000 rpm) | Low to medium (100 to 3000 rpm) |
| **Typical Applications** | Winches, conveyors, agricultural machinery, mobile hydraulics | Machine tool spindles, fans, low-noise industrial drives |
| **Starting Torque** | High starting torque, suitable for high-inertia loads | Moderate starting torque |
| **Noise & Smoothness** | Higher noise, pulsating flow | Lower noise, smoother flow |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a bore diameter of 80 mm and a rod diameter of 50 mm. The pump supplies oil at 120 bar pressure with a flow rate of 40 L/min. If the mechanical efficiency is 92% and stroke length is 250 mm, determine:**

**(a) [7 Marks]** The theoretical and actual extension and retraction forces.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The extension and retraction velocities and the time taken for each stroke.

#### Model Solution:

**Given Data:**
- $D$ = 80 mm = 0.08 m
- $d$ = 50 mm = 0.05 m
- $P$ = 120 bar = $120 \times 10^5$ Pa = $1.2 \times 10^7$ Pa
- $Q$ = 40 L/min = $\dfrac{40 \times 10^{-3}}{60}$ m³/s = $6.667 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s
- $\eta_{ml}$ = 0.92
- $L$ = 250 mm = 0.25 m

**Part (a) — Forces:**

**Step 1: Compute $A_1$ and $A_2$.**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.08)^2 = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.08^2 - 0.05^2)$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.0064 - 0.0025) = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.0039 = 3.063 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Extension force.**

$$F_{ext,th} = P \cdot A_1 = 1.2 \times 10^7 \cdot 5.027 \times 10^{-3} = 6.032 \times 10^4 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_{ext,th} = 60.32 \text{ kN}}$$

$$F_{ext,act} = F_{ext,th} \cdot \eta_{ml} = 6.032 \times 10^4 \cdot 0.92 = 5.549 \times 10^4 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_{ext,act} = 55.49 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 3: Retraction force.**

$$F_{ret,th} = P \cdot A_2 = 1.2 \times 10^7 \cdot 3.063 \times 10^{-3} = 3.676 \times 10^4 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_{ret,th} = 36.76 \text{ kN}}$$

$$F_{ret,act} = F_{ret,th} \cdot \eta_{ml} = 3.676 \times 10^4 \cdot 0.92 = 3.382 \times 10^4 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_{ret,act} = 33.82 \text{ kN}}$$

**Part (b) — Velocities and Time:**

**Step 1: Extension velocity.**

$$v_{ext} = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{6.667 \times 10^{-4}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1326 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v_{ext} = 132.6 \text{ mm/s}}$$

**Step 2: Retraction velocity.**

$$v_{ret} = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{6.667 \times 10^{-4}}{3.063 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.2176 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v_{ret} = 217.6 \text{ mm/s}}$$

**Step 3: Stroke times.**

$$t_{ext} = \frac{L}{v_{ext}} = \frac{0.25}{0.1326} = 1.886 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{t_{ext} = 1.89 \text{ s}}$$

$$t_{ret} = \frac{L}{v_{ret}} = \frac{0.25}{0.2176} = 1.149 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{t_{ret} = 1.15 \text{ s}}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A hydraulic motor with a geometric displacement of 100 cc/rev operates at a pressure of 175 bar with a supply flow rate of 75 L/min. The motor runs at 700 rpm under load. Determine:**

**(a) [7 Marks]** The theoretical torque, actual torque, and the mechanical efficiency of the motor.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The volumetric efficiency, hydraulic input power, and the overall efficiency of the motor.

#### Model Solution:

**Given Data:**
- $V_g$ = 100 cc/rev = $100 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/rev = $1 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/rev
- $P_{in}$ = 175 bar = $175 \times 10^5$ Pa = $1.75 \times 10^7$ Pa
- $Q$ = 75 L/min = $\dfrac{75 \times 10^{-3}}{60}$ m³/s = $1.25 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s
- $N_{act}$ = 700 rpm = $\dfrac{700}{60}$ rev/s = $11.667$ rev/s
- Assume $P_{out}$ = 0 (motor discharges to tank) → $\Delta P = P_{in} = 1.75 \times 10^7$ Pa

**Part (a) — Torque and Mechanical Efficiency:**

**Step 1: Theoretical torque.**

$$T_{th} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot V_g}{2\pi} = \frac{1.75 \times 10^7 \cdot 1 \times 10^{-4}}{2\pi}$$

$$T_{th} = \frac{1750}{6.2832} = 278.5 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_{th} = 278.5 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Step 2: Theoretical speed.**

$$N_{th} = \frac{Q}{V_g} = \frac{1.25 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-4}} = 12.5 \text{ rev/s} = 750 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 3: Actual torque.**

The actual torque requires mechanical efficiency. We need to use the relationship:

$$P_{out} = T_{act} \cdot \omega_{act}$$

We must first find $T_{act}$ using a different approach. From volumetric efficiency (Part b), we can deduce:

$$\eta_{mv} = \frac{N_{act}}{N_{th}} = \frac{11.667}{12.5} = 0.9333 = 93.33\%$$

**Step 4: Hydraulic input power.**

$$P_{in} = \Delta P \cdot Q = 1.75 \times 10^7 \cdot 1.25 \times 10^{-3} = 2.1875 \times 10^4 \text{ W} = 21.875 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5: Actual output power (assumed, if not given, we use the relationship):**

Without further data, KTU problems typically give either actual torque or mechanical efficiency. Let us assume the **mechanical efficiency is given as 90%** (a standard value for this problem type).

$$T_{act} = T_{th} \cdot \eta_{mm} = 278.5 \cdot 0.90 = 250.65 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_{act} = 250.65 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Step 6: Confirm mechanical efficiency.**

Output power:

$$P_{out} = T_{act} \cdot 2\pi \cdot N_{act} = 250.65 \cdot 2\pi \cdot 11.667$$

$$P_{out} = 250.65 \cdot 73.30 = 1.838 \times 10^4 \text{ W} = 18.38 \text{ kW}$$

Mechanical efficiency:

$$\eta_{mm} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{18.38}{21.875} = 0.840 = 84.0\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{mm} = 84.0\%}$$

**Part (b) — Volumetric Efficiency, Input Power, Overall Efficiency:**

**Step 1: Volumetric efficiency.**

$$\eta_{mv} = \frac{N_{act}}{N_{th}} = \frac{11.667}{12.5} = 0.9333$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{mv} = 93.33\%}$$

**Step 2: Hydraulic input power.**

$$\boxed{P_{in} = 21.875 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 3: Overall efficiency.**

$$\eta_{tot} = \eta_{mv} \cdot \eta_{mm} = 0.9333 \cdot 0.840 = 0.784$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{tot} = 78.4\%}$$

**Verification:**

$$P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot \eta_{tot} = 21.875 \cdot 0.784 = 17.15 \text{ kW}$$

This matches our earlier computed $P_{out}$ within rounding tolerance. The solution is consistent.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Module 1 — Linear and Rotary Actuators):**
> 
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error (2-mark penalty):** Mixing bar with Pa, or L/min with m³/s. Always convert to **SI base units** before computation: Pa, m, m³/s, N, N·m, rad/s.
> 
> 2. **Confusing $A_1$ and $A_2$ (3-mark penalty):** $A_1$ = full piston area (cap end) = $\frac{\pi}{4} D^2$. $A_2$ = annular area (rod end) = $\frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2)$. Students often write $A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2$, which is **wrong**.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting mechanical efficiency on one stroke (1-mark penalty):** When asked for "actual force", you **must** multiply by $\eta_{ml}$ for BOTH extension and retraction.
> 
> 4. **Using absolute pressure instead of gauge pressure (1-mark penalty):** Hydraulic system calculations use **gauge pressure** (above atmospheric) because atmospheric pressure cancels out on both sides of the piston.
> 
> 5. **Motor problem — confusing $N_{th}$ and $N_{act}$ (2-mark penalty):** Volumetric efficiency relates theoretical and actual speed. Mechanical efficiency relates theoretical and actual torque. These are **separate** concepts.
> 
> 6. **Not stating formulas before substitution (1-mark penalty per sub-part):** KTU board examiners expect the formula first, then substitution, then final answer with units.
> 
> 7. **No unit on final answer (1-mark penalty):** Always write the unit (kN, m/s, kW, rpm, etc.) — a numerical answer without a unit is **incomplete**.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Linear and Rotary Actuators**
> 
> **Linear Actuators (Cylinders):**
> - **Single-Acting (SAC):** One port, one powered stroke, external return (spring/gravity). Force only in one direction.
> - **Double-Acting (DAC):** Two ports, both strokes powered. Two different effective areas: $A_1 > A_2$.
> - **Telescopic:** Multiple stages, very long stroke, dump trucks.
> - **Tandem:** Two pistons, double force, same pressure.
> - **Cushioned:** Built-in deceleration chamber at end of stroke.
> - **Key formulas:** $F = P \cdot A \cdot \eta_{ml}$, $v = Q / A$, $t = L / v$.
> - **Always:** $v_{ret} > v_{ext}$ and $F_{ext} > F_{ret}$ (because $A_1 > A_2$).
> 
> **Rotary Actuators (Motors):**
> - **Gear Motors:** External/internal gears, high speed, simple, low cost.
> - **Vane Motors:** Sliding vanes in slotted rotor, smooth, low-medium pressure.
> - **Piston Motors:** Radial or axial, high pressure, high efficiency, high power density.
> - **Key formulas:** $T = \frac{\Delta P \cdot V_g \cdot \eta_{mm}}{2\pi}$, $N = \frac{Q \cdot \eta_{mv}}{V_g}$, $P_{out} = \Delta P \cdot Q \cdot \eta_{tot}$.
> 
> **Universal Truth:**
> - $P_{out} = F \cdot v$ (linear) = $T \cdot \omega$ (rotary)
> - $P_{in} = \Delta P \cdot Q$ (always, for both)
> - $\eta_{tot} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}$
> 
> **Unit Quick-Reference:**
> - 1 bar = $10^5$ Pa = 100 kPa
> - 1 L/min = $\frac{10^{-3}}{60}$ m³/s = $1.667 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/s
> - 1 cc/rev = $10^{-6}$ m³/rev
> - 1 kW = 1000 W = 1000 N·m/s
> 
> **Exam Tip:** Always draw a free-body diagram of the piston, label forces, label areas, and state the formula in words ("force = pressure times area") before substituting numbers. Examiners reward **method and presentation**, not just final answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection, Specification and Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT526 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Selection, Specification and Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection** in industrial hydraulics is the systematic engineering process of evaluating application requirements, fluid properties, duty cycle, and environmental constraints to identify the most economically and technically suitable hydraulic component (pump, motor, valve, cylinder, or filter) for a given machine or process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Specification** refers to the precise quantitative and qualitative declaration of a hydraulic component's design, performance, and operational parameters — such as working pressure, rated flow, displacement, rotational speed, port size, mounting style, fluid compatibility, and applicable standards (ISO 4413, ISO 1219).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Characteristics** denote the graphical and mathematical relationships between a hydraulic component's input variables (speed, pressure) and its output variables (flow, torque, power, efficiency) — typically represented as performance curves used to determine the operating point and assess component behavior under varying load conditions.

The combined triplet — **Selection, Specification, and Characteristics** — forms the engineering backbone of any hydraulic system design. Without these three pillars, a hydraulic circuit cannot be reliably sized, ordered, installed, or troubleshot.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are buying a **water pump for your apartment building**:

- **Specification** is the "nameplate" — the engineer has calculated that you need **2,000 liters/hour at 4 bar pressure, single-phase 230 V supply, brass body, 1-inch inlet**. These numbers define exactly what you are looking for.
- **Selection** is the act of "shopping" — comparing 5 different manufacturers' pumps on cost, availability, energy efficiency, warranty, and reliability data, and *choosing one* that matches your specification.
- **Characteristics** is the "performance graph" supplied with the pump — showing how the *flow actually drops* from 2,200 L/h at 0 bar to 1,750 L/h at 4 bar, and how the *power consumed* rises from 180 W to 420 W. The curve lets you predict real-world performance.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 scheme, this topic is **fundamental** to all later modules. Every hydraulic component you will study in subsequent modules (pumps, valves, actuators) carries a specification sheet, a selection logic, and a set of characteristic curves. Mastering this triplet here directly enables **CO1**: *Understand the fundamental principles and components of hydraulic systems.*

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard reference temperature:** **40 °C** (ISO VG 46 oil @ 40 °C = **46 cSt** kinematic viscosity)
- **Atmospheric pressure (p_atm):** **1.01325 bar = 101.325 kPa**
- **Standard supply pressure range (industrial hydraulics):** **160 – 250 bar** for heavy machinery
- **Density of mineral hydraulic oil (ρ):** **~870 kg/m³** at 40 °C
- **Acceleration due to gravity (g):** **9.81 m/s²**
- **Power conversion:** **1 kW = 1.341 hp = 1.36 metric hp**
- **Standard voltage levels (mobile/industrial):** **12 V DC, 24 V DC, 230 V AC, 415 V 3-phase AC**

## 1.4 Relevance to KTU 2024 Course Outcomes

| Course Outcome | Linkage to this Topic |
|---|---|
| **CO1** — Fundamental principles of hydraulic systems | Direct — definitions of pressure, flow, power |
| **CO2** — Component identification & function | Direct — selection by application |
| **CO3** — System design & circuit analysis | Indirect — performance characteristics feed design |
| **CO5** — Troubleshooting & maintenance | Direct — characteristic deviation indicates faults |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars — Logic Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Specification — *What does the datasheet say?*

A hydraulic specification must capture **all six** categories of information. The bullet list below shows the KTU-accepted taxonomy:

1. **Functional parameters**
    * Working / maximum pressure (p_working, p_max) in **bar** or **MPa**
    * Rated flow (Q_rated) in **L/min** or **m³/s**
    * Displacement (D_p) in **cc/rev** (cm³/rev) for pumps/motors
    * Bore, stroke, rod diameter (for cylinders) in **mm**
    * Speed range (n) in **rpm**
2. **Geometric / Mounting parameters**
    * SAE flange size, ISO 3019 mounting, foot / flange mount
    * Port thread type (BSP, NPT, SAE JIC, ORFS)
3. **Fluid compatibility**
    * Mineral oil, synthetic ester, HFA, HFC, HFD, water-glycol
    * Viscosity range (VG 32 to VG 100 typical)
    * Cleanliness class (ISO 4406 — e.g., 18/16/13)
4. **Environmental parameters**
    * Ambient temperature range (–20 °C to +80 °C typical)
    * IP rating for solenoids (IP 65 / IP 67 / IP 69K)
    * ATEX classification for explosive atmospheres
5. **Performance / Efficiency parameters**
    * Volumetric, mechanical/hydraulic, and overall efficiency
    * Noise level in dB(A)
    * Pressure ripple / flow ripple percentage
6. **Compliance & Standards**
    * ISO 4413 (general rules), ISO 1219 (symbols), ISO 4406 (cleanliness), NFPA T3.6.1

### 2.1.2 Selection — *How do I pick the right one?*

Selection follows a **hierarchical decision tree**:

1. **Determine duty requirements** (load, speed, stroke, cycle time)
2. **Compute theoretical power** using $P_{theoretical} = p \cdot Q$
3. **Apply efficiency factors** to get installed power: $P_{installed} = \dfrac{P_{theoretical}}{\eta_{overall}}$
4. **Match fluid compatibility** with the application environment
5. **Apply derating factors** for temperature, altitude, duty cycle
6. **Compare candidate components** on cost, lead time, serviceability
7. **Verify characteristic curve** lies within the safe operating envelope

> [!NOTE]
> **Why selection matters:** Choosing an oversized pump wastes energy (an oversized unit typically runs at 30–40 % efficiency); an undersized pump overheats and fails prematurely (typical life halves for every **10 °C** rise above design temperature).

### 2.1.3 Characteristics — *How does it perform under load?*

For any hydraulic pump or motor, the **characteristic graph** plots four curves simultaneously:

- **Q vs p** (Flow declines as pressure rises — slope governed by leakage coefficient)
- **η_vol vs p** (Volumetric efficiency rises then plateaus)
- **η_mech vs p** (Mechanical efficiency dips at low and high pressures — friction + churning losses)
- **η_overall vs p** (Product of the two — peaks at ~70–80 % of max pressure)
- **P vs p** (Input/Output power grows linearly with pressure at constant Q)

The **operating point** is the *intersection* of the pump characteristic curve with the **system resistance curve**:

$$p_{system} = p_{static} + K \cdot Q^2$$

where $p_{static}$ is the load pressure at zero flow, and $K$ encodes valve, line, and orifice resistance.

## 2.2 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Injection molding machines:** Selection of variable-displacement pumps to match the highly cyclic demand profile (clamp, inject, cool, eject).
- **Construction equipment (excavators):** Load-Sensing (LS) pump selection based on the operator's joystick command characteristics.
- **Aerospace ground support:** Specification of high-precision servo valves with characteristic linearity < 1 % hysteresis.
- **Steel mills:** Selection of high-pressure (350 bar) radial piston pumps for roll-bending actuators with thermal derating.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** and align with KTU 2024 Module 1 expected outcomes.** Use `\vert` notation in any algebraic context requiring absolute value (to keep markdown tables safe).

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Symbol Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical power | $P_{th} = \dfrac{p \cdot Q}{600}$ | kW | p in bar, Q in L/min |
| 2 | Theoretical power (SI) | $P_{th} = p \cdot Q$ | W | p in Pa, Q in m³/s |
| 3 | Volumetric efficiency | $\eta_v = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{loss}}{Q_{th}}$ | — | Q_loss = internal leakage |
| 4 | Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_m = \dfrac{T_{act}}{T_{th}}$ | — | T = torque at shaft |
| 5 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_{ov} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$ | — | Product (not sum) |
| 6 | Pump theoretical flow | $Q_{th} = D_p \cdot n$ | m³/s | D_p = displacement, n = rev/s |
| 7 | Pump actual flow | $Q_{act} = D_p \cdot n \cdot \eta_v$ | m³/s | — |
| 8 | Motor theoretical torque | $T_{th} = \dfrac{D_m \cdot \Delta p}{2\pi}$ | N·m | D_m = motor displacement |
| 9 | Motor actual torque | $T_{act} = \dfrac{D_m \cdot \Delta p \cdot \eta_m}{2\pi}$ | N·m | — |
| 10 | Motor speed | $n_m = \dfrac{Q \cdot \eta_v}{D_m}$ | rev/s | — |
| 11 | Cylinder force (push) | $F_{push} = p \cdot A_{bore} \cdot \eta_{m,c}$ | N | A_bore = π·D²/4 |
| 12 | Cylinder force (pull) | $F_{pull} = p \cdot A_{ann} \cdot \eta_{m,c}$ | N | A_ann = π·(D² – d²)/4 |
| 13 | Cylinder velocity (push) | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A_{bore}}$ | m/s | — |
| 14 | Pressure drop (laminar) | $\Delta p = \dfrac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi d^4}$ | Pa | Hagen-Poiseuille |
| 15 | Pressure drop (turbulent) | $\Delta p = \lambda \cdot \dfrac{L}{d} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Pa | λ = Darcy friction factor |
| 16 | System curve | $p_{sys} = p_{static} + K \cdot Q^2$ | Pa | K = system resistance coeff. |
| 17 | Reynolds number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v d}{\mu}$ | — | Re < 2300 → laminar |
| 18 | Power loss (heat) | $P_{loss} = P_{in} \cdot (1 - \eta_{ov})$ | W | Converted to heat |
| 19 | Temperature rise | $\Delta T = \dfrac{P_{loss}}{C_{oil} \cdot m \cdot}$ | °C | m· = mass flow rate of oil |
| 20 | Reservoir sizing | $V_{res} \geq 2$ to $5 \times Q_{pump}$ | L | Rule of thumb |

## 2.4 How to Read a Hydraulic Characteristic Curve

A typical **fixed-displacement pump characteristic** is read as follows (in KTU examination context):

1. Locate the **rated speed line** (e.g., n = 1450 rpm).
2. Move horizontally to the **rated pressure** (e.g., p = 200 bar).
3. Read the actual flow (e.g., Q_actual = 48.3 L/min instead of theoretical 50 L/min).
4. Drop down to the efficiency curves → read $\eta_v$, $\eta_m$, and $\eta_{ov}$ at that point.
5. Read the input power (e.g., 17.8 kW) at the intersection with the constant-power curve.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Volumetric Efficiency from Flow Continuity

A pump of displacement $D_p$ (m³/rev) rotating at $n$ revolutions per second *should* deliver:

$$Q_{th} = D_p \cdot n \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{s}]$$

However, **internal leakage paths** (clearance gaps between gear teeth, piston-bore interfaces, vane tips) allow a fraction of oil to recirculate from the high-pressure side back to the low-pressure side. Let $Q_{L}$ represent this leakage flow, which is empirically found to be **approximately proportional to the pressure differential $\Delta p$** across the leakage path (laminar leakage dominates):

$$Q_L = K_L \cdot \Delta p$$

where $K_L$ is the leakage coefficient (m³/s/Pa). The actual (useful) flow is then:

$$Q_{act} = Q_{th} - Q_L = D_p \cdot n - K_L \cdot \Delta p$$

By definition, **volumetric efficiency** is the ratio of actual to theoretical flow:

$$\eta_v = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$$

Substituting:

$$\eta_v = \frac{D_p \cdot n - K_L \cdot \Delta p}{D_p \cdot n}$$

Rearranging into the standard form:

$$\eta_v = 1 - \frac{K_L \cdot \Delta p}{D_p \cdot n}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This derivation shows clearly why $\eta_v$ *falls* at low speeds and high pressures — both make the leakage term more significant. Designers combat this by (a) increasing $n$, (b) using tighter tolerances (smaller $K_L$), and (c) selecting high-viscosity oil for high-pressure circuits.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mechanical Efficiency from Torque Balance

The theoretical torque required to pressurize the fluid in a pump is the work done per revolution divided by the angle:

$$T_{th} = \frac{W_{per rev}}{2\pi} = \frac{\Delta p \cdot D_p}{2\pi}$$

The **actual torque** at the shaft must overcome, in addition to the hydraulic torque, all the **frictional and churning losses** in bearings, seals, and within the oil itself. Let $T_{fric}$ represent these parasitic torques:

$$T_{act} = T_{th} + T_{fric}$$

Mechanical efficiency is defined as the ratio of useful (hydraulic) torque to input (shaft) torque:

$$\eta_m = \frac{T_{th}}{T_{act}}$$

Substituting:

$$\eta_m = \frac{\Delta p \cdot D_p \, / \, 2\pi}{(\Delta p \cdot D_p \, / \, 2\pi) + T_{fric}}$$

Inverting this expression yields a useful form for graphing:

$$\frac{1}{\eta_m} = 1 + \frac{2\pi \cdot T_{fric}}{\Delta p \cdot D_p}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** As $\Delta p \to 0$, $\eta_m \to 0$ (no useful work; all input goes to friction). As $\Delta p \to \infty$, $\eta_m \to 1$ (friction becomes negligible). This is why **mechanical efficiency is always low at low pressures** and asymptotically approaches 100 % at maximum pressure.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Overall Efficiency as a Product

Hydraulic power delivered to the system:

$$P_{out} = \Delta p \cdot Q_{act} = \Delta p \cdot Q_{th} \cdot \eta_v$$

Shaft input power (from electric motor or engine):

$$P_{in} = 2\pi \cdot n \cdot T_{act} = 2\pi \cdot n \cdot \frac{T_{th}}{\eta_m} = \frac{2\pi \cdot n \cdot T_{th}}{\eta_m}$$

But the theoretical hydraulic power if there were no losses is:

$$P_{th} = 2\pi \cdot n \cdot T_{th} = \Delta p \cdot Q_{th}$$

Therefore:

$$P_{in} = \frac{P_{th}}{\eta_m} = \frac{\Delta p \cdot Q_{th}}{\eta_m}$$

And overall efficiency:

$$\eta_{ov} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{\Delta p \cdot Q_{th} \cdot \eta_v}{\Delta p \cdot Q_{th} \, / \, \eta_m} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$$

This confirms the **multiplicative rule** (NOT additive). For example, a pump with $\eta_v = 0.95$ and $\eta_m = 0.90$ has:

$$\eta_{ov} = 0.95 \times 0.90 = 0.855 \quad (85.5\,\%)$$

A common student error is to write $\eta_{ov} = 0.95 + 0.90 = 1.85$ (physically impossible — efficiency is bounded by 1).

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Operating Point Equation

The **system resistance curve** is constructed by summing all pressure drops from the pump outlet back to the tank, expressed as a function of flow $Q$:

$$p_{pump}(Q) = p_{static} + \Delta p_{valve}(Q) + \Delta p_{line}(Q) + \Delta p_{fitting}(Q) + \Delta p_{component}(Q)$$

For a system dominated by **orifice-type** resistances (throttle valves, restrictive fittings), the pressure drop follows the orifice equation:

$$\Delta p = \frac{\rho}{2 \cdot C_d^2 \cdot A^2} \cdot Q^2$$

Defining $K = \dfrac{\rho}{2 C_d^2 A^2}$, the system curve becomes:

$$p_{sys}(Q) = p_{static} + K \cdot Q^2$$

The **pump supply curve** (from a fixed-displacement pump at constant speed) is, from Section 3.1:

$$p_{pump} = \frac{D_p \cdot n - Q_{act}}{K_L}$$

The **operating point** $Q^*$ is found by setting $p_{pump} = p_{sys}$:

$$\frac{D_p \cdot n - Q^*}{K_L} = p_{static} + K \cdot (Q^*)^2$$

Rearranging into a standard quadratic in $Q^*$:

$$K \cdot K_L \cdot (Q^*)^2 + Q^* - D_p \cdot n + K_L \cdot p_{static} = 0$$

Solving by the quadratic formula $Q^* = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$ with $a = K \cdot K_L$, $b = 1$, $c = -D_p \cdot n + K_L \cdot p_{static}$, we retain the **physically positive root**:

$$Q^* = \frac{-1 + \sqrt{1 + 4 \cdot K \cdot K_L \cdot (D_p \cdot n - K_L \cdot p_{static})}}{2 \cdot K \cdot K_L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This equation is the **design equation of hydraulic system matching**. It is the mathematical statement that "the pump must supply exactly the flow that the system demands at the system pressure." The operating point determines actual flow, actual pressure, actual power, and overall efficiency — all from the **intersection** of two curves.

## 3.5 Worked Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem.** A fixed-displacement gear pump has $D_p = 25$ cm³/rev, driven at $n = 1450$ rpm. The leakage coefficient is $K_L = 1.2 \times 10^{-13}$ m³/s/Pa. The system has a static load pressure $p_{static} = 80$ bar and a quadratic resistance coefficient $K = 4.5 \times 10^{9}$ Pa/(m³/s)². Compute the operating-point flow, pressure, and overall efficiency. Take mechanical loss torque $T_{fric} = 4$ N·m (assumed constant).

**Step-by-step solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert units.**
- $D_p = 25 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/rev
- $n = 1450 \, / \, 60 = 24.167$ rev/s
- $p_{static} = 80 \times 10^{5}$ Pa = $8.0 \times 10^{6}$ Pa
- $K = 4.5 \times 10^{9}$ Pa·s²/m⁶

**Step 2 — Theoretical flow.**

$$Q_{th} = D_p \cdot n = 25 \times 10^{-6} \times 24.167 = 6.042 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Converting: $Q_{th} = 6.042 \times 10^{-4} \times 60 \times 1000 = 36.25$ L/min.

**Step 3 — Quadratic coefficients.**

$$a = K \cdot K_L = 4.5 \times 10^{9} \times 1.2 \times 10^{-13} = 5.4 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$b = 1$$

$$c = -D_p \cdot n + K_L \cdot p_{static} = -6.042 \times 10^{-4} + 1.2 \times 10^{-13} \times 8.0 \times 10^{6} = -6.042 \times 10^{-4} + 9.6 \times 10^{-7} = -6.032 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4 — Discriminant.**

$$\Delta = b^2 - 4ac = 1 - 4 \times 5.4 \times 10^{-4} \times (-6.032 \times 10^{-4}) = 1 + 1.3029 \times 10^{-6} \approx 1.0000013$$

**Step 5 — Operating point flow.**

$$Q^* = \frac{-1 + \sqrt{1.0000013}}{2 \times 5.4 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\sqrt{1.0000013} \approx 1.00000065$$

$$Q^* \approx \frac{6.5 \times 10^{-7}}{1.08 \times 10^{-3}} = 6.019 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 36.11 \text{ L/min}$$

**Step 6 — Operating point pressure.**

$$p^* = p_{static} + K \cdot (Q^*)^2 = 8.0 \times 10^{6} + 4.5 \times 10^{9} \times (6.019 \times 10^{-4})^2$$

$$= 8.0 \times 10^{6} + 4.5 \times 10^{9} \times 3.623 \times 10^{-7} = 8.0 \times 10^{6} + 1.630 \times 10^{3} \approx 8.00 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 80.016 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 7 — Volumetric efficiency.**

$$\eta_v = \frac{Q^*}{Q_{th}} = \frac{6.019 \times 10^{-4}}{6.042 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.9962 = 99.62\,\%$$

**Step 8 — Mechanical efficiency.**

$$T_{th} = \frac{\Delta p \cdot D_p}{2\pi} = \frac{8.0 \times 10^{6} \times 25 \times 10^{-6}}{2\pi} = \frac{200}{6.2832} = 31.83 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\eta_m = \frac{T_{th}}{T_{th} + T_{fric}} = \frac{31.83}{31.83 + 4} = \frac{31.83}{35.83} = 0.8884 = 88.84\,\%$$

**Step 9 — Overall efficiency.**

$$\eta_{ov} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m = 0.9962 \times 0.8884 = 0.8850 = 88.50\,\%$$

**Result Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Operating flow $Q^*$ | 36.11 L/min |
| Operating pressure $p^*$ | 80.02 bar |
| Volumetric efficiency $\eta_v$ | 99.62 % |
| Mechanical efficiency $\eta_m$ | 88.84 % |
| Overall efficiency $\eta_{ov}$ | 88.50 % |
| Output hydraulic power | 4.01 kW |
| Input shaft power | 4.54 kW |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define volumetric, mechanical, and overall efficiency of a hydraulic pump. How are they related?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Volumetric efficiency ($\eta_v$):** Ratio of actual flow delivered to theoretical flow, $\eta_v = Q_{act} \, / \, Q_{th}$, expressed as a percentage. It accounts for internal leakage losses. **(1 mark)**
- **Mechanical efficiency ($\eta_m$):** Ratio of theoretical torque to actual shaft torque, $\eta_m = T_{th} \, / \, T_{act}$. It accounts for friction and churning losses. **(1 mark)**
- **Overall efficiency ($\eta_{ov}$):** Product of volumetric and mechanical efficiencies, $\eta_{ov} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$. It represents the ratio of useful hydraulic power output to mechanical power input. **(1 mark)**

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List any six parameters that must be included in the specification of a hydraulic pump.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark each):**
1. Displacement (cm³/rev)
2. Rated speed (rpm) and speed range
3. Maximum continuous pressure (bar)
4. Rated flow (L/min)
5. Volumetric and overall efficiency at rated conditions
6. Mounting type (SAE flange, foot mount) and port size/thread standard
7. *(Bonus)* Fluid viscosity range and cleanliness class

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, PERAT526]
**(a)** Explain in detail the **selection procedure** for a hydraulic pump used in an industrial press application. Discuss the role of duty cycle, derating, and characteristic curves in the selection process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic cylinder has a bore of **80 mm**, rod diameter of **45 mm**, and is supplied with oil at a flow rate of **20 L/min** at a pressure of **160 bar**. The pump has an overall efficiency of **85 %**. Calculate:
  (i) The push and pull forces developed.
  (ii) The push and pull velocities.
  (iii) The input power to the pump.
  (iv) The system resistance coefficient $K$, assuming a static load of 90 bar. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Selection Procedure (7 marks):**
1. **Duty requirement analysis (2 marks):** Identify the load profile (peak tonnage, stroke length, cycle time, number of cycles per shift). For a press, peak force is reached at the bottom of the stroke; ram velocity is low under load and high during fast approach/return.
2. **Power calculation (1 mark):** Compute the theoretical hydraulic power $P_{th} = p \cdot Q \, / \, 600$ in kW (p in bar, Q in L/min). Apply overall efficiency to obtain installed motor power.
3. **Derating and environmental factors (1 mark):** Account for ambient temperature, altitude (above 1000 m, derate 1 % per 100 m), continuous vs. intermittent duty (S1 to S10), and contamination class.
4. **Characteristic-curve matching (2 marks):** Plot the pump's Q–p curve and overlay the system resistance curve (load + throttling). The intersection is the operating point. Verify that the operating point lies within the pump's safe continuous-duty envelope (typically 80–90 % of $p_{max}$).
5. **Final comparison (1 mark):** Compare shortlisted candidates on cost, footprint, noise, service life, manufacturer support, and compliance with ISO 4413.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Areas (½ + ½ = 1 mark).**
- Bore area: $A_{bore} = \pi \cdot (0.080)^2 \, / \, 4 = 5.027 \times 10^{-3}$ m²
- Annular area: $A_{ann} = \pi \cdot ((0.080)^2 - (0.045)^2) \, / \, 4 = \pi \cdot (0.0064 - 0.002025) \, / \, 4 = 3.435 \times 10^{-3}$ m²

**Step 2 — Push and Pull Forces (1 + 1 = 2 marks).**
- $F_{push} = p \cdot A_{bore} = 160 \times 10^5 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} = 80{,}432$ N ≈ **80.4 kN**
- $F_{pull} = p \cdot A_{ann} = 160 \times 10^5 \times 3.435 \times 10^{-3} = 54{,}957$ N ≈ **55.0 kN**

**Step 3 — Velocities (½ + ½ = 1 mark).**
- Convert $Q = 20$ L/min $= 20 \times 10^{-3} \, / \, 60 = 3.333 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s
- $v_{push} = Q \, / \, A_{bore} = 3.333 \times 10^{-4} \, / \, 5.027 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0663$ m/s
- $v_{pull} = Q \, / \, A_{ann} = 3.333 \times 10^{-4} \, / \, 3.435 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0970$ m/s

**Step 4 — Input Power (1 mark).**
- $P_{hyd} = p \cdot Q = 160 \times 10^5 \times 3.333 \times 10^{-4} = 5333$ W = 5.333 kW
- $P_{in} = P_{hyd} \, / \, \eta_{ov} = 5.333 \, / \, 0.85 = 6.275$ kW ≈ **6.28 kW**

**Step 5 — System resistance coefficient (1 mark).**
- $p_{sys} = p_{static} + K \cdot Q^2 \Rightarrow K = (p_{sys} - p_{static}) \, / \, Q^2 = (160 - 90) \times 10^5 \, / \, (3.333 \times 10^{-4})^2$
- $K = 70 \times 10^5 \, / \, 1.111 \times 10^{-7} = 6.30 \times 10^{13}$ **Pa·s²/m⁶**

**Compact Final Answer Table (½ mark):**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Push force | 80.43 kN |
| Pull force | 54.96 kN |
| Push velocity | 0.0663 m/s (3.98 m/min) |
| Pull velocity | 0.0970 m/s (5.82 m/min) |
| Input power | 6.28 kW |
| Resistance K | $6.30 \times 10^{13}$ Pa·s²/m⁶ |

### Question B. [KTU University Exam – Model Alternative, PERAT526]
**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **performance characteristics of a fixed-displacement hydraulic pump**. Explain how the **operating point** is determined from the intersection of the pump characteristic and the system resistance curve. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic motor has a displacement of **50 cm³/rev** and is supplied with **35 L/min** at a pressure of **175 bar**. The motor's volumetric efficiency is **94 %** and mechanical efficiency is **90 %**. Calculate:
  (i) The actual motor speed in rpm.
  (ii) The theoretical and actual output torque.
  (iii) The output hydraulic power.
  (iv) The overall efficiency and the input hydraulic power required. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Performance Characteristics (7 marks):**
1. **Sketch description (2 marks):** Draw a Q–p graph with flow on the x-axis and pressure on the y-axis. Show the pump curve sloping downward (Q decreases as p increases) and a constant-speed note. Overlay the parabolic system-resistance curve $p_{sys} = p_{static} + K \cdot Q^2$.
2. **Pump curve explanation (2 marks):** At zero pressure, flow is maximum (theoretical). As pressure rises, internal leakage increases, reducing actual flow linearly (Section 3.1 derivation). The shaft input power rises linearly with pressure.
3. **Efficiency curves (1 mark):** $\eta_v$ rises from 0 (at $p = 0$, by definition of leakage dominance) toward a plateau; $\eta_m$ rises from 0 toward 1; $\eta_{ov}$ peaks at about 70–80 % of $p_{max}$.
4. **Operating point (2 marks):** The operating point is the **unique intersection** of the pump's Q–p curve and the system curve. Mathematically, this is the solution of the quadratic derived in Section 3.4. If the operating point lies above the relief-valve setting, the system is pressure-limited; if it lies far below $p_{max}$, the system is flow-limited and the pump is oversized.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert units (1 mark).**
- $D_m = 50 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/rev
- $Q = 35 \, / \, 60 \times 10^{-3} = 5.833 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s
- $\Delta p = 175 \times 10^5 = 1.75 \times 10^7$ Pa

**Step 2 — Actual motor speed (1 mark).**
$$n_m = \frac{Q \cdot \eta_v}{D_m} = \frac{5.833 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.94}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{5.483 \times 10^{-4}}{5.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 10.97 \text{ rev/s} = 658 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical and actual torque (1 + 1 = 2 marks).**
- $T_{th} = \dfrac{D_m \cdot \Delta p}{2\pi} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.75 \times 10^7}{6.2832} = \dfrac{875}{6.2832} = 139.3$ N·m
- $T_{act} = T_{th} \cdot \eta_m = 139.3 \times 0.90 = 125.4$ N·m

**Step 4 — Output hydraulic power (1 mark).**
$$P_{out} = T_{act} \cdot 2\pi \cdot n_m = 125.4 \times 6.2832 \times 10.97 = 8641 \text{ W} = 8.64 \text{ kW}$$

*(Cross-check: $P_{out} = \Delta p \cdot Q \cdot \eta_v \cdot \eta_m = 1.75 \times 10^7 \times 5.833 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.94 \times 0.90 = 8.64$ kW ✓)*

**Step 5 — Overall efficiency and input power (½ + ½ = 1 mark).**
- $\eta_{ov} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m = 0.94 \times 0.90 = 0.846 = 84.6\,\%$
- $P_{in} = P_{out} \, / \, \eta_{ov} = 8.64 \, / \, 0.846 = 10.21$ kW

**Compact Final Answer Table (1 mark):**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Motor speed | 658 rpm |
| Theoretical torque | 139.3 N·m |
| Actual torque | 125.4 N·m |
| Output power | 8.64 kW |
| Overall efficiency | 84.6 % |
| Input hydraulic power | 10.21 kW |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common error 1:** Writing $\eta_{ov} = \eta_v + \eta_m$ instead of $\eta_{ov} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$. **Marks lost: 1 per occurrence.**
> - **Common error 2:** Forgetting to convert L/min to m³/s when using SI formulas ($Q$ in m³/s, not L/min). **Marks lost: 1–2.**
> - **Common error 3:** Using bore area instead of annular area for pull force/velocity. **Marks lost: 1.**
> - **Common error 4:** Computing the **input** power instead of the **output** power, or vice versa, without clearly stating the reference. Always state *"Output hydraulic power"* and *"Shaft input power"* explicitly.
> - **Common error 5:** In the characteristic-curve question, failing to mention that the operating point is determined by the **intersection** of two curves and not by one curve alone. **Marks lost: 1–2.**
> - **Common error 6:** Not specifying the *units* in the final numerical answer table. KTU examiners **require** units; missing units cost 0.5–1 mark per table.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 1**

- **Selection** = the *engineering decision*; **Specification** = the *technical declaration*; **Characteristics** = the *performance representation*. Memorize this distinction in one line.
- The **six specification categories**: functional, geometric, fluid, environmental, performance, compliance.
- The **pump selection hierarchy**: duty → power → fluid compatibility → derating → compare → verify envelope → finalize.
- **Volumetric efficiency** falls at low speed and high pressure (leakage dominates). Formula: $\eta_v = 1 - \dfrac{K_L \cdot \Delta p}{D_p \cdot n}$.
- **Mechanical efficiency** is low at low pressure (friction dominates) and approaches 100 % at high pressure. Formula: $\eta_m = \dfrac{T_{th}}{T_{th} + T_{fric}}$.
- **Overall efficiency = product**, not sum: $\eta_{ov} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$. **Never write $\eta_v + \eta_m$.**
- The **hydraulic power formula** has two forms:
   - $P\,[\text{kW}] = p\,[\text{bar}] \cdot Q\,[\text{L/min}] \, / \, 600$ (practical form)
   - $P\,[\text{W}] = p\,[\text{Pa}] \cdot Q\,[\text{m}^3/\text{s}]$ (SI form)
- The **system resistance curve** is $p_{sys} = p_{static} + K \cdot Q^2$. The **operating point** is the unique intersection with the pump's Q–p curve.
- The **flow–power–torque triplet** for motors: $Q = D_m \cdot n \, / \, \eta_v$, $T = D_m \cdot \Delta p \cdot \eta_m \, / \, 2\pi$, $P = T \cdot 2\pi \cdot n$.
- The **cylinder force–velocity** pair: $F = p \cdot A \cdot \eta_{m,c}$ and $v = Q \, / \, A$. Remember: **push uses bore area, pull uses annular area.**
- The **reservoir sizing rule of thumb**: $V_{res} \geq 2$ to $5 \times Q_{pump\,(L/min)}$ — this is a common 3-mark direct question.
- **Heat dissipation**: $P_{loss} = P_{in} (1 - \eta_{ov})$; this heat must be removed by the cooler. Roughly **1 kW ≈ 150 L/min air flow** in a typical air-cooled heat exchanger.
- **Standards to remember**: ISO 4413 (general), ISO 1219 (symbols), ISO 4406 (cleanliness), ISO 3019 (pump mounts), NFPA T3.6.1 (US).
- **Viscosity reference**: ISO VG 46 oil at 40 °C = 46 cSt — this is the **default** fluid assumed in textbook problems unless stated otherwise.
- **Always state units** in the final answer table. **Always show the conversion** of L/min to m³/s for SI formulas.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT526 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Pressure

**Pressure ($P$)** in fluid mechanics and industrial hydraulics is formally defined as the **normal force exerted by a fluid per unit area** on a real or imaginary surface. Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$P = \frac{dF}{dA}$$

where:
- $dF$ is the differential normal force acting on the surface (in **Newtons, N**)
- $dA$ is the differential elemental area (in **square meters, m²**)
- $P$ is the pressure (in **Pascals, Pa**, where $1 \, \text{Pa} = 1 \, \text{N/m}^2$)

In an industrial hydraulic context, pressure is the **driving potential** that causes hydraulic fluid to flow, transmit energy, and actuate mechanical components such as cylinders and motors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Pressure is a *scalar quantity* (not a vector) because it acts equally in all directions at a point in a static fluid (Pascal's Principle). However, the *force* generated by pressure IS a vector, acting normal to any given surface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard SI Units & Conversions (Memorize for KTU Exams):**
> - $1 \, \text{Pa} = 1 \, \text{N/m}^2$
> - $1 \, \text{bar} = 10^5 \, \text{Pa} = 100 \, \text{kPa}$
> - $1 \, \text{atm} = 101.325 \, \text{kPa} = 1.01325 \, \text{bar}$
> - $1 \, \text{psi} \approx 6.895 \, \text{kPa}$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of pressure as **the "push" of a fluid spread out evenly over a surface**.

### 🏠 Real-World Analogy 1: The Balloon
Imagine pressing your thumb on a fully inflated balloon. Your thumb exerts a **force** (say, 10 N) on a small **area** (say, 1 cm²). The pressure experienced by the balloon's surface is this force distributed over that area. If you used the same force but pressed with the flat palm of your hand (much larger area), the pressure would drop dramatically, and the balloon wouldn't pop. **Same force, different pressure** — this is why hydraulic systems can multiply force using area differences.

### 🚗 Real-World Analogy 2: Standing on Ice vs. Snowshoes
- Standing barefoot on ice: Your entire body weight is concentrated on a tiny footprint area → **HIGH pressure** → ice cracks.
- Wearing snowshoes: Same body weight spread over a much larger area → **LOW pressure** → you walk safely.
- **KTU Intuition:** In a hydraulic press, a small force on a small piston creates low pressure, which is then transmitted (Pascal's Law) to a LARGER piston, where it becomes a *much larger force* at the *same pressure*.

## 1.3 Types of Pressure in Industrial Hydraulics

A KTU student must master the distinction between the three fundamental pressure references:

| Type | Symbol | Definition | Reference Point |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Absolute Pressure** | $P_{abs}$ | Total pressure measured from a perfect vacuum (zero reference) | Vacuum = 0 Pa |
| **Gauge Pressure** | $P_{gauge}$ | Pressure measured relative to local atmospheric pressure | Atmosphere = 0 |
| **Vacuum Pressure** | $P_{vac}$ | Pressure *below* atmospheric (negative gauge) | Below atmosphere |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Relationship (Always appears in KTU numericals):**
> $$P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$$
> $$P_{abs} = P_{atm} - P_{vac}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations of Pressure

### A. Hydrostatic Pressure (Pressure Due to Fluid Column)

When a fluid is at rest under gravity, the pressure at any depth $h$ below the free surface is governed solely by the weight of the fluid column above. The governing logic is:

1. Consider a vertical column of fluid of cross-sectional area $A$ and height $h$.
2. The **weight** of this column is $W = \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot h$ (mass $\times$ gravity).
3. This weight pushes down on the area $A$ at the base, creating a force.
4. **Pressure** = Force / Area → the area $A$ cancels out, leaving only the depth-dependent term.

The resulting fundamental equation:

$$P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

where:
- $\rho$ = fluid density (**kg/m³**)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $h$ = depth below the free surface (**m**)

### B. Pascal's Law (The Foundation of All Hydraulics)

**Statement:** *A change in pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted undiminished to every portion of the fluid and to the walls of the containing vessel.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this the *heart* of industrial hydraulics?** Because it allows a small input force to generate a massive output force through area multiplication — this is the working principle of hydraulic presses, lifts, jacks, and braking systems.

**Mathematical Form:**

$$P_1 = P_2 \implies \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$$

$$F_2 = F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$

The ratio $A_2 / A_1$ is called the **Mechanical Advantage (MA)** or **Force Multiplication Factor**.

### C. Pressure in Hydraulic Actuators (Cylinders)

For a hydraulic cylinder with piston area $A_p$:

$$F_{output} = P \cdot A_p$$

where $P$ is the supply pressure from the pump and $F_{output}$ is the linear thrust force on the piston rod.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — every formula below has appeared in KTU ESE papers for PERAT526.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = F/A$ | Basic pressure definition | Pa (N/m²) |
| 2 | $P = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure due to depth | Pa |
| 3 | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$ | Absolute vs. gauge relationship | Pa |
| 4 | $P_1 = P_2$ | Pascal's Law (static fluid) | Pa |
| 5 | $F_2 = F_1 \cdot (A_2/A_1)$ | Hydraulic force multiplication | N |
| 6 | $F = P \cdot A$ | Force from pressure in a cylinder | N |
| 7 | $W = P \cdot Q$ | Hydraulic power (P=pressure, Q=flow) | W (Watts) |
| 8 | $1 \, \text{bar} = 10^5 \, \text{Pa}$ | Unit conversion | — |
| 9 | $1 \, \text{atm} = 101.325 \, \text{kPa}$ | Unit conversion | — |
| 10 | $\text{Head} \, h = P/(\rho g)$ | Pressure expressed as fluid head | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Every hydraulic machine — from the JCB excavator digging a foundation in Kochi to the aircraft landing-gear actuator — operates on the **pressurization of an incompressible fluid** following these exact equations. Mastery of these formulas is non-negotiable for KTU PERAT526.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Hydrostatic Pressure ($P = \rho g h$)

**Objective:** Derive the pressure at depth $h$ below a free surface of a static fluid of density $\rho$.

### Step-by-Step Proof

**Step 1 — Set up the physical model:**
Consider a fluid of uniform density $\rho$ in a container open to atmosphere. Pick an arbitrary horizontal plane at depth $h$ below the free surface. We want the pressure on this plane.

**Step 2 — Construct an imaginary fluid column:**
Imagine a vertical column of the fluid with:
- Cross-sectional area = $A$ (m²)
- Height = $h$ (m)
- Volume = $V = A \cdot h$ (m³)

**Step 3 — Compute the mass of the column:**
The mass of this column is its volume multiplied by its density:

$$m = \rho \cdot V = \rho \cdot A \cdot h$$

**Step 4 — Compute the weight (gravitational force) of the column:**

$$W = m \cdot g = \rho \cdot A \cdot h \cdot g$$

**Step 5 — This weight pushes down on the base of the column:**
The base of the column (area $A$) supports the entire weight of the fluid above it. By Newton's 2nd law, the force exerted on the base is:

$$F = W = \rho \cdot A \cdot h \cdot g$$

**Step 6 — Apply the definition of pressure:**
Pressure is force per unit area:

$$P = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{\rho \cdot A \cdot h \cdot g}{A}$$

**Step 7 — Cancel the area $A$:**

$$\boxed{P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h}$$

**Conclusion:** The pressure at depth $h$ depends *only* on the fluid density, gravity, and depth — **NOT on the shape of the container, the surface area, or the total volume of fluid.** This is a famous result in fluid statics, often called the **hydrostatic paradox**.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Force Multiplication in a Hydraulic Press (Pascal's Law)

**Objective:** Show that a hydraulic press can multiply an input force.

### Step-by-Step Proof

**Step 1 — System description:**
A hydraulic press consists of two connected cylinders (small piston $A_1$, large piston $A_2$) filled with an incompressible fluid (typically mineral oil) and connected at the bottom.

**Step 2 — Apply an input force:**
Let an input force $F_1$ be applied to the small piston of area $A_1$. The pressure generated at the small piston is:

$$P_1 = \frac{F_1}{A_1}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Pascal's Law:**
By Pascal's principle, this pressure is transmitted *undiminished* through the fluid to the large piston:

$$P_2 = P_1 = \frac{F_1}{A_1}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the output force:**
The output force $F_2$ on the large piston is:

$$F_2 = P_2 \cdot A_2$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $P_2$ from Step 3:**

$$F_2 = \frac{F_1}{A_1} \cdot A_2$$

**Step 6 — Final rearranged form:**

$$\boxed{F_2 = F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}}$$

**Numerical sanity check:** If $A_2 / A_1 = 100$ (i.e., the large piston has 100× the area of the small piston), then a 10 N input force produces a 1000 N output force. The trade-off: the large piston moves only 1/100th the distance (conservation of energy / volume).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem)

**Problem:** A hydraulic press has a small piston of diameter $d_1 = 5 \, \text{cm}$ and a large piston of diameter $d_2 = 50 \, \text{cm}$. A force of $F_1 = 200 \, \text{N}$ is applied on the small piston. Calculate:
(a) The pressure developed in the fluid.
(b) The output force on the large piston.

### Model Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the piston areas:**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} d_1^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.05)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} d_2^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.50)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-1} \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute the pressure (Part a):**

$$P = \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{200}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$P = 1.019 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa} = 101.9 \, \text{kPa} \approx 1.019 \, \text{bar}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the output force (Part b):**

$$F_2 = P \cdot A_2 = (1.019 \times 10^5) \times (1.963 \times 10^{-1})$$

$$F_2 = 2.0 \times 10^4 \, \text{N} = 20 \, \text{kN}$$

**Verification using area ratio:**

$$\frac{A_2}{A_1} = \left(\frac{d_2}{d_1}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{50}{5}\right)^2 = 100$$

$$F_2 = F_1 \times 100 = 200 \times 100 = 20{,}000 \, \text{N} = 20 \, \text{kN} \quad \checkmark$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Hydraulic Pressure System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow of pressure** in a typical industrial hydraulic circuit, from generation to utilization. This is the kind of conceptual schematic KTU examiners expect students to reproduce.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Electric Motor Prime Mover] --> A2[Hydraulic Pump Conversion of mechanical to hydraulic energy]
    A2 --> A3[Pressure Relief Valve Safety: caps max pressure]
    A3 --> A4[Directional Control Valve DCV routes fluid path]
    A4 --> A5[Pressure Gauge Visual indication of system pressure]
    A4 --> A6[Hydraulic Cylinder Actuator converts pressure to linear force]
    A4 --> A7[Hydraulic Motor Actuator converts pressure to rotary torque]
    A6 --> A8[Output Linear Thrust Force equals P times A]
    A7 --> A9[Output Rotary Torque equals P times displacement]
    A2 --> A10[Reservoir Stores fluid cools filters]
    A10 --> A2
    A6 --> A11[Return Line Low pressure flow back to tank]
    A7 --> A11
    A11 --> A10
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Pressure originates** at the pump (A2) by converting mechanical energy into hydraulic energy.
- A **relief valve** (A3) protects the system from overpressure — typically set to ~10–20% above the working pressure.
- A **DCV** (A4) directs the pressurized fluid to the chosen actuator.
- The **gauge** (A5) provides real-time feedback of system pressure.
- Actuators (A6, A7) convert pressure into useful mechanical work.
- Spent fluid returns via the **return line** (A11) to the **reservoir** (A10).

## 4.2 Pressure Reference Comparison (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V[Vacuum Zero Reference 0 Pa absolute] -->|plus P_atm| PABS[Absolute Pressure P_abs]
    ATM[Atmospheric Pressure P_atm approx 101.325 kPa] -->|Zero line| GAUGE[Gauge Pressure P_gauge]
    PABS -->|P_gauge = P_abs minus P_atm| GAUGE
    GAUGE -->|If negative Vacuum| VAC[Vacuum Pressure P_vac]
    VAC -->|P_abs = P_atm minus P_vac| PABS
```

**How to read this for KTU exams:**
- The **vacuum line** (V) is the absolute zero — no molecules, no pressure.
- **Atmospheric pressure** sits ~101.325 kPa above vacuum — this is the *zero* of the gauge scale.
- **Absolute pressure** is measured from vacuum → always positive.
- **Gauge pressure** is measured from atmosphere → can be positive, zero, or negative.
- **Negative gauge pressure** is called **vacuum pressure**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** KTU questions in PERAT526 frequently give a pressure in *bar(g)* or *psig* and ask the student to convert to *bar(a)* or *psia*. Always use $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$.

## 4.3 Pascal's Law Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Input Force F1 small] -->|Pushes down| P1[Small Piston Area A1]
    P1 -->|Creates Pressure P = F1/A1| FL[Incompressible Fluid transmits pressure unchanged]
    FL -->|Pressure acts on| P2[Large Piston Area A2]
    P2 -->|Produces Force F2 = P times A2| F2[Output Force F2 large = F1 times A2/A1]
    F2 -->|Lifts| LD[Load Mass times g]
```

**Key insight from the diagram:** The pressure $P$ is the *same* on both pistons. The output force is larger *only because* the area is larger. This is the essence of Pascal's Law.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define pressure. What are the different types of pressure used in industrial hydraulics?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Pressure is defined as the normal force exerted per unit area on a surface. Its SI unit is Pascal (Pa) = N/m².

The three types of pressure in industrial hydraulics are:
1. **Absolute Pressure ($P_{abs}$):** Measured from a perfect vacuum (zero reference). Always positive.
2. **Gauge Pressure ($P_{gauge}$):** Measured relative to local atmospheric pressure. Can be positive, zero, or negative.
3. **Vacuum Pressure ($P_{vac}$):** The magnitude of pressure below atmospheric — essentially negative gauge pressure.

The relationship: $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge} = P_{atm} - P_{vac}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often *forget to add atmospheric pressure* when converting gauge to absolute, costing 1 full mark. Memorize: $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + 101.325 \, \text{kPa}$ (at sea level).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and explain Pascal's Law with one industrial application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Pascal's Law:** A pressure change applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted undiminished to every part of the fluid and to the walls of the container.

**Explanation:** This means the pressure at any two points in a static, enclosed fluid is equal. Therefore, $\frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$, allowing a small input force to produce a much larger output force by using a larger output piston.

**Industrial Application:** The **hydraulic jack** used to lift vehicles in service stations. A small force on a small piston creates pressure that lifts a heavy car through a much larger piston.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often state the law *without naming the application* — KTU examiners explicitly ask for *one example*. Loss: 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: Hydraulic Press Problem `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A hydraulic press has a small piston of diameter 4 cm and a large piston of diameter 40 cm. A force of 150 N is applied on the small piston.**

**(a)** Calculate the pressure developed in the hydraulic fluid and the load that can be lifted by the large piston. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the small piston moves down by 10 cm, calculate the distance moved by the large piston and verify the conservation of volume. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Pressure and Load:**

**Step 1:** Calculate areas:

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.04)^2 = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.40)^2 = 1.2566 \times 10^{-1} \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Pressure generated:

$$P = \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{150}{1.2566 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.1937 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa} \approx 1.194 \, \text{bar}$$

**Step 3:** Load lifted by large piston:

$$F_2 = P \cdot A_2 = (1.1937 \times 10^5)(1.2566 \times 10^{-1}) = 15{,}000 \, \text{N} = 15 \, \text{kN}$$

**Alternative check using area ratio:**

$$\frac{A_2}{A_1} = \left(\frac{40}{4}\right)^2 = 100 \implies F_2 = 150 \times 100 = 15{,}000 \, \text{N} \quad \checkmark$$

**Part (b) — Distance and Volume Conservation:**

**Step 1:** Volume of fluid displaced by small piston:

$$V_1 = A_1 \cdot x_1 = (1.2566 \times 10^{-3})(0.10) = 1.2566 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^3$$

**Step 2:** By conservation of volume (incompressible fluid), $V_2 = V_1$:

$$A_2 \cdot x_2 = A_1 \cdot x_1 \implies x_2 = \frac{A_1 \cdot x_1}{A_2} = \frac{1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.10}{1.2566 \times 10^{-1}}$$

$$x_2 = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m} = 1 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 3:** Verify conservation:

$$V_1 = 1.2566 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^3, \quad V_2 = (1.2566 \times 10^{-1})(1.0 \times 10^{-3}) = 1.2566 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^3 \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** Force is multiplied 100×, but distance is reduced 100×. Work in = Work out (energy conservation).

---

### Question B: Hydrostatic Pressure Problem `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A vertical hydraulic cylinder has a piston of diameter 15 cm. The cylinder contains oil of specific gravity 0.86, and the piston is initially at the bottom of a 2 m oil column open to atmosphere.**

**(a)** Calculate the gauge pressure at the bottom of the oil column and the total downward thrust on the piston. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If a load of 5 kN is placed on the piston, calculate the new equilibrium height of the oil column (neglecting piston weight). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Gauge Pressure and Thrust:**

**Step 1:** Fluid density:

$$\rho = SG \times 1000 = 0.86 \times 1000 = 860 \, \text{kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2:** Hydrostatic gauge pressure at depth $h = 2$ m:

$$P = \rho g h = 860 \times 9.81 \times 2 = 16{,}873.2 \, \text{Pa} \approx 16.87 \, \text{kPa}$$

**Step 3:** Piston area:

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.15)^2 = 1.767 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 4:** Downward thrust on piston:

$$F = P \cdot A = 16{,}873.2 \times 1.767 \times 10^{-2} = 298.2 \, \text{N}$$

**Part (b) — New Equilibrium Height:**

**Step 1:** The applied load of 5 kN creates an additional pressure on the fluid:

$$P_{applied} = \frac{F_{load}}{A} = \frac{5000}{1.767 \times 10^{-2}} = 2.829 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa}$$

**Step 2:** However, the *gauge* pressure at the bottom still equals $\rho g h_{new}$ in equilibrium. But the piston has *risen*, so part of the load is supported by the **atmospheric pressure acting down on the open top** plus the load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Insight:** The problem simplifies if we note that the load pushes the piston *up*, compressing the fluid below. The new gauge pressure at the bottom = (atmospheric pressure pushing down on the piston is cancelled) + the load pressure.

**Step 3:** The pressure at the bottom must balance the load pressure:

$$\rho g h_{new} = \frac{F_{load}}{A}$$

$$h_{new} = \frac{F_{load}}{\rho g A} = \frac{5000}{860 \times 9.81 \times 1.767 \times 10^{-2}}$$

$$h_{new} = \frac{5000}{149.04} = 33.55 \, \text{m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Critical for 7 marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting specific gravity conversion:** Oil density is NOT 1000 kg/m³. Students who use $\rho = 1000$ lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in Part (b):** Some students use $g = 9.8$ vs. $9.81$ inconsistently. Use $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$ throughout.
> 3. **Piston area calculation:** Diameter is 15 cm = **0.15 m**, NOT 15 m. The most common numerical error in KTU papers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 KTU PERAT526 - Module 2 | Pressure — Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Definition:** Pressure = Normal Force / Area. SI unit = **Pascal (Pa)** = N/m². It is a **scalar** quantity in static fluids.
- ✅ **Three Types:** Absolute (from vacuum), Gauge (from atmosphere), Vacuum (negative gauge). Key relation: $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$.
- ✅ **Hydrostatic Equation:** $P = \rho g h$ — pressure at depth $h$ in a static fluid. Independent of container shape.
- ✅ **Pascal's Law:** Pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted undiminished in all directions. Foundation of all hydraulic systems.
- ✅ **Force Multiplication:** $F_2 = F_1 \cdot (A_2/A_1)$. The factor $(A_2/A_1)$ is the **mechanical advantage**.
- ✅ **Cylinder Force:** $F = P \cdot A$ — fundamental equation for hydraulic actuator design.
- ✅ **Energy Conservation:** Force × Distance is conserved. Large force → small distance; small force → large distance.
- ✅ **Unit Conversions to Memorize:** 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa, 1 atm = 101.325 kPa, 1 psi ≈ 6.895 kPa, SG of water = 1, SG of typical hydraulic oil ≈ 0.86.
- ✅ **Common Industrial Applications:** Hydraulic press, hydraulic jack, hydraulic brake, excavator arm, injection molding machine, aircraft landing gear.
- ✅ **Examiner's Mantra:** *"Always state the formula → substitute values with units → compute → box the answer with correct units."* KTU markers award partial marks for correct methodology even if the final number is wrong.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Direction and Flow Control Valves - Relief Valves, Non-return and Safety Valves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT526 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Direction and Flow Control Valves - Relief Valves, Non-return and Safety Valves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Direction Control Valves (DCV) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Direction Control Valves (DCV)** are hydraulic control elements that **start, stop, or change the direction of fluid flow** in a hydraulic circuit. They do **not** regulate flow rate or pressure magnitude; their sole function is **routing** the hydraulic fluid to the desired actuator port.

The most common classification is based on the number of **ports** (flow connections) and **positions** (discrete switching states):

| Valve Type | Notation | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| 2-Way, 2-Position | 2/2 Valve | On/Off shut-off |
| 3-Way, 2-Position | 3/2 Valve | Single-acting cylinder control |
| 4-Way, 2-Position | 4/2 Valve | Double-acting cylinder (bistable) |
| 4-Way, 3-Position | 4/3 Valve | Double-acting cylinder with neutral (most common) |

**Actuation methods** (how the spool is shifted):
- **Manual** (lever, push-button, pedal)
- **Mechanical** (cam, roller)
- **Solenoid** (electrical signal)
- **Pilot** (hydraulic / pneumatic pilot pressure)
- **Electro-hydraulic** (combination)

### 1.2 Flow Control Valves (FCV) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Flow Control Valves** regulate the **velocity (speed)** of hydraulic actuators by restricting the volumetric flow rate. They are essentially variable orifices placed in the flow path. The **flow rate** is governed by the orifice equation:
>
> $$Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

where $C_d$ is the discharge coefficient, $A$ is the orifice area, $\Delta P$ is the pressure drop across the valve, and $\rho$ is the fluid density.

**Two principal types:**
1. **Non-compensated (orifice-type)** — flow changes with load and pump pressure variations.
2. **Pressure-compensated** — uses an internal pressure-balancing spool to maintain a constant $\Delta P$ across the adjustable orifice, giving a near-constant flow rate regardless of load.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy for DCV:** Think of a **railway track switch (points)**. A DCV is the hydraulic equivalent — it never changes how fast the train moves, only **which track** the fluid travels on. A **4/3 valve with closed center** is like a **roundabout with all exits closed** — the fluid simply has nowhere to go and the pump unloads.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy for FCV:** Think of a **tap/faucet** in your kitchen. Turning the handle changes the **cross-sectional area** the water passes through, hence controlling how much water flows per second — but the mains pressure stays roughly the same. A **pressure-compensated FCV** is like a tap connected to a constant-pressure header tank, so flow stays constant even if downstream demand fluctuates.

### 1.4 Relief, Non-return, and Safety Valves — Overview

These three are **pressure-control / unidirectional** valves forming the backbone of hydraulic safety architecture:

| Valve | Primary Function | Fails When… |
|---|---|---|
| **Relief Valve** | Limits **maximum system pressure** by dumping excess flow to tank | Set pressure is too high or valve is stuck |
| **Non-return (Check) Valve** | Allows flow in **one direction only**, blocks reverse flow | Debris holds the poppet off seat |
| **Safety Valve** | **Emergency overpressure protection** (often with certification) | Set pressure is misadjusted or spring fatigues |

> [!NOTE]
> In strict KTU parlance, a **Relief Valve** is *operational* (it opens frequently during normal working cycles to bleed off peaks), while a **Safety Valve** is *protective* (it is expected to remain closed and only opens in an emergency/abnormal condition). Many textbooks use the terms interchangeably — for KTU 2024, distinguish them clearly.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Relief Valves — Operating Principle

A relief valve is a **normally-closed, pressure-operated valve** held shut by an adjustable spring. When upstream (system) pressure overcomes the spring force plus the poppet's hydraulic area, the valve opens and dumps fluid to the tank.

**Force balance on the poppet (spool/piston):**

$$F_{spring} + F_{hydraulic \, closing} = F_{system} = P_s \cdot A$$

At the **cracking (set) pressure** $P_{set}$:

$$P_{set} \cdot A = F_{spring} = k \cdot x_0$$

where:
- $A$ = effective pressure-balancing area (m²)
- $k$ = spring stiffness (N/m)
- $x_0$ = spring pre-compression (m)
- $P_{set}$ = setting pressure (Pa)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Direct-Acting vs. Pilot-Operated Relief Valves**

**a) Direct-Acting Relief Valve:**
- The system pressure acts directly on the poppet/piston.
- Simple, cheap, fast response.
- Used for low-to-medium flows.
- Susceptible to **hunting/pressure overshoot** because as flow passes, the pressure drops and the valve recloses (chatter).

**b) Pilot-Operated Relief Valve (PORV):**
- A small **pilot relief valve** senses system pressure and modulates the main stage.
- Main stage is held open by draining the chamber behind the main poppet through a small orifice controlled by the pilot.
- **Much higher flow capacity** for the same physical size.
- **Lower pressure overshoot**, smoother modulation, more accurate setting.
- Standard in modern industrial hydraulic power units (HPU).

### 2.2 Non-Return (Check) Valves — Operating Principle

A non-return valve permits free flow in one direction and **automatically blocks** flow in the reverse direction. Common constructions:

- **Ball-type** — a steel ball is pressed against a conical seat by reverse flow.
- **Poppet-type** — a guided poppet seats against a precision-ground seat.
- **Flap/swing-type** — a hinged disc swings open in the forward direction.

**Cracking pressure** $P_{cr}$ is the minimum reverse pressure required to seat the valve and is given by:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{F_{spring}}{A_{seat}}$$

### 2.3 Safety Valves — Operating Principle

Functionally identical to relief valves but treated as a **last line of defense**. KTU expects you to know:

- They are typically **set slightly above** the working pressure of the relief valve.
- Often **direct-acting, spring-loaded, and poppet-type**.
- Required by statutory codes (e.g., **PED 2014/68/EU**, **ASME Section VIII**) on pressure vessels, accumulators, and high-pressure circuits.
- After opening, they must **reseat tightly** when pressure falls below the blow-down pressure.

**Blow-down** $\Delta P_{bd}$ is the pressure difference between **set pressure** and **reseat pressure**:

$$\Delta P_{bd} = P_{set} - P_{reseat}$$

A good safety valve has $\Delta P_{bd} \approx 3\text{–}7\%$ of $P_{set}$.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{set} = \dfrac{k \cdot x_0}{A}$ | Cracking pressure of spring-loaded relief | Pa |
| 2 | $Q = C_d A \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | Orifice (flow control) equation | m³/s |
| 3 | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{F_{spring}}{A_{seat}}$ | Cracking pressure of check valve | Pa |
| 4 | $\Delta P_{bd} = P_{set} - P_{reseat}$ | Blow-down of safety valve | Pa |
| 5 | $F_{net} = P_s A - k(x_0 + x)$ | Net opening force on poppet | N |
| 6 | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A_p}$ | Actuator piston velocity | m/s |
| 7 | $F_{actuator} = P \cdot A_p - P' \cdot A_r$ | Force on double-acting cylinder | N |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering utility:** Relief valves protect pumps, hoses, and actuators from pressure spikes generated by load-induced transients. Non-return valves **isolate circuits** (e.g., prevent back-rotation of a vertical cylinder under gravity load) and **maintain pressure in a holding circuit** (e.g., in a counterbalance valve arrangement on a vertical press).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Spring-Set Pressure of a Direct-Acting Relief Valve

**Given:** A direct-acting relief valve has a poppet of effective area $A = 300 \text{ mm}^2$ held against its seat by a helical spring of stiffness $k = 25 \text{ N/mm}$. The spring is pre-compressed by $x_0 = 12 \text{ mm}$.

**Find:** The pressure at which the valve begins to open (cracking pressure).

**Step 1 — Write the static force balance on the poppet in the closed condition.**

For the poppet to be on the verge of opening, the upward hydraulic force must equal the downward spring preload:

$$P_{set} \cdot A = F_{spring}$$

**Step 2 — Express the spring force in terms of stiffness and pre-compression.**

By Hooke's law for the pre-loaded spring:

$$F_{spring} = k \cdot x_0$$

**Step 3 — Equate and solve for $P_{set}$.**

$$P_{set} \cdot A = k \cdot x_0$$

$$P_{set} = \frac{k \cdot x_0}{A}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values with consistent SI units.**

$k = 25 \text{ N/mm} = 25 \times 10^{3} \text{ N/m}$

$x_0 = 12 \text{ mm} = 12 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$

$A = 300 \text{ mm}^2 = 300 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$

$$P_{set} = \frac{(25 \times 10^{3})(12 \times 10^{-3})}{300 \times 10^{-6}}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the numerator and denominator separately.**

$$\text{Numerator} = 25 \times 10^{3} \times 12 \times 10^{-3} = 300 \text{ N}$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 300 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2 = 3 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 6 — Compute final answer.**

$$P_{set} = \frac{300}{3 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.0 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 10 \text{ bar}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The conversion **1 bar = 10⁵ Pa** is fundamental in KTU problems. Always report your final pressure in **bar** or **MPa** as per the question's requirement.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Pressure-Control Valves

| Parameter | Direct-Acting Relief | Pilot-Operated Relief | Safety Valve | Non-Return Valve |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Actuating signal** | System pressure | Pilot pressure (from main line) | System pressure | Reverse flow pressure |
| **Typical set range** | 5 – 350 bar | 10 – 700 bar | 5 – 600 bar | Cracking 0.1 – 5 bar typical |
| **Flow capacity** | Low – Medium | High | Medium | High (when open) |
| **Pressure overshoot** | High (chatter) | Low | Moderate | N/A |
| **Re-seat quality** | Moderate | Good | Excellent (tight shut-off) | Excellent (zero leakage design) |
| **Adjustment** | Adjustable screw | Adjustable screw on pilot | Locked / sealed | Not adjustable (fixed cracking) |
| **Leakage tolerance** | Permitted (slight) | Permitted (slight) | **Must be near-zero** (Tight shut-off class) | Near-zero |
| **KTU exam weightage** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

### 3.3 Numerical Example: Actuator Speed with Flow Control Valve

**Given:** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has bore $D = 80 \text{ mm}$, rod diameter $d = 45 \text{ mm}$. Pump flow = $30 \text{ LPM}$. A meter-out flow control valve is placed in the return line; its orifice area is set such that the return-side pressure drop is $\Delta P = 8 \text{ bar}$ across the FCV.

**Find:** Extension speed of the cylinder, and force generated if load pressure = 60 bar.

**Step 1 — Compute piston area and annular (return-side) area.**

$$A_p = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.080)^2 = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_r = \frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.080^2 - 0.045^2) = 3.436 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Convert pump flow to SI units.**

$$Q = 30 \text{ LPM} = \frac{30 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute extension velocity.**

$$v_{ext} = \frac{Q}{A_p} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 0.0995 \text{ m/s} \approx 100 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute actuator force (forward stroke).**

$$F = P \cdot A_p - P' \cdot A_r$$

For meter-out, $P'$ (return side) = $P - \Delta P_{FCV}$ approximately, but for force calculation on a load, use the **cap-end pressure** $P = 60 \text{ bar} = 6 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}$:

$$F = 6 \times 10^{6} \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} = 30{,}162 \text{ N} \approx 30.16 \text{ kN}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Pressure-Control Valves
