<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`
**Differentiate between a direct-acting and a pilot-operated relief valve. State one application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Direct-Acting Relief Valve | Pilot-Operated Relief Valve (PORV) |
|---|---|---|
| Sensing element | Main poppet directly senses system pressure | Small pilot valve senses pressure; controls main stage |
| Flow capacity | Low to medium | High |
| Pressure overshoot | Significant (chatter possible) | Minimal (smooth modulation) |
| Cost & size | Simple, compact, cheap | Complex, larger, costlier |
| **Application** | Small mobile hydraulics, low-flow circuits | Industrial press HPUs, machine tools, large flow circuits |

*(1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for application — total 3 marks)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Exam]`
**What is the function of a non-return valve? Sketch a poppet-type check valve and label the seat, poppet, and spring.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Function (1 mark):** A non-return valve (check valve) allows free flow of hydraulic fluid in one direction and **automatically blocks** flow in the reverse direction.
- **Sketch (2 marks):** Must show:
  - Inlet port (left)
  - Conical **seat**
  - **Poppet** (guided) resting on seat
  - Light **return spring** (or no spring — depending on design)
  - Outlet port (right) with arrow showing allowed flow direction

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

**Q3. Question A (14 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **construction and working of a pilot-operated relief valve**. Discuss its advantages over a direct-acting relief valve. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A direct-acting relief valve has a poppet of effective area **600 mm²** held on its seat by a spring of stiffness **40 N/mm**. The spring is pre-compressed by **15 mm**. When the valve is fully open, the spring is compressed by an additional **5 mm** due to poppet lift.
   (i) Determine the **cracking pressure** of the valve. **(3 marks)**
   (ii) Determine the **full-open pressure** of the valve. **(4 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) PORV — Construction & Working (7 marks):**

**Construction:** A PORV consists of two stages in a single body:
1. **Main stage** — a large poppet or spool that handles the bulk of the flow.
2. **Pilot stage** — a small spring-loaded poppet that senses system pressure and meters a small bleed flow.

A **fixed orifice** is placed between the main inlet and the control chamber behind the main poppet. The pilot poppet controls the **drainage** of this control chamber to the tank.

**Working:**
- Below set pressure → pilot is closed → control chamber fills with trapped fluid → pressure acts on the back of main poppet, **holding it firmly closed** (pressure-balanced area design).
- Above set pressure → pilot opens → fluid in the control chamber drains through the pilot → pressure behind the main poppet **drops** → main poppet lifts → main flow passes to tank.
- As system pressure falls, pilot closes again, control chamber re-pressurises, and main poppet reseats.

**Advantages over direct-acting (4 key points, 1 mark each):**
1. Handles **much higher flows** in a compact body.
2. **Lower pressure overshoot** — smooth modulation, no chatter.
3. **Better pressure regulation accuracy** at high flow.
4. **Less prone to vibration/noise** during operation.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Cracking Pressure (3 marks):**

At the verge of opening, spring force = hydraulic force:

$$P_{set} = \frac{k \cdot x_0}{A}$$

$k = 40 \text{ N/mm} = 40 \times 10^{3} \text{ N/m}$

$x_0 = 15 \text{ mm} = 15 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$

$A = 600 \text{ mm}^2 = 6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$

$$P_{set} = \frac{40 \times 10^{3} \times 15 \times 10^{-3}}{6 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{600}{6 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.0 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 10 \text{ bar}$$

**Cracking pressure = 10 bar** ✅

**(ii) Full-Open Pressure (4 marks):**

When the poppet has lifted by an additional $x_{lift} = 5 \text{ mm}$, the total spring compression becomes:

$$x_{total} = x_0 + x_{lift} = 15 + 5 = 20 \text{ mm}$$

Total spring force = $k \cdot x_{total} = 40 \times 10^{3} \times 20 \times 10^{-3} = 800 \text{ N}$

Full-open pressure:

$$P_{fo} = \frac{k \cdot x_{total}}{A} = \frac{800}{6 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.333 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 13.33 \text{ bar}$$

**Full-open pressure ≈ 13.33 bar** ✅

---

**Q3. Question B (14 marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the **different types of non-return valves** used in hydraulic systems with neat sketches. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic circuit uses a **4/3 DCV** with closed-center position to control a double-acting cylinder. Pump flow = **40 LPM**, pump pressure = **100 bar**, system pressure setting = **80 bar**. The cylinder has bore **100 mm** and rod diameter **60 mm**. Calculate:
   (i) The **extension velocity** of the cylinder. **(3 marks)**
   (ii) The **retraction velocity** of the cylinder. **(4 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Types of Non-Return Valves (7 marks):**

1. **Ball-type check valve (2 marks):** A spherical ball is pressed against a conical seat. Reverse flow lifts the ball off the seat, allowing forward flow. The ball is held against the seat by a light spring. Simple, cheap, but limited to small sizes.

2. **Poppet-type check valve (2 marks):** A guided cylindrical/conical poppet seats against a precision-ground orifice. Provides better sealing and higher flow capacity than ball type. Used in industrial circuits and counterbalance applications.

3. **Flap/Swing-type check valve (2 marks):** A hinged disc swings open in the forward flow direction. No spring is required — the flow itself pushes the disc open. Used in low-pressure, high-flow applications such as reservoir return lines.

4. **Pilot-operated check valve (1 mark):** Used in **load-holding** circuits. A small pilot pressure on the opposite side of the poppet unseats the poppet, allowing reverse flow when desired. Common in vertical cylinder / press applications to lock the load.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Convert units:
- $Q = 40 \text{ LPM} = \dfrac{40 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- $D = 0.100 \text{ m}$, $d = 0.060 \text{ m}$

**(i) Extension Velocity (3 marks):**

During extension, fluid enters the cap (piston) side:

$$A_p = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.100)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$v_{ext} = \frac{Q}{A_p} = \frac{6.667 \times 10^{-4}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0849 \text{ m/s} \approx 84.9 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Extension velocity ≈ 85 mm/s** ✅

**(ii) Retraction Velocity (4 marks):**

During retraction, fluid enters the rod side and the cap-side fluid is discharged:

$$A_r = \frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.100^2 - 0.060^2) = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

By continuity, the **same flow** $Q$ enters the rod side, so the retraction velocity is:

$$v_{ret} = \frac{Q}{A_r} = \frac{6.667 \times 10^{-4}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1326 \text{ m/s} \approx 132.6 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Retraction velocity ≈ 132.6 mm/s** ✅

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Pressure-Control & Direction-Control Valves:**
> 1. **Unit conversion slip-ups** — Forgetting to convert mm² to m² gives pressure answers off by a factor of 10⁶. Always work in **SI base units** in intermediate steps; convert to bar/MPa at the end.
> 2. **Confusing cracking pressure with full-open pressure** — They differ by the additional spring compression due to poppet lift. KTU examiners love testing this distinction.
> 3. **Mixing up $A_p$ and $A_r$** — On extension, fluid enters the **cap** (full bore, $A_p$); on retraction, fluid enters the **rod** side ($A_r$). Retraction is **always faster** for a given pump flow.
> 4. **Forgetting to draw the symbol/sketch** — A 7-mark question without a sketch typically loses 1–2 marks even if the description is correct.
> 5. **Calling a relief valve a "safety valve"** — In KTU's strict terminology, relief = operational dump; safety = emergency protection. Examiners deduct marks for loose nomenclature.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **DCV** = routes fluid, does not control flow rate or pressure magnitude.
- ✅ **FCV** = controls flow rate ($Q = C_d A \sqrt{2\Delta P / \rho}$); use **pressure-compensated** type for stable speed.
- ✅ **Relief valve** = primary pressure-limiting device; **direct-acting** for simplicity, **pilot-operated** for high flow & accuracy.
- ✅ **Non-return valve** = unidirectional; **ball / poppet / swing / pilot-operated** are the four main constructions.
- ✅ **Safety valve** = emergency overpressure device with **tight shut-off** and small **blow-down** (3–7 %).
- ✅ Cracking pressure formula: $P_{set} = \dfrac{k x_0}{A}$ — remember unit conversion to SI.
- ✅ **4/3 closed-center DCV** blocks flow in neutral → pump unloads to tank via relief valve.
- ✅ Always draw a **neat, labelled sketch** in 7-mark questions.
- ✅ Extension velocity uses $A_p$ (full bore); retraction velocity uses $A_r$ (annular area).
- ✅ For vertical loads, use **pilot-operated check valve** or **counterbalance valve** for safety.
- ✅ **PORV** advantages: high flow, low overshoot, smooth modulation, better accuracy.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Actuation Systems — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the framework of fluid power engineering, an **Actuation System** (also called a hydraulic actuator) is defined as the **output mechanism of a hydraulic circuit that converts the hydraulic energy supplied by the pump (in the form of pressurized fluid) into mechanical energy in the form of controlled linear motion or rotary motion**.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *PERAT526 – Industrial Hydraulics*, actuation systems are categorized into two fundamental classes:

1. **Linear Actuators** → **Hydraulic Cylinders**
2. **Rotary Actuators** → **Hydraulic Motors**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Hydraulic cylinders and motors are the *final control elements* of any hydraulic circuit. Without an actuator, even a perfectly designed pump and valve system cannot perform useful mechanical work. They form the **load interface** of the entire hydraulic power transmission chain.

The general energy conversion chain is:

$$
\text{Electrical/Mechanical Energy} \rightarrow \text{Hydraulic Pump} \rightarrow \text{Pressurized Fluid} \rightarrow \text{Actuator} \rightarrow \text{Mechanical Work Output}
$$

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

**Analogy 1 — The Hydraulic Syringe (Cylinder):**
Imagine a large medical syringe filled with water, connected to a hand pump. When you press the pump plunger, the water pushes the syringe piston outward. This outward push can lift a small weight. The syringe is the *hydraulic cylinder*, the pump represents the *hydraulic power unit*, and the lifting of the weight is the *mechanical work done by the actuator*. The harder you pump (higher pressure) or the bigger the syringe cross-section, the more weight you can lift.

**Analogy 2 — The Water Wheel (Motor):**
Now imagine water from a tap falling onto a small turbine wheel, causing it to spin. The wheel is the *hydraulic motor*. The water pressure (hydraulic energy) is converted into rotational motion (mechanical energy). A bigger water flow spins the wheel faster, just as higher flow into a hydraulic motor increases its rotational speed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** A cylinder is like a *muscle* that pushes or pulls in a straight line. A hydraulic motor is like a *joint* that rotates. Industrial machines (presses, excavators, injection moulding machines, machine tools) use cylinders for pushing/pulling tasks and motors for rotational tasks like winches, conveyors, and rotary tables.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Hydraulic Actuators

$$
\text{Hydraulic Actuators} 
\begin{cases}
\text{Linear Actuators (Cylinders)} 
\begin{cases}
\text{Single-Acting Cylinders} \\
\text{Double-Acting Cylinders} \\
\text{Telescopic Cylinders} \\
\text{Tandem Cylinders} \\
\text{Cushioned Cylinders}
\end{cases} \\[2mm]
\text{Rotary Actuators (Motors)} 
\begin{cases}
\text{Gear Motors (External / Internal)} \\
\text{Vane Motors} \\
\text{Piston Motors} 
\begin{cases}
\text{Axial Piston} \\
\text{Radial Piston}
\end{cases}
\end{cases}
\end{cases}
$$

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics govern hydraulic actuator sizing:

- **Standard system pressure:** **210 bar** (21 MPa) for industrial hydraulics; up to **350–700 bar** for mobile and high-pressure systems.
- **Standard piston speeds:** Typically **0.1 to 1.5 m/s** for industrial cylinders.
- **Standard motor speeds:** Typically **500 to 3000 rpm** for industrial hydraulic motors.
- **Bulk modulus of hydraulic oil:** Approximately **1.5 to 1.7 GPa** at standard temperature.
- **Gravitational acceleration:** **g = 9.81 m/s²**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always carry forward the value of g = 9.81 m/s² in numerical problems involving gravitational loads, and assume standard oil properties unless specifically mentioned in the problem statement.

# Actuation Systems — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hydraulic Cylinders — Operational Principles

A hydraulic cylinder consists of a cylindrical **barrel**, a sliding **piston** connected to a **piston rod**, **end caps**, and **sealing elements**. Pressurized fluid admitted into one chamber exerts force on the differential piston area, producing linear motion.

### 2.1.1 Single-Acting Cylinder (SAC)

- Fluid pressure acts on **only one side** of the piston.
- The return stroke is achieved by an **external load force** (gravity, spring, or mechanical arrangement).
- **One** port connection; **one** direction of powered stroke.
- Used in: jacks, clamping devices, simple lifting applications.

### 2.1.2 Double-Acting Cylinder (DAC)

- Fluid pressure can be applied to **either side** of the piston.
- **Two** port connections enable powered motion in **both directions**.
- Used in: machine tools, mobile equipment booms, injection moulding machines, presses.

### 2.1.3 Telescopic Cylinders

- Multi-stage construction with progressively smaller pistons nested inside larger ones.
- Provides **very long strokes** from a compact retracted length.
- Used in: dump trucks, cranes, refuse collection vehicles.

### 2.1.4 Tandem Cylinders

- Two pistons on a common rod operating in a single barrel.
- Provides **twice the force output** of a standard cylinder of the same bore.
- Used in: high-force clamping, press applications.

### 2.1.5 Cushioned Cylinders

- Incorporate a **cushioning device** (typically a tapered plunger or floating sleeve) at the end cap.
- Traps fluid in a small cushion chamber near the end of stroke, gradually decelerating the piston.
- Used in: high-speed or high-inertia applications to prevent impact damage.

---

## 2.2 Cylinder Force, Velocity, Power, and Time Calculations

### 2.2.1 Areas of a Double-Acting Cylinder

Let:
- $D$ = **Bore diameter** (m)
- $d$ = **Piston rod diameter** (m)

**Full bore (head-end) area:**

$$
A_1 = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

**Annular (rod-end) area:**

$$
A_2 = \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}
$$

### 2.2.2 Cylinder Output Force

**Extension stroke force** (pressure $P_1$ on head side, back pressure $P_2$ on rod side):

$$
F_{ext} = P_1 \cdot A_1 - P_2 \cdot A_2
$$

**Retraction stroke force:**

$$
F_{ret} = P_2 \cdot A_2 - P_1 \cdot A_1
$$

If back pressure is negligible ($P_2 \approx 0$):

$$
F_{ext} \approx P_1 \cdot A_1, \qquad F_{ret} \approx P_2 \cdot A_2
$$

### 2.2.3 Cylinder Velocity

$$
v = \frac{Q}{A}
$$

where $Q$ is the flow rate supplied to the chamber and $A$ is the active piston area.

### 2.2.4 Cylinder Power Output

$$
P_{out} = F \cdot v = P \cdot Q \quad (\text{ideal})
$$

### 2.2.5 Stroke Time

$$
t_{stroke} = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{L \cdot A}{Q}
$$

where $L$ is the cylinder stroke length.

### 2.2.6 Mechanical Efficiency

$$
\eta_{mech} = \frac{F_{actual}}{F_{theoretical}} = \frac{F_{actual}}{P \cdot A}
$$

---

## 2.3 Hydraulic Motors — Operational Principles

A hydraulic motor converts **hydraulic energy** (pressure × flow) into **rotary mechanical energy** (torque × angular velocity). It is essentially the *inverse* of a hydraulic pump.

### 2.3.1 Gear Motors (External / Internal)

- Operate on the principle of meshing gear teeth displacing fluid from inlet to outlet.
- **External gear motor:** Two identical gears mesh inside a housing.
- **Internal gear motor:** An external spur gear meshes with an internal ring gear (crescent-shaped filling element).
- Suitable for: medium pressure, medium speed applications (≤ 200 bar, ≤ 3000 rpm).

### 2.3.2 Vane Motors

- Use a **rotor with sliding vanes** inside an elliptical cam ring.
- Centrifugal force and fluid pressure push vanes outward against the cam ring.
- Suitable for: low-to-medium pressure, high-speed applications.

### 2.3.3 Axial Piston Motors

- Pistons arranged **parallel to the axis of rotation**.
- Swash plate mechanism converts reciprocating piston motion into rotary motion.
- High efficiency, high pressure capability (up to 450 bar).
- Used in: heavy machinery, mobile hydraulics.

### 2.3.4 Radial Piston Motors

- Pistons arranged **radially** around a central cam (eccentric) ring.
- Pistons ride on the cam during rotation, delivering high torque at low speeds.
- Used in: winches, slewing drives, track drives.

---

## 2.4 Motor Torque, Speed, Power, and Efficiency

### 2.4.1 Displacement Volume ($V_D$ or $D_m$)

The volume of fluid displaced by the motor per revolution (cc/rev or m³/rev).

### 2.4.2 Theoretical Motor Speed

$$
N = \frac{Q}{V_D}
$$

where:
- $N$ = rotational speed (rev/s or rpm)
- $Q$ = flow rate supplied (m³/s or cc/s)
- $V_D$ = displacement volume per revolution

### 2.4.3 Theoretical Motor Torque

$$
T = \frac{V_D \cdot \Delta P}{2\pi}
$$

where $\Delta P$ is the pressure drop across the motor (inlet − outlet).

### 2.4.4 Theoretical Motor Power

$$
P_{out} = T \cdot \omega = \Delta P \cdot Q
$$

### 2.4.5 Motor Efficiency Definitions

**Volumetric efficiency:**

$$
\eta_v = \frac{Q_{theoretical}}{Q_{actual}} = \frac{V_D \cdot N}{Q_{actual}}
$$

**Mechanical (torque) efficiency:**

$$
\eta_m = \frac{T_{actual}}{T_{theoretical}}
$$

**Overall efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{overall} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m = \frac{T_{actual} \cdot \omega}{\Delta P \cdot Q_{actual}}
$$

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units (SI) |
|---|-----------|---------|------------|
| 1 | Cylinder head-end area | $A_1 = \pi D^2 / 4$ | m² |
| 2 | Cylinder rod-end area | $A_2 = \pi (D^2 - d^2) / 4$ | m² |
| 3 | Cylinder force (extension) | $F_{ext} = P_1 A_1 - P_2 A_2$ | N |
| 4 | Cylinder force (retraction) | $F_{ret} = P_2 A_2 - P_1 A_1$ | N |
| 5 | Cylinder velocity | $v = Q / A$ | m/s |
| 6 | Cylinder power output | $P_{out} = F \cdot v = P \cdot Q$ | W |
| 7 | Cylinder stroke time | $t = L \cdot A / Q$ | s |
| 8 | Motor theoretical speed | $N = Q / V_D$ | rev/s |
| 9 | Motor theoretical torque | $T = V_D \cdot \Delta P / 2\pi$ | N·m |
| 10 | Motor output power | $P_{out} = T \cdot \omega = \Delta P \cdot Q$ | W |
| 11 | Angular velocity | $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ | rad/s |
| 12 | Volumetric efficiency | $\eta_v = V_D N / Q_{actual}$ | dimensionless |
| 13 | Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_m = T_{actual} / T_{theoretical}$ | dimensionless |
| 14 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$ | dimensionless |
| 15 | Hydraulic pressure | $P = F / A$ | Pa (N/m²) |
| 16 | Flow rate to actuator | $Q = v \cdot A = \omega \cdot V_D / 2\pi$ | m³/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to convert units. For example, displacement $V_D$ is often given in **cc/rev** but flow $Q$ in **L/min** or **m³/s**. Always normalize units **before** substituting into formulas. Use **1 cc/rev = 1×10⁻⁶ m³/rev** and **1 L/min = 1/60000 m³/s**.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Actuation systems are the **work-producing heart** of virtually every modern machine. Industrial applications include:

- **Hydraulic presses** (forging, stamping, moulding) — high-force cylinders.
- **Excavators and earth-movers** — telescopic and double-acting cylinders + hydraulic motors.
- **Injection moulding machines** — high-speed double-acting clamping cylinders.
- **Aircraft flight control surfaces** — precision servo-actuated hydraulic cylinders.
- **Marine steering gear** — hydraulic motors with rack-and-pinion or rotary vane steering.
- **Steel mills and rolling mills** — large hydraulic cylinders for roll positioning.
- **Machine tools** — hydraulic feed cylinders and hydraulic spindle motors.

# Actuation Systems — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cylinder Output Force from First Principles

### Step 1: Starting Concept
A hydraulic cylinder converts pressure energy into mechanical force. Pressure, by definition, is force per unit area acting normal to a surface.

$$
P = \frac{F}{A} \quad \Rightarrow \quad F = P \cdot A
$$

### Step 2: Apply to the Head-End (Bore Side)
On the head-end, the entire piston face of diameter $D$ is exposed to pressure $P_1$. The effective area is the full circular area.

$$
A_1 = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

Therefore the force generated by pressure on the head-end is:

$$
F_1 = P_1 \cdot A_1 = P_1 \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

### Step 3: Apply to the Rod-End (Annular Side)
On the rod-end, the effective area is the piston face minus the rod cross-section, since the rod occupies a portion of the piston area.

$$
A_2 = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} - \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}
$$

The opposing force on the rod-end (from back pressure $P_2$):

$$
F_2 = P_2 \cdot A_2 = P_2 \cdot \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}
$$

### Step 4: Net Extension Force (Force Balance)
During extension, the head-end pressure pushes forward, while the rod-end back pressure pushes backward. The net usable force is:

$$
F_{ext} = F_1 - F_2
$$

Substituting:

$$
F_{ext} = P_1 \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} - P_2 \cdot \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}
$$

Factoring out $\pi / 4$:

$$
F_{ext} = \frac{\pi}{4} \left[ P_1 D^2 - P_2 (D^2 - d^2) \right]
$$

### Step 5: Special Case — Negligible Back Pressure
If $P_2 \approx 0$:

$$
F_{ext} \approx \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot P_1 D^2
$$

For retraction, simply swap the role of $P_1$ and $P_2$:

$$
F_{ret} \approx \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot P_2 (D^2 - d^2)
$$

This completes the cylinder force derivation. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hydraulic Motor Theoretical Torque from First Principles

### Step 1: Definition of Motor Displacement
The motor displacement $V_D$ is the volume of fluid required to turn the motor shaft through one complete revolution.

### Step 2: Energy Balance per Revolution
In one revolution, the motor receives a volume $V_D$ of fluid at pressure $\Delta P$ (pressure drop across motor). The hydraulic energy input per revolution is:

$$
E_{in} = \Delta P \cdot V_D \quad \text{(Joules)}
$$

### Step 3: Mechanical Work Output per Revolution
The mechanical work output per revolution is torque $T$ applied through one full rotation (angle = $2\pi$ radians):

$$
E_{out} = T \cdot 2\pi
$$

### Step 4: Equate Energy (Ideal, Lossless Case)
For an ideal motor:

$$
T \cdot 2\pi = \Delta P \cdot V_D
$$

Solving for $T$:

$$
T = \frac{\Delta P \cdot V_D}{2\pi}
$$

### Step 5: Actual Torque Accounting for Mechanical Losses
Real motors have mechanical friction losses. The actual (delivered) torque is:

$$
T_{actual} = \eta_m \cdot T_{theoretical} = \eta_m \cdot \frac{\Delta P \cdot V_D}{2\pi}
$$

This completes the motor torque derivation. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hydraulic Motor Speed Relation

### Step 1: Volume Flow Concept
If the motor has a displacement $V_D$ per revolution and rotates at $N$ revolutions per second, the theoretical volume of fluid required per second is:

$$
Q_{theoretical} = V_D \cdot N
$$

### Step 2: Actual Flow Consideration
Internal leakage means that the actual flow supplied to the motor is higher than the theoretical requirement.

$$
Q_{actual} = V_D \cdot N + Q_{leakage}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
Q_{actual} = \frac{V_D \cdot N}{\eta_v}
$$

### Step 3: Solving for Speed

$$
N = \frac{Q_{actual} \cdot \eta_v}{V_D}
$$

This shows that **higher flow** increases speed and **higher displacement** decreases speed, for the same supply flow. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Solved Numerical Examples

### Example 1: Double-Acting Cylinder Force and Velocity

**Problem Statement:**
A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a bore diameter $D = 100$ mm and a rod diameter $d = 60$ mm. The cylinder is supplied with a flow rate $Q = 60$ L/min. The supply pressure during extension is $P_1 = 120$ bar, and the back pressure on the rod side is $P_2 = 8$ bar. Calculate:
- (i) The extension force
- (ii) The retraction force
- (iii) The extension velocity
- (iv) The retraction velocity
- (v) The output power during extension

Assume mechanical efficiency = 100%.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Unit Conversions**

$$
D = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.100 \text{ m}, \qquad d = 60 \text{ mm} = 0.060 \text{ m}
$$

$$
P_1 = 120 \text{ bar} = 120 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 1.2 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
P_2 = 8 \text{ bar} = 8 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
Q = 60 \text{ L/min} = \frac{60 \times 10^{-3}}{60} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Areas**

$$
A_1 = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.100)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0100}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_2 = \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4} = \frac{\pi ((0.100)^2 - (0.060)^2)}{4}
$$

$$
= \frac{\pi (0.0100 - 0.0036)}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0064}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Extension Force**

$$
F_{ext} = P_1 A_1 - P_2 A_2
$$

$$
= (1.2 \times 10^7)(7.854 \times 10^{-3}) - (8 \times 10^5)(5.027 \times 10^{-3})
$$

$$
= 94247.8 - 4021.6 = 90226.2 \text{ N} \approx 90.23 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Retraction Force**

$$
F_{ret} = P_2 A_2 - P_1 A_1 \quad (\text{assuming roles swap with similar magnitudes})
$$

Or simply the symmetric case — using the rod-end area as the driving area at supply pressure $P_1$ and bore-side as back pressure:

$$
F_{ret} = P_1 A_2 - P_2 A_1
$$

$$
= (1.2 \times 10^7)(5.027 \times 10^{-3}) - (8 \times 10^5)(7.854 \times 10^{-3})
$$

$$
= 60318.6 - 6283.2 = 54035.4 \text{ N} \approx 54.04 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 5 — Extension Velocity**

$$
v_{ext} = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1273 \text{ m/s} = 7.64 \text{ m/min}
$$

**Step 6 — Retraction Velocity**

$$
v_{ret} = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1989 \text{ m/s} = 11.94 \text{ m/min}
$$

**Step 7 — Output Power During Extension**

$$
P_{out} = F_{ext} \cdot v_{ext} = 90226.2 \times 0.1273 = 11488 \text{ W} \approx 11.49 \text{ kW}
$$

Alternative verification using $P = \Delta P \cdot Q$ (ideal):

$$
P_{ideal} = (P_1 - P_2) \cdot Q = (1.2 \times 10^7 - 8 \times 10^5)(1.0 \times 10^{-3}) = 11200 \text{ W} = 11.2 \text{ kW}
$$

(The slight difference arises from the back-pressure area discrepancy; both values are acceptable approximations.)

---

### Example 2: Hydraulic Motor Torque, Speed, and Power

**Problem Statement:**
A hydraulic axial piston motor has a displacement $V_D = 75$ cc/rev. It is supplied with a flow rate $Q = 90$ L/min at a pressure of $\Delta P = 200$ bar. If the volumetric efficiency $\eta_v = 0.96$ and mechanical efficiency $\eta_m = 0.92$, calculate:
- (i) The actual rotational speed of the motor
- (ii) The theoretical torque developed
- (iii) The actual torque delivered to the load
- (iv) The output power delivered to the load
- (v) The overall efficiency

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Unit Conversions**

$$
V_D = 75 \text{ cc/rev} = 75 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev}
$$

$$
Q = 90 \text{ L/min} = \frac{90 \times 10^{-3}}{60} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
\Delta P = 200 \text{ bar} = 200 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 2.0 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 2 — Actual Rotational Speed**

$$
N = \frac{Q \cdot \eta_v}{V_D} = \frac{(1.5 \times 10^{-3})(0.96)}{75 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.44 \times 10^{-3}}{75 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
N = 19.2 \text{ rev/s} = 19.2 \times 60 = 1152 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical Torque**

$$
T_{theoretical} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot V_D}{2\pi} = \frac{(2.0 \times 10^7)(75 \times 10^{-6})}{2\pi}
$$

$$
= \frac{1500}{6.2832} = 238.73 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 4 — Actual Torque Delivered**

$$
T_{actual} = \eta_m \cdot T_{theoretical} = 0.92 \times 238.73 = 219.63 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 5 — Output Power**

$$
\omega = 2\pi N = 2\pi (19.2) = 120.64 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
P_{out} = T_{actual} \cdot \omega = 219.63 \times 120.64 = 26501 \text{ W} \approx 26.5 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 6 — Overall Efficiency**

$$
\eta_{overall} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m = 0.96 \times 0.92 = 0.8832 \approx 88.32\%
$$

Alternative verification — Input power:

$$
P_{in} = \Delta P \cdot Q = (2.0 \times 10^7)(1.5 \times 10^{-3}) = 30000 \text{ W} = 30 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\eta_{overall} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{26501}{30000} = 0.8834 \approx 88.34\%
$$

Both methods agree (minor rounding differences). $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Cylinders vs. Motors

| Property | Hydraulic Cylinder | Hydraulic Motor |
|----------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Output motion | Linear (translational) | Rotary (rotational) |
| Output parameter | Force (N) | Torque (N·m) |
| Speed parameter | Linear velocity (m/s) | Angular velocity (rpm, rad/s) |
| Key dimension | Bore diameter $D$ | Displacement $V_D$ |
| Power formula | $P = F \cdot v$ | $P = T \cdot \omega$ |
| Energy balance | $F \cdot L = P \cdot Q \cdot t$ | $T \cdot 2\pi N = \Delta P \cdot V_D \cdot N$ |
| Primary efficiency | Mechanical | Volumetric + Mechanical |
| Typical applications | Presses, lifts, clamps | Winches, conveyors, drives |

# Actuation Systems — Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Energy Conversion Flow in a Hydraulic Actuation System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pwr1[Electrical Power Supply] --> pwr2[Electric Motor / Prime Mover]
    pwr2 --> pwr3[Hydraulic Pump]
    pwr3 -->|Pressurized Oil| ctrl1[Direction Control Valve]
    ctrl1 -->|Flow Q at Pressure P| act1[Hose and Pipe Network]
    act1 -->|Flow Q at Pressure P| act2[ACTUATOR Cylinder or Motor]
    act2 -->|Mechanical Energy F x v or T x omega| ldg1[Industrial Load]
    ldg1 -->|Reaction Forces| act2
    rsv1[Reservoir] --> pwr3
    pwr3 -->|Internal Leakage| rsv1
    ctrl1 -->|Return Flow| rsv1
    act2 -->|Return Flow| rsv1
    flt1[Filter] --> pwr3
    rsv1 --> flt1
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Classification of Hydraulic Actuators

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root1[HYDRAULIC ACTUATORS]
    root1 --> lin1[Linear Actuators]
    root1 --> rot1[Rotary Actuators]
    lin1 --> cyl1[Single Acting Cylinder]
    lin1 --> cyl2[Double Acting Cylinder]
    lin1 --> cyl3[Telescopic Cylinder]
    lin1 --> cyl4[Tandem Cylinder]
    lin1 --> cyl5[Cushioned Cylinder]
    rot1 --> mtr1[Gear Motors]
    rot1 --> mtr2[Vane Motors]
    rot1 --> mtr3[Piston Motors]
    mtr1 --> mtr1a[External Gear]
    mtr1 --> mtr1b[Internal Gear]
    mtr3 --> mtr3a[Axial Piston]
    mtr3 --> mtr3b[Radial Piston]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Double-Acting Cylinder Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph cylbody[CYLINDER BODY]
        cap1[Head End Cap with Port A]
        bar1[Cylindrical Barrel]
        cap2[Rod End Cap with Port B]
    end
    pist1[Piston with Seal]
    rod1[Piston Rod]
    cush1[Head End Cushion Chamber]
    cush2[Rod End Cushion Chamber]
    seal1[Rod Seal Wiper]
    cap1 --- bar1
    bar1 --- cap2
    pist1 -.slides inside.-> bar1
    pist1 === rod1
    rod1 -.extends outside.-> cap2
    cush1 -.part of.-> cap1
    cush2 -.part of.-> cap2
    seal1 -.prevents contamination.-> cap2
    portA[Port A Inlet] --> cap1
    portB[Port B Inlet] --> cap2
```

**Description of Operation:**
- Pressurized fluid enters **Port A** → acts on full piston area $A_1$ → rod **extends**.
- Pressurized fluid enters **Port B** → acts on annular area $A_2$ → rod **retracts**.
- Cushion chambers **decelerate** piston near end-of-stroke to prevent impact damage.
- Rod seal/wiper prevents external contamination ingress.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Hydraulic Motor Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph mtrbody[HYDRAULIC MOTOR ASSEMBLY]
        st1[Inlet Port]
        rotm[Rotating Group Gears Vanes or Pistons]
        st2[Outlet Port]
        shaf[Motor Output Shaft]
    end
    inp[Pressurized Fluid Supply at DeltaP] --> st1
    st1 --> rotm
    rotm -->|Torque T developed| shaf
    rotm --> st2
    st2 --> rtn[Return to Tank at Low Pressure]
    shaf --> load[Mechanical Load]
    load -.reaction torque.-> rotm
```

**Description of Operation:**
- Pressurized fluid enters at **Inlet Port** → enters displacement chambers → pushes rotating group.
- Rotation of the group produces **torque** on the output shaft.
- Spent fluid exits via **Outlet Port** and returns to tank.
- Speed is governed by flow rate; torque is governed by pressure drop.

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — Force Velocity Trade-off in Hydraulic Cylinders

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    qflow[Constant Pump Flow Q] --> cylcase{Cylinder Bore Size}
    cylcase -->|Small Bore D| f1[High Pressure for given Force]
    cylcase -->|Large Bore D| f2[Lower Pressure for same Force]
    cylcase -->|Small Bore D| v1[High Velocity]
    cylcase -->|Large Bore D| v2[Low Velocity]
    f1 -.inverse relationship.-> v1
    f2 -.inverse relationship.-> v2
```

**Engineering Insight:** For a fixed pump flow, **larger bore cylinders produce higher force but lower velocity**; smaller bore cylinders produce lower force but higher velocity. This trade-off is fundamental to actuator selection.

---

## 4.6 Mermaid Diagram 6 — Cushioning Arrangement at End of Stroke

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    mov1[Piston Moving Toward End Cap] --> c1[Final 20 to 30 mm of Stroke]
    c1 --> c2[Piston Enters Cushion Zone]
    c2 --> c3[Tapered Cushion Plunger Seals Annular Gap]
    c3 --> c4[Remaining Fluid Forced Through Small Orifice]
    c4 --> c5[Back Pressure Rises Rapidly]
    c5 --> c6[Piston Decelerates Smoothly]
    c6 --> stop1[Piston Stops without Impact]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Actuation Systems — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a single-acting and a double-acting hydraulic cylinder. State one industrial application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Single-Acting Cylinder | Double-Acting Cylinder |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------------|
| Pressure ports | One port only | Two ports (A and B) |
| Powered strokes | One direction only | Both directions |
| Return mechanism | External load, spring, or gravity | Hydraulic pressure |
| Construction | Simpler, lighter | More complex, heavier |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Application example | Hydraulic jack, simple clamping device | Excavator boom cylinder, machine tool feed cylinder |
| Control | Simpler (one DCV position) | Requires 4/3 or 4/2 directional valve |

**Industrial Applications:**
- Single-acting: Hydraulic floor jack in automobile workshops.
- Double-acting: Hydraulic cylinder used in an excavator arm for bidirectional motion.

*(3 marks — definition 1 mark, comparison table 1 mark, applications 1 mark)*

---

### **Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]`
**Define volumetric efficiency and mechanical efficiency of a hydraulic motor. Write their mathematical expressions.**

**Model Answer:**

**Volumetric efficiency ($\eta_v$):**
It is the ratio of the theoretical flow rate required by the motor to the actual flow rate supplied to it. It accounts for internal leakage losses inside the motor.

$$
\eta_v = \frac{Q_{theoretical}}{Q_{actual}} = \frac{V_D \cdot N}{Q_{actual}}
$$

**Mechanical efficiency ($\eta_m$):**
It is the ratio of the actual torque delivered by the motor shaft to the theoretical torque developed by the pressure acting on the displacement. It accounts for friction and mechanical losses.

$$
\eta_m = \frac{T_{actual}}{T_{theoretical}} = \frac{T_{actual} \cdot 2\pi}{\Delta P \cdot V_D}
$$

**Overall efficiency:** $\eta_{overall} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$

*(3 marks — each definition 1 mark, each expression 0.5 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a bore diameter of 80 mm and a rod diameter of 45 mm. It is supplied with hydraulic fluid at a flow rate of 40 L/min and a pressure of 160 bar during extension. The back pressure on the return side is 5 bar. Calculate:**
- **(i) The effective piston areas on the head-end and rod-end.**
- **(ii) The extension and retraction forces developed by the cylinder.**
- **(iii) The extension and retraction velocities.**
- **(iv) The theoretical output power during extension.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the construction and working of a double-acting hydraulic cylinder with the help of a neat functional sketch. Discuss the function of cushioning arrangements at the end of stroke.**

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**An axial piston hydraulic motor has a displacement of 50 cc/rev. It receives a flow of 75 L/min at a pressure drop of 180 bar. If the volumetric efficiency is 95% and the mechanical efficiency is 90%, determine:**
- **(i) The actual speed of the motor in rpm.**
- **(ii) The theoretical torque and the actual torque delivered.**
- **(iii) The output power delivered to the load.**
- **(iv) The overall efficiency of the motor.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**With the help of a classification chart, explain the different types of hydraulic motors used in industry. Compare axial piston motors and radial piston motors on the basis of construction, operating pressure range, speed range, efficiency, and typical applications.**

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A, Part (a)

**Given:**
- Bore diameter $D = 80$ mm $= 0.080$ m
- Rod diameter $d = 45$ mm $= 0.045$ m
- Flow rate $Q = 40$ L/min $= \frac{40 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s
- Supply pressure $P_1 = 160$ bar $= 1.6 \times 10^7$ Pa
- Back pressure $P_2 = 5$ bar $= 5 \times 10^5$ Pa

**(i) Effective Areas:**

$$
A_1 = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.080)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0064}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_2 = \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4} = \frac{\pi ((0.080)^2 - (0.045)^2)}{4}
$$

$$
= \frac{\pi (0.0064 - 0.002025)}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.004375}{4} = 3.436 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**(ii) Forces:**

$$
F_{ext} = P_1 A_1 - P_2 A_2 = (1.6 \times 10^7)(5.027 \times 10^{-3}) - (5 \times 10^5)(3.436 \times 10^{-3})
$$

$$
= 80424.7 - 1718.0 = 78706.7 \text{ N} \approx 78.71 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
F_{ret} = P_1 A_2 - P_2 A_1 = (1.6 \times 10^7)(3.436 \times 10^{-3}) - (5 \times 10^5)(5.027 \times 10^{-3})
$$

$$
= 54976.0 - 2513.5 = 52462.5 \text{ N} \approx 52.46 \text{ kN}
$$

**(iii) Velocities:**

$$
v_{ext} = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{6.667 \times 10^{-4}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1326 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
v_{ret} = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{6.667 \times 10^{-4}}{3.436 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1940 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(iv) Output Power (Extension):**

$$
P_{out} = F_{ext} \cdot v_{ext} = 78706.7 \times 0.1326 = 10437 \text{ W} \approx 10.44 \text{ kW}
$$

---

### Solution to Question A, Part (b)

**Construction of a Double-Acting Cylinder:**

A double-acting hydraulic cylinder consists of the following main components:
1. **Cylinder Barrel** — A precision-machined tube (usually steel) providing the inner bore surface.
2. **Piston** — A cylindrical disc that slides inside the barrel, dividing it into two chambers. It is fitted with seal grooves holding **piston seals** (typically O-rings or T-seals).
3. **Piston Rod** — A hardened, ground steel rod attached to the piston, transmitting force to the external load. The rod surface is chrome-plated for wear resistance.
4. **Head End Cap** — Seals one end of the barrel; contains Port A and often a cushioning device.
5. **Rod End Cap (Gland)** — Seals the other end of the barrel; contains the rod sealing arrangement (combination of rod seal, wiper seal, and sometimes a wear ring).
6. **Ports A and B** — Threaded connections for supply and return of pressurized fluid.
7. **Cushioning Devices** — Tapered plungers or needle valves at end caps that throttle the exhaust fluid near the end of stroke.

**Working:**
- **Extension Stroke:** Pressurized oil enters Port A, pushes the piston. The oil on the rod side is pushed out through Port B and returned to tank.
- **Retraction Stroke:** Pressurized oil enters Port B, pushes the piston in the opposite direction. The oil on the head side exits through Port A.

**Cushioning Function:**
Near the end of stroke, the piston enters the cushion zone. A tapered plunger progressively reduces the exhaust port area, throttling the outgoing fluid. This creates a rapidly rising back pressure that decelerates the piston smoothly, preventing mechanical impact and reducing noise. Cushioning is essential in:
- High-inertia loads (large masses)
- High-speed cylinders (velocities > 0.5 m/s)
- Precision machines requiring vibration-free stopping

*(7 marks — construction 3 marks, working 2 marks, cushioning explanation 2 marks)*

---

### Solution to Question B, Part (a)

**Given:**
- Displacement $V_D = 50$ cc/rev $= 50 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/rev
- Flow rate $Q = 75$ L/min $= \frac{75 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 1.25 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s
- Pressure drop $\Delta P = 180$ bar $= 1.8 \times 10^7$ Pa
- Volumetric efficiency $\eta_v = 0.95$
- Mechanical efficiency $\eta_m = 0.90$

**(i) Actual Motor Speed:**

$$
N = \frac{Q \cdot \eta_v}{V_D} = \frac{(1.25 \times 10^{-3})(0.95)}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.1875 \times 10^{-3}}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = 23.75 \text{ rev/s}
$$

$$
N = 23.75 \times 60 = 1425 \text{ rpm}
$$

**(ii) Torques:**

$$
T_{theoretical} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot V_D}{2\pi} = \frac{(1.8 \times 10^7)(50 \times 10^{-6})}{2\pi} = \frac{900}{6.2832} = 143.24 \text{ N·m}
$$

$$
T_{actual} = \eta_m \cdot T_{theoretical} = 0.90 \times 143.24 = 128.92 \text{ N·m}
$$

**(iii) Output Power:**

$$
\omega = 2\pi N = 2\pi (23.75) = 149.23 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
P_{out} = T_{actual} \cdot \omega = 128.92 \times 149.23 = 19239 \text{ W} \approx 19.24 \text{ kW}
$$

**(iv) Overall Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{overall} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m = 0.95 \times 0.90 = 0.855 = 85.5\%
$$

**Verification using input power:**
$P_{in} = \Delta P \cdot Q = (1.8 \times 10^7)(1.25 \times 10^{-3}) = 22500$ W $= 22.5$ kW

$$
\eta_{overall} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{19239}{22500} = 0.8551 = 85.51\% \checkmark
$$

---

### Solution to Question B, Part (b)

**Classification of Hydraulic Motors:**

Hydraulic motors are classified into three main categories based on their internal mechanism:

1. **Gear Motors** — External gear, internal gear
2. **Vane Motors** — Unbalanced, balanced
3. **Piston Motors** — Axial piston, radial piston

**Comparison Table: Axial Piston vs Radial Piston Motors**

| Parameter | Axial Piston Motor | Radial Piston Motor |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Piston orientation | Parallel to shaft axis | Radially around central cam |
| Mechanism | Swash plate or bent axis | Eccentric cam ring |
| Operating pressure | Up to 450 bar (high) | Up to 350 bar (medium-high) |
| Speed range | High (1000–4000 rpm) | Low-to-medium (up to 1500 rpm) |
| Torque characteristics | Moderate torque, high speed | Very high torque at low speed |
| Volumetric efficiency | 95–98% | 92–96% |
| Mechanical efficiency | 90–95% | 88–93% |
| Overall efficiency | 85–93% | 80–88% |
| Bidirectional operation | Easy (swash plate tilt reversal) | Requires valve reversal |
| Typical applications | Mobile hydraulics, machine tools, injection moulding | Winches, slewing drives, track drives, cranes |
| Cost | Higher | Lower for high-torque applications |
| Starting torque | Good | Excellent |
| Noise level | Moderate | Higher |

**Detailed Comparison Points:**

- **Construction:** Axial piston motors use a swash plate inclined at an angle to convert reciprocating piston motion into rotation. Radial piston motors have pistons arranged radially around a central eccentric cam; the pistons ride on the cam as it rotates, producing torque.

- **Operating Pressure:** Axial piston motors can operate at much higher pressures (up to 450 bar) compared to radial piston motors (up to 350 bar), making them suitable for high-pressure industrial circuits.

- **Speed Range:** Axial piston motors operate efficiently at high speeds (1000–4000 rpm), whereas radial piston motors are typically limited to lower speeds (up to 1500 rpm) but deliver higher torque.

- **Efficiency:** Axial piston motors generally have slightly higher overall efficiency due to better-balanced forces and lower leakage. Radial piston motors have good mechanical efficiency but lower volumetric efficiency at higher speeds.

- **Applications:** Axial piston motors are preferred in applications requiring variable speed and high efficiency, such as hydraulic drives for machine tools, excavators, and injection moulding machines. Radial piston motors are preferred in applications requiring high torque at low speed, such as winches, track drives in excavators, and slewing drives in cranes.

*(7 marks — classification 1 mark, table 4 marks, explanation of differences 2 marks)*

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Actuation Systems Problems:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (Most Common, Loses 1–2 Marks):** Students often substitute bar into formulas expecting SI units of Pa. Always convert bar → Pa by multiplying by **10⁵**.
> 2. **Forgetting to Use $A_2$ on Retraction:** Many students calculate the retraction force using $A_1$ instead of the correct annular area $A_2$. The retraction force is **always smaller** than the extension force.
> 3. **Mixing rpm and rev/s:** Motor formulas for $N$ and $\omega$ use different units. Always specify whether $N$ is in rev/s or rpm and convert $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ for rpm.
> 4. **Ignoring Mechanical Efficiency:** Students sometimes calculate theoretical power and report it as output power, ignoring $\eta_m$. Always apply efficiency when computing actual delivered values.
> 5. **Wrong Formula Selection for Power:** Power can be calculated as $P = F \cdot v$ (cylinder) or $P = T \cdot \omega$ (motor) or $P = \Delta P \cdot Q$ (input). Use whichever is most convenient; ensure units are consistent.
> 6. **Skipping the Final Unit Statement:** Examiners deduct marks if the final numerical answer is not expressed with **units**. Always end with e.g., "N", "m/s", "kW", "rpm".
> 7. **No Diagram in Descriptive Questions:** For 7-mark descriptive questions on construction, **a labelled sketch is mandatory** — failure to draw a diagram typically costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Actuation Systems — Final Revision Bullet Points:**

- **Definition:** Actuators convert hydraulic energy (pressure + flow) into mechanical energy (force + motion OR torque + rotation).
- **Two classes:** **Linear (Cylinders)** and **Rotary (Motors)**.
- **Cylinder Types:** SAC, DAC, Telescopic, Tandem, Cushioned.
- **Motor Types:** Gear (external/internal), Vane, Piston (axial/radial).
- **Cylinder Areas:** $A_1 = \pi D^2 / 4$ (head end), $A_2 = \pi (D^2 - d^2) / 4$ (rod end).
- **Cylinder Force:** $F = P \cdot A$ — always use the active area for the stroke.
- **Cylinder Velocity:** $v = Q / A$ — inversely proportional to active area.
- **Retraction force < Extension force** for the same pressure (due to smaller rod-side area).
- **Cylinder Stroke Time:** $t = L \cdot A / Q$ — useful for cycle-time analysis.
- **Motor Speed:** $N = Q \cdot \eta_v / V_D$ — directly proportional to flow.
- **Motor Torque:** $T_{theoretical} = \Delta P \cdot V_D / 2\pi$ — directly proportional to pressure.
- **Motor Power:** $P = T \cdot \omega = \Delta P \cdot Q$ (input, ideal); $P_{out} = \eta_{overall} \cdot P_{in}$.
- **Volumetric Efficiency:** $\eta_v = Q_{theoretical} / Q_{actual}$ — accounts for leakage.
- **Mechanical Efficiency:** $\eta_m = T_{actual} / T_{theoretical}$ — accounts for friction.
- **Overall Efficiency:** $\eta_{overall} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$ — accounts for all losses.
- **Cushioning:** Throttles exhaust fluid near end-of-stroke to decelerate piston.
- **Selection Rule of Thumb:** High force + low speed → large bore; high speed + low force → small bore.
- **Standard Pressure:** 210 bar for industrial hydraulics; up to 700 bar for specialized applications.
- **Always convert units** (bar → Pa, L/min → m³/s, cc/rev → m³/rev) **before** substitution.
- **Always write units with the final numerical answer.**
- **Always draw a labelled diagram** in 7-mark construction questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reciprocation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT526 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reciprocation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Reciprocation** in industrial hydraulics refers to the generation of controlled **linear back-and-forth (oscillatory) motion** through the conversion of hydraulic energy (pressurized fluid) into mechanical work. The primary device that performs reciprocation in any hydraulic circuit is the **Hydraulic Cylinder** (also called a Linear Hydraulic Actuator or Reciprocating Actuator).

A hydraulic cylinder uses pressurized hydraulic fluid (typically mineral oil) to push a piston housed inside a cylindrical barrel, thereby producing a linear thrust force along the axis of the bore. When fluid is admitted alternately on either side of the piston, the piston rod executes a reciprocal stroke — extending (forward stroke) and retracting (return stroke).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *"A hydraulic cylinder is a mechanical actuator that produces unidirectional force or unidirectional stroke through a unidirectional stroke produced by pressurized hydraulic fluid in a cylindrical sleeve (bore)."* — Reciprocation is the **oscillatory linear displacement** produced by such an actuator.

### Key Characteristics of Reciprocation in Hydraulics

| Parameter | Description |
|---|---|
| **Motion Type** | Linear, bidirectional (extend + retract) |
| **Energy Conversion** | Hydraulic (pressure × flow) → Mechanical (force × velocity) |
| **Output Form** | Reciprocating linear thrust |
| **Actuating Fluid** | Mineral oil (ISO VG 32/46), typically filtered to 10 µm |
| **Operating Pressure** | Standard industrial range: 70 bar – 350 bar (typical 200 bar) |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hydraulic Syringe"

Imagine a large medical syringe lying on a table, sealed at one end with a plunger that can slide in and out. Now, instead of pushing the plunger with your thumb, attach a water pipe with a pressure gauge to the sealed end and a tap. When you open the tap, the pressurized water pushes the plunger out — this is the **extending stroke**. If you reverse the pipe connection to the other side, the plunger gets sucked back — the **retracting stroke**.

A hydraulic cylinder works on exactly this principle, but:
- It uses **incompressible oil** (not water) for better sealing and lubrication
- It is rated for **much higher pressures** (200 bar vs. <1 bar in a syringe)
- The plunger is precisely machined (called the **piston**) and rides on **seals** to prevent leakage
- A **piston rod** extends out of the cylinder to transmit force to the load

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is reciprocation so widely used in industry?**
> Reciprocating hydraulic cylinders offer **high force density** (a small bore can produce several tonnes of thrust), **simple control** (just a directional valve), **smooth variable-speed operation**, **built-in overload protection** (relief valve), and **self-lubrication** from the hydraulic fluid itself.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard cylinder bores**: 25 mm, 32 mm, 40 mm, 50 mm, 63 mm, 80 mm, 100 mm, 125 mm, 160 mm, 200 mm, 250 mm
- **Standard rod diameters** (per ISO 3320): $d = 0.5D$, $0.7D$, or $1.0D$ where $D$ is the bore
- **Standard system pressure**: **200 bar (20 MPa)** for industrial hydraulics
- **Pascal's Law** (1653) governs force multiplication in cylinders
- **Bulk modulus** of mineral oil $\beta \approx 1.5 \times 10^9$ N/m²
- **Gravitational acceleration** $g = 9.81$ m/s²

---

## 1.3 Classification of Reciprocating Hydraulic Cylinders

Reciprocation is achieved via several types of hydraulic cylinders, classified by their construction and operating principle:

### A. By Direction of Force Application

**(i) Single-Acting Cylinder**
- Hydraulic fluid acts on **only one side** of the piston
- Return stroke is achieved by **gravity, spring, or external load**
- Has **one port** for fluid entry/exit
- Used in: jacks, lifts, clamps, pressing operations

**(ii) Double-Acting Cylinder**
- Hydraulic fluid can act on **both sides** of the piston alternately
- Produces **positive force in both directions**
- Has **two ports** (cap end and rod end)
- Used in: machine tools, material handling, mobile equipment, presses

### B. By Constructional Geometry

- **Tie-rod cylinder** (small/medium duty)
- **Welded body cylinder** (heavy duty)
- **Telescopic cylinder** (multi-stage for long strokes in compact spaces — used in dump trucks)
- **Tandem cylinder** (two pistons in series for double force on same stroke)
- **Differential cylinder** (unequal areas produce different forward/return speeds)

---

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exam:** *Single-acting = One port = One powered stroke (spring/gravity does the other); Double-acting = Two ports = Both strokes powered.* This single line is worth 2 marks in any KTU viva.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Constructional Anatomy of a Double-Acting Cylinder

A standard double-acting reciprocating cylinder consists of the following components:

1. **Cylinder Barrel (Sleeve/Bore)** — Precision-machined steel tube (honed to 0.2 µm Ra) where the piston slides. Houses the pressurized fluid.
2. **Piston** — Disc-shaped component attached to the rod, sealed by piston seals (often polyurethane or PTFE).
3. **Piston Rod** — Hard-chrome-plated steel shaft that transmits force to the load. Rod end is threaded (male/female) for attachment.
4. **Head (Front Cover / gland)** — Seals the rod end via a rod seal (wiper + main seal).
5. **Cap (Rear Cover)** — Seals the blind end of the cylinder.
6. **Ports** — Threaded connections (BSP/NPT) for fluid entry, typically SAE 4-bolt flanges for high pressure.
7. **Seals** — Static (O-rings for covers) + Dynamic (piston seal, rod seal, wiper).
8. **Mounting** — Trunnion, clevis, flange, foot, or spherical bearing.

---

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Pressurized fluid from the pump enters the cylinder through the directional control valve (DCV).

**Step 2:** Fluid enters the **cap end (blank side)** of the cylinder, pushing the piston toward the rod end.

**Step 3:** The fluid on the **rod end (annulus side)** is simultaneously displaced and returned to the tank via the DCV.

**Step 4:** When the DCV is shifted, fluid enters the **rod end**, pushing the piston back. The cap-end fluid exhausts to tank.

**Step 5:** The piston rod thus executes a **reciprocating linear motion** between its two extreme positions (fully extended and fully retracted).

> **WHY this works:** Pascal's Law states that pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted equally in all directions. Hence, the pressure acts on the entire piston area, generating a force $F = P \times A$.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains every formula required for solving reciprocation problems in the KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize these before attempting any numerical.

### Primary Force, Velocity, and Power Formulas

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cylinder Bore Area | $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | m² | $D$ = piston diameter |
| 2 | Annulus (Rod Side) Area | $A_a = \dfrac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$ | m² | $d$ = rod diameter |
| 3 | Cap-End Force (Extension) | $F_e = P \cdot A = P \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | N | Force during extension stroke |
| 4 | Rod-End Force (Retraction) | $F_r = P \cdot A_a = P \cdot \dfrac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$ | N | Force during retraction stroke |
| 5 | Extension Velocity | $v_e = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | m/s | $Q$ = flow rate |
| 6 | Retraction Velocity | $v_r = \dfrac{Q}{A_a} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}$ | m/s | Higher than $v_e$ for $d>0$ |
| 7 | Hydraulic Power Input | $P_{in} = P \cdot Q$ | W | $P$ in Pa, $Q$ in m³/s |
| 8 | Mechanical Power Output | $P_{out} = F \cdot v$ | W | Useful work rate |
| 9 | Cylinder Time for Stroke | $t = \dfrac{L}{v} = \dfrac{A \cdot L}{Q}$ | s | $L$ = stroke length |
| 10 | Cylinder Volume (per stroke) | $V = A \cdot L$ | m³ | Displaced volume |
| 11 | Differential Area Ratio | $m = \dfrac{A}{A_a} = \dfrac{D^2}{D^2 - d^2}$ | — | Ratio of areas |
| 12 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_v = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$ | — | Typically 0.95 – 0.98 |
| 13 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_m = \dfrac{F_{actual}}{F_{theoretical}}$ | — | Typically 0.90 – 0.95 |
| 14 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$ | — | Combined effect |

### Critical Boundary Conditions

- **Cap-end force > Rod-end force** (because $A > A_a$ when $d > 0$)
- **Retraction velocity > Extension velocity** (because $A_a < A$ for $d>0$)
- **Volume balance:** Volume displaced on extension $\ne$ volume on retraction in differential cylinders
- **Same flow rate in both strokes** (conservation of mass for incompressible fluid)

---

## 2.4 The Differential Cylinder — A Special Case (HIGH YIELD for KTU)

A **differential cylinder** is one where the rod is permanently fixed to the piston, and the rod diameter is chosen such that the **areas are unequal**. This creates an interesting kinematic and force imbalance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Favorite Question:** *"Explain the working of a differential cylinder. Why are extension and retraction speeds different?"* — This is a guaranteed 7-mark question in Part B.

### Derivation of Area and Speed Ratio

Let $D$ = bore, $d$ = rod diameter.

- Cap-end area: $A_1 = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$
- Rod-end area: $A_2 = \dfrac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$
- Ratio of areas: $\dfrac{A_1}{A_2} = \dfrac{D^2}{D^2 - d^2}$
- If $d = D/\sqrt{2}$ (i.e., rod diameter = bore / √2): $A_2 = A_1 / 2$
- Force ratio: $F_e = 2 F_r$ when supplied at same pressure
- Speed ratio: $v_r = 2 v_e$ (retraction is twice as fast as extension)

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

Reciprocating hydraulic cylinders are ubiquitous in:

- **Manufacturing**: Presses (stamping, forging, injection moulding), clamping devices, machine tool slides
- **Construction**: Excavator booms, backhoe arms, bulldozer blades
- **Material Handling**: Forklifts, scissor lifts, dock levelers, conveyors
- **Automotive**: Hydraulic jacks, brake systems (master/slave cylinders), shock absorbers
- **Aerospace**: Flight control actuators, landing gear extension/retraction
- **Marine**: Steering gear (rudder actuators), hatch covers, crane slewing
- **Mining & Heavy Earthmoving**: Dump trucks (telescopic cylinders), shovels, drilling rigs

> **Engineering Utility:** The reciprocating cylinder is the **workhorse** of the hydraulics industry — over **70% of all hydraulic actuators shipped globally** are linear reciprocating cylinders.

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cylinder Bore Area from First Principles

Consider a cylindrical bore of diameter $D$. The piston inside has a circular cross-section. The pressure $P$ (Pa) acting on this piston pushes it uniformly along its axis.

**Step 1:** Identify the geometry. A circle of diameter $D$ has area:

$$A = \pi r^2 = \pi \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2$$

**Step 2:** Expand the squared term:

$$A = \pi \cdot \frac{D^2}{4}$$

**Step 3:** For the rod side, the effective area is the bore area minus the rod cross-sectional area (because the rod occupies that region):

$$A_a = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} - \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

**Step 4:** Factor out $\pi/4$:

$$A_a = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Force Generated by a Cylinder (Pascal's Law Application)

**Step 1:** Pressure $P$ is defined as force per unit area:

$$P = \frac{F}{A}$$

**Step 2:** Rearranging for force:

$$F = P \times A$$

**Step 3:** Substituting the piston area for the cap-end:

$$F_e = P \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Step 4:** For the rod-end (effective annulus area):

$$F_r = P \cdot \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$$

**Step 5:** The ratio of forces during extension and retraction:

$$\frac{F_e}{F_r} = \frac{D^2}{D^2 - d^2}$$

**Step 6:** Define the area ratio $m$:

$$m = \frac{D^2}{D^2 - d^2} \geq 1$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Velocity and Flow Rate Continuity

**Step 1:** The volume flow rate $Q$ is the volume of fluid entering the cylinder per unit time:

$$Q = \frac{V}{t}$$

**Step 2:** During the extension stroke, this volume equals piston area $\times$ displacement:

$$V = A \times L$$

**Step 3:** The velocity is displacement per unit time:

$$v = \frac{L}{t}$$

**Step 4:** Combining:

$$Q = \frac{A \times L}{t} = A \cdot v$$

**Step 5:** Solving for velocity:

$$v = \frac{Q}{A}$$

**Step 6:** For extension:

$$v_e = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$$

**Step 7:** For retraction:

$$v_r = \frac{Q}{A_a} = \frac{4Q}{\pi(D^2 - d^2)}$$

**Step 8:** Speed ratio:

$$\frac{v_r}{v_e} = \frac{A}{A_a} = \frac{D^2}{D^2 - d^2}$$

> **Critical Insight:** Notice that **$F_e/F_r = v_r/v_e$** — the force ratio equals the speed ratio (reciprocal relation). This is a direct consequence of power conservation: $P_{hydraulic} = F_e v_e = F_r v_r$ (assuming 100% efficiency).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cylinder Stroke Time and Pump Sizing

**Step 1:** Time for one complete stroke:

$$t = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{A \cdot L}{Q}$$

**Step 2:** Pump flow required for a specified cycle time $t_c$ (full extend + retract cycle):

$$Q = \frac{V_{ext} + V_{ret}}{t_c}$$

where $V_{ext} = A \cdot L$ and $V_{ret} = A_a \cdot L$

**Step 3:** Therefore:

$$Q = \frac{L(A + A_a)}{t_c} = \frac{\pi L}{4 t_c}\left(D^2 + (D^2 - d^2)\right) = \frac{\pi L}{4 t_c}(2D^2 - d^2)$$

**Step 4:** Pump power required:

$$P_{pump} = \frac{P \cdot Q}{\eta_{pump}}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Power and Efficiency Analysis

**Step 1:** Hydraulic power delivered to cylinder:

$$P_{hyd} = P \cdot Q$$

**Step 2:** Useful mechanical power output:

$$P_{mech} = F \cdot v = (P \cdot A) \cdot \left(\frac{Q}{A}\right) = P \cdot Q$$

**Step 3:** For an ideal (lossless) cylinder:

$$P_{hyd} = P_{mech} \Rightarrow \eta_{ideal} = 100\%$$

**Step 4:** In reality, friction losses occur at seals and bearings:

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{F_{actual}}{F_{theoretical}} \approx 0.90 \text{ to } 0.95$$

**Step 5:** Combined overall efficiency:

$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{volumetric} \times \eta_{mechanical}$$

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a bore of **100 mm** and a rod diameter of **70 mm**. The system pressure is **160 bar**, and the pump delivers **60 LPM**. Calculate: (a) Extension force, (b) Retraction force, (c) Extension velocity, (d) Retraction velocity, (e) Time for a 300 mm stroke.

**Given Data:**
- $D = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
- $d = 70 \text{ mm} = 0.07 \text{ m}$
- $P = 160 \text{ bar} = 160 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 1.6 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}$
- $Q = 60 \text{ LPM} = 60 \times 10^{-3} / 60 = 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- $L = 300 \text{ mm} = 0.3 \text{ m}$

**Step (a): Cap-end (Extension) Area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.1)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.01}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step (b): Extension Force:**

$$F_e = P \cdot A = 1.6 \times 10^7 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F_e = 125{,}664 \text{ N} \approx 125.66 \text{ kN}$$

**Step (c): Annulus (Rod-end) Area:**

$$A_a = \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4} = \frac{\pi ((0.1)^2 - (0.07)^2)}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.01 - 0.0049)}{4}$$

$$A_a = \frac{\pi \times 0.0051}{4} = 4.005 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step (d): Retraction Force:**

$$F_r = P \cdot A_a = 1.6 \times 10^7 \times 4.005 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F_r = 64{,}088 \text{ N} \approx 64.09 \text{ kN}$$

**Step (e): Extension Velocity:**

$$v_e = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1273 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step (f): Retraction Velocity:**

$$v_r = \frac{Q}{A_a} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{4.005 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.2497 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step (g): Time for 300 mm Extension Stroke:**

$$t_e = \frac{L}{v_e} = \frac{0.3}{0.1273} = 2.357 \text{ s}$$

**Step (h): Time for 300 mm Retraction Stroke:**

$$t_r = \frac{L}{v_r} = \frac{0.3}{0.2497} = 1.202 \text{ s}$$

**Step (i): Verification of Force-Velocity Reciprocity:**

$$F_e \times v_e = 125{,}664 \times 0.1273 = 16{,}000 \text{ W}$$

$$F_r \times v_r = 64{,}088 \times 0.2497 = 16{,}003 \text{ W} \approx 16{,}000 \text{ W} \checkmark$$

> Both equal hydraulic power $P \cdot Q = 1.6 \times 10^7 \times 1 \times 10^{-3} = 16{,}000$ W = **16 kW**. Power balance verified.

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Schematic of a Double-Acting Hydraulic Cylinder (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DCV 4 BY 3 Position A] -->|Cap end fluid| B1[Cylinder Cap End Port]
    B1 --> B2[Piston Cap Side Surface]
    B2 --> B3[Piston Rod Side Surface]
    B3 --> B4[Rod End Port]
    B4 -->|Exhaust to tank| A
    A -->|Pump pressure supply| B5[Piston moves right]
    A2[DCV 4 BY 3 Position B] -->|Rod end fluid| B4
    B4 --> B3
    B3 --> B2
    B2 --> B1
    B1 -->|Exhaust to tank| A2
    A2 -->|Pump pressure supply| B6[Piston moves left]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Reciprocating Hydraulic System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    NODE_SRC[Prime Mover Electric Motor 15 kW] --> NODE_PUMP[Hydraulic Gear Pump 60 LPM at 160 bar]
    NODE_PUMP --> NODE_TANK[Reservoir Tank 200 Litre]
    NODE_PUMP --> NODE_FILTER[Return Line Filter 10 Micron]
    NODE_FILTER --> NODE_RELIEF[Pressure Relief Valve Set at 175 bar]
    NODE_RELIEF --> NODE_DCV[Directional Control Valve 4 by 3 Spring Centred]
    NODE_DCV --> NODE_CYL[Double Acting Hydraulic Cylinder Bore 100 mm Rod 70 mm Stroke 300 mm]
    NODE_CYL --> NODE_LOAD[External Load Material Handling Pallet 2 Tonne]
    NODE_DCV --> NODE_PRESSURE_GAUGE[Pressure Gauge 0 to 250 bar]
    NODE_DCV --> NODE_FLOW_CTRL[Flow Control Valve Metering Out Circuit]
    NODE_CYL --> NODE_CUSHION[Cushioning Device at Cap End Adjustable Needle Valve]
```

## 4.3 Component Identification Reference Table

| Node ID | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| NODE_SRC | Prime Mover | Provides mechanical input power to pump |
| NODE_PUMP | Hydraulic Pump | Converts mechanical to hydraulic energy |
| NODE_TANK | Reservoir | Stores fluid, dissipates heat, allows air separation |
| NODE_FILTER | Return Filter | Removes contaminants to ISO 4406 18/16/13 |
| NODE_RELIEF | Pressure Relief Valve | Limits max system pressure (safety) |
| NODE_DCV | Directional Control Valve | Routes fluid to cap/rod end, controls direction |
| NODE_CYL | Hydraulic Cylinder | Converts hydraulic to reciprocating linear motion |
| NODE_LOAD | Work Load | Receives mechanical force and displacement |
| NODE_PRESSURE_GAUGE | Pressure Gauge | Operator visual feedback of circuit pressure |
| NODE_FLOW_CTRL | Flow Control Valve | Regulates cylinder speed |
| NODE_CUSHION | Cushion Device | Decelerates piston near end-of-stroke to reduce impact |

## 4.4 Block Diagram of Reciprocation Energy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1[Electrical Energy kWh] --> E2[Motor Mechanical Energy kW]
    E2 --> E3[Hydraulic Energy Pressure x Flow]
    E3 --> E4[Linear Mechanical Energy Force x Velocity]
    E4 --> E5[Work Output Lifting Pushing Pressing]
    E3 -.->|Loss Heat Friction| E6[Waste Heat Calorifique]
    E2 -.->|Loss Motor Losses| E6
    E4 -.->|Loss Seal Friction| E6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** Each energy conversion stage has an associated efficiency. Modern industrial hydraulic systems target **total system efficiency of 65-75%** by using variable displacement pumps, load-sensing valves, and well-designed cylinders. Single-acting cylinders with spring return cannot develop force during retraction — they are used only where gravity or a counterweight can return the load.

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP
