<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Synchronizing Circuits

### Formal KTU Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Synchronizing Circuits** are hydraulic circuits designed to coordinate the motion of two or more hydraulic cylinders (or a cylinder and a motor) such that they operate either with **equal velocity** (synchronized motion) or with a **predetermined velocity ratio**. Synchronization is achieved through two principal methods: (a) **Series Connection** of cylinders (matched flow path) and (b) **Parallel Connection using Flow Dividers** (proportional flow splitting).

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine two friends walking together to a destination. To keep them perfectly side-by-side, you either **tie them with a rope of fixed length** (Series — both get the same flow, so the smaller one's piston travels faster) or you give them a **pace-setter device** that adjusts each step (Parallel — a flow divider ensures exact proportioning). Hydraulically, "the rope" is a common oil line, and "the pace-setter" is a flow divider valve.

---

## 1.2 Accumulator Circuits

### Formal KTU Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydraulic Accumulator** is a pressure vessel containing a **compressed gas** (typically nitrogen) and **hydraulic fluid**, separated by a piston, bladder, or diaphragm. It stores potential energy in the form of pressurized fluid and releases it on demand. In a circuit, it functions as an auxiliary power source, pressure compensator, shock absorber, and thermal expansion compensator.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a **water tower** in a city. The pump fills the tower when demand is low; when many taps open simultaneously, gravity-fed water from the tower supplements the pump. The accumulator does exactly the same — it stores pressurized oil (energy) and discharges it when system demand momentarily exceeds pump supply, or when the pump is off-loaded.

**Standard Pre-charge Pressure for Bladder-Type Accumulators:** typically **0.9 × P_min** (or **0.5–0.9 × working pressure** as per **ISO 4413**).

---

## 1.3 Industrial Hydraulic Press Circuits

### Formal KTU Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Hydraulic Press Circuit** is an industrial hydraulic circuit used in forging, compression molding, deep drawing, punching, and briquetting. It typically requires very high force over a short stroke, with a fast approach stroke, slow working stroke, and rapid return stroke. The circuit uses **dual-pressure pumps**, **accumulator assistance**, **counterbalance valves**, and **sequence valves**.

### Conceptual Analogy
The press works like a **gym power rack**: fast descent to the bar (approach), slow controlled push (working stroke), and quick return up. The accumulator is like a friend who "spots" you at the heaviest moment — providing the extra power only when the load is at its peak.

---

## 1.4 Hydraulic Milling Machine Circuit

### Formal KTU Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Hydraulic Milling Machine Circuit** drives the table feed, cross-feed, vertical feed, and the spindle-clamping mechanism. The circuit typically combines a **hydraulic motor** (for spindle) and **feed cylinders**, with a **feed-reverse directional valve** for automatic table reciprocation controlled by **limit valves / sequence valves**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Like the **automatic winding of a grandfather clock** — the table travels left, hits a stop, reverses automatically, hits the other stop, and repeats — all without the operator touching the handle.

---

## 1.5 Hydraulic Grinding Circuit

### Formal KTU Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Hydraulic Grinding Circuit** powers the **work-piece reciprocation** (long, slow oscillating stroke) and the **wheel-head in-feed**. It is characterized by very low, smooth, vibration-free feed rates with **meter-out flow control** for stable reciprocation and **pressure-compensated flow control** for spark-out passes.

### Conceptual Analogy
Like an experienced watchmaker's steady hand — extremely slow, vibration-free motion with precise reversal of the workpiece under the grinding wheel.

---

## 1.6 Syllabus Mapping (KTU 2024 — Module 3)

| Sub-Topic | KTU Cognitive Level | Key Concept |
|---|---|---|
| Synchronizing Circuits | Understand / Apply | Series & Parallel with Flow Divider |
| Accumulator Circuits | Apply / Analyze | Gas-law sizing, charging circuit |
| Press Circuits | Analyze | Dual-pump, accumulator-assisted |
| Milling Circuit | Apply | Auto-reversing, feed control |
| Grinding Circuit | Apply | Meter-out, low vibration feed |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Synchronizing Circuits — Detailed Analysis

### A. Series Synchronization
- Two cylinders are **connected in series** (tail of cylinder 1 → cap of cylinder 2).
- **Same oil flow** passes through both pistons ⇒ same **volume displaced**.
- The piston **velocities** are inversely proportional to piston areas.
- Same **force** is transmitted (force = P × A_eff, but the effective thrust differs if areas differ).

**Operating Logic:**
1. Pump supplies flow Q.
2. Flow enters cap end of cylinder A; rod-end oil exits to cap end of cylinder B.
3. Cylinder B's rod-end oil returns to tank.
4. Both pistons move together as long as the flow path is unobstructed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** If loads differ greatly, the cylinder with the lighter load moves first → de-synchronization. Series connection is used only when loads are **nearly equal** and **small friction differences** exist.

### B. Parallel Synchronization (with Flow Divider)
- A **flow divider valve** splits the incoming flow Q in a fixed ratio (e.g., 1:1 for equal cylinders).
- Two cylinders are in **parallel** lines.
- Each cylinder gets its **predetermined share** independent of load variations.
- Cylinders can have **different areas / strokes** yet maintain a fixed **time relationship**.

**Why?**
- Industrial systems (e.g., clamping + lifting simultaneously) require that two functions complete together.
- Flow dividers can be **equal-ratio (50:50)** or **proportional-ratio (e.g., 30:70)**.

---

## 2.2 Accumulator Circuits — Detailed Analysis

### Functions of an Accumulator
1. **Auxiliary Power Source** — discharge when pump output is inadequate.
2. **Pressure Compensator** — maintains line pressure during pump off-load.
3. **Shock Absorber (Pulsation Dampener)** — smooths pressure spikes from pumps or valves.
4. **Thermal Expansion Compensator** — accommodates fluid expansion due to heat.
5. **Leakage Make-up** — supplies small makeup flow to maintain pressure.

### Charging Circuit Operation
- Pump 1 (high-flow, low-pressure) supplies the actuator.
- Pump 2 (low-flow, high-pressure) charges the accumulator through a check valve.
- Relief valve sets maximum system pressure.
- When pressure reaches cut-out setting, the unloading valve opens; pump 1 unloads to tank.
- Accumulator alone supplies the actuator; when it discharges below cut-in pressure, pump 1 re-loads.

---

## 2.3 Press Circuit — Detailed Operational Analysis

A press cycle has **four distinct phases**:

| Phase | Required Force | Required Speed | Hydraulic Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Fast Approach | Low | Very High | Pump 1 (LP, HP flow) |
| 2. Working Stroke | Very High | Very Low | Pump 1 + Accumulator |
| 3. Hold | Static (no movement) | Zero | Accumulator only |
| 4. Fast Return | Low | High | Pump 1 only (rod-end) |

**Key Components:**
- **Dual Pressure / Dual Flow Pump** (e.g., vane + piston combination)
- **Accumulator** (sized using Boyle's Law)
- **Counterbalance Valve** (prevents ram from falling under gravity)
- **Sequence Valve** (switches from fast approach to slow pressing)
- **Pressure Relief Valve** (sets maximum pressing force)
- **Pilot-operated check valve** (locks ram in position during hold)

---

## 2.4 Milling Machine Circuit — Step Logic

1. Spindle starts (hydraulic motor ON).
2. Feed directional valve (4/3 DCV) energizes to **forward** position.
3. Table moves forward at controlled feed rate (via flow control valve).
4. Table hits **limit valve 1** → DCV shifts to **reverse**.
5. Table returns; hits **limit valve 2** → DCV shifts to **forward** again.
6. Cycle continues until operator stops the circuit.

**Spindle clamping** is done by a separate clamping cylinder, energized only when spindle is stationary (interlock with motor pressure switch).

---

## 2.5 Grinding Circuit — Step Logic

1. Work-piece clamped hydraulically.
2. Wheel-head advances at rapid traverse (high speed, no contact).
3. Upon contact → automatic switch to **in-feed rate** (very slow, typically 0.5–5 mm/min).
4. After main in-feed → **spark-out passes** (no further in-feed, just reciprocation to remove residual marks).
5. Wheel retracts; work-piece unclamped.

**Key valves:** Meter-out flow control, pressure compensated flow control, sequence valves for rapid-to-feed transition, counterbalance valve for stability.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = P \cdot A$ | Cylinder thrust force | $N$ |
| 2 | $F_{rod} = P \cdot A_{piston} - P \cdot A_{rod}$ | Net forward force accounting for back pressure | $N$ |
| 3 | $Q = A \cdot v$ | Flow rate = Area × Velocity | $m^3/s$ |
| 4 | $v_s = v \cdot \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}$ | Series-sync velocity ratio (subscript 1,2 = cyl 1, 2) | $m/s$ |
| 5 | $Q_1 = \dfrac{A_1}{A_1+A_2} \cdot Q$ | Flow divider split (parallel) | $m^3/s$ |
| 6 | $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ | Boyle's Law (Isothermal) — gas-side accumulator | $Pa \cdot m^3$ |
| 7 | $P_1 V_1^{\,n} = P_2 V_2^{\,n}$ | Polytropic (n=1.4 for nitrogen adiabatic) | $Pa \cdot m^n$ |
| 8 | $\Delta V = V_1 \left(1 - \dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right)$ | Fluid volume discharged from accumulator (isothermal) | $m^3$ |
| 9 | $V_0 = \dfrac{\Delta V \cdot P_2}{P_0 \,(P_2-P_1)}$ | Required pre-charge / total gas volume sizing | $m^3$ |
| 10 | $W = P_1 \Delta V \, \ln \!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | Energy stored (isothermal) | $J$ |
| 11 | $W_{adiabatic} = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{n-1}\!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\tfrac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$ | Energy stored (adiabatic, n=1.4) | $J$ |
| 12 | $P_{pre} \le 0.9 \cdot P_{min}$ | Pre-charge rule (bladder, ISO 4413) | $Pa$ |
| 13 | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$ | Volumetric efficiency of pump/motor | — |
| 14 | $P_{hyd} = \dfrac{F \cdot v}{A} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | Hydraulic power (P=F·v/η for loss accounting) | $W$ |
| 15 | $t = \dfrac{V}{Q_p}$ | Time for cylinder stroke | $s$ |
| 16 | $t_{disch} = \dfrac{V_0 \, n}{A_{eff} \cdot (n-1)} \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_0}\right)^{\tfrac{n-1}{n}}-\left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_0}\right)^{\tfrac{n-1}{n}}\right]$ | Accumulator discharge time | $s$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on `|` symbol:** In the above table the absolute-value / division notation is shown in plain LaTeX as a fraction (`\dfrac{...}{...}`) — **never use the vertical bar `|`** inside markdown table cells as it breaks the table syntax.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Circuit | Industry Application |
|---|---|
| Synchronizing (Series) | Mobile cranes (outriggers + lift), injection-mold opening/closing |
| Synchronizing (Parallel) | Machine tool dual-rail tables, robotic lift platforms |
| Accumulator (Charging) | Punch presses, die casting, emergency braking, ride-control trucks |
| Press Circuit | Forging, compression molding, sheet-metal deep drawing |
| Milling Circuit | Knee-type milling machines, plano-millers, bed-type mills |
| Grinding Circuit | Surface, cylindrical, centerless, tool-and-cutter grinders |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Accumulator Sizing (Boyle's Law Method)

### Problem Context
A bladder-type accumulator must deliver $\Delta V = 4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$ (4 litres) of oil when discharging from $P_1 = 12 \text{ MPa}$ to $P_2 = 8 \text{ MPa}$. The pre-charge pressure is $P_0 = 6 \text{ MPa}$ (gas side). Find the required pre-charge gas volume $V_0$.

### Step 1 — Identify the Three Pressure States
- **Pre-charge (gas only, no oil):** Pressure = $P_0$, Volume = $V_0$ (full gas volume).
- **Charged (max oil, accumulator ready to discharge):** Pressure = $P_1$, Gas volume = $V_1$.
- **Discharged (oil expelled, low pressure):** Pressure = $P_2$, Gas volume = $V_2$.

### Step 2 — Apply Boyle's Law
Boyle's Law (isothermal): $P \cdot V = \text{constant}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pre-charge} \rightarrow \text{Charged:} \quad & P_0 \, V_0 = P_1 \, V_1 \\
\text{Pre-charge} \rightarrow \text{Discharged:} \quad & P_0 \, V_0 = P_2 \, V_2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Express Discharged Oil Volume
The fluid volume discharged = the increase in gas volume from charged to discharged state:

$$
\Delta V = V_2 - V_1
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Boyle's Law into $\Delta V$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V &= V_2 - V_1 = \dfrac{P_0 V_0}{P_2} - \dfrac{P_0 V_0}{P_1} \\
\Delta V &= P_0 V_0 \left(\dfrac{1}{P_2} - \dfrac{1}{P_1}\right) \\
\Delta V &= P_0 V_0 \left(\dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{P_1 P_2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Solve for the Pre-Charge Gas Volume $V_0$

$$
V_0 = \dfrac{\Delta V \cdot P_1 \cdot P_2}{P_0 \, (P_1 - P_2)}
$$

### Step 6 — Plug in Numerical Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_0 &= \dfrac{(4 \times 10^{-3})(12 \times 10^6)(8 \times 10^6)}{(6 \times 10^6)(12 \times 10^6 - 8 \times 10^6)} \\
V_0 &= \dfrac{(4 \times 10^{-3})(96 \times 10^{12})}{(6 \times 10^6)(4 \times 10^6)} \\
V_0 &= \dfrac{384 \times 10^{9}}{24 \times 10^{12}} \\
V_0 &= 0.016 \text{ m}^3 \\
V_0 &= 16 \text{ litres}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The required gas pre-charge volume is **16 litres**. The accumulator must therefore be a 16-L (or larger) size charged to 6 MPa.

### Step 7 — Cross-Check Using Polytropic Form (Adiabatic, n = 1.4)
For a more accurate (fast-acting) scenario, the polytropic law applies:

$$
V_0 = \dfrac{\Delta V \cdot P_1^{\tfrac{1}{n}}}{P_0^{\tfrac{1}{n}} \left[\left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_0}\right)^{\tfrac{1}{n}} - \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_0}\right)^{\tfrac{1}{n}}\right]}
$$

Plugging in (with same numbers, n=1.4): yields approximately **$V_0 \approx 18.2$ L** — somewhat larger, which is why **designers always use the adiabatic or polytropic value for safety** in fast-response circuits.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Series Synchronization Velocity Relationship

### Problem Context
Two cylinders A and B are connected in **series**. Cylinder A has piston area $A_1$, Cylinder B has piston area $A_2$. Pump flow is $Q$. Derive the velocity of cylinder B.

### Step 1 — Continuity of Flow
In series, the same flow $Q$ must fill cylinder A and also pass into cylinder B:

$$
Q = A_1 \, v_1 = A_2 \, v_2
$$

### Step 2 — Solve for $v_2$

$$
v_2 = v_1 \cdot \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}
$$

### Step 3 — Express $v_2$ in Terms of Pump Flow

$$
v_2 = \dfrac{Q}{A_2}
$$

### Step 4 — Verify with Numerical Example
If $A_1 = 30 \text{ cm}^2$, $A_2 = 50 \text{ cm}^2$, $Q = 6 \text{ L/min} = 100 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}$:

$$
v_1 = \dfrac{Q}{A_1} = \dfrac{100}{30} = 3.33 \text{ cm/s}
$$

$$
v_2 = \dfrac{Q}{A_2} = \dfrac{100}{50} = 2.00 \text{ cm/s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Velocity ratio:** $v_1 : v_2 = 5 : 3$ — exactly the **inverse of the area ratio**. The smaller cylinder moves faster.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Press Force & Energy Stored in Accumulator

### Problem Context
A 100-ton hydraulic press has a ram of diameter $D = 200 \text{ mm}$ working at $P_{max} = 31.2 \text{ MPa}$. The accumulator is sized to support peak load for **5 seconds**, with the cylinder requiring $Q_{cyl} = 10 \text{ L/min}$. Verify that a **25 L accumulator** (charged to 14 MPa, pre-charge 8 MPa, isothermal) suffices.

### Step 1 — Press Force
$$
F = P \cdot A = P \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = (31.2 \times 10^6) \cdot \dfrac{\pi (0.2)^2}{4} = 980,178 \text{ N} \approx 100 \text{ tonnes-force.}
$$

### Step 2 — Required Oil Volume in 5 s

$$
\Delta V = Q_{cyl} \cdot t = \left(\dfrac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{60}\right)(5) = 8.33 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 = 0.833 \text{ L}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Boyle's Law to Find Effective Delivery

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_0 &= 25 \text{ L} = 25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 \\
P_0 &= 8 \text{ MPa} \\
P_1 &= 14 \text{ MPa} \\
P_2 &= \text{?}
\end{aligned}
$$

After discharge of $\Delta V$ of oil, gas expands to:

$$
V_2 = V_0 \cdot \dfrac{P_0}{P_2}
$$

And $V_1 = V_0 \cdot \dfrac{P_0}{P_1}$. Then:

$$
\Delta V = V_0 P_0 \left(\dfrac{1}{P_2} - \dfrac{1}{P_1}\right)
$$

Solving for $P_2$ when $\Delta V = 0.833 \text{ L}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.833 \times 10^{-3} &= (25 \times 10^{-3})(8 \times 10^6)\left(\dfrac{1}{P_2} - \dfrac{1}{14 \times 10^6}\right) \\
\dfrac{0.833 \times 10^{-3}}{200} &= \dfrac{1}{P_2} - \dfrac{1}{14 \times 10^6} \\
4.165 \times 10^{-6} &= \dfrac{1}{P_2} - 7.14 \times 10^{-8} \\
\dfrac{1}{P_2} &= 4.236 \times 10^{-6} \\
P_2 &= 2.36 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} \quad ??? 
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Recalculation** (correcting decimal order): $\Delta V$ and $V_0$ are in litres; convert consistently to m³.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V &= 0.833 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3,\ V_0 = 0.025 \text{ m}^3,\ P_0 = 8 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \\
0.833 \times 10^{-3} &= (0.025)(8 \times 10^6)\left(\dfrac{1}{P_2} - \dfrac{1}{14 \times 10^6}\right) \\
0.833 \times 10^{-3} &= 200\left(\dfrac{1}{P_2} - 7.143 \times 10^{-8}\right) \\
4.165 \times 10^{-6} &= \dfrac{1}{P_2} - 7.143 \times 10^{-8} \\
\dfrac{1}{P_2} &= 4.236 \times 10^{-6} \\
P_2 &\approx 2.36 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 0.236 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The accumulator **over-supplies** — pressure drops from 14 MPa to 0.236 MPa, well below the 31.2 MPa working pressure. **Therefore a 25 L accumulator is grossly oversized for this delivery.** Re-sizing using:

$$
V_0 = \dfrac{\Delta V \cdot P_2 \cdot P_1}{P_0 (P_1 - P_2)}
$$

…with $P_2 = 30 \text{ MPa}$ (i.e., minimum 30 MPa) gives the **correct required size**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Industrial Circuit Features

| Feature | Series Sync | Parallel Sync w/ FD | Press Circuit | Milling Circuit | Grinding Circuit |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Primary Function | Match cylinder stroke | Matched velocity ratio | Very high static force | Reciprocating feed | Slow, smooth oscillation |
| Typical Pressure | 70–200 bar | 100–250 bar | 150–700 bar | 30–80 bar | 20–60 bar |
| Speed Control | Inherent (area ratio) | Flow divider | Pump + accumulator | Flow control valve | Meter-out flow control |
| Energy Storage | Not required | Not required | **Accumulator** (essential) | Optional | Not typical |
| Cycle Time | Long | Medium | Short (high force phase) | Long (table travel) | Very long (per pass) |
| Main Directional Valve | 4/2 or 4/3 | 4/3 (with FDV) | 4/3 + counterbalance | 4/3 + limit valves | 4/3 + sequence |
| Common Failure | De-sync on unequal load | Flow divider leakage | Pressure drop in hold | Limit-valve sticking | Vibration marks on work |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> All Mermaid diagrams use **alphanumeric-prefixed node IDs** (e.g., `node1`, `pumpA`, `cylB`) to avoid reserved-keyword conflicts. Labels inside double-quotes are **plain uppercase alphanumeric text** only — no bold or HTML formatting.

---

## 4.1 Synchronizing Circuit — Series Connection (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[TANK] --> node2[PUMP FIXED DISPLACEMENT]
    node2 --> node3[RELIEF VALVE SET 100 BAR]
    node3 --> node1
    node2 --> node4[DCV 4 BY 3]
    node4 --> node5[CYLINDER A CAP END]
    node5 --> node6[ROD END OF A FEEDS CAP END OF B]
    node6 --> node7[CYLINDER B CAP END]
    node7 --> node8[ROD END B RETURNS TO TANK]
    node8 --> node1
    node9[LOAD A APPLIES ON A] -.-> node5
    node10[LOAD B APPLIES ON B] -.-> node7
    style node1 fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#333
    style node2 fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#333
    style node3 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#333
    style node4 fill:#fff2a8,stroke:#333
    style node5 fill:#ffc4c4,stroke:#333
    style node6 fill:#cccccc,stroke:#333
    style node7 fill:#ffc4c4,stroke:#333
```

> **Block-architecture interpretation:** Pump → DCV → Cylinder A → internal flow transfer line → Cylinder B → Tank. **Both pistons move in the same direction at speeds inversely proportional to their areas.**

---

## 4.2 Synchronizing Circuit — Parallel with Flow Divider

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeT[TANK] --> nodeP[PUMP]
    nodeP --> nodePRV[RELIEF VALVE]
    nodePRV --> nodeT
    nodeP --> nodeDCV[DCV 4 BY 3]
    nodeDCV --> nodeFD[FLOW DIVIDER 1 TO 1]
    nodeFD --> nodeA[FLOW CONTROL A]
    nodeFD --> nodeB[FLOW CONTROL B]
    nodeA --> nodeCYLA[CYLINDER A]
    nodeB --> nodeCYLB[CYLINDER B]
    nodeCYLA --> nodeT
    nodeCYLB --> nodeT
    style nodeT fill:#e1f0ff
    style nodeP fill:#ffd9b3
    style nodeFD fill:#d4f4dd
    style nodeCYLA fill:#ffc4c4
    style nodeCYLB fill:#ffc4c4
```

> **Interpretation:** Flow divider **guarantees equal flow** to both cylinders even if their loads differ significantly — this is the **most common industrial synchronization method**.

---

## 4.3 Accumulator Charging Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeT1[TANK] --> nodeP1[PUMP 1 HIGH FLOW LOW PRESSURE]
    nodeT1 --> nodeP2[PUMP 2 LOW FLOW HIGH PRESSURE]
    nodeP1 --> nodeUNL[UNLOADING VALVE SET 14 MPA]
    nodeUNL --> nodeT1
    nodeP1 --> nodeCK1[CHECK VALVE 1]
    nodeP2 --> nodeCK2[CHECK VALVE 2]
    nodeCK1 --> nodeLINE[PRESSURE LINE]
    nodeCK2 --> nodeLINE
    nodeLINE --> nodeACC[ACCUMULATOR BLADDER TYPE]
    nodeLINE --> nodeDCV[DCV 4 BY 3]
    nodeDCV --> nodeCYL[HYDRAULIC CYLINDER]
    nodeCYL --> nodeRV[RELIEF VALVE]
    nodeRV --> nodeT1
    nodeCYL --> nodeT1
    style nodeT1 fill:#e1f0ff
    style nodeP1 fill:#ffd9b3
    style nodeP2 fill:#ffd9b3
    style nodeACC fill:#d4f4dd
    style nodeCYL fill:#ffc4c4
```

> **Interpretation:** **Pump 1 (HP flow, LP)** drives the cylinder directly through a check valve. **Pump 2 (LP flow, HP)** charges the accumulator. The **unloading valve** cuts off Pump 1 once line pressure reaches the cut-out setpoint.

---

## 4.4 Hydraulic Press Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeTT[TANK] --> nodePP[PRESS PUMP 1 LP HIGH FLOW]
    nodeTT --> nodeP2[PRESS PUMP 2 HP LOW FLOW]
    nodePP --> nodeCK1[CHECK VALVE]
    nodeP2 --> nodeCK2[CHECK VALVE]
    nodeCK1 --> nodePCV[PRESSURE COMPENSATED DCV]
    nodeCK2 --> nodePCV
    nodePCV --> nodeSEQSQ[SEQUENCE VALVE]
    nodeSEQSQ --> nodeCBV[COUNTERBALANCE VALVE]
    nodeCBV --> nodePRAM[PRESS RAM DOUBLE ACTING]
    nodePRAM --> nodePRV[PRESSURE RELIEF VALVE]
    nodePRV --> nodeTT
    nodePRAM --> nodeTT
    nodeACC[ACCUMULATOR CHARGED] --> nodePCV
    style nodeTT fill:#e1f0ff
    style nodePP fill:#ffd9b3
    style nodeP2 fill:#ffd9b3
    nodePCV fill:#fff2a8
    nodeACC fill:#d4f4dd
    nodePRAM fill:#ffc4c4
```

> **Functional sequence:** Fast approach (Pump 1 alone) → Sequence valve shifts → Slow pressing (Pump 1 + Pump 2 + Accumulator) → Hold (Accumulator alone) → Fast return (Pump 1 to rod end).

---

## 4.5 Hydraulic Milling Machine Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeMT[TANK] --> nodeMP[MILLING PUMP FIXED]
    nodeMP --> nodeMRV[RELIEF VALVE]
    nodeMRV --> nodeMT
    nodeMP --> nodeMDCV[DCV 4 BY 3 FOR TABLE]
    nodeMDCV --> nodeMFC[FLOW CONTROL VALVE]
    nodeMFC --> nodeMTAB[MILLING TABLE CYLINDER]
    nodeMTAB --> nodeMT
    nodeMTAB --> nodeL1[LIMIT VALVE 1 TRAVEL END]
    nodeL1 -.-> nodeMDCV
    nodeMTAB --> nodeL2[LIMIT VALVE 2 RETURN END]
    nodeL2 -.-> nodeMDCV
    nodeMP --> nodeSDCV[DCV 4 BY 2 FOR SPINDLE MOTOR]
    nodeSDCV --> nodeSM[HYDRAULIC SPINDLE MOTOR]
    nodeSM --> nodeMT
    nodeMP --> nodeCLDCV[CLAMP DCV]
    nodeCLDCV --> nodeCL[WORK CLAMP CYLINDER]
    nodeCL --> nodeMT
    style nodeMT fill:#e1f0ff
    nodeMP fill:#ffd9b3
    nodeMTAB fill:#ffc4c4
    nodeSM fill:#c4e1ff
    nodeL1 fill:#fff2a8
    nodeL2 fill:#fff2a8
    nodeCL fill:#d4f4dd
```

> **Interpretation:** Pump powers three parallel functions — **table feed (auto-reversing via limit valves)**, **spindle drive (hydraulic motor)**, and **work-piece clamping (single-acting cylinder)**.

---

## 4.6 Hydraulic Grinding Machine Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeGT[TANK] --> nodeGP[GRINDING PUMP]
    nodeGP --> nodeGRV[RELIEF VALVE LOW SET]
    nodeGRV --> nodeGT
    nodeGP --> nodeGDCV[DCV 4 BY 3]
    nodeGDCV --> nodeGPCFV[PRESSURE COMPENSATED FLOW CONTROL]
    nodeGPCFV --> nodeGMOC[METER OUT FLOW CONTROL]
    nodeGMOC --> nodeGWHC[GRINDING WHEEL HEAD CYLINDER]
    nodeGWHC --> nodeGT
    nodeGP --> nodeGDCV2[DCV 4 BY 3 FOR TABLE]
    nodeGDCV2 --> nodeGTC[WORK TABLE CYLINDER]
    nodeGTC --> nodeGT
    nodeGTC --> nodeGLV[LIMIT VALVES REVERSING]
    nodeGLV -.-> nodeGDCV2
    style nodeGT fill:#e1f0ff
    nodeGP fill:#ffd9b3
    nodeGPCFV fill:#fff2a8
    nodeGMOC fill:#d4f4dd
    nodeGWHC fill:#ffc4c4
    nodeGTC fill:#ffc4c4
    nodeGLV fill:#cccccc
```

> **Interpretation:** The wheel-head advances via **pressure-compensated flow control** for stable in-feed. The work-table reciprocates via **meter-out** flow control (exit-side control) — this is critical to **prevent cavitation and vibration** during grinding.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a hydraulic accumulator and list any four of its functions in a hydraulic circuit.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A hydraulic accumulator is a **pressure vessel** that stores hydraulic energy in the form of a **compressed gas** (typically nitrogen) and pressurized oil, separated by a piston, bladder, or diaphragm. *(1 mark)*

> The four principal functions are:
> 1. **Auxiliary power source** — supplies oil during peak demand when pump flow is insufficient.
> 2. **Emergency power backup** — keeps actuators operational for a short time after pump failure.
> 3. **Pulsation / shock dampener** — absorbs pressure spikes in pump or valve circuits.
> 4. **Pressure compensator / thermal expansion compensator** — maintains line pressure and absorbs oil expansion. *(2 marks — 0.5 each)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Compare series and parallel synchronization of hydraulic cylinders. In which situation is a flow divider essential?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> | Feature | Series | Parallel with Flow Divider |
> |---|---|---|
> | Synchronization method | Same flow path (mechanical) | Fixed ratio flow split |
> | Velocity relationship | Inverse of area ratio | Fixed by FD ratio (e.g., 1:1) |
> | Load sensitivity | High (de-syncs if loads differ) | Low (FD compensates) |
> | Cylinder orientation | Must align | Independent |
> | Cost | Lower | Higher |
>
> *(2 marks — 0.4 each row)*

> A **flow divider is essential** when the **two cylinders experience different and varying loads** (e.g., a clamping cylinder + a lift cylinder in a work-holding fixture), and exact synchronism must be maintained regardless of load imbalance. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Set B]`

> **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the operation of a **hydraulic press circuit using an accumulator**. Discuss the role of the sequence valve, counterbalance valve, and pressure relief valve. *(7 marks)*

> **(b)** A **bladder-type accumulator** has a total gas volume of 20 L and is pre-charged to 7 MPa. The system is charged to a maximum hydraulic pressure of 14 MPa. The accumulator is allowed to discharge oil until the pressure drops to 9 MPa. Assuming **isothermal** conditions, calculate:
> - (i) The volume of oil discharged by the accumulator. *(3 marks)*
> - (ii) The energy stored in the accumulator at 14 MPa. *(2 marks)*
> - (iii) The time for which the accumulator can deliver oil to a cylinder requiring a constant flow of 5 L/min. *(2 marks)*

---

### Model Solution for Q-A

#### (a) Hydraulic Press Circuit with Accumulator (7 marks)

**Components & Layout:** *(1 mark)*
- Pump 1 (high flow, low pressure) — for fast approach and fast return.
- Pump 2 (low flow, high pressure) — for working stroke.
- Accumulator (bladder-type) — for peak-load assist and hold.
- Sequence valve — switches from fast to slow stroke.
- Counterbalance valve — prevents ram from falling under gravity.
- Pressure relief valve — limits maximum pressing force.
- DCV (4/3) — controls direction of ram motion.

**Cycle of Operation:** *(3 marks)*
1. **Fast Approach:** Pump 1 supplies oil directly to cap end of ram cylinder; ram moves rapidly with no load. Pressure remains below sequence-valve setting.
2. **Working Stroke:** When ram contacts the workpiece, line pressure rises. Sequence valve opens, **engaging Pump 2 and the accumulator**. Pressing occurs at high force, low speed.
3. **Hold:** Once full pressure is reached, DCV returns to neutral. **Accumulator maintains pressure** on the ram, while pumps may be unloaded — useful for **dwell time** in compression molding.
4. **Fast Return:** DCV shifts to connect Pump 1 to rod end; ram retracts quickly. Counterbalance valve prevents uncontrolled descent.

**Valve Roles:** *(3 marks — 1 each)*
- **Sequence valve:** Detects pressure rise (signaling ram contact with workpiece) and shifts flow path to engage the high-pressure sources (Pump 2 + accumulator).
- **Counterbalance valve:** Creates back-pressure on the rod end during retraction so that the heavy ram cannot free-fall; provides smooth controlled descent.
- **Pressure relief valve:** Protects the system by limiting maximum pressure; in conjunction with the sequence valve, it sets the **maximum pressing force** $F_{max} = P_{set} \times A_{ram}$.

#### (b) Accumulator Calculations (7 marks)

**Given:**
$V_0 = 20 \text{ L} = 0.020 \text{ m}^3$, $P_0 = 7 \text{ MPa}$, $P_1 = 14 \text{ MPa}$ (charged), $P_2 = 9 \text{ MPa}$ (discharged), $Q = 5 \text{ L/min}$.

**(i) Volume of oil discharged** *(3 marks)*

Boyle's Law at charged state:
$V_1 = \dfrac{P_0 V_0}{P_1} = \dfrac{(7 \times 10^6)(0.020)}{14 \times 10^6} = 0.010 \text{ m}^3 = 10 \text{ L}$

Boyle's Law at discharged state:
$V_2 = \dfrac{P_0 V_0}{P_2} = \dfrac{(7 \times 10^6)(0.020)}{9 \times 10^6} = 0.01556 \text{ m}^3 = 15.56 \text{ L}$

Discharged oil volume:
$\Delta V = V_2 - V_1 = 15.56 - 10 = 5.56 \text{ L}$

**(ii) Energy stored at 14 MPa (isothermal)** *(2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= P_1 \Delta V \, \ln \left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_0}\right) \\
&= (14 \times 10^6)(0.010)\ln\left(\dfrac{14}{7}\right) \\
&= (14 \times 10^6)(0.010)(\ln 2) \\
&= 140,000 \times 0.6931 \\
&\approx 97,037 \text{ J} \approx 97 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Discharge time at 5 L/min** *(2 marks)*

$$
t = \dfrac{\Delta V}{Q} = \dfrac{5.56 \text{ L}}{5 \text{ L/min}} = 1.11 \text{ min} = 66.7 \text{ s}
$$

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Set A]`

> **(a)** Explain the working of a **hydraulic milling machine circuit** with the help of a circuit diagram. Describe how automatic table reversal is achieved using limit valves. *(7 marks)*

> **(b)** A hydraulic cylinder of piston diameter **100 mm** and rod diameter **50 mm** is used in a **hydraulic press** circuit. The pump delivers oil at **50 L/min** at a maximum pressure of **20 MPa**. The accumulator (gas volume 30 L, pre-charge 8 MPa) supports the cylinder during the working stroke.
> - (i) Calculate the **forward thrust force** of the cylinder at 20 MPa. *(2 marks)*
> - (ii) Calculate the **velocity** of the piston during fast approach if the pump flow is fully available. *(2 marks)*
> - (iii) Calculate the **maximum oil volume** that the accumulator can deliver if it discharges from 20 MPa to 12 MPa (isothermal). *(3 marks)*

---

### Model Solution for Q-B

#### (a) Hydraulic Milling Machine Circuit (7 marks)

**Main Components:** *(2 marks)*
- Fixed-displacement pump with relief valve.
- **DCV (4/3, spring-centered)** for table feed direction.
- **Flow control valve (FCV)** for adjusting feed rate.
- **Two limit valves** (or pilot-operated sequence valves) at the ends of table travel.
- Feed cylinder (double-acting).
- Hydraulic motor (for spindle drive) with its own DCV.
- Work-piece clamping cylinder (single-acting) with pilot check valve.

**Auto-Reversal Logic:** *(3 marks)*

1. Operator energizes DCV to "Forward" → pressurized oil flows to cap end of feed cylinder → table moves forward.
2. Flow rate is governed by FCV → controllable feed.
3. As the table approaches the end of its forward travel, the **limit valve 1** (cam-actuated) is triggered.
4. The pilot signal from limit valve 1 **shifts the DCV** to "Reverse" position.
5. Oil is now routed to the rod end of the feed cylinder; table retracts.
6. **Limit valve 2** at the return end triggers another shift → DCV returns to "Forward".
7. The cycle repeats automatically until the operator's stop button is pressed.

**Spindle & Clamp Logic:** *(2 marks)*
- Spindle motor runs continuously; ON/OFF controlled by a separate DCV.
- Clamping cylinder is energized only when the spindle is running (interlock via pressure switch), ensuring the work-piece is held only while cutting is in progress.

#### (b) Cylinder & Accumulator Calculations (7 marks)

**Given:** $D = 100$ mm = 0.1 m, $d = 50$ mm = 0.05 m, $Q = 50$ L/min = $8.33 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s, $P = 20$ MPa, $V_0 = 30$ L = 0.030 m³, $P_0 = 8$ MPa, $P_1 = 20$ MPa, $P_2 = 12$ MPa.

**(i) Forward thrust** *(2 marks)*

$$
A_p = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.1)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
F = P \cdot A_p = (20 \times 10^6)(7.854 \times 10^{-3}) = 157,080 \text{ N} \approx 157.1 \text{ kN}
$$

**(ii) Velocity during fast approach** *(2 marks)*

$$
v = \dfrac{Q}{A_p} = \dfrac{8.33 \times 10^{-4}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1061 \text{ m/s} = 106.1 \text{ mm/s}
$$

**(iii) Oil volume delivered by accumulator** *(3 marks)*

Charged gas volume:
$V_1 = \dfrac{P_0 V_0}{P_1} = \dfrac{(8 \times 10^6)(0.030)}{20 \times 10^6} = 0.012 \text{ m}^3 = 12 \text{ L}$

Discharged gas volume:
$V_2 = \dfrac{P_0 V_0}{P_2} = \dfrac{(8 \times 10^6)(0.030)}{12 \times 10^6} = 0.020 \text{ m}^3 = 20 \text{ L}$

$$
\Delta V = V_2 - V_1 = 20 - 12 = 8 \text{ L}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes that COST MARKS in this module:**
>
> 1. **Mixing isothermal and adiabatic equations.** For **slow-acting** circuits (press holding, leakage compensation) use **Boyle's Law (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)**. For **fast-acting** circuits (impact, emergency release, pulsation damping) use the **polytropic / adiabatic** form with **n = 1.4** for nitrogen. Examiners WILL check this. *(−1 to −2 marks)*
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert litres to m³** (or vice versa) in the gas law. Always state units explicitly. 1 L = 1×10⁻³ m³. Mixing units is the #1 source of numerical errors. *(−1 mark)*
>
> 3. **In series synchronization, confusing FORCE and VELOCITY.** Both cylinders carry the **same FLOW**, not the same force. The thrust differs because **areas differ**. Examiners frequently ask this as a 2-mark concept. *(−2 marks if missed)*
>
> 4. **Press-circuit students forget the COUNTERBALANCE valve.** Without it, a heavy ram free-falls and creates dangerous pressure transients. Always mention it. *(−1 mark)*
>
> 5. **In grinding circuits, students write "meter-IN" instead of "meter-OUT".** Meter-OUT is used on the **return side** to prevent cavitation and vibration in low-speed reciprocating motion. *(−1 mark)*
>
> 6. **Flow divider ≠ flow control valve.** A flow divider is **rigid in its ratio**; a flow control valve sets a flow but the ratio is not fixed. Examiners will deduct if confused. *(−1 mark)*

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Synchronization — Series:** Same flow, **velocity inverse to area**, force proportional to its own area. Used only when loads are **nearly equal**.
- ✅ **Synchronization — Parallel:** Uses a **flow divider** to enforce a fixed flow ratio. Cylinders can have **different sizes, strokes, and loads**.
- ✅ **Flow Divider rule of thumb:** Total flow in = sum of two branch flows; the ratio is fixed by valve design (e.g., 50:50, 30:70).
- ✅ **Accumulator types:** Bladder, Piston, Diaphragm — bladder is **most common** in industrial circuits.
- ✅ **Pre-charge rule:** $P_0 \le 0.9 \, P_{min}$ for bladder (per ISO 4413).
- ✅ **Boyle's Law (isothermal):** $P_0 V_0 = P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ — for **slow** processes (press holding, leak compensation).
- ✅ **Polytropic Law (n = 1.4):** $P_0 V_0^{1.4} = P_1 V_1^{1.4} = P_2 V_2^{1.4}$ — for **fast** processes (impact damping, emergency release).
- ✅ **Discharged volume:** $\Delta V = V_0 P_0 \left(\dfrac{1}{P_2} - \dfrac{1}{P_1}\right)$.
- ✅ **Sizing equation:** $V_0 = \dfrac{\Delta V \cdot P_1 P_2}{P_0 (P_1 - P_2)}$.
- ✅ **Press circuit elements:** Dual pump (LP+HP), accumulator, sequence valve, counterbalance valve, pressure relief valve, pilot check.
- ✅ **Press cycle:** Fast approach → Slow working → Hold → Fast return.
- ✅ **Milling machine:** Auto-reversing via **limit valves (mechanical cam) or pilot-operated sequence valves**.
- ✅ **Grinding machine:** Uses **pressure-compensated flow control** for in-feed and **meter-OUT flow control** for table reciprocation (avoids cavitation and chatter).
- ✅ **Common formulas to memorize:** $F = PA$, $Q = Av$, $P_1V_1 = P_2V_2$, $V_0$ sizing, $W = P_1 \Delta V \ln(P_1/P_0)$.
- ✅ **Units consistency:** Use SI throughout; convert L ↔ m³ (×10⁻³); convert bar ↔ Pa (×10⁵); convert mm ↔ m (×10⁻³).
- ✅ **Energy stored in accumulator:** Isothermal $W = P_1 \Delta V \ln(P_1/P_0)$ ; Adiabatic $W = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{n-1}\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$.

> **End of Module 3 — Industrial Hydraulics Notes.** Best of luck for your KTU examinations! 🎓
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT526 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Industrial Hydraulics**, **Planning** refers to the systematic, engineering-driven process of designing, sizing, selecting, and laying out a complete hydraulic power and control system to meet a defined industrial application. It is the pre-installation phase where every functional requirement (force, speed, power, duty cycle, environment, safety, and economics) is translated into a coordinated arrangement of hydraulic components — namely the **power pack (prime mover + reservoir + pump)**, **valves (DCV, pressure, flow, check)**, **actuators (cylinders, motors)**, **conditioning elements (filters, coolers, accumulators)**, and the **piping/hose network**.

According to KTU 2024 scheme nomenclature, hydraulic system planning encompasses:

- **Functional Planning** — what the system must *do* (motion profile, sequence, force/velocity envelope).
- **Technical Planning** — what *components* and *parameters* achieve the function (pressures, flows, power losses, efficiency).
- **Economic Planning** — cost of ownership, life-cycle cost, energy cost, and reliability targets.
- **Layout Planning** — physical arrangement of power unit, actuators, valves, and pipeline routing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 – Planning):** The student is expected to develop the competency of converting a *mechanical requirement* into a *complete hydraulic circuit specification*, including the derivation of operating pressure, pump displacement, flow rate, motor/cylinder sizing, power losses, heat dissipation, and reservoir sizing. This is a high-weightage topic for KTU ESE questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **Planning a Hydraulic System** like **Planning a City Water Supply Network**:
- The **reservoir** is the *water tank* on top of the building.
- The **pump** is the *motor-pump set* that pushes water up.
- The **valves** are the *taps and control valves* in each apartment.
- The **pipes** are the *plumbing lines* sized to carry the required flow without choking.
- The **actuators (cylinders/motors)** are the *end appliances* (shower, washing machine inlet) that actually consume the water.

If you undersize the pipes, the shower pressure drops. If the pump is too small, the top floor gets no water. If the reservoir is too small, there is no buffer during peak demand. **Hydraulic planning is exactly this — but with oil instead of water, and with pressures often above 100 bar instead of gravity-fed 2-3 bar.**

## 1.3 Key Planning Parameters (Standard Metrics)

- **System Operating Pressure ($P$)** — typically **70 bar to 350 bar** in modern industrial hydraulics.
- **Pump Volumetric Displacement ($V_p$)** — in **cm³/rev (cc/rev)**.
- **Flow Rate ($Q$)** — in **L/min (LPM)**.
- **Power ($P_{hyd}$)** — in **kW**, with **1 kW ≈ 1.341 HP**.
- **Oil Viscosity** — typically **ISO VG 32, 46, or 68** for industrial systems.
- **Filtration** — usually **NAS 1638 Class 8 / ISO 4406 20/18/15** for general industrial circuits.
- **Duty Cycle** — expressed as a **percentage (%)** or **seconds ON / total cycle time (s)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference:** Industry standard circuit symbols follow **ISO 1219** (current version **ISO 1219-1:2012** for symbols, ISO 1219-2:2012 for circuits). All KTU-expected diagrams should follow ISO conventions.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Phased Planning of a Hydraulic System (Structured Logic Steps)

A standard KTU-style planning exercise is executed in the following ordered phases. Each phase feeds numerical input to the next, and a single wrong assumption at Phase 1 propagates to a wrong actuator at Phase 6.

### Phase 1 — Load and Motion Analysis
- Identify the **maximum working load** (force $F_L$ in N) or **maximum torque** ($T_L$ in N·m).
- Identify the **required linear speed** ($v$ in m/s) or **rotational speed** ($N$ in rpm).
- Identify the **stroke length** ($L$ in m) and **duty cycle** ($DC$ in %).
- Identify the **environmental constraints**: ambient temperature, dust, moisture, explosion-proof requirement, cleanliness (food/pharma industry).

### Phase 2 — Selection of Operating Pressure ($P_{sys}$)
- Operating pressure is *not* a free choice. It is derived from actuator sizing and economic trade-off.
- **Rule of Thumb (KTU-Standard):** For industrial presses, injection moulding machines, and heavy mobile equipment → $P_{sys} \in [160, 350]\ \text{bar}$.
- For low-power machine tool hydraulics → $P_{sys} \in [70, 140]\ \text{bar}$.
- **Economic Trade-off:** Higher pressure ⇒ smaller actuators, smaller oil volume, smaller reservoir, but *higher* pump cost, *higher* leak risk, *higher* filtration demand, *higher* noise.

### Phase 3 — Actuator Sizing
**For a hydraulic cylinder:**
- Required piston area: $A = \dfrac{F_L}{P_{sys} \cdot \eta_m}$
- Required bore: $D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4A}{\pi}}$
- Rod diameter (for tension load, safety $\geq 1.5\times$): $d \approx 0.7D$ typical.
- Required flow: $Q_{cyl} = A \cdot v$

**For a hydraulic motor:**
- Required displacement: $V_m = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot T_L}{\Delta P \cdot \eta_m}$
- Required flow: $Q_m = \dfrac{V_m \cdot N}{1000}$ (in L/min if $V_m$ in cc/rev, $N$ in rpm).

### Phase 4 — Pump Selection
- Total flow = sum of simultaneous actuator flows + leakage reserve (typically **+10% to +20%**).
- Pump displacement: $V_p = \dfrac{Q_{pump} \cdot 1000}{N_{pump}}$ (cc/rev).
- Choose between **fixed displacement** (gear, vane, axial piston) and **variable displacement** (load-sensing, pressure-compensated).
- For high-pressure industrial systems ≥ 250 bar, **axial piston pumps** are preferred for efficiency.

### Phase 5 — Power Pack Sizing (Prime Mover + Reservoir)
- Hydraulic power: $P_{hyd} = \dfrac{P_{sys} \cdot Q}{600}$ (kW, when $P$ in bar, $Q$ in LPM).
- Electric motor rating: $P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{hyd}}{\eta_{pump} \cdot \eta_{motor}}$ (with $\eta_{pump} \cdot \eta_{motor} \approx 0.7 - 0.85$).
- **Reservoir sizing rule:** $V_{tank} = (2 \text{ to } 4) \times Q_{pump}$ (in L, with $Q$ in LPM), or **3× to 5×** the flow of the largest actuator per minute, whichever is greater.

### Phase 6 — Heat Dissipation and Cooling
- Power loss = Input power − Useful hydraulic power = $P_{loss} = P_{motor} - P_{hyd} \cdot \eta_{overall}$ (in kW).
- Heat to be removed by the reservoir (natural convection, no cooler): $P_{dissipated} \approx 0.06 \times A_{tank}$ to $0.12 \times A_{tank}$ in kW, where $A_{tank}$ is the wetted surface in m².
- If $P_{loss} > P_{dissipated}$, a **oil cooler** is mandatory.

### Phase 7 — Valves, Filtration, and Piping
- **Directional Control Valves (DCV)**: 4/3, 4/2, 3/2 based on motion logic; cetop/NG6/NG10/NG16/NG25 mounting.
- **Pressure Control**: relief valve set ~10% above max working pressure; sequence, counterbalance, unloading valves as required.
- **Flow Control**: Throttle (inlet/meter-out) or proportional for speed regulation.
- **Filtration**: Suction strainer (100–150 µm), return line filter (10–25 µm), optional pressure line filter (3–10 µm for servo systems).
- **Piping**: Velocity limits — **suction line ≤ 1.0 m/s**, **return line ≤ 2.0 m/s**, **pressure line ≤ 4.0 to 6.0 m/s**. This determines the minimum pipe bore $d_{pipe} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4Q}{v \cdot 60000 \pi}}$ (in m, $Q$ in LPM).

### Phase 8 — Safety, Standards, and Documentation
- Compliance with **ISO 4413** (Hydraulic Fluid Power – General Rules for Systems), **ISO 4414** (Pneumatic counterpart), and machine safety directives.
- Inclusion of **emergency stop**, **pressure relief**, **lockout valves**, and **accumulator isolation**.
- Documentation: circuit diagram (ISO 1219), component list, Bill of Materials (BOM), maintenance schedule.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units (KTU Standard) | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------------|-------|
| 1 | Cylinder piston area | $A = \dfrac{F_L}{P \cdot \eta_m}$ | m², N, Pa | $\eta_m \approx 0.90\text{–}0.95$ for cylinders |
| 2 | Cylinder bore | $D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4A}{\pi}}$ | m | Round to standard size |
| 3 | Cylinder flow rate | $Q = A \cdot v \cdot 600$ | LPM, m², m/s | Multiply by 60,000 for cc/s |
| 4 | Hydraulic power | $P_{hyd} = \dfrac{P \cdot Q}{600}$ | kW, bar, LPM | Classic KTU formula |
| 5 | Motor displacement | $V_m = \dfrac{2\pi T_L}{\Delta P \cdot \eta_m}$ | m³/rev, N·m, Pa | Convert cc/rev by $\times 10^6$ |
| 6 | Motor flow rate | $Q_m = \dfrac{V_m \cdot N}{1000}$ | LPM, cc/rev, rpm | Standard KTU form |
| 7 | Pump displacement | $V_p = \dfrac{Q \cdot 1000}{N}$ | cc/rev, LPM, rpm | Inverse of motor flow |
| 8 | Motor shaft power | $P_{out} = \dfrac{2\pi T \cdot N}{60{,}000}$ | kW, N·m, rpm | For pump/motor sizing |
| 9 | Pipe bore (velocity limit) | $d = \sqrt{\dfrac{4Q}{v \cdot 60{,}000 \cdot \pi}}$ | m, LPM, m/s | Suction: $v \le 1\ \text{m/s}$ |
| 10 | Reservoir capacity | $V_{tank} = 3 \times Q_{pump}$ | L (with $Q$ in LPM) | Or $2\text{–}4 \times Q$ |
| 11 | Heat dissipation (reservoir) | $P_{heat} \approx 0.07 \cdot A_{surface}$ | kW, m² | Empirical for natural convection |
| 12 | Pressure setting of relief valve | $P_{set} = 1.1 \cdot P_{max}$ | bar | 10% margin over working pressure |
| 13 | Flow through orifice | $Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | m³/s, Pa, kg/m³ | $C_d \approx 0.6\text{–}0.65$ for sharp edge |
| 14 | Force from pressure (cylinder) | $F = P \cdot A \cdot \eta_m$ | N, Pa, m² | For double-acting cylinder |
| 15 | Pump input power | $P_{in} = \dfrac{P \cdot Q}{600 \cdot \eta_{overall}}$ | kW, bar, LPM | $\eta_{overall} \approx 0.70\text{–}0.85$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion to memorize:**
> 1 bar = $10^5$ Pa = $10^5$ N/m².
> 1 HP = 0.7457 kW.
> 1 LPM = $16.67 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/s = 16.67 cc/s.
> 1 cc/rev = $10^{-6}$ m³/rev.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Manufacturing Industries:** Press brakes, plastic injection moulding machines (clamping tonnage up to 10,000 kN), die-casting machines.
- **Construction & Earthmoving:** Excavators, loaders, dump trucks, cranes — where compact high-pressure hydraulics deliver huge force.
- **Marine & Offshore:** Steering gear, deck cranes, hatch covers, anchor windlasses.
- **Aerospace:** Flight control actuators (servohydraulic), landing gear extension/retraction.
- **Steel & Metals:** Rolling mills, hydraulic presses, scrap shears, forging hammers.
- **Energy Sector:** Wind turbine pitch and yaw control, dam gate actuation, oil rig draw-works.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Reservoir Size is Roughly 3× Pump Flow

The reservoir must hold *at least* the volume of oil discharged by the pump during the time it takes for the largest actuator to retract (the worst-case oil return surge). Additionally, it must dissipate heat by natural convection through its surface.

**Logical Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Worst-case return surge volume.**
The largest single-acting cylinder of stroke $L$ and piston area $A$ returns a volume $V_{cyl} = A \cdot L$ to the tank during its return stroke.

**Step 2 — Pump delivery during that same stroke.**
At a speed $v$, return stroke time is $t = L/v$. The pump delivers $Q_{pump} \cdot t$ during this time.

**Step 3 — Total oil that *must* be buffered.**
$V_{buffer} = V_{cyl} + Q_{pump} \cdot t = A \cdot L + Q_{pump} \cdot L / v$.

**Step 4 — Normalize to pump flow.**
Divide by $t = L/v$ to express in terms of "pump-flow-minutes":

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{buffer,\,per\,minute} &= \frac{V_{cyl}}{t} + Q_{pump} \\
&= \frac{A \cdot v}{1} + Q_{pump} \\
&= Q_{cyl,\,extend} + Q_{pump}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a typical design where the cylinder flow is comparable to pump flow, $V_{tank} \approx 2 Q_{pump}\ \text{to}\ 4 Q_{pump}$ in LPM·minutes, i.e., 2× to 4× the pump flow when expressed in litres with $Q$ in LPM.

**Step 5 — Add thermal margin.**
Heat dissipation requires extra surface area; therefore, in practice, designers use **$3\times$ to $5\times$ $Q_{pump}$** for high-duty industrial systems and **$2\times$ to $3\times$ $Q_{pump}$** for low-duty or mobile systems with active coolers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This 3× rule is a **sizing thumb-rule**, not a physical law. In high-cycle systems (e.g., injection moulding, ≥ 30 cycles/min), designers often go to 5×–6× $Q_{pump}$ to prevent foaming, aeration, and cavitation in the return line.

## 3.2 Derivation: Pipe Sizing from Velocity Constraint

**Problem:** Given a flow rate $Q$ (in LPM) and a target maximum velocity $v$ (in m/s), find the minimum internal pipe diameter.

**Step 1 — Convert flow to SI volume flow rate.**
$Q$ in LPM means litres per minute, so:

$$
Q_{SI} = \frac{Q}{1000 \times 60} = \frac{Q}{60{,}000} \quad \left[\frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{s}}\right]
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the volumetric flow equation.**
For a circular pipe of cross-section $A_c$ and average fluid velocity $v$:

$$
Q_{SI} = A_c \cdot v
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the pipe cross-section.**
For an internal diameter $d$:

$$
A_c = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $d$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{SI} &= \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot v \\
d^2 &= \frac{4 Q_{SI}}{\pi v} = \frac{4 Q}{\pi v \cdot 60{,}000} \\
d &= \sqrt{\frac{4 Q}{60{,}000 \pi v}} = \sqrt{\frac{Q}{15{,}000 \pi v}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply velocity limits.**
- Suction line: $v \le 1.0\ \text{m/s}$ (to prevent cavitation at pump inlet).
- Return line: $v \le 2.0\ \text{m/s}$.
- Pressure line: $v \le 4.0$ to $6.0\ \text{m/s}$ (4 m/s for ≤ 100 bar, up to 6 m/s for ≥ 200 bar to keep bore small).

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Check:** For $Q = 60$ LPM and $v = 4$ m/s:
> $d = \sqrt{\dfrac{60}{15000 \cdot \pi \cdot 4}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{60}{188{,}496}} = \sqrt{3.18 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.01784$ m $\approx$ **18 mm** internal bore. Selecting a **3/4 inch (DN20)** steel pipe (inside diameter ≈ 20 mm) is the correct KTU-style answer.

## 3.3 Derivation: Hydraulic Power and Motor Sizing Chain

**Step 1 — Theoretical hydraulic power delivered to the load.**

$$
P_{hyd} = \Delta P \cdot Q_{vol} = P \cdot Q_{SI} \quad [\text{Watts}]
$$

with $P$ in Pa, $Q_{SI}$ in m³/s.

**Step 2 — Convert to KTU-friendly units (bar and LPM).**
$1$ Pa $= 10^{-5}$ bar, and $Q_{SI} = Q_{LPM}/60{,}000$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{hyd} &= P_{\text{bar}} \cdot 10^5 \cdot \frac{Q_{LPM}}{60{,}000} \\
P_{hyd} &= \frac{P_{\text{bar}} \cdot Q_{LPM}}{0.6} \quad [\text{Watts}]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert watts to kilowatts.**

$$
P_{hyd} = \frac{P_{\text{bar}} \cdot Q_{LPM}}{600} \quad [\text{kW}]
$$

This is the **classic KTU formula** that must be committed to memory.

**Step 4 — Motor shaft power required.**

$$
P_{motor} = \frac{P_{hyd}}{\eta_{overall}} = \frac{P_{hyd}}{\eta_{pump} \cdot \eta_{mech}}
$$

**Step 5 — Electric motor nameplate rating.**
Apply a service factor of **1.15 to 1.25** to account for transient peaks and starting loads:

$$
P_{motor,\,nameplate} = 1.20 \cdot P_{motor}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 1.2 service factor is the *minimum* KTU-acceptable value. For hydraulic presses with shock loads, designers often go to 1.5×–2.0× the continuous power.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Planning Decision Tree

| Planning Decision | Driver Constraint | Typical Range | If Oversized | If Undersized |
|-------------------|-------------------|---------------|--------------|---------------|
| Operating Pressure | Load force, cost trade-off | 100–350 bar | Wasteful, expensive seals, noisy | Cylinder bore becomes huge |
| Pump Type | Pressure + efficiency | Gear / Vane / Piston | Cost penalty | Cavitation, inefficiency |
| Pipe Bore | Velocity limit | 10–50 mm | Cost, weight, oil volume | Pressure loss, heat, noise |
| Reservoir | Heat dissipation + buffer | 50–2000 L | Floor space, oil cost | Foaming, overheating |
| Filtration | Component sensitivity | 3–25 µm | High cost, slow ΔP | Pump wear, valve sticking |
| Cooling | Heat load | Natural / Fan / Water | Cost | Oil degrades, seals fail |

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow: Hydraulic System Planning Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Industrial Requirement] --> B[Phase 1: Load & Motion Analysis]
    B --> B1[Identify Force F_L or Torque T_L]
    B1 --> B2[Identify Speed v or rpm N]
    B2 --> B3[Identify Stroke and Duty Cycle]
    B3 --> B4[Identify Environment and Safety Standards]
    B4 --> C[Phase 2: Select Operating Pressure P_sys]
    C --> D{Actuator Type?}
    D -- Linear --> E1[Phase 3a: Size Cylinder]
    D -- Rotary --> E2[Phase 3b: Size Hydraulic Motor]
    E1 --> F1[Compute A, D, rod d, Q_cyl]
    E2 --> F2[Compute V_m, Q_m, T_out]
    F1 --> G[Phase 4: Sum Total Flow + 15% Reserve]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H[Phase 5: Select Pump Type and Displacement]
    H --> I[Phase 6: Size Power Pack]
    I --> I1[Select Electric Motor with 1.2 SF]
    I --> I2[Size Reservoir 3x Q_pump]
    I2 --> J[Phase 7: Heat Calculation]
    J --> J1{Heat > Natural Dissipation?}
    J1 -- Yes --> J2[Add Oil Cooler]
    J1 -- No --> J3[Reservoir Alone Sufficient]
    J2 --> K[Phase 8: Select Valves, Filters, Accumulators]
    J3 --> K
    K --> L[Phase 9: Pipe Sizing by Velocity Limits]
    L --> M[Phase 10: Safety & ISO 4413 Compliance]
    M --> N[Phase 11: Documentation and BOM]
    N --> O[Final Hydraulic Plan Approved]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    end
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**List any six essential data items that must be collected before starting the planning of an industrial hydraulic system.**

**Model Answer (6 items expected):**
1. Maximum working load (force in N or torque in N·m).
2. Required linear speed (m/s) or rotational speed (rpm).
3. Stroke length (m) or angular travel (rad/rev).
4. Duty cycle (% or ON/OFF time).
5. Ambient and operating temperature range (°C).
6. Power supply available (3-phase voltage, frequency, kVA).
7. (Any other valid: cleanliness, safety class, IP rating, mounting orientation.)

*(1 mark per correct item, max 3. Aim for six bullets to ensure 3 marks even if one is considered borderline.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**State the standard velocity limits recommended for hydraulic pressure, return, and suction lines. Why is the suction-line limit stricter than the pressure-line limit?**

**Model Answer:**

| Line | Maximum Velocity (m/s) |
|------|------------------------|
| Pressure line (≤ 200 bar) | 4.0 |
| Pressure line (> 200 bar) | 5.0 – 6.0 |
| Return line | 2.0 |
| Suction line | 1.0 |

**Reason (2 marks for this):** The suction line is the *inlet* to the pump, and at the pump inlet the absolute pressure is already reduced below atmospheric by the suction head. If the velocity is high, the local static pressure drops further (Bernoulli effect), causing **cavitation** at the pump inlet — leading to erosion, noise, vibration, and reduced pump life. Hence the stricter limit of ≤ 1 m/s.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks (Choose A or B)

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample ESE Pattern, Module 3 Internal Choice]**

**A hydraulic press is required to deliver a maximum forming force of 800 kN. The ram (main cylinder) is to advance at a speed of 50 mm/s during the working stroke and return at 80 mm/s. The working pressure is 200 bar. Assume a mechanical efficiency of 90% for the cylinder. The system is supplied by a fixed displacement pump running at 1450 rpm. Plan the system by determining: (a) the cylinder dimensions (bore and rod diameter), (b) the pump flow rate, displacement, and motor power required, and (c) the reservoir size, and pipe bore for the pressure line. Assume velocity in pressure line = 4 m/s and overall efficiency = 80%.**

#### (a) Cylinder Dimensions — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Compute the required piston area.**
Given $F_L = 800\ \text{kN} = 800{,}000\ \text{N}$, $P = 200\ \text{bar} = 200 \times 10^5\ \text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^7\ \text{Pa}$, $\eta_m = 0.90$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{F_L}{P \cdot \eta_m} \\
&= \frac{800{,}000}{2 \times 10^7 \times 0.90} \\
&= \frac{800{,}000}{1.8 \times 10^7} \\
&= 0.04444\ \text{m}^2 \\
&= 444.4\ \text{cm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the bore (piston diameter).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \sqrt{\frac{4A}{\pi}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.04444}{\pi}} \\
&= \sqrt{0.05660} \\
&= 0.238\ \text{m} = 238\ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Standard size.** Round up to next standard bore: **$D = 250$ mm** (standard ISO/IS series). This gives:

$$
A_{std} = \frac{\pi (0.25)^2}{4} = 0.04909\ \text{m}^2 = 490.9\ \text{cm}^2
$$

**Step 4: Rod diameter (rule: $d \approx 0.7D$).**
$d = 0.7 \times 250 = 175\ \text{mm}$. Adopt **$d = 180\ \text{mm}$** as a standard size.

**Step 5: Verify return-side force is positive (no pull-back issue).** Return-side area:

$$
A_{ret} = A_{std} - \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = 0.04909 - \frac{\pi (0.18)^2}{4} = 0.04909 - 0.02545 = 0.02364\ \text{m}^2
$$

Force generated during retraction at 200 bar:

$$
F_{ret} = 200 \times 10^5 \times 0.02364 \times 0.90 = 425{,}520\ \text{N} \approx 425.5\ \text{kN}
$$

Sufficient for empty-retract duty. ✓

---

#### (b) Pump Sizing and Motor Power — 4 Marks

**Step 1: Required flow during advance (working stroke).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{adv} &= A_{std} \cdot v_{adv} \cdot 60{,}000 \\
&= 0.04909 \times 0.050 \times 60{,}000 \\
&= 147.3\ \text{LPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Required flow during return.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{ret} &= A_{ret} \cdot v_{ret} \cdot 60{,}000 \\
&= 0.02364 \times 0.080 \times 60{,}000 \\
&= 113.5\ \text{LPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Governing flow** = max of advance and return = **147.3 LPM**.

**Step 4: Add 15% leakage/system reserve.**

$$
Q_{pump} = 1.15 \times 147.3 = 169.4\ \text{LPM}
$$

**Step 5: Pump displacement at 1450 rpm.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_p &= \frac{Q_{pump} \times 1000}{N} \\
&= \frac{169.4 \times 1000}{1450} \\
&= 116.8\ \text{cc/rev}
\end{aligned}
$$

Adopt a standard size **120 cc/rev axial piston pump** (fixed or variable displacement).

**Step 6: Motor shaft power at working pressure.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{motor} &= \frac{P \cdot Q_{pump}}{600 \cdot \eta_{overall}} \\
&= \frac{200 \times 169.4}{600 \times 0.80} \\
&= \frac{33{,}880}{480} \\
&= 70.58\ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Apply service factor 1.20.**

$$
P_{nameplate} = 1.20 \times 70.58 = 84.7\ \text{kW}
$$

Adopt a **90 kW, 3-phase, 4-pole (1450 rpm)** squirrel-cage induction motor.

---

#### (c) Reservoir and Pipe Sizing — 3 Marks

**Step 1: Reservoir capacity (using 3× rule).**

$$
V_{tank} = 3 \times Q_{pump} = 3 \times 169.4 = 508.2\ \text{L}
$$

Adopt a standard size **500 L** reservoir (or 600 L for thermal margin).

**Step 2: Pressure-line bore for $Q_{pump} = 169.4\ \text{LPM}$ and $v = 4\ \text{m/s}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \sqrt{\frac{Q}{15{,}000 \pi v}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{169.4}{15{,}000 \cdot \pi \cdot 4}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{169.4}{188{,}496}} \\
&= \sqrt{8.988 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&= 0.02998\ \text{m} \approx 30\ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Adopt **1.25 inch (DN32)** steel pipe with inside diameter ≈ 32 mm, or **DN25** if the *actual* working flow is the lower 113.5 LPM return value (re-check based on line routing).

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model ESE Pattern, Module 3 Internal Choice]**

**A hydraulic system is required to drive a rotary table through a hydraulic motor. The motor must deliver a torque of 250 N·m at a speed of 300 rpm. The pressure differential across the motor is 175 bar and the volumetric efficiency is 95%. The pump runs at 1450 rpm and overall efficiency is 80%. Plan: (a) the motor displacement and flow requirement, (b) the pump displacement, motor input power, and reservoir capacity, and (c) the recommended filtration class and the reason for placing the relief valve close to the pump.**

#### (a) Motor Displacement and Flow — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Motor displacement (cc/rev).**

$$
V_m = \frac{2\pi T_L}{\Delta P \cdot \eta_m} = \frac{2\pi \times 250}{175 \times 10^5 \times 0.95}
$$

**Step 2: Numerator evaluation.**

$$
2\pi \times 250 = 1570.8
$$

**Step 3: Denominator evaluation.**

$$
175 \times 10^5 \times 0.95 = 1.6625 \times 10^7
$$

**Step 4: Division.**

$$
V_m = \frac{1570.8}{1.6625 \times 10^7} = 9.448 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^3/\text{rev}
$$

**Step 5: Convert to cc/rev (multiply by $10^6$).**

$$
V_m = 9.448 \times 10^{-5} \times 10^6 = 94.48\ \text{cc/rev}
$$

Adopt a standard **100 cc/rev** hydraulic motor (axial piston or gear type depending on pressure class — 175 bar suits axial piston or high-pressure gear).

**Step 6: Flow required by motor.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{motor} &= \frac{V_m \cdot N}{1000} \\
&= \frac{100 \times 300}{1000} \\
&= 30\ \text{LPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Using the adopted 100 cc/rev size; the theoretically computed 94.48 cc/rev gives 28.34 LPM, both within 5% — KTU accepts either.)

---

#### (b) Pump, Motor Power, Reservoir — 4 Marks

**Step 1: Pump flow with 15% reserve.**

$$
Q_{pump} = 1.15 \times 30 = 34.5\ \text{LPM}
$$

**Step 2: Pump displacement at 1450 rpm.**

$$
V_p = \frac{34.5 \times 1000}{1450} = 23.79\ \text{cc/rev}
$$

Adopt a **25 cc/rev** fixed-displacement vane or axial piston pump.

**Step 3: Motor input power (electric motor rating).**

$$
P_{motor} = \frac{P \cdot Q_{pump}}{600 \cdot \eta_{overall}} = \frac{175 \times 34.5}{600 \times 0.80} = \frac{6037.5}{480} = 12.58\ \text{kW}
$$

With 1.20 service factor: $P_{nameplate} = 1.20 \times 12.58 = 15.1\ \text{kW}$. Adopt a **15 kW (or 18.5 kW standard) 3-phase motor**.

**Step 4: Reservoir size (3× rule).**

$$
V_{tank} = 3 \times 34.5 = 103.5\ \text{L}
$$

Adopt a **120 L** standard tank (with baffles, return-line filter, and breather).

---

#### (c) Filtration and Relief Valve Placement — 3 Marks

**Filtration Class:**
- Suction strainer: 100–150 µm (ISO 4406 ~22/20/18).
- Return line filter: 25 µm absolute (ISO 4406 20/18/15 — NAS 1638 Class 8).
- Optional pressure filter for servo: 3–10 µm (ISO 4406 16/14/12 — NAS Class 6).

**Reason for placing the relief valve close to the pump (2 marks):**
The relief valve must be placed as close to the pump discharge port as possible (and before any significant flow restriction or directional valve) so that:
1. The pump is protected from **over-pressurization** at the instant the system is dead-headed (e.g., DCV in centre-blocked position).
2. Pressure surges from sudden valve closure do not travel back to the pump and cause **hydraulic shock** damage to the pump shaft, bearings, or seals.
3. The valve can sense and limit the **true pump discharge pressure** without lag or sensing error caused by line pressure drops.

This is a **standard KTU safety-planning** answer expected in the valuation key.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.10/1.15 safety multiplier on pressure/flow** — a relief valve set exactly at the working pressure will *open* during every working stroke, dumping oil and causing overheating. Always set the relief at **1.10× the working pressure**.
> 2. **Wrong unit conversion (bar ↔ Pa):** Students often forget to multiply bar by $10^5$ when using SI. KTU markers deduct **1 full mark** for this in Part B.
> 3. **Neglecting the volumetric/mechanical efficiency** in the actuator sizing — the force available *at the load* will be less than $P \times A$ if efficiency is ignored. KTU explicitly tests this.
> 4. **Forgetting the service factor on the motor** — even if the motor calculation is correct, a *raw* value without the 1.2 multiplier loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Picking a non-standard bore or rod size** — always round *up* to the nearest ISO/IS standard (250 mm bore → not 238 mm). KTU awards 1 mark for selecting the right standard size.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Planning is the bridge** between an industrial *requirement* (force, speed, duty) and a *hydraulic circuit* (pump, valves, actuators, plumbing).
- **Operating pressure is the master variable** — it controls actuator size, pump type, seal class, and cost.
- **The classic power formula** is $P_{hyd} = \dfrac{P_{bar} \cdot Q_{LPM}}{600}$ in kW. This single equation appears in nearly every KTU Part B question.
- **Suction-line velocity ≤ 1 m/s**, return line ≤ 2 m/s, pressure line ≤ 4–6 m/s. These limits prevent cavitation, pressure loss, and noise.
- **Reservoir sizing:** $V_{tank} = 3 Q_{pump}$ (L, with $Q$ in LPM) is the KTU standard rule for industrial systems.
- **Filtration matters:** Dirty oil is the #1 cause of hydraulic failure. NAS 8 / ISO 20/18/15 is the industrial default.
- **Efficiencies must be carried through every calculation:** $\eta_m$ for actuators, $\eta_{vol}$ for pumps/motors, $\eta_{overall}$ for motor sizing.
- **Standard references to cite in KTU answers:** ISO 1219 (symbols), ISO 4413 (hydraulic safety), ISO 4406 (cleanliness codes).
- **Always apply a safety/service factor** — 10% on relief valve setting, 15% on pump flow, 20% on motor power.
- **Cylinder bore must be a standard size** (e.g., 63, 80, 100, 125, 160, 200, 250, 320 mm ISO series). Never quote a non-standard theoretical size.
- **The motor service factor ≥ 1.20** is the universal KTU expectation; for high-shock loads, use 1.5 or even 2.0.
- **Heat dissipation check** is mandatory — if reservoir surface area × 0.07 kW/m² < $P_{loss}$, a cooler is required.
- **Piping velocity check** is mandatory for every line in the circuit — suction, return, and pressure.
- **Documentation deliverables** for any KTU planning answer: (i) component list, (ii) ISO 1219 circuit diagram, (iii) operating pressure, (iv) power rating, (v) reservoir size, (vi) filtration class, (vii) safety/relief valve setting.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Copying
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT526 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Copying

# 📘 MODULE 3 — COPYING IN INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Copying (Template Copying)** is a precision machining technique employed in hydraulic machine tools in which a **tracer stylus (probe)** follows the profile of a **master template**, and through a **hydraulic servo-control loop**, the **cutting tool** is made to reproduce an **identical geometric profile** on the workpiece with high dimensional accuracy.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework for **PERAT526 – Industrial Hydraulics**, copying is studied as a *closed-loop hydraulic servo application* where the principle of **negative feedback**, **hydraulic amplification**, and **flow control** converge to achieve contour-generation on cylindrical, conical, or contoured workpieces.

Mathematically, the copying operation enforces the constraint:

$$y_{tool}(x) \;=\; y_{template}(x) \quad \forall \; x \in \text{scan length}$$

subject to a bounded tracking error $\varepsilon(t) = y_{tool}(t) - y_{template}(t)$, which is minimized by the servo-hydraulic system.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> 🧠 **Think of it like tracing a picture through carbon paper.**
> - You (the *stylus*) move your finger along the outline of a drawing (the *template*).
> - Beneath the drawing lies a blank sheet (the *workpiece*) and a pencil (the *cutting tool*) connected to your finger by a hydraulic "muscle" (the *servo valve + cylinder*).
> - Whatever curve you trace on top appears **exactly** on the bottom sheet — automatically, continuously, and accurately.

In **industrial terms**, this is how a single "master" component can be used to mass-produce hundreds of identical contoured parts (e.g., turbine blade profiles, cam shafts, steering knuckles, shoe lasts) without re-programming or manual skill.

---

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Pressure | $p$ | **70 – 210 bar** |
| Tracing Accuracy | $\varepsilon$ | **± 0.01 mm to ± 0.05 mm** |
| Tracer Tip Radius | $r_t$ | **0.5 – 3 mm** |
| Servo Bandwidth | $f_b$ | **5 – 25 Hz** |
| Template Material | — | Hardened steel / phenolic resin |
| Hydraulic Fluid Viscosity | $\nu$ | **ISO VG 32 – VG 46** |

---

### 1.4 Classification of Copying Operations (KTU Module-3 Taxonomy)

> [!TIP]
> The KTU syllabus explicitly classifies copying under **Module 3 — Hydraulic Machine Tools & Circuits**. The two canonical classifications are:

1. **Longitudinal Copying** — Tool traverses *along the axis* of the workpiece (commonly used for turning tapers, contours on hydraulic copying lathes).
2. **Transverse Copying** — Tool moves *perpendicular* to the workpiece axis (used for facing and shoulder-generation).
3. **Combined / Two-Dimensional (2-D) Copying** — Both motions are simultaneously coordinated via the tracer; this is the classic **hydraulic copying lathe** configuration.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The copying system is fundamentally a **closed-loop position servo**. The logical chain is:

> **[Tracer Deflection] → [Error Signal] → [Servo Valve] → [Hydraulic Cylinder] → [Tool Movement] → [Error Minimization]**

### Step 1 — Kinematic Tracking Initiation
The tracer stylus is mechanically biased against the template with a small, constant **contact force** ($F_c \approx 2$–$10\ \text{N}$). As the longitudinal carriage feeds the toolpost toward the rotating workpiece, the stylus is dragged across the template's contoured surface.

### Step 2 — Error Generation
Any radial mismatch between the **template contour** and the **tool position** causes a measurable **deflection** $\delta$ of the tracer. This deflection is the system error:

$$\varepsilon(t) \;=\; y_{template}(t) \;-\; y_{tool}(t)$$

### Step 3 — Hydraulic Amplification
The tracer is mechanically linked to the **spool of a pilot-operated or direct-operated servo valve**. A deflection of even **0.01 mm** at the spool is sufficient to meter oil into one side of the hydraulic cylinder, generating thrust forces up to **kN**.

### Step 4 — Actuator Response
The double-acting hydraulic cylinder repositions the tool slide, reducing the error $\varepsilon \to 0$. The cylinder velocity is given by:

$$v_{cyl} \;=\; \frac{Q}{A_p} \;=\; C_d \cdot A_v \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta p}{\rho}}$$

### Step 5 — Feedback Closure
The new tool position is fed back (mechanically, as the stylus continues along the template), continuously driving $\varepsilon$ toward zero.

---

## 2.2 Why and How — The Engineering Rationale

> **Why a hydraulic servo?** Because the cutting force ($F_c$ up to several kN) must be supplied by a robust actuator, and hydraulic systems offer the highest **power-to-weight ratio** (≈ **5 kW/kg**) of any prime mover.

> **How is accuracy achieved?** By minimizing **steady-state error** through high-gain feedback and minimizing **damping ratio** issues through proper valve-port geometry.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in ≈ 60% of KTU Module-3 problems.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v_{cyl} = \dfrac{Q}{A_p}$ | Cylinder velocity from flow | m/s |
| 2 | $F_{thrust} = p \cdot A_p \cdot \eta_m$ | Available cutting thrust force | N |
| 3 | $Q = C_d \cdot A_v \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta p}{\rho}}$ | Flow through servo valve orifice | m³/s |
| 4 | $\varepsilon(t) = y_{template}(t) - y_{tool}(t)$ | Position tracking error | m |
| 5 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Natural frequency of tool-slide | rad/s |
| 6 | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{k \cdot m}}$ | Damping ratio | dimensionless |
| 7 | $K_{loop} = K_v \cdot K_a \cdot K_{fb}$ | Open-loop gain | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\varepsilon_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_{loop}}$ | Steady-state error | dimensionless |
| 9 | $P_{pump} = \dfrac{p \cdot Q}{\eta_{overall}}$ | Hydraulic pump input power | W |
| 10 | $N_{Re} = \dfrac{v \cdot D_h}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number for flow stability | dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> 🔍 **Note:** All table cells use `\vert` or `\mid` style where needed (e.g., $K_{loop} = K_v \cdot K_a \cdot K_{fb}$) to avoid breaking Markdown table syntax.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application of Copying |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Turbine blade root profiling on Ni-superalloys |
| **Automotive** | Steering knuckle, cam-shaft lobe contouring |
| **Defense** | Artillery shell ogive (nose) profiling |
| **Heavy Machinery** | Earth-mover bucket teeth, dozer blades |
| **Medical** | Orthopedic implant contoured surfaces |
| **Shipbuilding** | Propeller blade rough-machining |

---

## 2.5 Advantages vs. Disadvantages of Hydraulic Copying

| ✅ Advantages | ⚠️ Limitations |
|---|---|
| High repetition accuracy (± 0.02 mm) | Higher initial setup cost |
| Single template → 1000s of parts | Sensitive to thermal expansion |
| Operator skill-independent | Requires clean hydraulic fluid (ISO 4406) |
| Handles large cutting forces | Servo-valve maintenance intensive |
| Easily programmable via template change | Limited to 2.5-D contours (no undercuts) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation I — Cutting Thrust from Hydraulic Pressure

### Problem Setup
A hydraulic copying lathe uses a double-acting cylinder of piston area $A_p$ supplied at pressure $p$. The mechanical efficiency is $\eta_m$. Derive the available **cutting thrust force**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Write the ideal (no-loss) thrust on the piston rod:

$$F_{ideal} \;=\; p \cdot A_p$$

**Step 2:** Account for frictional and leakage losses via mechanical efficiency $\eta_m$:

$$F_{thrust} \;=\; p \cdot A_p \cdot \eta_m$$

**Step 3:** Substitute a numerical check — let $p = 140\ \text{bar} = 14 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Pa}$, $A_p = 30\ \text{cm}^2 = 30 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2$, $\eta_m = 0.92$:

$$F_{thrust} \;=\; 14 \times 10^{6} \cdot 30 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 0.92$$

**Step 4:** Simplify intermediate products:

$$F_{thrust} \;=\; (14 \times 30) \cdot 10^{2} \cdot 0.92 \;=\; 42000 \cdot 0.92$$

**Step 5:** Final answer:

$$F_{thrust} \;=\; 38640 \;\text{N} \;\approx\; \mathbf{38.6\ kN}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Cylinder Feed Velocity from Pump Flow

### Problem Setup
A fixed-displacement pump delivers flow $Q$ to a single-rod cylinder with piston area $A_p$ and rod-side area $A_r$. Derive the **extension velocity**.

**Step 1:** Apply the conservation-of-volume principle:

$$Q_{in} \;=\; A_p \cdot v_{extend}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $v_{extend}$:

$$v_{extend} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_{in}}{A_p}$$

**Step 3:** For **retraction**, the same pump flow must fill the smaller rod-side area, so:

$$v_{retract} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_{in}}{A_r}$$

**Step 4:** Since $A_r < A_p$, retraction is faster than extension (a key design insight):

$$\dfrac{v_{retract}}{v_{extend}} \;=\; \dfrac{A_p}{A_r} \;>\; 1$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Steady-State Tracking Error

For a **unity-feedback position servo**, the closed-loop transfer function is:

$$\dfrac{Y_{tool}(s)}{Y_{template}(s)} \;=\; \dfrac{K_{loop}}{1 + K_{loop}}$$

**Step 1:** For a step-input template command of magnitude $Y_0$, the steady-state output is:

$$Y_{tool}(\infty) \;=\; \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \dfrac{K_{loop}}{s(1 + K_{loop})} \cdot \dfrac{Y_0}{s}$$

**Step 2:** Simplify using the **Final Value Theorem**:

$$Y_{tool}(\infty) \;=\; \dfrac{K_{loop}}{1 + K_{loop}} \cdot Y_0$$

**Step 3:** The steady-state error is:

$$\varepsilon_{ss} \;=\; Y_0 - Y_{tool}(\infty) \;=\; Y_0 \left[1 - \dfrac{K_{loop}}{1 + K_{loop}}\right]$$

**Step 4:** Therefore:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{ss} \;=\; \dfrac{Y_0}{1 + K_{loop}}}$$

> [!TIP]
> 📈 **Engineering Insight:** Doubling the loop gain $K_{loop}$ halves the steady-state error — but excessive gain reduces phase margin and causes **hunting oscillation**. A typical KTU-acceptable value is $K_{loop} \approx 50$–$200$, giving $\varepsilon_{ss} \approx 1\%$–$2\%$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Servo Valve Flow Equation (Orifice Model)

**Step 1:** The general orifice flow equation is:

$$Q \;=\; C_d \cdot A_v \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta p}{\rho}}$$

**Step 2:** For a typical copying lathe servo valve, the manufacturer specifies:

- $C_d = 0.62$ (discharge coefficient)
- $A_v = 10\ \text{mm}^2 = 10 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2$
- $\Delta p = 70\ \text{bar} - 5\ \text{bar} = 65 \times 10^{5}\ \text{Pa}$ (valve pressure drop)
- $\rho = 870\ \text{kg/m}^3$ (hydraulic oil at 50 °C)

**Step 3:** Substitute:

$$Q \;=\; 0.62 \cdot 10 \times 10^{-6} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot 65 \times 10^{5}}{870}}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the inner radical:

$$\dfrac{2 \cdot 65 \times 10^{5}}{870} \;=\; \dfrac{1.30 \times 10^{6}}{870} \;\approx\; 1494.25\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

**Step 5:** Take the square root:

$$\sqrt{1494.25} \;\approx\; 38.66\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 6:** Final flow:

$$Q \;\approx\; 0.62 \cdot 10^{-5} \cdot 38.66 \;\approx\; \mathbf{2.397 \times 10^{-4}\ m^3/s} \;\approx\; \mathbf{23.97\ L/min}$$

---
