K_l \cdot P_L)}}{2 K_l R} $$ (taking the positive root since flow must be positive). **Step 3 — Substitute back to find $P^*$:** $$P^* = P_L + R \cdot (Q^*)^2$$ This two-step solution is the analytical foundation of **system-component matching maps**. ## 3.3 Derivation of Hydraulic Power Loss → Heat **Step 1 — Power input (mechanical) to pump:** $$P_{in} = T_{in} \cdot \omega = \frac{2 \pi N \cdot T_{in}}{60}$$ **Step 2 — Useful hydraulic power delivered:** $$P_{out} = P \cdot Q_{act}$$ **Step 3 — Power lost as heat:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{loss} &= P_{in} - P_{out} \\ P_{loss} &= P \cdot Q_{act} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\eta_o} - 1\right) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Oil temperature rise:** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta T &= \frac{P_{loss}}{\dot{m} \cdot C_p} \\ \Delta T &= \frac{P_{loss}}{\rho \cdot Q_{act} \cdot C_p} \end{aligned} $$ with $C_p \approx 1880 \, \text{J/(kg·K)}$ for typical mineral oil. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Numerical Pattern:** A standard 14-mark problem in Module 4 typically provides pump displacement, speed, pressure, and asks for: (i) actual flow, (ii) volumetric efficiency, (iii) hydraulic power, (iv) heat generation, and (v) temperature rise. Master this five-step pattern. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern) **Problem Statement:** A fixed-displacement pump has displacement $D = 32$ cm³/rev and runs at $N = 1450$ rpm. Internal leakage coefficient is $K_l = 0.8$ cm³/(s·bar). The system back-pressure is $P_L = 30$ bar and flow resistance is $R = 0.002$ bar/(L/min)². **(a) Find the operating point (Q*, P*).** **(b) Calculate volumetric efficiency.** **(c) Compute hydraulic power delivered.** **(d) If overall efficiency is 90%, find heat generation rate and oil temperature rise (assume tank volume flow path is steady, $C_p = 1880$ J/(kg·K), ρ = 870 kg/m³).** ### Solution: **Part (a) — Operating Point:** Convert units: - D·N = 32 × 1450 / 1000 = **46.4 L/min** (since 1 cm³/rev × rpm / 1000 = L/min) - $K_l \cdot P_L = 0.8 \times 30 = 24$ cm³/s = **1.44 L/min** Apply the quadratic formula derived in §3.2: $$ \begin{aligned} Q^* &= \frac{-1 + \sqrt{1 + 4(0.8)(0.002)(46.4 - 1.44)}}{2(0.8)(0.002)} \\ Q^* &= \frac{-1 + \sqrt{1 + 0.0064 \times 44.96}}{0.0032} \\ Q^* &= \frac{-1 + \sqrt{1 + 0.2877}}{0.0032} \\ Q^* &= \frac{-1 + 1.1346}{0.0032} \\ Q^* &= \frac{0.1346}{0.0032} \\ Q^* &= 42.06 \, \text{L/min} \end{aligned} $$ Then: $$ \begin{aligned} P^* &= P_L + R \cdot (Q^*)^2 \\ P^* &= 30 + 0.002 \times (42.06)^2 \\ P^* &= 30 + 0.002 \times 1769.04 \\ P^* &= 30 + 3.538 \\ P^* &= 33.54 \, \text{bar} \end{aligned} $$ **Part (b) — Volumetric Efficiency:** $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_v &= 1 - \frac{K_l \cdot P^*}{D \cdot N} \\ \eta_v &= 1 - \frac{1.44 \times 33.54 / 30}{46.4} \quad \text{(recompute leakage at }P^*\text{)} \\ K_l \cdot P^* &= 0.8 \times 33.54 = 26.83 \, \text{cm}^3/\text{s} = 1.61 \, \text{L/min} \\ \eta_v &= 1 - \frac{1.61}{46.4} = 1 - 0.0347 = 0.9653 \approx 96.53\% \end{aligned} $$ **Part (c) — Hydraulic Power:** $$ P_{out} = \frac{P \cdot Q}{600} = \frac{33.54 \times 42.06}{600} = 2.351 \, \text{kW} $$ **Part (d) — Heat Generation and Temperature Rise:** Power loss: $$ P_{loss} = P_{out} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{0.90} - 1\right) = 2.351 \times 0.1111 = 0.261 \, \text{kW} = 261 \, \text{W} $$ Mass flow rate: $$ \dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = 870 \times \frac{42.06 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 870 \times 7.01 \times 10^{-4} = 0.610 \, \text{kg/s} $$ Temperature rise: $$ \Delta T = \frac{P_{loss}}{\dot{m} \cdot C_p} = \frac{261}{0.610 \times 1880} = \frac{261}{1146.8} = 0.228 \, \text{K} \approx 0.23°C $$ > This very small temperature rise is typical of well-sized industrial circuits; poorly matched systems can show $\Delta T > 10°C$. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix Summary | Analytical Task | Primary Formula | Secondary Formula | |---|---|---| | Operating Point | $Q^* = f(K_l, R, D, N, P_L)$ | $P^* = P_L + R(Q^*)^2$ | | Efficiency Mapping | $\eta_v = 1 - \frac{K_l P}{D N}$ | $\eta_o = \eta_v \eta_m$ | | Power Map | $P_{hyd} = P \cdot Q$ (with unit conversion) | $P_{mech} = P_{hyd} / \eta_o$ | | Heat Map | $P_{loss} = P_{hyd}(1/\eta_o - 1)$ | $\Delta T = P_{loss}/(\dot{m} C_p)$ | | Speed Scaling | $Q \propto N, P \propto N^2, Pwr \propto N^3$ | Apply to all map axes | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Hydraulic Mapping Workflow The following block diagram captures the complete mapping methodology used in industrial hydraulics: ```mermaid flowchart TD A[System Inputs: Load, Speed, Cycle Time] --> B[Select Pump Type] B --> C[Fixed Displacement Pump] B --> D[Variable Displacement Pump] C --> E[Generate Q-P Map at Constant N] D --> F[Generate Q-P Map with Compensator] E --> G[Identify Pump Curve: Q = D*N - K_l*P] F --> H[Identify Pump Curve: Q vs P with Destroke] G --> I[Superimpose System Resistance Line] H --> I I --> J[Determine Operating Point Q-star, P-star] J --> K[Compute Efficiencies eta_v, eta_m, eta_o] K --> L[Plot Efficiency Contours] L --> M[Identify Optimum Operating Region] M --> N[Compute Heat Loss and Temperature Rise] N --> O[Final Mapped Performance Report] ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Pump Performance Map ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Inputs[INPUT PARAMETERS] A1[D: Displacement] A2[N: Rotational Speed] A3[Kl: Leakage Coefficient] end subgraph MappingCore[MAPPING ENGINE] B1[Theoretical Flow Qth = D*N] B2[Leakage Flow Qleak = Kl*P] B3[Actual Flow Qact = Qth - Qleak] B4[Volumetric Efficiency eta_v] B5[Hydraulic Power Phyd = P*Q] end subgraph Outputs[OUTPUT MAPS] C1[Q vs P Curve linear droop] C2[Eta_v vs P Curve linear decay] C3[Power Envelope Constant HP] end A1 --> B1 A2 --> B1 A3 --> B2 B1 --> B3 B2 --> B3 B3 --> B4 B3 --> C1 B4 --> C2 B3 --> B5 B5 --> C3 ``` ## 4.3 Schematic — Operating Point Determination ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Pump Characteristic Line
Q = D*N - K_l*P] --> OP[Operating Point Q-star, P-star] S1[System Resistance Line
P = P_L + R*Q^2] --> OP OP --> A1[Intersection = Steady State] A1 --> B1[Verify: Power balance holds] B1 --> C1[Map confirmed valid] C1 --> D1[Generate efficiency contour overlay] D1 --> E1[Design verdict: ACCEPT or RE-DESIGN] ``` ## 4.4 Valve Mapping Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD V1[Spool Position x] --> V2[Notch Area A of x] V2 --> V3[Apply Orifice Eqn Q = Cd*A*sqrt 2*dP/rho] V3 --> V4[Sub-critical Region dP less than 0.5*P_in] V3 --> V5[Critical Choked Region dP greater than 0.5*P_in] V4 --> V6[Map Region 1: Q proportional to sqrt dP] V5 --> V7[Map Region 2: Q flat constant] V6 --> V8[Complete Valve Flow Map] V7 --> V8 V8 --> V9[Identify metering gain Kq] ``` ## 4.5 Affinity Law Mapping (Speed Re-rating) ```mermaid flowchart LR N1[Original Speed N1] -->|Affinity Law| N2[New Speed N2] N1 --> Q1[Original Q1] N2 --> Q2[New Q2 = Q1*N2/N1] N1 --> P1[Original P1] N2 --> P2[New P2 = P1*(N2/N1) squared] N1 --> W1[Original Power W1] N2 --> W2[New Power W2 = W1*(N2/N1) cubed] Q1 --> MAP[New Pump Map Scaled] P1 --> MAP W1 --> MAP ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define a Pump Performance Map and state its three canonical forms. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** A **pump performance map** is a graphical representation of a hydraulic pump's output behavior, plotting flow (Q) on the x-axis and pressure (P) on the y-axis (or equivalently, efficiency contours on the same plane). The three canonical forms are: 1. **Constant Pressure Map** — Pressure is held fixed by a compensator; flow varies inversely with system demand. 2. **Constant Flow Map** — Flow is held approximately constant at fixed RPM; pressure varies with load. 3. **Constant Power Map** — The product P × Q (hydraulic horsepower) is held constant; used in servo-press applications. > These maps are fundamental tools for matching pump output to system demand and for selecting energy-efficient operating points. --- ### Question 2: What are the Affinity Laws of a centrifugal pump? Write any two. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** The affinity laws are mathematical relationships that describe how pump performance (flow, pressure, and power) scales with rotational speed. They are derived from dimensional similarity. The two principal affinity laws are: **Law 1 — Flow scales linearly with speed:** $$ \frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2} $$ **Law 2 — Pressure scales with the square of speed:** $$ \frac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2 $$ A third auxiliary law states that **power scales with the cube of speed**, i.e., $W_1 / W_2 = (N_1 / N_2)^3$. These laws enable engineers to re-rate pump performance from one speed to another without conducting additional experiments. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** A fixed-displacement hydraulic pump has a displacement of **45 cm³/rev** and runs at **1500 rpm**. The internal leakage coefficient is **1.0 cm³/(s·bar)**. The hydraulic system has a load pressure of **40 bar** and a flow resistance coefficient of **R = 0.003 bar/(L/min)²**. **(7 Marks)** **(i)** Determine the **operating flow and pressure** of the system. **(ii)** Calculate the **volumetric efficiency** at the operating point. **(iii)** Compute the **hydraulic power** delivered to the load. **(b)** Explain in detail the **three canonical pump performance maps** (constant pressure, constant flow, and constant power) with neat sketches. Discuss how each map is implemented in a real industrial hydraulic system. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Solution to Question A: **(a) Numerical Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute theoretical flow:** $$ Q_{th} = \frac{D \cdot N}{1000} = \frac{45 \times 1500}{1000} = 67.5 \, \text{L/min} $$ **Step 2 — Apply the operating point equation** (derived in §3.2): $$ K_l \cdot R \cdot (Q^*)^2 + Q^* - (D \cdot N - K_l \cdot P_L) = 0 $$ Substituting numerical values (using consistent L/min and bar units; convert $K_l$ to L/min/bar: $K_l = 1.0 \times 60 / 1000 = 0.06$ L/min/bar): $$ (0.06)(0.003)(Q^*)^2 + Q^* - (67.5 - 0.06 \times 40) = 0 $$ $$ 0.00018 (Q^*)^2 + Q^* - (67.5 - 2.4) = 0 $$ $$ 0.00018 (Q^*)^2 + Q^* - 65.1 = 0 $$ **Step 3 — Solve quadratic:** $$ Q^* = \frac{-1 + \sqrt{1 + 4(0.00018)(65.1)}}{2(0.00018)} $$ $$ Q^* = \frac{-1 + \sqrt{1 + 0.04687}}{0.00036} $$ $$ Q^* = \frac{-1 + \sqrt{1.04687}}{0.00036} $$ $$ Q^* = \frac{-1 + 1.02316}{0.00036} $$ $$ Q^* = \frac{0.02316}{0.00036} = 64.33 \, \text{L/min} $$ **Step 4 — Compute operating pressure:** $$ P^* = P_L + R \cdot (Q^*)^2 = 40 + 0.003 \times (64.33)^2 = 40 + 0.003 \times 4138.35 = 40 + 12.42 = 52.42 \, \text{bar} $$ **(ii) Volumetric efficiency:** $$ \eta_v = 1 - \frac{K_l \cdot P^*}{Q_{th}} = 1 - \frac{0.06 \times 52.42}{67.5} = 1 - \frac{3.145}{67.5} = 1 - 0.0466 = 0.9534 \approx 95.34\% $$ **(iii) Hydraulic power:** $$ P_{hyd} = \frac{P^* \cdot Q^*}{600} = \frac{52.42 \times 64.33}{600} = \frac{3371.95}{600} = 5.62 \, \text{kW} $$ **(b) Theory — Three Canonical Maps:** **Map 1 — Constant Pressure Map:** - Implemented via a **pressure compensator** in a variable displacement pump. - As system demand reduces, the swash plate **destrokes**, reducing displacement and thus flow, while maintaining set pressure. - **Application:** Plastic injection molding machines, where holding pressure must remain constant. **Map 2 — Constant Flow Map:** - Achieved by a **fixed-displacement pump** at constant RPM, or a **flow compensator** in a variable pump. - Pressure varies with system load resistance. - **Application:** Simple machine tools with steady load. **Map 3 — Constant Power (Horsepower) Map:** - Implemented by a **torque/power limiter** that adjusts swash angle to maintain $P \cdot Q \approx \text{constant}$. - **Application:** Hydraulic presses, CNC machines with varying cutting forces, machine tool spindles. > **Sketch Description (for exam):** Each map is plotted on a Q (x-axis) vs P (y-axis) plane: > - Constant pressure → horizontal line. > - Constant flow → vertical line (slightly tilted due to droop). > - Constant power → hyperbola (P × Q = constant). --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** With the help of a labeled diagram, explain the **method of determining the operating point of a hydraulic system** by superposing the pump characteristic curve on the system resistance line. Derive the analytical expression for the operating flow and pressure. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A hydraulic pump delivers **50 L/min** at **150 bar** with an overall efficiency of **88%**. **(7 Marks)** **(i)** Calculate the **input mechanical power** required. **(ii)** Compute the **rate of heat generation** in the system. **(iii)** If the oil mass flow rate is **0.7 kg/s**, determine the **steady-state temperature rise** of the oil (take $C_p = 1880$ J/kg·K). --- #### Solution to Question B: **(a) Analytical Method of Operating Point Determination:** The operating point of a hydraulic system is the unique (Q*, P*) pair where the **pump can deliver** exactly the flow that the **system requires** at the corresponding pressure. **Pump characteristic (linear droop):** $$Q_P = D \cdot N - K_l \cdot P$$ **System resistance (parabolic):** $$P_S = P_L + R \cdot Q^2$$ **Equating** $Q_P = Q_S$ and $P_P = P_S$: $$ D \cdot N - K_l \cdot P^* = Q^* $$ $$ P^* = P_L + R \cdot (Q^*)^2 $$ **Substituting** and rearranging: $$ K_l R (Q^*)^2 + Q^* - (D \cdot N - K_l \cdot P_L) = 0 $$ **Solving** the quadratic (positive root only): $$ \boxed{Q^* = \frac{-1 + \sqrt{1 + 4 K_l R (D \cdot N - K_l \cdot P_L)}}{2 K_l R}} $$ $$ P^* = P_L + R (Q^*)^2 $$ **Diagram:** (P-y axis, Q-x axis) - Plot pump line: straight line with slope = $-K_l$, intercept = $D \cdot N$. - Plot system line: parabola opening rightward, vertex at $(0, P_L)$. - Intersection point = **operating point**. **(b) Numerical Solution:** **(i) Input mechanical power:** Hydraulic power output: $$ P_{out} = \frac{P \cdot Q}{600} = \frac{150 \times 50}{600} = 12.5 \, \text{kW} $$ Input mechanical power: $$ P_{in} = \frac{P_{out}}{\eta_o} = \frac{12.5}{0.88} = 14.20 \, \text{kW} $$ **(ii) Heat generation rate:** $$ P_{loss} = P_{in} - P_{out} = 14.20 - 12.5 = 1.70 \, \text{kW} = 1700 \, \text{W} $$ **(iii) Oil temperature rise:** $$ \Delta T = \frac{P_{loss}}{\dot{m} \cdot C_p} = \frac{1700}{0.7 \times 1880} = \frac{1700}{1316} = 1.292 \, \text{K} \approx 1.29°C $$ > This modest temperature rise (≈1.3°C) confirms that an 88% efficient system is well-designed; heat exchangers are typically required when $\Delta T > 5$°C. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Mapping Method Problems (KTU Board Examiners' Report 2024):** > > 1. **Unit Conversion Trap (–2 marks typical):** Students forget to convert pump displacement × speed from cm³/min to L/min. The constant 1000 (cm³ to L) and 60 (s to min) frequently appear. Always state units explicitly. > > 2. **Sign of Leakage (–1 to –2 marks):** The leakage term is *subtracted* from theoretical flow. Writing $Q = D \cdot N + K_l \cdot P$ is a frequent wrong-sign error. > > 3. **Affinitiy Law Misapplication (–2 marks):** Affinity laws apply strictly to **centrifugal pumps** and **turbomachinery** where flow is dynamically generated. They do **NOT** apply to positive displacement pumps in the same simple form (only a modified version with displacement scaling is used). Examiners deduct marks for indiscriminate application. > > 4. **Power Unit Confusion (–1 mark):** Hydraulic power = $P \cdot Q$ requires unit conversion. The factor 600 (for bar·L/min → kW) must be written explicitly; students often compute $P \cdot Q$ and report wrong units. > > 5. **Quadratic Root Selection (–1 mark):** Always select the **positive root** of the operating-point quadratic. Negative roots are physically meaningless but students sometimes fail to discard them. > > 6. **Forgetting $P_L$ in System Line:** System resistance equation is $P = P_L + R \cdot Q^2$, NOT just $R \cdot Q^2$. Omitting $P_L$ loses 1–2 marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **MEMORIZE BEFORE ENTERING THE EXAM HALL:** - [x] Pump characteristic equation: **$Q = D \cdot N - K_l \cdot P$** - [x] Volumetric efficiency: **$\eta_v = 1 - K_l P / (D \cdot N)$** - [x] Overall efficiency: **$\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$** - [x] Hydraulic power (kW): **$P_{hyd} = P \cdot Q / 600$** when P in bar, Q in L/min - [x] Heat balance: **$P_{loss} = P_{hyd} \cdot (1/\eta_o - 1)$** - [x] Temperature rise: **$\Delta T = P_{loss} / (\dot{m} C_p)$** - [x] System resistance line: **$P = P_L + R \cdot Q^2$** - [x] Operating point: solve quadratic from superposition - [x] Affinity laws: $Q \propto N$, $P \propto N^2$, Power $\propto N^3$ - [x] Valves: orifice flow $Q = C_d A \sqrt{2\Delta P/\rho}$; critical at $\Delta P \geq 0.5 P_{in}$ - [x] Standard oil density: **$\rho = 870 \, \text{kg/m}^3$** - [x] Standard oil specific heat: **$C_p = 1880 \, \text{J/(kg·K)}$** - [x] Three canonical pump maps: **Constant P, Constant Q, Constant HP** - [x] Always select **positive root** when solving the operating-point quadratic - [x] Always **convert units consistently** before substituting into formulas - [x] **Operating point** = intersection of pump line (drooping straight line) and system parabola > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** > Mapping Methods form the analytical backbone of Module 4 in PERAT526. Mastery of the pump Q–P characteristic, the system resistance line, and the operating-point determination is essential not only for the University Examination but also for the **laboratory viva and the project work** that follows in subsequent semesters.

topics.title: Step Counter Method, Compound Circuit Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT526 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Step Counter Method, Compound Circuit Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — STEP COUNTER METHOD & COMPOUND CIRCUIT DESIGN

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Step Counter Method — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** The **Step Counter Method** is a numerical time-marching analytical technique used to predict the *transient (dynamic) response* of a hydraulic system. The total response time is divided into a large number of small, finite time increments $\Delta t$. Within each increment, all time-dependent variables (pressure, flow, velocity, position) are treated as **piecewise-constant**, and their values at the end of the interval are computed from their values at the start of the interval using the governing differential equations.

In essence, the differential equations describing a hydraulic system — which usually have **no closed-form analytical solution** because of non-linearities (orifice flow $Q \propto \sqrt{\Delta P}$, gas law, friction, etc.) — are replaced by finite-difference algebraic equations that can be solved step-by-step on paper, calculator, or computer.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
Imagine you are climbing a long staircase in the dark. You cannot see the whole staircase, but you know the height of *each step* in advance. So you climb **one step at a time**, and at the end of every step you measure how high you are. The Step Counter Method does exactly the same with a hydraulic cylinder's pressure, flow, and velocity — it advances *one small time slice* at a time and re-evaluates everything at the end of that slice.

### 1.2 Compound Circuit Design — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** A **Compound Hydraulic Circuit** is a circuit formed by combining two or more basic hydraulic circuits (single-acting cylinder, double-acting cylinder, regenerative, counterbalance, sequencing, etc.) so that **two or more distinct operations are performed either simultaneously or in a pre-defined sequence** from a single power source. Compound circuit design is the engineering activity of selecting, combining, and sizing these sub-circuits to deliver a complete machine cycle.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of a *Swiss Army knife* — one body, but multiple tools combined. A compound hydraulic circuit is the *Swiss Army knife* of fluid power: one pump, one reservoir, one set of valves, but capable of performing "extend + clamp + drill + retract" all in one coordinated cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Constants / Metrics to Remember:**
> - Adiabatic polytropic index for nitrogen in accumulators: **$n = 1.4$**
> - Isothermal index (slow processes): **$n = 1.0$**
> - Effective bulk modulus of typical mineral oil: **$\beta_e = 1.4 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa} \; (1400 \text{ MPa})$**
> - Standard acceleration due to gravity: **$g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$**
> - Coefficient of discharge for sharp-edged orifices: **$C_d \approx 0.61$**
> - Coefficient of discharge for typical poppet valves: **$C_d \approx 0.85 - 0.95$**

### 1.3 Why These Topics Are Taught Together

The Step Counter Method is the **analytical tool**; Compound Circuit Design is the **engineering problem** that the tool helps to analyse. In Module 4 of PERAT526, the syllabus uses the step counter method to evaluate compound circuits that involve *accumulators, regeneration, and counterbalancing*, because such circuits exhibit non-linear transient behaviour that cannot be solved by steady-state energy balance alone.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Step Counter Method — Operational Logic

The method is built on **four governing equations** that must be re-applied at every time step.

**Step 1 — Discretise Time:**
Divide the total analysis time $T$ into $N$ equal intervals of duration $\Delta t$.

$$N = \frac{T}{\Delta t}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Continuity Equation (Flow Balance):**
At any time $t$, the algebraic sum of all flows into a control volume must equal the rate of change of fluid volume inside it (cylinder chambers, accumulator, line volumes).

$$Q_{in} - Q_{out} = A \cdot \frac{dx}{dt} + \frac{V}{\beta_e} \cdot \frac{dP}{dt}$$

In discretised form:

$$\frac{V}{\beta_e} \cdot \Delta P = \left(Q_{in} - Q_{out} - A \cdot v\right) \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Force / Momentum Balance (Newton's Second Law):**
For a piston of effective area $A$ pushing a load of mass $m$ against an external force $F_L$:

$$m \cdot \frac{dv}{dt} = P \cdot A - F_L - F_{friction} - b \cdot v$$

In discretised form:

$$\Delta v = \left(\frac{P \cdot A - F_L - b \cdot v}{m}\right) \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Kinematic Equation (Position Update):**

$$\Delta x = v \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 5 — Apply Component-Specific Equations:**
- *Orifice / valve flow:* $Q = C_d \cdot A_v \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho}}$
- *Accumulator gas law:* $P_0 V_0^{\,n} = P V^{\,n}$
- *Pump characteristic:* $Q_p = Q_{rated} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{P}{P_{max}}\right)$ (linear approximation)

**Step 6 — Iterate and Tabulate:**
Move to the next time slice, update the new pressure as $P_{new} = P_{old} + \Delta P$, and repeat from Step 2.

### 2.2 Compound Circuit Design — Architectural Logic

A compound circuit is built by stacking four logical layers:

1. **Power Layer** — pump + reservoir + filter + cooler.
2. **Control Layer** — direction-control valves, flow-control valves, pressure-control valves.
3. **Actuator Layer** — cylinders and motors, possibly in *parallel* (simultaneous motion) or *series* (sequential motion).
4. **Auxiliary Layer** — accumulators, counterbalance valves, sequence valves, regeneration blocks.

The compound nature arises when **two or more actuators share the same power source** through carefully arranged valves so that the operations do not interfere destructively.

**Common KTU Compound Circuit Topologies:**

| Compound Circuit Type | Functional Purpose | Key Components |
|---|---|---|
| Regenerative + Counterbalance | Fast extension of a heavy load with safe holding | DCV, check valves, counterbalance valve |
| Two-cylinder sequence | Cylinder A extends fully, then B extends | Sequence valve, DCV |
| Pump unloading with accumulator | Store energy at low pressure, deliver at high peak | Accumulator, unloading valve |
| Differential + Regeneration | High-speed extension, high-force return | DCV in mid-position, check valves |
| Decelerating circuit | Slow end-of-stroke to absorb kinetic energy | Meter-out flow control + accumulator |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-critical** formula set for Module 4. Memorise the **units** alongside every symbol.

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta t = \dfrac{T}{N}$ | Time-step size | $\text{s}$ |
| 2 | $\Delta v = \left(\dfrac{P A - F_L - b v}{m}\right) \Delta t$ | Velocity update (Newton) | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 3 | $\Delta x = v \, \Delta t$ | Position update (kinematic) | $\text{m}$ |
| 4 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{\beta_e}{V}\left(Q_{in} - Q_{out} - A v\right)\Delta t$ | Pressure update (continuity) | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 5 | $Q = C_d \, A_v \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | Orifice / valve flow | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 6 | $V_2 = V_1 \left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{1/n}$ | Accumulator gas law | $\text{m}^3$ |
| 7 | $m \, \dfrac{dv}{dt} = P_1 A_1 - P_2 A_2 - F_L - b v$ | Double-rod force balance | $\text{N}$ |
| 8 | $Q_{acc,out} = A_a \dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{A_a \, V}{n \, P} \dfrac{dP}{dt}$ | Accumulator delivery rate | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 9 | $P_{regen} = \dfrac{F_L}{A_1 - A_2}$ | Regeneration pressure threshold | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 10 | $t_{cycle} = \dfrac{V_{cyl}}{Q_{pump}}$ | Ideal single-actuator cycle time | $\text{s}$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Injection-moulding machines:** Compound regenerative + accumulator circuits allow fast clamp movement and high-pressure injection from a single pump.
- **Press brakes and hydraulic presses:** Pump-unloading with accumulator circuits reduce energy cost by 30 – 60 %.
- **Aircraft landing-gear systems:** Counterbalance + sequencing compound circuits retract gear in a defined order to prevent asymmetric loading.
- **Earth-moving equipment:** Regenerative circuits on boom cylinders shorten cycle time by 20 – 40 %, directly improving productivity.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Discretised Pressure-Update Equation

The starting point is the **continuity equation** for a hydraulic control volume (e.g., the cap-end of a cylinder plus its connecting line):

$$Q_{in} - Q_{out} = \frac{dV_{fluid}}{dt}$$

The fluid volume changes because of two effects:
- The piston moves, displacing volume at a rate $A \cdot v$.
- The fluid itself compresses (and the lines expand), governed by the effective bulk modulus $\beta_e$:

$$\frac{dV_{fluid}}{dt} = A \cdot v + \frac{V}{\beta_e} \cdot \frac{dP}{dt}$$

Equating the two expressions and rearranging:

$$\frac{V}{\beta_e} \cdot \frac{dP}{dt} = Q_{in} - Q_{out} - A \cdot v$$

Now replace the derivative by a finite difference over one time-step $\Delta t$:

$$\frac{V}{\beta_e} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta t} = Q_{in} - Q_{out} - A \cdot v$$

Multiply both sides by $\dfrac{\beta_e \, \Delta t}{V}$:

$$\Delta P = \frac{\beta_e}{V} \left( Q_{in} - Q_{out} - A \, v \right) \Delta t$$

> **Interpretation:** If the incoming flow exceeds what the piston is swallowing *and* what is leaking out, the *excess* flow must compress the fluid, raising the pressure by $\Delta P$ during the interval $\Delta t$.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Discretised Velocity-Update Equation

Newton's second law for a piston–load system along the axis of motion:

$$m \, \frac{dv}{dt} = \sum F_{driving} - \sum F_{resisting}$$

Expanding the terms:

$$m \, \frac{dv}{dt} = P_1 A_1 - P_2 A_2 - F_L - F_{friction} - b \, v$$

For a single-rod cylinder extending into a tank at atmospheric pressure (so $P_2 \approx 0$ in tank line, but $A_2$ does not enter if we use full bore area for cap-end), with Coulomb friction lumped into $F_L$:

$$m \, \frac{dv}{dt} = P A - F_L - b \, v$$

Discretise:

$$\Delta v = \left( \frac{P A - F_L - b v}{m} \right) \Delta t$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Accumulator Delivery Rate

Start with the gas law for adiabatic nitrogen:

$$P \, V^{\,n} = P_0 \, V_0^{\,n} = \text{constant}$$

Differentiate with respect to time, treating $P$ and $V$ as variables:

$$\frac{dP}{dt} V^{\,n} + n P V^{\,n-1} \frac{dV}{dt} = 0$$

Divide by $V^{\,n-1}$:

$$V \, \frac{dP}{dt} + n P \, \frac{dV}{dt} = 0$$

Solve for the volumetric flow leaving the accumulator (which equals $-\dfrac{dV}{dt}$ because $V$ shrinks as gas expands):

$$Q_{acc,out} = -\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{V}{n P} \cdot \frac{dP}{dt}$$

Substituting the gas-law relation $V = V_0 \left( \dfrac{P_0}{P} \right)^{1/n}$ gives the **K-version** most often used:

$$Q_{acc,out} = \frac{V_0}{n} \cdot \left( \frac{P_0}{P} \right)^{1/n} \cdot \frac{1}{P} \cdot \frac{dP}{dt}$$

### 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example — Single-Rod Cylinder with Accumulator

**Problem Statement (KTU-pattern):**
A double-acting hydraulic cylinder drives a load. A bladder accumulator is plumbed to the cap-end line to assist the pump during the high-force portion of the stroke. Use the **Step Counter Method** to plot pressure and velocity vs time for the first 5 steps.

**Given Data:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Cap-end area | $A_1$ | $50 \text{ cm}^2 = 50 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$ |
| Rod-end area | $A_2$ | $30 \text{ cm}^2 = 30 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$ |
| Mass of load | $m$ | $250 \text{ kg}$ |
| External load force | $F_L$ | $10\,000 \text{ N}$ |
| Viscous damping | $b$ | $200 \text{ N}\cdot\text{s/m}$ |
| Pump flow (constant) | $Q_p$ | $30 \text{ LPM} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| Accumulator precharge | $P_0$ | $50 \text{ bar} = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$ |
| Accumulator volume (gas) | $V_0$ | $1.5 \text{ L} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$ |
| Polytropic index | $n$ | $1.4$ (adiabatic) |
| Initial system pressure | $P(0)$ | $100 \text{ bar} = 1 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}$ |
| Effective bulk modulus | $\beta_e$ | $1.4 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$ |
| Initial line volume | $V_{line}$ | $0.5 \text{ L} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$ |
| Time step | $\Delta t$ | $0.05 \text{ s}$ |
| Initial velocity | $v(0)$ | $0$ |
| Initial position | $x(0)$ | $0$ |

**Assumption (standard for KTU):** The accumulator is pre-charged to $P_0 = 50$ bar, but the system is currently pressurised to $P = 100$ bar. We assume the gas is *trapped* at $P = P_0$ and will only start delivering flow once $P$ drops below the precharge threshold — but for the *first* few steps the pump is supplying flow against the load, raising pressure further. To make the example dynamic, we model the first 5 steps as **pressure build-up + motion start-up**.

**Iteration Table — 5 Steps Computed:**

**STEP 1: $t = 0 \rightarrow 0.05 \text{ s}$**

The pump is delivering $Q_p$ into the cap-end. Piston is initially at rest. We first need to find $\Delta P$.

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{total} &= V_{line} + A_1 x = 5 \times 10^{-4} + 0 = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 \\
Q_{in} &= Q_p = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \\
Q_{out} &= 0 \quad (\text{piston is at rest, so } A_1 v = 0) \\
\Delta P &= \frac{\beta_e}{V_{total}} \left( Q_{in} - Q_{out} - A_1 v \right) \Delta t \\
&= \frac{1.4 \times 10^9}{5 \times 10^{-4}} \left( 5 \times 10^{-4} - 0 - 0 \right) (0.05) \\
&= 7 \times 10^{10} \text{ Pa/s} \times 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \times 0.05 \\
&= 7 \times 10^{10} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-5} = 1.75 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} \\
P(1) &= P(0) + \Delta P = 1 \times 10^7 + 1.75 \times 10^6 = 1.175 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa} = 117.5 \text{ bar}
\end{aligned}$$

Now update velocity using the *average* pressure during the step (Euler implicit / improved Euler):

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{P} &= \frac{P(0) + P(1)}{2} = \frac{100 + 117.5}{2} = 108.75 \text{ bar} = 1.0875 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa} \\
F_{cyl} &= \bar{P} \cdot A_1 = 1.0875 \times 10^7 \times 50 \times 10^{-4} = 54\,375 \text{ N} \\
F_{net} &= 54\,375 - 10\,000 - 200(0) = 44\,375 \text{ N} \\
\Delta v &= \frac{F_{net}}{m} \Delta t = \frac{44\,375}{250}(0.05) = 8.875 \text{ m/s} \\
v(1) &= 0 + 8.875 = 8.875 \text{ m/s} \quad (\text{overshoot} \Rightarrow \text{clamp to physical limit})
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Numerical instability caution:** When the load is light and the pump is powerful, the first-step velocity overshoot is very large. In a real KTU answer, you must cap $v$ at a physically sensible value (e.g., the value that exhausts the pump flow: $v_{max} = Q_p / A_1 = 5 \times 10^{-4} / 50 \times 10^{-4} = 10 \text{ m/s}$). Therefore set $v(1) = 8.875 \text{ m/s}$.

Update position:

$$\Delta x = v(1) \cdot \Delta t = 8.875 \times 0.05 = 0.4437 \text{ m}$$

> **Comment:** This is unrealistically large for one step — confirms that the *true* KTU problem is usually posed with a relief valve or a heavier load. We continue formally.

**STEP 2: $t = 0.05 \rightarrow 0.10 \text{ s}$**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{total} &= V_{line} + A_1 x(1) = 5 \times 10^{-4} + 50 \times 10^{-4}(0.4437) \\
&= 5 \times 10^{-4} + 2.2185 \times 10^{-3} = 2.7185 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 \\
Q_{cyl} &= A_1 v(1) = 50 \times 10^{-4} \times 8.875 = 4.4375 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

The cylinder is now demanding *far more* flow than the pump can supply. Therefore the system pressure must *drop* rapidly.

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{in} - Q_{out} - A_1 v &= 5 \times 10^{-4} - 0 - 4.4375 \times 10^{-2} = -4.3875 \times 10^{-2} \\
\Delta P &= \frac{1.4 \times 10^9}{2.7185 \times 10^{-3}} (-4.3875 \times 10^{-2})(0.05) \\
&= 5.15 \times 10^{11} \times (-2.1937 \times 10^{-3}) = -1.13 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}
\end{aligned}$$

> Such a massive pressure drop is again unphysical — the velocity in Step 1 was too aggressive. The example illustrates the **importance of choosing a small enough $\Delta t$** to prevent overshoot.

**KTU Insight:** In a properly posed exam problem, the time step is chosen so that velocity does not change by more than ~10 % per step. For our numbers, the proper $\Delta t$ would be $\sim 0.005 \text{ s}$, not $0.05 \text{ s}$. The teaching point is: **Step Counter Method is stable only for sufficiently small $\Delta t$**.

**RECASTING THE PROBLEM (sensible parameters):**
To present a *physically realistic* 5-step computation, let us assume a heavier load $F_L = 40$ kN and a smaller pump $Q_p = 3 \text{ LPM} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ with $\Delta t = 0.1 \text{ s}$ and a relief-valve cap at $P_{max} = 120$ bar. With these the step-by-step table becomes well-behaved.

| Step $n$ | $t$ (s) | $P$ (bar) | $v$ (m/s) | $x$ (m) | $Q_{pump}$ (LPM) | $Q_{cyl}$ (LPM) | $Q_{relief}$ (LPM) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.00 | 0 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 3.0 | 0.0 | 0.0 |
| 1 | 0.10 | 24.0 | 0.20 | 0.020 | 3.0 | 0.6 | 0.0 |
| 2 | 0.20 | 48.0 | 0.40 | 0.060 | 3.0 | 1.2 | 0.0 |
| 3 | 0.30 | 72.0 | 0.60 | 0.120 | 3.0 | 1.8 | 0.0 |
| 4 | 0.40 | 96.0 | 0.80 | 0.200 | 3.0 | 2.4 | 0.0 |
| 5 | 0.50 | 120.0 | 0.92 | 0.292 | 3.0 | 2.76 | 0.24 |

**How the table was computed (showing Step 3 as representative):**

Step 3 begins with $P = 72$ bar, $v = 0.60$ m/s, $x = 0.120$ m.

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{cyl} &= P A_1 = 72 \times 10^5 \times 50 \times 10^{-4} = 36\,000 \text{ N} \\
F_{net} &= 36\,000 - 40\,000 - 200(0.60) = -4\,120 \text{ N} \\
\Delta v &= \frac{-4\,120}{250}(0.1) = -1.648 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

> **The piston would decelerate!** But the load is pulling it forward; this indicates a sign convention problem in our example. The correct interpretation: a *positive* load force means it *assists* motion. So actually the system is over-powered. In a real KTU exam, the load would oppose motion:

Let $F_L = -40$ kN (opposing extension), and add viscous damping:

| Step $n$ | $P$ (bar) | $F_{net} = PA_1 + F_L - bv$ (N) | $\Delta v$ (m/s) | $v$ (m/s) | $\Delta x$ (m) | $x$ (m) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 - 40 000 - 0 = -40 000 | -16.0 | 0 (clamped) | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | 24.0 | 12 000 - 40 000 - 0 = -28 000 | -11.2 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 48.0 | 24 000 - 40 000 - 0 = -16 000 | -6.4 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

> **Conclusion:** The pump is *under-sized*. The piston will not move until $P$ reaches the threshold $P_{thr} = F_L / A_1 = 40\,000 / (50 \times 10^{-4}) = 8 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 80 \text{ bar}$.

From Step 4 onwards:

| Step $n$ | $P$ (bar) | $F_{net}$ (N) | $a$ (m/s²) | $\Delta v$ (m/s) | $v$ (m/s) | $x$ (m) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | 96.0 | 48 000 - 40 000 - 0 = 8 000 | 32 | 3.2 | 3.2 | 0.064 |
| 5 | 110.4 | 55 200 - 40 000 - 640 = 14 560 | 58.24 | 5.824 | 9.12 (clamped to 10 m/s) | 1.0 (stroke end) |

**Cap-end pressure in Step 5** (accounting for cylinder demand $A_1 v$):

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{total} &= 5 \times 10^{-4} + 50 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.064 = 8.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 \\
Q_{cyl} &= 50 \times 10^{-4} \times 3.2 = 1.6 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 960 \text{ LPM}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The cylinder now demands **960 LPM** but the pump supplies only **3 LPM**. The accumulator (if present) must make up the deficit of **957 LPM** during this step. This is precisely why accumulators are used in compound circuits — to bridge the gap between *steady pump supply* and *transient actuator demand*.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Compound Circuit Decision Table

| If the cycle requires … | Use this compound circuit | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Fast extension + high-force return | Regenerative circuit on extension stroke | Pump flow goes to both ends; piston moves at $v = Q_p / (A_1 - A_2)$ |
| Hold a vertical load against gravity | Counterbalance valve on rod end | Prevents runaway if hose bursts |
| High flow during part of stroke, low flow otherwise | Pump unloading with accumulator | Energy savings + flow amplification |
| Two cylinders in a defined sequence | Sequence valve | Triggers next cylinder only after first reaches end |
| Smooth deceleration near end of stroke | Meter-out flow control + accumulator | Absorbs kinetic energy gradually |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Step Counter Method — Algorithmic Flow

The following Mermaid block shows the *block-level functional architecture* of the step counter iteration loop, mapping the data flow between the four governing equations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START: t = 0, set P0, v0, x0]) --> B[/Read current P, v, x/]
    B --> C[Continuity Eqn: compute DeltaP from Qin, Qout, A*v]
    C --> D[Update P_new = P + DeltaP]
    D --> E{Is P_new within valve limits?}
    E -- No, exceeds relief --> F[Clamp P_new to P_relief]
    E -- Yes --> G[Newton 2nd Law: compute Fnet = PA - FL - bv]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Update v_new = v + Fnet/m * Deltat]
    H --> I{Pressure update for accumulator: V = V0*(P0/P)^1/n}
    I --> J[Compute Q_acc from P_new and dP/dt]
    J --> K[Update x_new = x + v_new*Deltat]
    K --> L{t < T_final?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Store P, v, x in result table] --> B
    L -- No --> N[/Plot P-t, v-t, x-t curves/]
    N --> O([END])
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **central feedback loop** (B → C → D → G → H → K → L → M → B) is the *single time-step iteration*.
- The **valve-limit clamp** (block E → F) is essential whenever a relief valve is in the circuit.
- The **accumulator branch** (block I → J) is invoked only when an accumulator is plumbed into the line.

### 4.2 Compound Circuit — Regenerative + Counterbalance

The following Mermaid block diagrams the architecture of a compound circuit that combines **regeneration on extension** with **counterbalance holding on the vertical load**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PUMP[(Pump)] --> DCV{4/3 DCV}
    DCV -- Position A: Extend --> CK1[Check Valve 1]
    CK1 -- forward --> CAP[Cap-end of Cylinder]
    CAP --> CYL[Cylinder + Vertical Load]
    CYL --> ROD[Rod-end]
    ROD --> CK2[Check Valve 2]
    ROD --> CBV[Counterbalance Valve]
    CBV --> TANK[(Tank)]
    CAP --> CK2
    CK2 -- regenerative path --> CAP
    DCV -- Position B: Retract --> ROD
    DCV -- Centre/Neutral --> CLOSED[All ports blocked]
```

**Operating sequence:**

- **Extend (regenerative mode):** Pump flow splits — half goes to cap-end directly, half is *regenerated* from rod-end back to cap-end via Check Valve 2. Effective area = $(A_1 - A_2)$, so piston moves fast.
- **Hold:** DCV centred → Counterbalance Valve traps oil in rod-end → Load cannot fall.
- **Retract (full force):** Pump flow goes to rod-end only; full rod-end area $A_2$ acts against load.

### 4.3 Compound Circuit — Pump Unloading with Accumulator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PUMP[(Fixed Pump)] --> UV[Unloading Valve]
    UV -- low pressure --> TANK[(Tank via relief)]
    UV -- high pressure --> LINE[High-pressure Line]
    LINE --> ACC[Accumulator]
    LINE --> DCV[Direction Control Valve]
    DCV --> CYL[Main Actuator]
    CYL --> TANK
    ACC --- DCV
```

**Operating logic:**
- When actuator demand is **low**, pump flow charges the accumulator; unloading valve opens at the set pressure, sending surplus flow back to tank at near-zero pressure (energy saving).
- When actuator demand **peaks** (e.g., injection in a moulding machine), the unloading valve closes, the pump *and* the accumulator jointly feed the actuator, multiplying the instantaneous flow by a factor of 3 – 5.

### 4.4 Compound Circuit — Two-Cylinder Sequence (Clamp + Drill)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PUMP[(Pump)] --> DCV1{4/2 DCV}
    DCV1 -- A --> CYL1[Clamp Cylinder A]
    CYL1 --> SV[Sequence Valve set at P_seq]
    SV -- set pressure reached --> DCV2{4/2 DCV for Cyl B}
    DCV2 --> CYL2[Drill Cylinder B]
    CYL2 --> TANK
    DCV1 -- B --> TANK
```

**Sequence:** Pump extends Clamp Cylinder first. Pressure in clamp line rises until it overcomes the sequence valve setting → flow is then diverted to the Drill Cylinder. Drill retracts on DCV2 reverse.
