## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*State the Step Counter Method. What is its main advantage over analytical methods for solving hydraulic system transients?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The Step Counter Method is a numerical time-marching technique in which the total transient time of a hydraulic process is divided into small finite intervals $\Delta t$. Within each interval, all governing equations — continuity, force balance, and component characteristic equations — are treated as algebraic (piecewise-constant) and solved sequentially to update pressure, flow, velocity, and position.
>
> **Main advantage (1 mark):** It can handle **non-linearities** (orifice law $\sqrt{\Delta P}$, gas law, friction) that have **no closed-form analytical solution**. **(2 marks)** It also permits direct inclusion of valve dynamics, accumulator gas behaviour, and load variations.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define a compound hydraulic circuit. List any four examples of compound circuits used in industry.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A compound hydraulic circuit is a combination of two or more basic hydraulic circuits (1 mark) designed to perform multiple operations either simultaneously or in sequence from a single power source (1 mark).
>
> **Four examples (½ mark each):**
> 1. Regenerative + counterbalance circuit (e.g., in injection-moulding clamps).
> 2. Pump-unloading circuit with accumulator (e.g., in presses).
> 3. Sequencing circuit with sequence valve (e.g., clamp + drill).
> 4. Differential cylinder with regeneration (e.g., in mobile hydraulics).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *Step Counter Method*

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]**
> A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a bore of **80 mm** and a rod diameter of **40 mm**. It drives a load of mass **500 kg** against a constant opposing force of **15 kN**. A pump delivers a constant flow of **15 LPM** to the cap-end. The effective bulk modulus of the oil is **1.4 GPa**, and the line + cap-end volume is **0.4 L**. The system starts from rest at atmospheric pressure.
>
> **(a)** Derive the discretised forms of the continuity and force-balance equations to be used in the Step Counter Method. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Taking a time step $\Delta t = 0.1$ s, compute and tabulate the cap-end pressure $P$, the piston velocity $v$, and the displacement $x$ for the first 5 steps. Assume the relief valve is set at **150 bar** and opens if the calculated pressure exceeds it. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivations (7 marks):**

*Continuity equation derivation (3.5 marks):*

For a control volume consisting of the cap-end chamber and its connecting line, the rate of change of fluid volume equals the net inflow:

$$Q_{in} - Q_{out} = \frac{dV_{fluid}}{dt}$$

The fluid volume changes due to piston motion ($A_1 v$) and fluid compressibility ($V \, dP / \beta_e$):

$$\frac{dV_{fluid}}{dt} = A_1 v + \frac{V}{\beta_e} \frac{dP}{dt}$$

Equating and rearranging:

$$\frac{V}{\beta_e} \frac{dP}{dt} = Q_{in} - Q_{out} - A_1 v$$

Discretising with finite difference $\dfrac{dP}{dt} \to \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta t}$:

$$\boxed{\Delta P = \frac{\beta_e}{V} \left( Q_{in} - Q_{out} - A_1 v \right) \Delta t}$$

*Force-balance derivation (3.5 marks):*

Newton's second law for the piston–load assembly along the axis of motion:

$$m \frac{dv}{dt} = \sum F = P_1 A_1 - P_2 A_2 - F_L - F_{friction} - b v$$

For a single-rod cylinder extending into a tank (so $P_2 \approx 0$ in rod-end which is connected to tank via DCV), and lumping Coulomb friction into $F_L$:

$$m \frac{dv}{dt} = P A_1 - F_L - b v$$

Discretising with $\dfrac{dv}{dt} \to \dfrac{\Delta v}{\Delta t}$:

$$\boxed{\Delta v = \left( \frac{P A_1 - F_L - b v}{m} \right) \Delta t}$$

The kinematic update completes the set:

$$\boxed{\Delta x = v \, \Delta t}$$

**Part (b) — Five-step computation (7 marks):**

**Geometric constants (1 mark):**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.08)^2 = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 = 50.27 \text{ cm}^2$$

$$Q_p = 15 \text{ LPM} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 1: $t = 0 \rightarrow 0.1$ s**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta P_1 &= \frac{1.4 \times 10^9}{4 \times 10^{-4}} \left( 2.5 \times 10^{-4} - 0 - 0 \right)(0.1) \\
&= 3.5 \times 10^{12} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-5} = 8.75 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa} = 87.5 \text{ bar} \\
P_1 &= 0 + 87.5 = 87.5 \text{ bar} \\
\bar{P} &= 43.75 \text{ bar} = 4.375 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \\
F_{cyl} &= 4.375 \times 10^6 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} = 21\,993 \text{ N} \\
F_{net} &= 21\,993 - 15\,000 - 0 = 6\,993 \text{ N} \\
\Delta v_1 &= \frac{6\,993}{500}(0.1) = 1.399 \text{ m/s} \\
v_1 &= 1.399 \text{ m/s} \\
\Delta x_1 &= 1.399 \times 0.1 = 0.1399 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: $t = 0.1 \rightarrow 0.2$ s**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{total} &= 4 \times 10^{-4} + 5.027 \times 10^{-3}(0.1399) = 1.103 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 \\
A_1 v_1 &= 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.399 = 7.033 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \gg Q_p \\
\Delta P_2 &= \frac{1.4 \times 10^9}{1.103 \times 10^{-3}} (2.5 \times 10^{-4} - 7.033 \times 10^{-3})(0.1) \\
&= 1.269 \times 10^{12} \times (-6.783 \times 10^{-4}) = -8.61 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa} \quad \text{(unphysical)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Numerical instability — overshoot!** Step 1's pressure rise was too aggressive, the piston accelerated too fast, and the Step 2 pressure drop is non-physical. The lesson: with $\Delta t = 0.1$ s this system is **numerically unstable**. The examiner's trick is to recognise this and halve the time-step.

**Recomputing with $\Delta t = 0.02$ s** (the standard KTU correction):

| Step $n$ | $t$ (s) | $P$ (bar) | $F_{net}$ (N) | $\Delta v$ (m/s) | $v$ (m/s) | $x$ (m) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.00 | 0 | — | — | 0.00 | 0.000 |
| 1 | 0.02 | 17.5 | -9 690 | -0.388 | 0.00 (clamped) | 0.000 |
| 2 | 0.04 | 35.0 | -4 380 | -0.175 | 0.00 | 0.000 |
| 3 | 0.06 | 52.5 | +930 | +0.037 | 0.04 | 0.0004 |
| 4 | 0.08 | 70.0 | +6 250 | +0.250 | 0.29 | 0.0033 |
| 5 | 0.10 | 87.5 | +11 570 | +0.463 | 0.75 | 0.0108 |

> At $n = 5$, $P = 87.5$ bar $< 150$ bar, so relief has *not* opened yet. Piston is now moving at $0.75$ m/s and the cumulative displacement is $\approx 11$ mm.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Compound Circuit Design*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]**
> A hydraulic press requires a compound circuit that performs the following cycle:
> 1. **Fast extension** of the ram (mass 1000 kg) under no load, using regeneration.
> 2. **Pressing** at high force (250 kN) at low speed, using pump + accumulator assist.
> 3. **Safe holding** of the load against gravity during power-off.
> 4. **Controlled retraction** with meter-out speed control.
>
> **(a)** Sketch and explain a suitable compound hydraulic circuit, naming every valve and its function. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Calculate the pump delivery time, the accumulator delivery volume, and the regen-stroke velocity for the following data: cylinder bore = 100 mm, rod diameter = 60 mm, stroke = 400 mm, pump flow = 20 LPM, accumulator volume (gas) = 4 L precharged at 60 bar, system pressure during press = 200 bar. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Circuit description (7 marks):**

The compound circuit consists of the following elements in series along the flow path:

1. **Pump (P)** — supplies 20 LPM at up to 200 bar.
2. **Unloading valve (UV)** — set at 180 bar; when accumulator is full it diverts pump flow to tank.
3. **Bladder Accumulator (A)** — precharged 60 bar; stores energy and supplies flow during pressing.
4. **4/3 Closed-centre DCV (V1)** — direction control: Position A = extend, Position B = retract, Centre = locked.
5. **Check Valve CV1** — allows pump flow *into* cap-end, blocks reverse.
6. **Check Valve CV2** — allows rod-end oil to return to cap-end *during regeneration only*; pilot-operated to open only in regen mode.
7. **Counterbalance Valve (CBV)** — on the rod-end line; prevents the ram from falling under gravity.
8. **Meter-out Flow Control Valve (FCV)** — on the rod-end line during retraction to control retract speed.
9. **Pressure Relief Valve (PRV)** — safety override at 210 bar.
10. **Tank (T)** — return reservoir.

**Operating sequence:**

- **Regen extend:** DCV in Position A. CV1 is open (pump → cap-end). CV2 is piloted open (rod-end → cap-end). Effective area = $A_1 - A_2$. Ram extends *fast* at $v_{regen} = Q_p / (A_1 - A_2)$.
- **Press:** Once the workpiece is contacted and pressure climbs above 60 bar, the pilot on CV2 closes CV2 (regeneration ends). Pump + accumulator jointly push the ram at $v_{press} = (Q_p + Q_{acc}) / A_1$ with force $F = P A_1$.
- **Hold:** DCV centred. CBV locks oil in rod-end. Pump can be shut off.
- **Retract:** DCV in Position B. Pump supplies rod-end via meter-out FCV. Cap-end oil returns to tank freely.

**Part (b) — Calculations (7 marks):**

*Geometric constants (1 mark):*

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.1)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 = 78.54 \text{ cm}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.1^2 - 0.06^2) = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 = 50.27 \text{ cm}^2$$

*Regen-stroke velocity (2 marks):*

$$v_{regen} = \frac{Q_p}{A_1 - A_2} = \frac{20 \times 10^{-3}/60}{(78.54 - 50.27) \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{3.33 \times 10^{-4}}{2.827 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.118 \text{ m/s}$$

*Pump delivery time for full stroke (2 marks):*

The press-stroke length depends on the application; assuming the entire 400 mm stroke is pumped (worst case, no regen):

$$t_{pump} = \frac{V_{cyl}}{Q_p} = \frac{A_1 \cdot L}{Q_p} = \frac{7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.4}{3.33 \times 10^{-4}} = 9.43 \text{ s}$$

*Accumulator delivery volume (2 marks):*

The accumulator precharge is $P_0 = 60$ bar and is fully charged to system pressure $P_2 = 200$ bar. After isolation, when the press is actuated, the gas expands from $P_2 = 200$ bar back down to $P_1 = 100$ bar (say) supplying the cylinder.

$$V_1 = V_0 \left( \frac{P_0}{P_1} \right)^{1/n} = 4 \times 10^{-3} \left( \frac{60}{100} \right)^{1/1.4} = 4 \times 10^{-3} \times (0.6)^{0.714}$$

Computing $(0.6)^{0.714} = e^{0.714 \ln 0.6} = e^{0.714 \times (-0.511)} = e^{-0.365} = 0.694$

$$V_1 = 4 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.694 = 2.776 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 = 2.78 \text{ L}$$

Volume of fluid delivered (gas expansion):

$$\Delta V_{gas} = V_1 - V_0 = 2.776 - 4.000 = -1.224 \text{ L} \quad \text{(gas shrinks — negative sign wrong sign)}$$

Re-checking: as gas *expands* from $P_2 = 200$ to $P_1 = 100$, the gas volume *increases*. The fluid volume *delivered* is the change in gas volume:

$$V_1 = V_0 \left( \frac{P_0}{P_1} \right)^{1/n} \quad \text{where the accumulator is initially at } P_2 = 200 \text{ bar}$$

Re-labelling: gas initially at $P_2 = 200$ bar, $V_{gas,2} = V_0 = 4$ L (this is the gas volume at the *maximum* pressure, which by KTU convention is the "rated" precharge). When the gas expands to $P_1 = 100$ bar, the gas volume becomes:

$$V_{gas,1} = V_0 \left( \frac{200}{100} \right)^{1/1.4} = 4 \times (2)^{0.714}$$

Computing $(2)^{0.714} = e^{0.714 \ln 2} = e^{0.714 \times 0.693} = e^{0.495} = 1.640$

$$V_{gas,1} = 4 \times 1.640 = 6.56 \text{ L}$$

$$\Delta V_{fluid} = 6.56 - 4.00 = 2.56 \text{ L}$$

> **The accumulator delivers 2.56 L of fluid** during the pressure swing from 200 bar to 100 bar. The remaining 7.54 L needed for the full stroke $(V_{cyl} = A_1 L = 3.14 \text{ L})$ is *not* matched — note that 2.56 L is actually greater than the cylinder volume, so the accumulator alone could complete the stroke if the pressure drop were deeper. The pump merely *tops up* any deficit.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Step Counter / Compound Circuit questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the units** — most KTU markers deduct **½ mark per inconsistency** in units. Always show Pa, m, m/s, LPM.
> 2. **No iteration table** — the step counter method is a *tabular* exercise. A long derivation with no table = 0 marks for the iteration part. Always present Step, $t$, $P$, $v$, $x$ in a clean table.
> 3. **Wrong time-step choice** — if your $\Delta t$ causes a velocity overshoot, you must demonstrate that you noticed and *justify* a smaller $\Delta t$.
> 4. **Confusing $A_1$ and $A_2$** — cap-end area is *larger* than rod-end area. Using the wrong one in the force equation is a 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram in compound circuits** — a 14-mark question on compound circuit *always* demands a labelled circuit diagram. Without it, **−3 marks minimum**.
> 6. **Ignoring the relief valve clamp** — if your computed $P$ exceeds the relief setting, you must explicitly state $P = P_{relief}$ for that step.
> 7. **Wrong accumulator gas-law indexing** — $P_0 V_0^n$ refers to the *precharge* state, *not* the maximum pressure. State it clearly.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Step Counter Method & Compound Circuit Design**

- **Step Counter Method** is *numerical*, *iterative*, and *table-based*. It cannot be done without a table.
- The **three master equations** are: (i) Continuity → $\Delta P$, (ii) Force balance → $\Delta v$, (iii) Kinematics → $\Delta x$.
- $\Delta t$ must be small enough that $v$ does not change by more than ~10 % per step. A common KTU test is to give you an unstable $\Delta t$ and expect you to fix it.
- The **accumulator gas law** is $P_0 V_0^n = P_1 V_1^n$ with $n = 1.4$ for nitrogen (adiabatic) and $n = 1.0$ for isothermal (slow processes).
- The accumulator's **fluid delivery** rate is $Q_{acc} = (V / n P) \cdot dP/dt$, where $V$ is the *instantaneous* gas volume, not $V_0$.
- A **Compound Circuit** combines $\geq 2$ basic circuits. The four most-tested combinations are: (1) Regenerative + Counterbalance, (2) Pump + Accumulator (unloading), (3) Sequencing, (4) Regenerative + Meter-out.
- **Regenerative velocity** is $v_{regen} = Q_p / (A_1 - A_2)$; **full-force velocity** is $v_{press} = Q_p / A_1$ (no regen). Always slower but more forceful.
- **Counterbalance valve** setting must satisfy $P_{CBV} \geq F_{load} / A_2$ to prevent runaway under gravity.
- **Sequence valve** setting must exceed the *maximum pressure* required by the first actuator, otherwise the second actuator will trigger prematurely.
- For Part B (14 marks), a **labelled circuit diagram** is mandatory — KTU examiners explicitly allocate 3–4 marks for the diagram.
- Always state the **assumptions** at the start of your Step Counter solution: constant bulk modulus, lumped parameters, no leakage (unless stated), Euler time integration.
- **Watch for sign conventions** — a load that *assists* motion gives a *negative* $F_L$ in the standard $F_{net} = P A - F_L$ equation; clarify by writing $F_{net} = P A - F_{opposing}$.
- The **effective bulk modulus** $\beta_e$ combines oil compressibility, line expansion, and air content. Standard value **1.4 GPa** for de-aerated oil in steel pipes.
- Numerical stability: if $\Delta t > 0.1$ s is given in the question, you are expected to *halve* it and re-iterate.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Combination Circuit Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT526 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Combination Circuit Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Combination Circuit** in industrial hydraulics is a **multi-actuator hydraulic circuit** in which **two or more hydraulic actuators** — typically a combination of a **linear actuator (hydraulic cylinder)** and a **rotary actuator (hydraulic motor)** — are energized by a **single common pressure source (hydraulic pump)** through a network of **directional control valves (DCVs)**, **flow control valves (FCVs)**, and **pressure control valves (PCVs)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Canonical):**
> A *combination circuit* is a hydraulic circuit designed to perform **multiple sequential or simultaneous operations** by combining basic circuits (meter-in, meter-out, bleed-off, sequencing, counterbalance, regeneration, etc.) into one integrated system governed by a single prime mover and pump.

The **design objective** of a combination circuit is to ensure:

1. **Independent speed/velocity control** of each actuator despite sharing one pump.
2. **Sequential operation** (priority logic) when simultaneous actuation is not desirable.
3. **Pressure isolation** so that the high-pressure demand of one actuator does not stall another.
4. **Energy efficiency** through unloading, regeneration, or accumulator support where appropriate.
5. **Safety and synchronization** of moving parts (e.g., clamp-then-feed, drill-then-tap).

The three principal families of combination circuits studied under the **KTU 2024 PERAT526 syllabus** are:

| S.No. | Combination Type | Typical Industrial Use |
|:-----:|:----------------|:-----------------------|
| 1 | **Cylinder + Cylinder** (linear + linear) | Clamping-feeding on a fixture; door-lift-and-lock |
| 2 | **Cylinder + Motor** (linear + rotary) | Drill-then-tap; press-feed-and-rotate |
| 3 | **Motor + Motor** (rotary + rotary) | Conveyor-with-spindle in CNC tool changers |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Household Water Supply**
> Imagine your home has a **single overhead water tank (pump)** that supplies both the **shower** (cylinder) and the **washing machine** (motor) at the same time. If both are open, the pressure drops, and neither works well. To fix this, you install:
> - **A pressure regulator** at the washing machine (pressure control valve),
> - **A flow knob** at the shower (flow control valve / meter-out), and
> - **An automatic valve** that lets the washing machine run *only after* the shower valve is closed (sequence valve / pilot-operated check).
>
> This is exactly what a **combination hydraulic circuit** does — it orchestrates **who gets how much flow, at what pressure, and in what order** from one pump.

Think of the combination circuit as a **traffic controller** at a junction shared by two roads (two actuators). The controller decides:

- **Which road moves first** (sequencing).
- **How fast vehicles move on each** (meter-in / meter-out).
- **When to halt a road to prevent collision** (counterbalance / pressure relief).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants, Standard Metrics, and Conventions

The following **standard engineering constants and units** are used throughout KTU 2024 hydraulic circuit design:

- **System pressure (P):** measured in **bar (1 bar ≈ 10⁵ Pa)** or **MPa (1 MPa = 10 bar)**.
- **Flow rate (Q):** measured in **litres per minute (LPM or L/min)**; **1 LPM ≈ 1.667 × 10⁻⁵ m³/s**.
- **Pump displacement (V_D):** measured in **cc/rev (cm³/rev)**.
- **Pump speed (N):** measured in **rpm (revolutions per minute)**.
- **Cylinder bore (D) and rod diameter (d):** measured in **mm**; piston areas in **cm²**.
- **Motor displacement (v_D):** measured in **cc/rev**.
- **Volumetric efficiency (η_v):** dimensionless, typically **0.90 to 0.98**.
- **Mechanical efficiency (η_m):** dimensionless, typically **0.90 to 0.95**.
- **Overall efficiency (η_o = η_v × η_m):** dimensionless.
- **Oil bulk modulus (β):** typically **1.0 × 10⁹ to 1.7 × 10⁹ Pa** (≈ **1000 to 1700 bar** for mineral oil).
- **Specific gravity of mineral oil:** ≈ **0.86 to 0.90** (kinematic viscosity 32–68 cSt at 40 °C).
- **Acceleration due to gravity (g):** **9.81 m/s²**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Convention:** All flow and force calculations in this module assume **ideal (100% efficient) components** unless otherwise stated. Where **efficiency is mentioned**, it is included as a multiplicative factor on the **input side** of the equation.

---

## 1.4 Why Combination Circuits Matter — Engineering Motivation

Modern industrial machines — from **CNC machining centres** and **plastic injection moulding presses** to **paper-roll handling cranes** and **hydraulic presses in forging** — must perform **multiple coordinated tasks in a single cycle**. A combination circuit:

- **Eliminates redundant pumps**, reducing capital cost.
- **Centralizes hydraulic power**, simplifying maintenance.
- **Enables automated sequencing**, improving cycle time and safety.
- **Supports energy recovery** via regeneration, reducing heat generation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Outcome Mapping:**
> This topic directly addresses **CO4: Design combination hydraulic circuits and evaluate actuator performance** under the **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy levels Apply (L3) and Analyse (L4)**. Mastery requires both qualitative circuit drawing and quantitative performance calculation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principles

A combination circuit operates on **three coupled principles** which together govern its behaviour:

### 2.1.1 Pressure Coupling (Parallel Supply Logic)

When two actuators are connected in **parallel across the same pump**, the **system pressure is determined by the actuator demanding the highest pressure** (the **load-sensing** or **highest-pressure-wins** principle). This is analogous to two parallel resistors in an electrical circuit where the voltage is common.

$$\boxed{P_{\text{pump}} = \max \left( P_{\text{actuator 1}}, \; P_{\text{actuator 2}} \right)}$$

This is **load-induced** pressure. The pump must generate at least this pressure for either actuator to move.

### 2.1.2 Flow Partition (Series/Parallel Distribution)

The **pump flow** $Q_P$ is split between actuators based on their **flow demand and the resistance of the throttling elements**. The general flow-balance equation is:

$$Q_P = Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_{\text{leakage}}$$

Assuming negligible leakage:

$$\boxed{Q_P = Q_1 + Q_2}$$

If an actuator is held stationary (e.g., clamped), it draws only leakage flow, and almost all pump flow is delivered to the moving actuator.

### 2.1.3 Sequencing Logic (Priority-Based Operation)

Sequencing ensures that **one actuator completes its stroke before the other begins** (or vice versa). This is achieved using:

- **Sequence valves** (pressure-actuated: opens only when pilot pressure exceeds a set value).
- **Pilot-operated check valves** (locks one actuator while another operates).
- **Pressure-compensated flow dividers** (proportional split when simultaneous operation is required).
- **Deceleration / counterbalance valves** for overrunning loads.

---

## 2.2 Component Inventory of a Combination Circuit

| S.No. | Component | Function in Combination Circuit |
|:-----:|:----------|:--------------------------------|
| 1 | **Fixed or variable displacement pump** | Primary energy source |
| 2 | **Relief valve** | Sets maximum system pressure (safety) |
| 3 | **Unloading valve** | Sends pump output to tank at low pressure during idle |
| 4 | **Directional Control Valve (DCV)** — 4/3, 4/2 | Directs flow to actuator ports |
| 5 | **Flow Control Valve (FCV)** — meter-in, meter-out, bleed-off | Sets actuator speed |
| 6 | **Sequence valve (pilot-operated)** | Enforces priority order |
| 7 | **Counterbalance valve** | Prevents load-induced overrunning on vertical cylinders |
| 8 | **Pilot-operated check valve** | Holds actuator position when pump is idle |
| 9 | **Pressure-reducing valve** | Provides lower pressure to a sub-circuit |
| 10 | **Pressure-compensated flow divider** | Splits flow in fixed or proportional ratio |
| 11 | **Hydraulic cylinder** | Linear actuator |
| 12 | **Hydraulic motor** | Rotary actuator |
| 13 | **Accumulator (optional)** | Stores energy, dampens pressure pulsations |
| 14 | **Cooler & filter** | Thermal management and contamination control |

---

## 2.3 The Four Canonical Sub-Circuits Used in Combinations

A combination circuit is **always an assembly of basic sub-circuits**. KTU 2024 emphasizes the following four:

### 2.3.1 Meter-In Circuit
A flow control valve is placed **on the inlet side** of the actuator. Used for **horizontal loads** or when **meter-out causes cavitation**.

$$Q_{\text{actuator}} = Q_{\text{throttled}} = A \cdot v_{\text{cyl}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_{\text{cyl}} = \frac{Q_{\text{throttled}}}{A}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cavitation Warning:** In a meter-in circuit, the **return side** of the cylinder is unrestricted. If the return pressure drops below atmospheric (typically < 0.7 bar absolute), dissolved air comes out of solution and **cavitation** occurs. Therefore, **meter-out is preferred for vertical downward loads**, and **meter-in is preferred for horizontal loads**.

### 2.3.2 Meter-Out Circuit
A flow control valve is placed on the **outlet (return) side** of the actuator. The return flow is metered, providing **positive back-pressure** that prevents cavitation and **controls overrunning loads** (e.g., gravity-loaded vertical cylinders).

$$v_{\text{cyl}} = \frac{Q_{\text{out, throttled}}}{A}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule of Thumb:** *Use **meter-out** for any actuator that is **vertically loaded**, has an **overrunning load**, or is **subject to negative loads**. Use **meter-in** for purely horizontal, constant-friction loads.*

### 2.3.3 Bleed-Off Circuit
The flow control valve is placed **between the pump line and the tank line**, so a portion of pump flow is *bled off* to the tank, leaving a controlled reduced flow to the actuator.

$$Q_{\text{actuator}} = Q_P - Q_{\text{bleed}}$$

This is a **less expensive** way to control speed but is **inefficient** because the bled flow still consumes energy at full pump pressure. It generates more heat.

### 2.3.4 Counterbalance (Back-pressure) Circuit
A **counterbalance valve** (a pilot-operated relief valve) is placed on the **outlet** of a vertical cylinder. It creates a **back-pressure** equal to the load pressure so that the cylinder does not run away.

$$P_{\text{back-pressure}} = P_{\text{counterbalance, set}} \geq \frac{W_{\text{load}}}{A_{\text{piston}}}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the canonical KTU 2024 formula sheet for Module 4 — Combination Circuit Design. Memorize these expressions; they appear in **every** ESE Part B question on this topic.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks / Application |
|:-:|:---------|:--------|:------:|:-----------------------|
| 1 | Pump flow rate | $Q_P = V_D \cdot N \cdot \eta_v$ | LPM | $V_D$ in cc/rev, $N$ in rpm |
| 2 | Cylinder piston area (full bore side) | $A_1 = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | cm² | $D$ in cm |
| 3 | Cylinder piston area (rod side) | $A_2 = \dfrac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$ | cm² | $d$ = rod diameter |
| 4 | Cylinder extending velocity | $v_{\text{ext}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{in}}}{A_1}$ | m/s | Q in m³/s, A in m² |
| 5 | Cylinder retracting velocity | $v_{\text{ret}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{out}}}{A_2}$ | m/s | Outflow is on rod side |
| 6 | Cylinder extension force | $F_{\text{ext}} = P \cdot A_1$ | N | $P$ in Pa, $A$ in m² |
| 7 | Cylinder retraction force | $F_{\text{ret}} = P \cdot A_2$ | N | Rod-side force |
| 8 | Motor speed | $N_m = \dfrac{Q_m}{v_D \cdot \eta_v}$ | rpm | $Q$ in LPM, $v_D$ in cc/rev |
| 9 | Motor torque | $T = \dfrac{v_D \cdot \Delta P \cdot \eta_m}{2 \pi}$ | N·m | $\Delta P$ in Pa |
| 10 | Motor power (output) | $P_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{2 \pi N_m T}{60}$ | W | $N$ in rpm |
| 11 | Motor power (hydraulic input) | $P_{\text{hyd}} = \Delta P \cdot Q_m$ | W | Input to motor |
| 12 | Cylinder stroke time | $t = \dfrac{V_{\text{displaced}}}{Q_{\text{actuator}}} = \dfrac{A \cdot L}{Q}$ | s | $L$ = stroke length |
| 13 | Load-induced pressure | $P_{\text{load}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{load}}}{A}$ | Pa | Static force balance |
| 14 | Pressure intensification ratio (rod vs bore) | $P_{\text{rod}} = P_{\text{bore}} \cdot \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}$ | Pa | Rod side pressure (regen) |
| 15 | Sequence valve setting | $P_{\text{seq}} = P_{\text{load of first actuator}} + \text{margin}$ | Pa | Typically + 5 to 10 bar |
| 16 | Counterbalance valve setting | $P_{CB} = \dfrac{W_{\text{load}}}{A_1}$ | Pa | Equal to load pressure |
| 17 | Unloading pressure | $P_{\text{unload}} = 0$ (or low) | bar | Pump dead-heads via vented relief |
| 18 | Regeneration savings | $Q_{\text{regen}} = A_2 \cdot v_{\text{cyl}}$ | LPM | Outflow redirected to cap end |
| 19 | Power dissipation in FCV | $P_{\text{loss}} = \Delta P_{FCV} \cdot Q$ | W | Throttling heat |
| 20 | Total cycle time (combination) | $t_{\text{cycle}} = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + \dots$ | s | Sum of all sub-operations |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Conversion Trap (Major Source of Lost Marks):**
> KTU examiners frequently test unit conversions. Always convert:
> - **Pressure:** 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa = 0.1 MPa
> - **Flow:** 1 LPM = 10⁻³ / 60 m³/s ≈ 1.667 × 10⁻⁵ m³/s
> - **Area:** 1 cm² = 10⁻⁴ m²
> - **Volume:** 1 cc = 1 cm³ = 10⁻⁶ m³
> - **Torque:** 1 N·m = 10⁷ dyne·cm
> Mixing cm² and m² is the **#1 mark-losing mistake** in KTU Part B.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Combination circuits are the **backbone of industrial automation**. Their real-world utility spans:

- **Machine tools:** Automatic tool changers (clamp + rotate + index).
- **Forging presses:** Clamp + ram + ejector synchronization.
- **Injection moulding:** Clamp + injection + carriage traverse.
- **Mobile hydraulics (JCBs, excavators):** Boom + arm + bucket combined actuation from a single load-sensing pump.
- **Aerospace ground support:** Multi-axis actuators on landing gear test rigs.
- **Marine hydraulics:** Hatch + door + crane interlock systems.

---

## 2.6 Energy-Efficiency Considerations

A combination circuit is **only as good as its energy strategy**. Three high-yield techniques are:

1. **Regeneration:** Outflow from the rod side is redirected to the cap (bore) side, halving pump flow requirement and doubling force. The cylinder becomes a *single-rod intensifier*.
2. **Unloading:** When all actuators are idle, the pump is **dead-headed at near-zero pressure** through a vented relief or a dedicated unloading valve. Power consumption drops by **80–90%**.
3. **Pressure matching:** Pressure-reducing valves and load-sensing pumps feed each actuator at *just enough* pressure, avoiding throttling losses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Hot Topic:** ESE questions on combination circuits increasingly emphasize **regeneration** and **unloading**, as these align with **Sustainable Engineering** and **Green Manufacturing** themes of NEP 2020.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical Design Problem: Clamp-and-Feed Combination Circuit

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example — KTU ESE Pattern (14-Mark Question Style)**
> The following worked example mirrors the **exact pattern** of KTU 2024 ESE Module-4 Part B questions. Read every step; the logic of every transition is given in plain English.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A hydraulic press in a **forging shop** performs two operations in sequence:

1. **Operation 1 — Clamping (Linear Actuator):**
   A double-acting hydraulic cylinder (bore $D = 100$ mm, rod diameter $d = 60$ mm, stroke $L_c = 300$ mm) clamps a workpiece. The clamping force required is $F_{\text{clamp}} = 50$ kN.

2. **Operation 2 — Feeding (Rotary Actuator):**
   Once clamped, a hydraulic motor ($v_D = 32$ cc/rev, $\eta_m = 0.92$, $\eta_v = 0.95$) drives a feed screw that advances the tool. The torque required is $T_{\text{feed}} = 80$ N·m at a desired speed of $N_m = 1500$ rpm.

A **single fixed-displacement pump** (displacement $V_D = 50$ cc/rev, drive speed $N_P = 1450$ rpm, volumetric efficiency $\eta_{v,p} = 0.96$) supplies both actuators through a **sequence valve** that energizes the motor **only after the cylinder reaches the end of its clamping stroke**.

**Tasks:**
- (a) Determine the **pump delivery** $Q_P$.
- (b) Calculate the **clamping pressure** and **clamping time**.
- (c) Calculate the **flow required by the motor** and the **pressure drop across the motor**.
- (d) Check whether the pump can supply both **simultaneously** if the sequence valve is bypassed; if so, what is the **maximum simultaneous speed** of the cylinder?
- (e) Calculate the **total cycle time** for one complete operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumptions (Standard KTU Convention):**
> 1. Pump volumetric efficiency $\eta_{v,p} = 0.96$.
> 2. No leakage in actuators (ideal).
> 3. Friction losses in pipelines are neglected unless otherwise stated.
> 4. The return line is at atmospheric pressure (≈ 0 bar gauge) unless a back-pressure is specified.
> 5. The sequence valve has a **cracking pressure** of $P_{\text{seq}} = P_{\text{clamp}} + 5$ bar.

---

### 3.1.2 PART (a) — Pump Delivery

**Step 1 — Convert units.**

Pump displacement is given in **cc/rev**. Flow rate in LPM comes from:

$$Q_P \; [\text{LPM}] = V_D \; [\text{cc/rev}] \times N_P \; [\text{rpm}] \times \eta_{v,p}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_P &= 50 \; \text{cc/rev} \times 1450 \; \text{rpm} \times 0.96 \\
    &= 50 \times 1450 \times 0.96 \\
    &= 69{,}600 \; \text{cc/min} \\
    &= 69.6 \; \text{L/min} \quad (\text{since } 1000 \; \text{cc} = 1 \; \text{L})
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{Q_P = 69.6 \; \text{L/min}}$$

**Logic explanation:** The pump displaces 50 cc of oil per revolution, completes 1450 revolutions every minute, but loses 4% to internal leakage, so the *net* delivery is **69.6 LPM**.

---

### 3.1.3 PART (b) — Clamping Pressure and Clamping Time

**Step 1 — Calculate piston areas.**

The bore diameter is $D = 100$ mm $= 10$ cm, and the rod diameter is $d = 60$ mm $= 6$ cm.

$$\begin{aligned}
A_1 \;(\text{cap side, full bore}) &= \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 10^2}{4} = 78.54 \; \text{cm}^2 \\
A_2 \;(\text{rod side}) &= \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (10^2 - 6^2)}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 64}{4} = 50.27 \; \text{cm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Clamping pressure (cap-side pressurization).**

The clamping force must be sustained on the **cap side** (extending direction). Force balance on the piston:

$$F_{\text{clamp}} = P_{\text{clamp}} \cdot A_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_{\text{clamp}} = \frac{F_{\text{clamp}}}{A_1}$$

Convert units: $F_{\text{clamp}} = 50$ kN $= 50{,}000$ N; $A_1 = 78.54 \; \text{cm}^2 = 78.54 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^2$.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{clamp}} &= \frac{50{,}000}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 6.366 \times 10^{6} \; \text{Pa} \\
                  &= 63.66 \; \text{bar} \quad (\text{1 bar} = 10^5 \; \text{Pa})
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{clamp}} \approx 63.7 \; \text{bar}}$$

**Logic explanation:** The piston is pushed by the **cap-side pressure** acting on area $A_1$. To deliver 50 kN, the pressure must be **63.7 bar**.

**Step 3 — Clamping time.**

During the clamping stroke, the pump supplies the cap side at the full pump flow $Q_P$ (no other actuator is active). The cylinder extends at velocity:

$$v_{\text{ext}} = \frac{Q_P}{A_1}$$

Convert $Q_P$ to consistent units: $Q_P = 69.6$ LPM $= \dfrac{69.6 \times 10^{-3}}{60} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 1.16 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
v_{\text{ext}} &= \frac{1.16 \times 10^{-3}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1477 \; \text{m/s} = 147.7 \; \text{mm/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute time using $t = L / v$.**

Stroke length $L_c = 300$ mm $= 0.3$ m.

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{clamp}} &= \frac{L_c}{v_{\text{ext}}} = \frac{0.3}{0.1477} = 2.03 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{t_{\text{clamp}} \approx 2.03 \; \text{s}}$$

**Logic explanation:** The cylinder travels 300 mm at 147.7 mm/s, so it takes about **2 seconds** to fully clamp.

---

### 3.1.4 PART (c) — Motor Flow and Pressure Drop

**Step 1 — Motor flow requirement.**

The motor speed formula, including volumetric efficiency:

$$N_m = \frac{Q_m}{v_D \cdot \eta_v} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_m = N_m \cdot v_D \cdot \eta_v$$

Substitute $N_m = 1500$ rpm, $v_D = 32$ cc/rev, $\eta_v = 0.95$:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_m &= 1500 \times 32 \times 0.95 \\
    &= 45{,}600 \; \text{cc/min} \\
    &= 45.6 \; \text{L/min}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{Q_m = 45.6 \; \text{L/min}}$$

**Logic explanation:** The motor must *receive* 45.6 LPM to spin at 1500 rpm, since 5% of the oil leaks internally.

**Step 2 — Pressure drop across the motor.**

Motor torque equation (with mechanical efficiency):

$$T = \frac{v_D \cdot \Delta P \cdot \eta_m}{2 \pi}$$

Solve for $\Delta P$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= \frac{2 \pi T}{v_D \cdot \eta_m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Convert units carefully** (this is the KTU trap):

- $T = 80$ N·m.
- $v_D = 32$ cc/rev $= 32 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/rev.
- $\eta_m = 0.92$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= \frac{2 \pi \times 80}{32 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.92} \\
         &= \frac{502.65}{2.944 \times 10^{-5}} \\
         &= 1.708 \times 10^{7} \; \text{Pa} \\
         &= 170.8 \; \text{bar}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P_{\text{motor}} \approx 170.8 \; \text{bar}}$$

**Logic explanation:** To deliver 80 N·m of torque, the motor experiences a pressure drop of **170.8 bar** across its ports.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Trap:** This pressure is **higher than the clamping pressure (63.7 bar)**. So the pump must generate at least **170.8 bar** during the feeding phase, not 63.7 bar. The **highest-load pressure governs the pump sizing**.

---

### 3.1.5 PART (d) — Simultaneous Operation Check

**Question:** Can the pump run the cylinder AND the motor at the same time, given $Q_P = 69.6$ LPM?

**Step 1 — Sum of flow demands.**

$$Q_{\text{total}} = Q_{\text{cyl}} + Q_m = 69.6 + 45.6 = 115.2 \; \text{L/min}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with pump capacity.**

$$Q_{\text{total}} = 115.2 \; \text{L/min} \quad > \quad Q_P = 69.6 \; \text{L/min}$$

**Conclusion:** $\boxed{\text{NO, the pump cannot supply both actuators at full speed simultaneously.}}$

**Step 3 — Maximum simultaneous cylinder speed if motor runs at full load.**

If the motor is given priority (gets its full 45.6 LPM), the residual flow for the cylinder is:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{cyl, max}} &= Q_P - Q_m = 69.6 - 45.6 = 24.0 \; \text{L/min}
\end{aligned}$$

Corresponding cylinder velocity (extending):

$$v_{\text{cyl, max}} = \frac{Q_{\text{cyl, max}}}{A_1} = \frac{24.0 \times 10^{-3} / 60}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0509 \; \text{m/s} = 50.9 \; \text{mm/s}$$

$$\boxed{v_{\text{cyl, max, sim}} \approx 50.9 \; \text{mm/s}}$$

**Logic explanation:** When the motor runs at full load, the cylinder slows to roughly **one-third of its solo speed** (50.9 vs 147.7 mm/s) because the motor is consuming **two-thirds** of the available pump flow.

---

### 3.1.6 PART (e) — Total Cycle Time

The cycle has three phases:

1. **Clamping** (cylinder only): $t_1 = 2.03$ s (from part b).
2. **Feeding** (motor only): $t_2$ = stroke / linear feed rate of screw.

The motor drives a feed screw of **lead $\ell = 10$ mm/rev** (assumed). The linear feed rate is:

$$v_{\text{feed}} = N_m \times \ell = 1500 \; \text{rpm} \times 10 \; \text{mm/rev} = 15{,}000 \; \text{mm/min} = 250 \; \text{mm/s}$$

If the feed stroke is $L_f = 200$ mm:

$$t_2 = \frac{L_f}{v_{\text{feed}}} = \frac{200}{250} = 0.8 \; \text{s}$$

3. **Retraction + Unclamping** (cylinder retracts, motor is idle):

   - Retraction velocity (rod side): $v_{\text{ret}} = Q_P / A_2 = 1.16 \times 10^{-3} / 5.027 \times 10^{-3} = 0.2307$ m/s $= 230.7$ mm/s.
   - Retraction time: $t_3 = L_c / v_{\text{ret}} = 300 / 230.7 = 1.30$ s.

4. **Idle / dead time** (valve transitions, safety): $t_4 = 0.5$ s (assumed).

**Total cycle time:**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{cycle}} &= t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + t_4 \\
                  &= 2.03 + 0.80 + 1.30 + 0.50 \\
                  &= 4.63 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{t_{\text{cycle}} \approx 4.63 \; \text{s}}$$

---

### 3.1.7 Analytical Matrix — Result Summary

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit | Source Step |
|:---------|:------:|:------|:----:|:-----------:|
| Pump flow | $Q_P$ | 69.6 | LPM | §3.1.2 |
| Cap-side area | $A_1$ | 78.54 | cm² | §3.1.3 |
| Rod-side area | $A_2$ | 50.27 | cm² | §3.1.3 |
| Clamping pressure | $P_{\text{clamp}}$ | 63.7 | bar | §3.1.3 |
| Clamping time | $t_{\text{clamp}}$ | 2.03 | s | §3.1.3 |
| Motor flow demand | $Q_m$ | 45.6 | LPM | §3.1.4 |
| Motor pressure drop | $\Delta P$ | 170.8 | bar | §3.1.4 |
| Max simultaneous $v_{\text{cyl}}$ | $v_{\text{sim,max}}$ | 50.9 | mm/s | §3.1.5 |
| Retraction velocity | $v_{\text{ret}}$ | 230.7 | mm/s | §3.1.6 |
| Retraction time | $t_3$ | 1.30 | s | §3.1.6 |
| **Total cycle time** | $t_{\text{cycle}}$ | **4.63** | **s** | §3.1.6 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Take-Away:** The **highest-pressure actuator** dictates the **pump pressure setting** (relief valve setting). In this example, the **relief valve must be set to at least 170.8 bar + safety margin (say, 5%) ≈ 180 bar**, *not* 63.7 bar. This is a common exam pitfall.

---

## 3.2 Derivative Calculation: Sequence Valve Setting

The **sequence valve** must crack (open) only after the cylinder has reached the end of its clamping stroke. The required setting is:

$$P_{\text{seq, set}} = P_{\text{clamp, max}} + P_{\text{margin}}$$

Including the 5-bar margin and accounting for pressure drop in the line (assume 2 bar in supply line to sequence valve):

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{seq, set}} &= 63.7 + 5 + 2 = 70.7 \; \text{bar}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{seq, set}} \approx 70.7 \; \text{bar}}$$

**Logic explanation:** The sequence valve must *not* open prematurely. Its setting must exceed the maximum clamping pressure (with margin) so that it only opens once the cylinder is fully clamped.

---

## 3.3 Derivative Calculation: Power and Heat Dissipation

### 3.3.1 Hydraulic Power Required

**During clamping phase** (cylinder only):

$$P_{\text{hyd, clamp}} = P_{\text{clamp}} \cdot Q_P = 63.7 \times 10^{5} \; \text{Pa} \times 1.16 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 7392 \; \text{W} \approx 7.4 \; \text{kW}$$

**During feeding phase** (motor only):

$$P_{\text{hyd, feed}} = \Delta P_{\text{motor}} \cdot Q_m = 170.8 \times 10^{5} \times 7.6 \times 10^{-4} = 12{,}981 \; \text{W} \approx 13.0 \; \text{kW}$$

**Peak pump power (governing motor sizing):**

$$\boxed{P_{\text{hyd, peak}} = 13.0 \; \text{kW} \quad \text{(during feeding)}}$$

### 3.3.2 Electric Motor Sizing

Assuming an **overall electric-motor-to-pump efficiency** $\eta_{\text{el}} = 0.90$:

$$P_{\text{motor, electric}} = \frac{P_{\text{hyd, peak}}}{\eta_{\text{el}}} = \frac{13.0}{0.90} = 14.4 \; \text{kW}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{elec}} \approx 14.4 \; \text{kW} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{select standard 15 kW motor}}$$

### 3.3.3 Heat Generation During Cycle

The total energy per cycle is:

$$E_{\text{cycle}} = P_{\text{hyd, clamp}} \cdot t_1 + P_{\text{hyd, feed}} \cdot t_2 + P_{\text{hyd, ret}} \cdot t_3$$

Assuming retraction pressure is low (~ 5 bar to overcome friction only):

$$P_{\text{hyd, ret}} = 5 \times 10^{5} \times 1.16 \times 10^{-3} = 580 \; \text{W}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{cycle}} &= 7.4 \times 2.03 + 13.0 \times 0.80 + 0.58 \times 1.30 \\
                  &= 15.02 + 10.40 + 0.75 \\
                  &= 26.17 \; \text{kJ}
\end{aligned}$$

**Average power dissipation** (assuming continuous cycling with $t_{\text{cycle}} = 4.63$ s):

$$P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{E_{\text{cycle}}}{t_{\text{cycle}}} = \frac{26.17}{4.63} = 5.65 \; \text{kW}$$

This is the **heat load** the oil cooler must reject. Select a cooler rated for **≥ 6 kW** of heat rejection (with 10% safety factor).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Design Principle:** *Always compute the **average power dissipation** for combination circuits — it determines the cooler size, reservoir size, and pump duty cycle rating.*
