# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pattern:** KTU 2024 ESE Part A consists of **2 questions per module** at 3 marks each. Answers should be **concise (60–100 words)** with a **diagram or formula** wherever applicable.

---

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]

> **"Define a combination hydraulic circuit. With the help of a labelled schematic, explain the operation of a clamp-and-feed circuit used in a drilling machine."**

#### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **combination hydraulic circuit** is a circuit that combines **two or more basic hydraulic sub-circuits** (meter-in, meter-out, sequencing, counterbalance, etc.) to perform **multiple coordinated operations** from a single pump source.

**Operation of clamp-and-feed circuit (drilling machine):**

1. A **fixed-displacement pump** supplies oil through a **relief valve** and a **sequence valve** to a **4/3 DCV** that controls a **clamping cylinder**.
2. When the DCV is actuated, oil flows to the cap end of the cylinder, and the piston extends at a metered rate, **clamping the workpiece** against a fixed jaw.
3. The pressure rises to the **sequence valve setting**, causing the sequence valve to open and direct flow to a **second DCV** that drives a **hydraulic motor** connected to the drill spindle.
4. The motor rotates the drill, advancing the tool into the clamped workpiece.
5. After drilling, the sequence valve blocks motor flow, and the cylinder retracts, releasing the workpiece.

**Diagram elements to be drawn** *(not shown in text, but expected in answer sheet)*: pump, relief valve, sequence valve, two DCVs, two flow control valves, clamping cylinder, drill motor, and tank.

> **Mark Distribution Hint:** 1 mark for definition, 1.5 marks for operation explanation, 0.5 mark for mentioning the sequencing role.

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]

> **"Differentiate between meter-in and meter-out speed control circuits. State one application where each is preferred."**

#### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | **Meter-In Circuit** | **Meter-Out Circuit** |
|:-------:|:--------------------|:----------------------|
| **FCV location** | On the **inlet line** to actuator | On the **outlet (return) line** from actuator |
| **Throttled flow** | **Inlet flow** to actuator | **Outlet flow** from actuator |
| **Return side** | **Unrestricted** (free flow to tank) | **Pressurized** (back-pressure present) |
| **Cavitation risk** | **High** (negative pressure on return side) | **Low** (positive back-pressure) |
| **Heat dissipation** | Heat dissipated in the **FCV**; cylinder sees near-atmospheric return | Heat dissipated in the **FCV**; cylinder sees pressurized return |
| **Best for** | **Horizontal loads** (constant friction) | **Vertical loads** (overrunning/gravity loads) |

**Preferred applications:**

- **Meter-in** → Horizontal conveyor cylinder, drilling feed cylinder, machine tool slides (where there is no overrunning load).
- **Meter-out** → Vertical press ram, crane boom, hoist cylinder, lowering under gravity (to prevent runaway).

> **Mark Distribution Hint:** 1.5 marks for table of differences, 1 mark for application, 0.5 mark for any diagram or example.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pattern:** KTU 2024 ESE Part B gives **internal choice**. You must answer **either Question A or Question B**, each worth 14 marks, with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Each sub-part must include **a diagram, derivation, and final numerical answer with units**.

---

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU ESE Pattern — May 2024 Style]

> **"A combination hydraulic circuit on an automatic lathe is required to (i) clamp the workpiece using a double-acting cylinder and (ii) rotate the spindle using a hydraulic motor, in that order. The system uses a single fixed-displacement pump with a sequence valve to enforce the clamp-then-rotate order."**

**Given Data:**
- Pump: $V_D = 40$ cc/rev, $N_P = 1440$ rpm, $\eta_{v,p} = 0.95$.
- Clamping cylinder: bore $D = 80$ mm, rod $d = 50$ mm, stroke $L_c = 250$ mm, clamping force required $F_c = 30$ kN.
- Spindle motor: $v_D = 25$ cc/rev, $\eta_v = 0.96$, $\eta_m = 0.90$, required speed $N_m = 1200$ rpm, required torque $T_m = 60$ N·m.
- Sequence valve margin: **+ 5 bar** above clamping pressure.

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Compute:**
1. Pump flow rate $Q_P$.
2. Cylinder piston areas $A_1$ and $A_2$.
3. Clamping pressure $P_c$.
4. Clamping time $t_c$.
5. Sequence valve setting $P_{\text{seq}}$.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Compute:**
1. Motor flow requirement $Q_m$.
2. Pressure drop across motor $\Delta P_m$.
3. Maximum simultaneous cylinder velocity (if motor runs at full load).
4. Retraction time of cylinder (assume return pressure = 5 bar friction-only).
5. **Sketch the ISO 1219 schematic** of the complete combination circuit.

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question A**

##### **Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1: Pump flow $Q_P$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_P &= V_D \times N_P \times \eta_{v,p} \\
    &= 40 \times 1440 \times 0.95 \\
    &= 54{,}720 \; \text{cc/min} \\
    &= 54.72 \; \text{L/min}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Piston areas.** Convert diameters to cm: $D = 8$ cm, $d = 5$ cm.

$$\begin{aligned}
A_1 &= \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 64}{4} = 50.27 \; \text{cm}^2 \\
A_2 &= \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (64 - 25)}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 39}{4} = 30.63 \; \text{cm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Clamping pressure $P_c$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_c &= \frac{F_c}{A_1} = \frac{30{,}000 \; \text{N}}{50.27 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^2} \\
    &= 5.97 \times 10^6 \; \text{Pa} = 59.7 \; \text{bar}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Clamping time $t_c$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
v_{\text{ext}} &= \frac{Q_P}{A_1} = \frac{54.72 \times 10^{-3} / 60}{50.27 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.1814 \; \text{m/s} = 181.4 \; \text{mm/s} \\
t_c &= \frac{L_c}{v_{\text{ext}}} = \frac{250}{181.4} = 1.378 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Sequence valve setting $P_{\text{seq}}$.**

$$P_{\text{seq}} = P_c + 5 = 59.7 + 5 = 64.7 \; \text{bar}$$

> **Marks Split for (a):** 1 + 1 + 1.5 + 1.5 + 2 = 7 marks.

---

##### **Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1: Motor flow $Q_m$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_m &= N_m \times v_D \times \eta_v = 1200 \times 25 \times 0.96 \\
    &= 28{,}800 \; \text{cc/min} = 28.8 \; \text{L/min}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Pressure drop across motor $\Delta P_m$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta P_m &= \frac{2 \pi T_m}{v_D \times \eta_m} = \frac{2 \pi \times 60}{25 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.90} \\
           &= \frac{376.99}{2.25 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.676 \times 10^7 \; \text{Pa} = 167.6 \; \text{bar}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Maximum simultaneous cylinder velocity (motor at full load).**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{cyl, max}} &= Q_P - Q_m = 54.72 - 28.8 = 25.92 \; \text{L/min} \\
v_{\text{cyl, max}} &= \frac{25.92 \times 10^{-3}/60}{50.27 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.0859 \; \text{m/s} = 85.9 \; \text{mm/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Retraction time.**

Return flow is full pump flow; return pressure is 5 bar (given), so the cylinder retracts with $Q_P$ through the rod-side area $A_2$.

$$\begin{aligned}
v_{\text{ret}} &= \frac{Q_P}{A_2} = \frac{54.72 \times 10^{-3}/60}{30.63 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.2977 \; \text{m/s} = 297.7 \; \text{mm/s} \\
t_{\text{ret}} &= \frac{250}{297.7} = 0.84 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: ISO 1219 schematic** *(in answer sheet, draw the following layout):*

```
[Tank] → [Strainer] → [Pump] → [Relief] → [Manifold]
                                          ├── [Sequence Valve P_seq=64.7 bar] → [DCV1 4/3] → [FCV meter-out] → [Cylinder Clamp]
                                          └── [DCV2 4/3] → [FCV meter-in] → [Motor Spindle]
        All returns → [Cooler] → [Filter 10 μm] → [Tank]
```

> **Marks Split for (b):** 1 + 2 + 1.5 + 1 + 1.5 = 7 marks.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks [KTU ESE Pattern — Dec 2023 Style]

> **"Design a combination hydraulic circuit for a broaching machine in which (i) a hydraulic cylinder drives the broach in a linear feed motion, and (ii) a hydraulic motor rotates an indexing table. The two operations occur simultaneously after an initial clamping phase."**

**Given Data:**
- Pump: $V_D = 60$ cc/rev, $N_P = 1500$ rpm, $\eta_{v,p} = 0.96$.
- Broach cylinder: bore $D = 120$ mm, rod $d = 70$ mm, stroke $L_b = 500$ mm, broach feed force $F_b = 80$ kN.
- Indexing motor: $v_D = 50$ cc/rev, $\eta_v = 0.94$, $\eta_m = 0.91$, torque $T_i = 120$ N·m at $N_i = 100$ rpm.
- Cylinder and motor operate **simultaneously** (no sequence valve; a flow divider is used).

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Compute:**
1. Pump flow $Q_P$.
2. Broach cylinder area $A_1$ (cap side) and feed velocity.
3. Broach feed time $t_b$.
4. Cylinder pressure $P_b$ at load.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Compute:**
1. Motor flow $Q_m$ at rated speed.
2. Motor pressure drop $\Delta P_m$.
3. **Total flow demanded** $Q_{\text{total}}$ and check if pump can supply both.
4. **Design a flow divider ratio** such that the cylinder gets $Q_c$ and motor gets $Q_m$ from the available $Q_P$ — find the ratio.
5. **Determine the relief valve setting** with 5% margin over the higher of the two pressures.

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question B**

##### **Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1: Pump flow $Q_P$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_P &= 60 \times 1500 \times 0.96 = 86{,}400 \; \text{cc/min} = 86.4 \; \text{L/min}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Broach cylinder area $A_1$.**

$D = 120$ mm $= 12$ cm, $d = 70$ mm $= 7$ cm.

$$\begin{aligned}
A_1 &= \frac{\pi \times 12^2}{4} = 113.10 \; \text{cm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Broach pressure $P_b$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_b &= \frac{F_b}{A_1} = \frac{80{,}000}{113.10 \times 10^{-4}} = 7.07 \times 10^6 \; \text{Pa} = 70.7 \; \text{bar}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Broach feed time $t_b$.**

Cylinder velocity depends on how much flow is delivered. Initially, with the motor **clamped/idle** (before simultaneous operation), the full pump flow goes to the cylinder:

$$\begin{aligned}
v_{\text{ext, max}} &= \frac{Q_P}{A_1} = \frac{86.4 \times 10^{-3}/60}{113.10 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.1273 \; \text{m/s} = 127.3 \; \text{mm/s} \\
t_b \;(\text{initial}) &= \frac{500}{127.3} = 3.93 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

If the motor is active simultaneously and consumes a portion of the flow, the time will be longer (computed in part b).

> **Marks Split for (a):** 1 + 1.5 + 1.5 + 3 = 7 marks.

---

##### **Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1: Motor flow $Q_m$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_m &= N_i \times v_D \times \eta_v = 100 \times 50 \times 0.94 = 4700 \; \text{cc/min} = 4.7 \; \text{L/min}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Motor pressure drop $\Delta P_m$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta P_m &= \frac{2 \pi T_i}{v_D \times \eta_m} = \frac{2 \pi \times 120}{50 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.91} \\
            &= \frac{753.98}{4.55 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.657 \times 10^7 \; \text{Pa} = 165.7 \; \text{bar}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Total flow demanded vs. pump supply.**

$$Q_{\text{total}} = Q_{\text{cyl,full}} + Q_m = 86.4 + 4.7 = 91.1 \; \text{L/min}$$

$$Q_P = 86.4 \; \text{L/min} \quad < \quad Q_{\text{total}} = 91.1 \; \text{L/min}$$

**Conclusion:** The pump **cannot** supply the cylinder at full flow AND the motor at full flow simultaneously. The cylinder must slow down, **or** the motor must run at reduced speed.

**Step 4: Flow divider ratio design.**

Let the cylinder get flow $Q_c$ and the motor get $Q_m = 4.7$ LPM. The flow balance:

$$Q_P = Q_c + Q_m \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_c = Q_P - Q_m = 86.4 - 4.7 = 81.7 \; \text{L/min}$$

The corresponding cylinder velocity during simultaneous operation:

$$v_{\text{cyl, sim}} = \frac{81.7 \times 10^{-3}/60}{113.10 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.1204 \; \text{m/s} = 120.4 \; \text{mm/s}$$

Broach time during simultaneous operation:

$$t_{b,\text{sim}} = \frac{500}{120.4} = 4.15 \; \text{s}$$

The flow divider ratio is:

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{Q_c}{Q_m} = \frac{81.7}{4.7} = 17.4 : 1$$

$$\boxed{\text{Use a pressure-compensated flow divider with ratio } 17.4:1}$$

**Step 5: Relief valve setting.**

The pump must generate the **higher of the two pressures**, which is $\Delta P_m = 165.7$ bar (since the motor demands more pressure than the cylinder's 70.7 bar).

With 5% safety margin:

$$P_{\text{relief}} = 1.05 \times 165.7 = 174.0 \; \text{bar}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{relief, set}} \approx 174 \; \text{bar}}$$

> **Marks Split for (b):** 1 + 1.5 + 1.5 + 2 + 1 = 7 marks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in Combination Circuit Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** (cc → m³, bar → Pa, mm → m): This alone costs **1–2 marks per sub-part**. Always show the unit conversion step.
> 2. **Setting the relief valve to the *clamping pressure* instead of the *maximum load pressure***: The relief valve must be sized for the **highest-pressure actuator**, not the first one. Many students lose 2 marks here.
> 3. **Using bore area on the *return* side of the cylinder**: When a cylinder retracts, the *rod-side* area $A_2$ governs the velocity, not $A_1$. Mixing these areas costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting the sequence valve margin**: A common error is to set $P_{\text{seq}} = P_{\text{clamp}}$ exactly. This causes chatter and premature opening. Always add **3–10 bar** of margin.
> 5. **Not drawing an ISO 1219 schematic**: KTU examiners **deduct up to 3 marks** for using pictorial or non-standard symbols in Part B answers. Always use square boxes, arrows, and standard symbols.
> 6. **Ignoring pump volumetric efficiency**: When the question gives $\eta_{v,p} < 1$, you MUST apply it. The "university-level" examiner will deduct a full mark if you write $Q_P = V_D \times N$ without the efficiency.
> 7. **Skipping the "simultaneous operation" check**: Most KTU questions ask "what happens if both run together?" Failing to address this loses 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — Combination Circuit Design: Rapid Revision Checklist**
>
> **Core Concepts:**
> - A **combination circuit** integrates two or more basic sub-circuits (meter-in, meter-out, sequencing, regeneration, counterbalance) into a single multi-actuator system powered by one pump.
> - Three main types: **Cylinder + Cylinder**, **Cylinder + Motor**, **Motor + Motor**.
> - The **pump pressure** is set by the **highest-load actuator**; the **pump flow** is **shared** between actuators (series/parallel logic).
>
> **Key Formulas (must memorize):**
> - $Q_P = V_D \times N_P \times \eta_{v,p}$
> - $A_1 = \pi D^2 / 4$, $A_2 = \pi (D^2 - d^2) / 4$
> - $v = Q / A$
> - $F = P \times A$
> - $N_m = Q_m / (v_D \times \eta_v)$
> - $T = (v_D \times \Delta P \times \eta_m) / (2 \pi)$
> - $t_{\text{stroke}} = L \times A / Q$
> - $P_{\text{seq}} = P_{\text{first actuator load}} + 3 \text{ to } 10$ **bar**
>
> **Design Rules of Thumb (KTU 2024):**
> - **Meter-out** for vertical / overrunning loads.
> - **Meter-in** for horizontal, friction-only loads.
> - **Bleed-off** is cheap but wasteful; avoid in energy-sensitive applications.
> - **Regeneration** doubles force, halves flow, halves speed — use for clamp or low-speed high-force.
> - **Counterbalance valve** on vertical retract side to prevent runaway.
> - **Relief valve setting** = max($P_{\text{cyl load}}, P_{\text{motor load}}$) + 5–10% safety margin.
> - **Sequence valve margin** = + 5 bar (minimum) above first actuator's peak pressure.
> - **Always compute total cycle time** and **average power dissipation** for cooler sizing.
>
> **Common Exam Mistakes to Avoid:**
> - Unit conversion slip-ups (cm vs m, bar vs Pa, cc vs m³).
> - Using $A_1$ instead of $A_2$ on the return side.
> - Setting the relief valve to the *clamping* pressure rather than the *maximum* pressure.
> - Drawing non-ISO 1219 schematics.
> - Forgetting pump efficiency factors.
> - Not analyzing the *simultaneous* operation case when the actuators share the pump.
>
> **Higher-Order Concepts (for full marks in ESE):**
> - **Regeneration** in combination circuits: $Q_{\text{regen}} = A_2 \cdot v_{\text{cyl}}$; force boost = $A_1^2 / (A_1 - A_2) \times P$.
> - **Unloading logic** for energy saving: pump dead-heads at < 5 bar during idle.
> - **Flow divider types**: fixed-ratio, pressure-compensated, priority-type.
> - **Cavitation avoidance** in meter-in circuits: maintain return pressure > 0.7 bar absolute.
> - **Heat balance**: cooler size = (average hydraulic power × (1 − η_system)) / (oil specific heat × ΔT).
> - **Cycle time optimization**: critical path = sum of all dependent actuator times; parallel paths take max time.
>
> **Cross-References to Other KTU Modules:**
> - **Module 1** (Hydraulic Pumps): for $Q_P$ and $P_P$ fundamentals.
> - **Module 2** (Control Valves): for DCV, FCV, and sequence valve behavior.
> - **Module 3** (Hydraulic Actuators): for cylinder and motor sizing.
> - **Module 5** (Pneumatics & Electro-hydraulics): for solenoid-operated variants.
> - **Module 6** (Maintenance & Troubleshooting): for failure modes in combination circuits.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection and Sizing - Selection of Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT526 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Selection and Sizing - Selection of Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Selection** in Industrial Hydraulics refers to the engineering decision-making process of choosing the most appropriate hydraulic component (pump, valve, actuator, reservoir, filter, accumulator, hose, etc.) from the available standard catalog options such that it satisfies the functional, safety, economic, and lifecycle requirements of a defined hydraulic circuit.

**Sizing** is the subsequent analytical procedure of determining the exact geometric or performance parameters of that chosen component — bore diameter, stroke length, displacement volume, reservoir capacity, wall thickness, etc. — based on the system's operating load profile, pressure, flow rate, duty cycle, and ambient conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**
> *Selection and Sizing is the systematic engineering methodology that transforms a load-duty specification (force, speed, stroke, duty cycle, environment) into a defined bill of materials and component ratings, ensuring the hydraulic power unit operates safely, efficiently, and reliably within its design envelope.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine you are planning a **water supply system for a 10-storey apartment building**.

- **Selection** is like deciding *whether* to use a centrifugal pump or a submersible pump, and *which brand*. You are not calculating dimensions yet — you are matching a *type* of pump to a *type* of need.
- **Sizing** is the next step — asking *"What kW motor? What pipe diameter? What tank size?"* — so that 500 liters per minute actually reach the 10th floor with adequate pressure, without the pipe bursting or the motor burning out.

In hydraulics, this same logic applies to industrial presses, machine tools, injection moulding machines, earth-movers, and aircraft systems. The engineer **first selects** the *family* of component (gear pump, double-acting cylinder, pilot-operated DCV, etc.), and **then sizes** it (displacement 25 cc/rev, bore 80 mm, rod 45 mm, 25 L reservoir, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A wrongly *selected* component cannot be rescued by correct *sizing*. A wrongly *sized* but correctly *selected* component will at best consume more power and at worst fail catastrophically. KTU examiners frequently test both halves of this logic.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics Used

The following performance metrics govern the selection-sizing logic across **all** hydraulic components and are treated as **boundary constants** in KTU numerical problems:

- **Maximum Working Pressure ($p_{max}$)** — typically **210 bar** for industrial presses, **350 bar** for mobile equipment, **700 bar** for aerospace.
- **Nominal Flow Rate ($Q$)** — in **L/min** (litres per minute) or **m³/s**.
- **Hydraulic Fluid Temperature Range** — standard **50 °C to 80 °C** operating window.
- **Volumetric Efficiency ($\eta_v$)** — typically **0.92 to 0.98** for modern pumps.
- **Mechanical Efficiency ($\eta_m$)** — typically **0.90 to 0.95**.
- **Overall Efficiency ($\eta_o$)** — product of $\eta_v$ and $\eta_m$.
- **Velocity of Fluid in Pressure Lines** — limited to **4.5 m/s** (suction: **1.5 m/s**, return: **2.0 m/s**).
- **Standard Safety Factor (S.F.)** on pressure rating — minimum **1.25 to 1.5**.
- **Design Duty Cycle** — expressed as *X minutes/hour* or as a percentage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to (i) justify the *choice* of each component with engineering reasoning, and (ii) compute numerical sizes using the standard formulae shown in Section 2. Memorising component types alone is *not* sufficient for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Selection-Sizing Philosophy (Step-by-Step Logic)

The decision flow is **NOT linear** — it is iterative. A bad sizing result sends the engineer *back* to the selection stage. The rigorous workflow is:

1. **Define the Load-Duty Profile.** Identify the external work the system must perform: maximum force, stroke length, speed of actuation, number of cycles per hour, ambient temperature, contamination level, and operator environment (industrial floor, mobile chassis, mining, marine).
2. **Compute the Hydraulic Power Envelope.** Translate the mechanical load into pressure-flow products. The minimum hydraulic power is:
   $$P_{hyd} = \dfrac{F \cdot v}{\eta_{total}} = \dfrac{Q \cdot p}{600} \quad \text{[kW]}$$
3. **Select the Actuator (Cylinder/Motor).** Choose *type* (single-acting / double-acting / telescopic / rotary vane) based on the load profile, then *size* the bore, rod, and stroke.
4. **Select the Pump.** Choose the *displacement class* (gear, vane, piston) and *control type* (fixed or variable), then size the displacement in cc/rev.
5. **Select the Control Valves.** Direction Control (DCV), Pressure Relief, Flow Control, Check — chosen on flow capacity, pressure rating, and actuation method (manual, solenoid, pilot).
6. **Size the Reservoir.** Tank capacity is linked to pump flow and heat dissipation.
7. **Select and Size Filtration.** Based on ISO 4406 cleanliness code and contamination sensitivity.
8. **Select and Size Accumulators** (if required) for energy storage, pulsation damping, or emergency power.
9. **Select Conduits (Hoses/Pipes).** Sized by recommended velocity, pressure rating, and bending radius.
10. **Apply Safety Factors and Standards.** Cross-check against ISO 4413, ISO 1219, OSHA, and manufacturer catalogs.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** The vertical pipe symbol `|` is forbidden inside table rows. Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute values.

| # | Formula | Equation | Variables & Units | Application / When to Use |
|---|---------|----------|-------------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | Cylinder Thrust Force | $F = p \cdot A$ | $F$ [N], $p$ [N/m²], $A$ [m²] | Linear actuator pushing load |
| 2 | Cylinder Bore Area | $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2$ | $D$ = bore diameter [m] | Sizing piston diameter |
| 3 | Annular (Rod-side) Area | $A_r = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$ | $d$ = rod diameter [m] | Retract force calculation |
| 4 | Required Flow for Cylinder | $Q = \dfrac{60 \cdot A \cdot v}{10^6}$ | $Q$ [L/min], $A$ [mm²], $v$ [m/s] | Pump flow sizing |
| 5 | Pump Displacement | $V_D = \dfrac{Q \cdot 10^6}{N \cdot \eta_v}$ | $V_D$ [cc/rev], $N$ [rpm], $Q$ [L/min] | Fixed displacement pump sizing |
| 6 | Hydraulic Power | $P = \dfrac{Q \cdot p}{600}$ | $P$ [kW], $Q$ [L/min], $p$ [bar] | Motor/drive sizing |
| 7 | Pump Torque | $T = \dfrac{V_D \cdot \Delta p}{2 \pi}$ | $T$ [N·m], $V_D$ [m³/rev] | Drive coupling sizing |
| 8 | Hydraulic Motor Speed | $N_m = \dfrac{Q \cdot \eta_v}{V_D}$ | $N_m$ [rpm] | Motor RPM verification |
| 9 | Motor Torque | $T_m = \dfrac{V_D \cdot \Delta p \cdot \eta_m}{2 \pi}$ | $T_m$ [N·m] | Motor torque verification |
| 10 | Reservoir Capacity | $V_t = k \cdot Q$ | $V_t$ [L], $Q$ [L/min], $k = 2 \text{ to } 3$ | Heat dissipation sizing |
| 11 | Pipe Flow Velocity | $v = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | $Q$ [m³/s], $D$ [m], $v$ [m/s] | Pipe bore sizing |
| 12 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{v \cdot D}{\nu}$ | $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity [m²/s] | Flow regime (Laminar $\vert$ Turbulent) |
| 13 | Hagen-Poiseuille (Laminar) | $\Delta p = \dfrac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi D^4}$ | $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity [Pa·s] | Suction line / low Re |
| 14 | Darcy-Weisbach | $\Delta p = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | $f$ = friction factor, $\rho$ = density | Pressure line loss |
| 15 | Accumulator Gas Pre-charge | $P_0 = 0.9 \cdot p_1$ | $P_0$ = pre-charge, $p_1$ = min pressure | Nitrogen pre-fill rule |
| 16 | Accumulator Effective Vol. (Isothermal) | $V_0 = V_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_1 - P_2}\right)$ | Isothermal $(n=1)$ | Slow-acting systems |
| 17 | Accumulator Effective Vol. (Adiabatic) | $V_0 = V_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{1 - (P_1/P_2)^{1/n}}\right)$ | Adiabatic $n = 1.4$ | Fast-acting systems |
| 18 | Heat to be Dissipated | $H = P_{in} (1 - \eta_{total})$ | $H$ [kW] | Reservoir cooler sizing |
| 19 | Filter Beta Ratio | $\beta_x = N_{upstream}/N_{downstream}$ at $x$ µm | dimensionless | ISO 4406 classification |
| 20 | Hose Min. Burst Pressure | $P_{burst} \geq 4 \cdot P_{working}$ | safety factor 4:1 | Hose selection |

## 2.3 Engineering Utility — Why This Topic Matters

The selection-and-sizing methodology is the **bridge between hydraulic theory and hydraulic practice**. It is the single most employable skill in fluid-power industry, used daily by:

- **Design engineers** in machine-tool and injection-moulding industries.
- **Application engineers** in mobile hydraulics (JCB, Tata Hitachi, Komatsu).
- **Maintenance and reliability engineers** in petrochemical refineries and steel plants.
- **Aerospace fluid-power designers** for flight-control actuation systems.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners consistently award full 14-mark solutions to students who (a) state the *selection rationale* before each sizing step, and (b) close each numerical answer with a **design verification sentence** such as *"Hence, the selected pump operates at 85% of its rated capacity, which is within the 70–90% design sweet-spot for industrial presses."*

## 2.4 Component-by-Component Selection Logic (Condensed Map)

| Component | Primary Selection Driver | Primary Sizing Parameter |
|-----------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Pump** | Pressure class, flow demand, fluid compatibility | Displacement $V_D$ [cc/rev] |
| **DCV** | Flow capacity ($Q_{max}$), actuation (solenoid / pilot) | Port size, $C_v$ value |
| **Relief Valve** | Max system pressure, response time | Set pressure (with S.F. on $p_{max}$) |
| **Cylinder** | Load profile, stroke, mounting style | Bore $D$, Rod $d$, Stroke $L$ |
| **Motor** | Torque-speed requirement, starting load | Displacement, max RPM |
| **Reservoir** | Heat dissipation, fluid de-aeration | Capacity $V_t = (2\text{ to }3) Q$ |
| **Filter** | Cleanliness target (ISO code), $\Delta p$ limit | Beta ratio at target micron |
| **Accumulator** | Energy storage / emergency / pulsation | $V_0$, pre-charge $P_0$ |
| **Hoses/Pipes** | Pressure rating, velocity limit, flexibility | Internal diameter, S.A.E. dash size |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sizing a Double-Acting Hydraulic Cylinder

**Given:** A hydraulic press must develop a pushing force of **$F_{push} = 80 \text{ kN}$** during extension and a pulling force of **$F_{pull} = 50 \text{ kN}$** during retraction. Working pressure available from the system is **$p = 160 \text{ bar}$**. Cylinder extension speed is **$v_{ext} = 0.1 \text{ m/s}$**. Standard rod-to-bore ratios are $d/D = 0.5$ or $0.7$.

### Step 1 — Compute the required piston (bore) area during extension

Since extension force uses the full piston area on the cap end:

$$A_{req} = \dfrac{F_{push}}{p} = \dfrac{80 \times 10^3 \text{ N}}{160 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2}$$

$$A_{req} = \dfrac{80 \times 10^3}{1.6 \times 10^7} = 5.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

The value $160 \text{ bar} = 160 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2$ because $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$.

### Step 2 — Solve for the bore diameter $D$

Using $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2$, we invert:

$$D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot A_{req}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.02}{3.14159}} = \sqrt{6.366 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$D = 0.0798 \text{ m} \approx 80 \text{ mm}$$

**Selection:** Round up to next standard bore — **$D = 80 \text{ mm}$**.

### Step 3 — Choose rod diameter ratio and verify retract force

For industrial presses the standard ratio is **$d/D = 0.7$**, giving:

$$d = 0.7 \times 80 = 56 \text{ mm} \quad (\text{round to standard } 55 \text{ mm or } 56 \text{ mm})$$

The retract (annular) area is:

$$A_{ann} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.080^2 - 0.056^2)$$

$$A_{ann} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(6.40 \times 10^{-3} - 3.136 \times 10^{-3}) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(3.264 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$A_{ann} = 2.563 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

Available retract force:

$$F_{retract} = p \cdot A_{ann} = 160 \times 10^5 \times 2.563 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F_{retract} = 4.10 \times 10^4 \text{ N} = 41.0 \text{ kN}$$

> **Design Check:** Required retract force is **50 kN**, but only **41 kN** is available at $d/D = 0.7$. We must **upgrade the rod diameter**.

### Step 4 — Iterative correction: choose $d/D = 0.5$ for higher retract force

Try $d = 0.5 \times 80 = 40 \text{ mm}$:

$$A_{ann} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(80^2 - 40^2) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(6400 - 1600) \text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_{ann} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(4800) = 3769.9 \text{ mm}^2 = 3.770 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$F_{retract} = 160 \times 10^5 \times 3.770 \times 10^{-3} = 6.03 \times 10^4 \text{ N} = 60.3 \text{ kN}$$

**Verified:** $F_{retract} = 60.3 \text{ kN} > 50 \text{ kN}$ required. **Selection: $D = 80 \text{ mm}$, $d = 40 \text{ mm}$, $d/D = 0.5$** is acceptable.

### Step 5 — Required pump flow rate

$$Q = A_{piston} \cdot v_{ext} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \times 0.1 \text{ m/s}$$

$$Q = 5.027 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 30.16 \text{ L/min}$$

Applying a leak/heat-loss factor of **1.15**, design flow becomes:

$$Q_{design} = 1.15 \times 30.16 = 34.69 \text{ L/min}$$

**Select pump rated ≈ 36 L/min or 40 L/min** (next standard size).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pump Sizing and Power Calculation

**Given:** The cylinder above requires $Q = 34.69 \text{ L/min}$. The pump is driven by an electric motor at **$N = 1450 \text{ rpm}$**. Volumetric efficiency $\eta_v = 0.95$.

### Step 1 — Pump displacement

$$V_D = \dfrac{Q \cdot 10^6}{N \cdot \eta_v} = \dfrac{34.69 \times 10^6}{1450 \times 0.95}$$

$$V_D = \dfrac{34.69 \times 10^6}{1377.5} = 25{,}184 \text{ cc/rev} \times 10^{-6}$$

$$V_D \approx 25.18 \text{ cc/rev}$$

**Select standard displacement: $V_D = 25 \text{ cc/rev}$** (most catalogs offer 25 cc/rev gear pumps).

### Step 2 — Verify delivered flow at rated RPM

$$Q_{actual} = V_D \cdot N \cdot \eta_v = 25 \times 10^{-6} \times 1450 \times 0.95 \times 60 \times 10^3$$

$$Q_{actual} = 25 \times 1450 \times 0.95 \times 60 = 2{,}066{,}250 \text{ cc/min} = 20.66 \text{ L/min}$$

This is **less than 34.69 L/min** required. Therefore the designer must either:

- (a) Increase pump speed (limited by manufacturer), or
- (b) Select a larger pump — try $V_D = 45 \text{ cc/rev}$.

Recalculate:

$$Q = 45 \times 10^{-6} \times 1450 \times 0.95 \times 60 \times 10^{-3} = 37.21 \text{ L/min}$$

**Selection: $V_D = 45 \text{ cc/rev}$** is acceptable (delivers 37.21 L/min > 34.69 L/min required).

### Step 3 — Hydraulic and shaft power

$$P_{hyd} = \dfrac{Q \cdot p}{600} = \dfrac{37.21 \times 160}{600} = 9.92 \text{ kW}$$

$$P_{shaft} = \dfrac{P_{hyd}}{\eta_{total}} = \dfrac{9.92}{0.95 \times 0.93} \approx 11.23 \text{ kW}$$

**Select motor: 15 kW (next standard size with 25% margin).**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reservoir Sizing

Using the standard rule $V_t = k \cdot Q$ where $k = 2$ to $3$:

$$V_t = 3 \times 37.21 = 111.6 \text{ L}$$

Round up to **standard size: 120 L reservoir**.

Heat dissipation check:

$$H = P_{shaft} - P_{hyd} = 11.23 - 9.92 = 1.31 \text{ kW}$$

A 120 L reservoir with natural convection dissipates approximately **1.5 to 2 kW** at 50 °C fluid temperature — **acceptable without a cooler**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Selection-Sizing Trade-offs

| Scenario | Selection Choice | Sizing Adjustment | Engineering Trade-off |
|----------|------------------|-------------------|----------------------|
| Heavy industrial press | Fixed gear pump, simple relief | Larger $V_D$, oversized relief | Lower cost, lower efficiency |
| Energy-saving machine tool | Variable piston pump + LS valve | Smaller reservoir, accumulator | Higher initial cost, 30% energy saving |
| Mobile crane | Load-sensing piston pump | Compact reservoir, return filter | High power density, complex control |
| Injection moulding | Servo-motor-driven fixed pump | Add accumulator for peak demand | Quiet operation, low heat |
| Mining drill | High-pressure piston pump (350 bar) | Robust filtration, water-glycol fluid | Reliability over efficiency |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Iterative Selection-Sizing Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Load-Duty Profile] --> B[Compute Required Force F and Speed v]
    B --> C[Compute Hydraulic Power P_hyd]
    C --> D[Select Actuator Type: Cylinder or Motor]
    D --> E{Selected Component is Cylinder?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Size Bore D from F=p*A]
    F --> G[Choose Rod Ratio d to D]
    G --> H[Verify Retract Force F_retract]
    H --> I{Retract Force Sufficient?}
    I -- No --> J[Increase Rod Diameter d]
    J --> G
    I -- Yes --> K[Compute Required Flow Q]
    E -- No --> L[Size Hydraulic Motor: V_D from Torque]
    L --> M[Verify Motor Speed and Torque]
    M --> N{Sufficient?}
    N -- No --> O[Re-select Motor]
    O --> L
    N -- Yes --> K
    K --> P[Select Pump Type: Gear, Vane, Piston]
    P --> Q[Size Pump Displacement V_D]
    Q --> R{Delivered Q meets Required Q?}
    R -- No --> S[Increase V_D or N]
    S --> Q
    R -- Yes --> T[Select Pressure Relief Valve with SF=1.25]
    T --> U[Size Reservoir V_t = 3 times Q]
    U --> V[Select Filter based on ISO 4406 Code]
    V --> W[Select Conduits by Velocity Limits]
    W --> X[Final Design Verification and Standards Check]
    X --> Y[End: Bill of Materials Generated]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Sized Hydraulic System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PowerPack[Power Pack Module]
        RES[Reservoir Vt equals 120 L] --> SUC[Suction Strainer 100 micron]
        SUC --> PMP[Fixed Gear Pump V_D equals 45 cc per rev]
        PMP --> RELV[Pressure Relief Valve set at 200 bar]
        RELV --> RET[Return Line Filter 25 micron Beta 10]
        RET --> RES
    end

    subgraph Control[Control Manifold]
        PMP --> DCV[4 by 3 Direction Control Valve]
        DCV --> FCV[Flow Control Valve Qmax 40 L per min]
        FCV --> PCV[Pressure Compensator]
        PCV --> CHK1[Check Valve Pilot Operated]
    end

    subgraph Actuator[Actuator Section]
        CHK1 --> CYL[Double Acting Cylinder D equals 80 mm d equals 40 mm]
        CYL --> ACC[Accumulator V0 equals 5 L P0 equals 144 bar]
        ACC --> DCV
    end

    subgraph Monitor[Monitoring]
        PS1[Pressure Switch set at 160 bar]
        PS2[Temperature Switch 60 degree C]
        LS[Level Switch in Reservoir]
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Selection-Sizing Workflow Across Engineering Stages

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CE as Customer Engineer
    participant DS as Design Engineer
    participant AE as Application Engineer
    participant QA as Quality Standards

    CE->>DS: Provide load-duty spec: Force 80 kN, Speed 0.1 m per s
    DS->>DS: Compute hydraulic power envelope
    DS->>AE: Request actuator and pump sizing recommendation
    AE->>DS: Recommend double-acting cylinder 80 by 40 mm
    AE->>DS: Recommend gear pump 45 cc per rev
    DS->>QA: Submit component list for ISO 4413 compliance
    QA->>DS: Verify safety factors and S.A.E. dash sizes
    DS->>CE: Return complete bill of materials with rated specifications
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain any three factors that govern the selection of a hydraulic pump for an industrial application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three principal factors governing pump selection are:

1. **Operating Pressure:** The pump's maximum continuous pressure rating must exceed the system's relief-valve setting with a minimum safety factor of **1.25**. For high-pressure circuits above **350 bar**, axial piston pumps are selected; for low-pressure circuits below **175 bar**, cost-effective gear pumps are chosen.
2. **Required Flow Rate:** Determined by cylinder/motor demand and cycle time. Fixed displacement pumps are selected when flow demand is steady, while variable displacement pumps are chosen when flow varies with load or speed.
3. **Fluid Compatibility and Operating Environment:** The pump materials (seals, bearings) must be compatible with the chosen hydraulic fluid (mineral oil, synthetic, water-glycol, or bio-fluid). For high-temperature or mobile applications, heavy-duty piston pumps are preferred.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the standard rule used to size a hydraulic reservoir and justify the constant used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The standard reservoir sizing rule is:
$$V_t = k \cdot Q$$
where $V_t$ is the tank capacity in **litres**, $Q$ is the pump flow rate in **L/min**, and $k$ is a dimensionless constant typically taken as **2 to 3**.

**Justification of k = 3:** The factor of 3 accounts for:
- (i) Sufficient fluid volume to allow air separation and de-aeration (residence time ≥ 1 minute).
- (ii) Adequate surface area for natural-convection heat dissipation without an external cooler.
- (iii) Reserve capacity to accommodate thermal expansion and minor leakage over service life.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A double-acting hydraulic cylinder is required to push a load of 60 kN during extension and 40 kN during retraction. The system working pressure is 140 bar, extension speed is 0.08 m/s, and the standard rod ratio d/D = 0.6. Assume pump efficiency ηv = 0.95, motor speed N = 1440 rpm, and reservoir factor k = 3.**

**(a) [7 marks]** Size the cylinder bore and rod diameter, and verify that the retract force is sufficient.

**(b) [7 marks]** Select and size the pump, compute the required motor power, and size the reservoir capacity.

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1:** Required piston area on extension side.

$$A_{piston} = \dfrac{F_{push}}{p} = \dfrac{60 \times 10^3}{140 \times 10^5} = 4.286 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Solve for bore diameter.

$$D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 A_{piston}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 4.286 \times 10^{-3}}{3.14159}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{5.456 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0739 \text{ m} \approx 74 \text{ mm}$$

**Select standard bore: $D = 75 \text{ mm}$.**

**Step 3:** Choose rod diameter.

$$d = 0.6 \times 75 = 45 \text{ mm (standard size)}$$

**Step 4:** Compute retract (annular) area.

$$A_{ann} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(75^2 - 45^2) \text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_{ann} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(5625 - 2025) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(3600) = 2827.4 \text{ mm}^2 = 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5:** Verify retract force.

$$F_{retract} = p \cdot A_{ann} = 140 \times 10^5 \times 2.827 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F_{retract} = 3.958 \times 10^4 \text{ N} = 39.58 \text{ kN}$$

Since $F_{retract} = 39.58 \text{ kN} < 40 \text{ kN}$ required, the rod diameter is **slightly undersized**. The student should either select a slightly higher rod (e.g., 50 mm) or note in the conclusion that the deficit of 0.42 kN (≈1%) is within design tolerance.

> **Acceptable final selection: $D = 75 \text{ mm}$, $d = 50 \text{ mm}$, $d/D = 0.667$.**

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1:** Compute required flow rate.

$$Q = A_{piston} \cdot v_{ext} = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \times 0.08 \text{ m/s}$$

$$Q = 3.534 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 21.21 \text{ L/min}$$

Apply 1.15 leak factor: $Q_{design} = 24.39 \text{ L/min}$.

**Step 2:** Size pump displacement.

$$V_D = \dfrac{Q_{design} \times 10^6}{N \cdot \eta_v} = \dfrac{24.39 \times 10^6}{1440 \times 0.95} = 17{,}828 \text{ cc/rev} \times 10^{-6}$$

$$V_D \approx 17.83 \text{ cc/rev}$$

**Select standard $V_D = 18 \text{ cc/rev}$ gear pump.**

**Step 3:** Compute hydraulic power.

$$P_{hyd} = \dfrac{Q_{design} \cdot p}{600} = \dfrac{24.39 \times 140}{600} = 5.69 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4:** Compute shaft power.

$$P_{shaft} = \dfrac{P_{hyd}}{\eta_{total}} = \dfrac{5.69}{0.95 \times 0.93} = 6.44 \text{ kW}$$

**Select motor: 7.5 kW** (next standard with 16% margin).

**Step 5:** Reservoir capacity.

$$V_t = 3 \times Q_{design} = 3 \times 24.39 = 73.2 \text{ L}$$

**Select standard 80 L reservoir.**

---

### QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**An accumulator is to be selected for an emergency power-supply system. The actuator requires 4 litres of fluid between pressures of 140 bar and 180 bar. The pre-charge pressure is 0.9 times the minimum system pressure. Calculate:**

**(a) [7 marks]** The required accumulator gas volume for (i) isothermal and (ii) adiabatic operation. Take $\gamma = 1.4$.

**(b) [7 marks]** If the same accumulator is used for pulsation damping in a pump delivering 30 L/min at 1500 rpm with 5% flow ripple, calculate the permissible pressure rise and verify the accumulator size.

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Given:** $V_1 = 4 \text{ L}$, $P_1 = 140 \text{ bar}$, $P_2 = 180 \text{ bar}$, $P_0 = 0.9 \times 140 = 126 \text{ bar}$.

**Case (i) — Isothermal (n = 1):**

$$V_0 = V_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_1 - P_2}\right) = 4 \times \dfrac{140}{140 - 180}$$

Since $P_1 - P_2 = -40$, we take absolute values for physical interpretation. For an accumulator discharging from high to low pressure, the convention is:

$$V_0 = V_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_0}\right) \cdot \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n} - 1\right]^{-1}$$

Let us use the standard formula:

$$V_0 = \dfrac{V_1 \cdot P_1}{P_0 \cdot \left[1 - (P_0/P_2)^{1/n}\right]}$$

Substituting for $n = 1$ (isothermal):

$$V_0 = \dfrac{4 \times 140}{126 \times \left[1 - (126/180)\right]} = \dfrac{560}{126 \times 0.30} = \dfrac{560}{37.8}$$

$$V_0 \approx 14.8 \text{ L (isothermal)}$$

**Case (ii) — Adiabatic (n = 1.4):**

$$V_0 = \dfrac{4 \times 140}{126 \times \left[1 - (126/180)^{1/1.4}\right]}$$

Compute $(126/180)^{0.714}$:

$$(0.7000)^{0.714} = e^{0.714 \cdot \ln(0.7000)} = e^{0.714 \times (-0.3567)} = e^{-0.2547} \approx 0.7751$$

Therefore:

$$V_0 = \dfrac{560}{126 \times (1 - 0.7751)} = \dfrac{560}{126 \times 0.2249} = \dfrac{560}{28.34}$$

$$V_0 \approx 19.76 \text{ L (adiabatic)}$$

**Select accumulator with $V_0 = 20 \text{ L}$** (next standard size).

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $Q = 30 \text{ L/min} = 0.5 \text{ L/s}$, $N = 1500 \text{ rpm}$, ripple $= 5\% = 0.05$, accumulator $V_0 = 20 \text{ L}$, pre-charge $P_0 = 126 \text{ bar}$, mean system pressure $\approx 160 \text{ bar}$.

**Step 1:** Ripple flow volume per cycle.

$$Q_{ripple} = 0.05 \times 0.5 = 0.025 \text{ L/s}$$

**Step 2:** Permissible pressure rise.

For isothermal pulsation damping:

$$\Delta p = \dfrac{Q_{ripple} \cdot t}{C}$$

where $C$ is the accumulator capacitance:

$$C = \dfrac{V_0 \cdot n \cdot P_0^{1/n}}{P^{(n+1)/n}}$$

With $P \approx 160 \text{ bar}$ and $n = 1.4$:

$$C = \dfrac{20 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.4 \times 126^{1/1.4}}{160^{2.4/1.4}} \text{ m}^3/\text{Pa}$$

For typical industrial rule-of-thumb:

$$\Delta p \approx 3\% \text{ to } 5\% \text{ of mean pressure}$$

**Conclusion:** A 20 L accumulator with pre-charge at 126 bar limits pulsation to approximately **5 bar**, which is acceptable for industrial systems.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
> 1. **Mixing up $P_0$, $P_1$, $P_2$** in the accumulator formula — always remember $P_0 < P_1 < P_2$.
> 2. **Forgetting the safety factor** on relief valve setting (must be 1.25 × working pressure).
> 3. **Not verifying the retract force** after sizing the rod — this is a separate 2-mark check.
> 4. **Using un-rounded intermediate values** in the final answer — KTU expects standard catalog sizes (75 mm, not 73.9 mm).
> 5. **Forgetting to compute motor power** — pump flow alone is *not* a complete design.
> 6. **Selecting rod diameter before verifying annular area** — wrong order loses 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Skipping the design-verification sentence** at the end of the solution.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top 12 Things to Remember (Print and Pin to Your Study Wall):**
> 1. **Cylinder force** $F = pA$ and **area** $A = \pi D^2/4$ — these two equations open 80% of KTU numerical problems.
> 2. **Retract force** uses the annular area $A_r = \pi(D^2 - d^2)/4$ — always verify this after sizing $d$.
> 3. **Pump displacement** $V_D = (Q \times 10^6)/(N \eta_v)$ — never forget the volumetric efficiency factor.
> 4. **Hydraulic power** $P = (Q \cdot p)/600$ with $Q$ in L/min, $p$ in bar, gives kW directly.
> 5. **Reservoir** size is $V_t = 3Q$ in litres — factor 3 is universally accepted.
> 6. **Pressure-line velocity** limit is **4.5 m/s**; **suction-line** limit is **1.5 m/s**; **return-line** limit is **2.0 m/s**.
> 7. **Pre-charge** $P_0 = 0.9 P_1$ — never exceed $P_1$ during nitrogen charging.
> 8. **Hose safety factor** is **4:1** on burst pressure — always verify with manufacturer data.
> 9. **Standard rod ratios** are 0.5 (high retract force) and 0.7 (high push force, common in presses).
> 10. **Selection must precede sizing** — wrong order costs a 2-mark penalty on KTU valuation.
> 11. **Heat dissipation** $H = P_{in}(1 - \eta_{total})$ governs reservoir and cooler sizing.
> 12. **Always end with a design-verification sentence** confirming component operates at 70–90% of its rating.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Calculations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT526 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design Calculations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
In the context of **PERAT526 – Industrial Hydraulics**, **Design Calculations** refer to the systematic, mathematically rigorous procedure used to determine the geometric, kinematic, and thermodynamic parameters of every component in a hydraulic power system so that the final assembly reliably delivers the required force, speed, torque, and duty cycle without exceeding safe stress, temperature, or efficiency limits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Design Calculation* is the analytical process by which the working pressure, piston/cylinder dimensions, pump displacement, motor speed, pipe sizes, reservoir capacity, accumulator pre-charge, and heat dissipation requirements are quantitatively derived from the load specification (force, velocity, stroke, duty cycle) and the governing physical laws (Pascal's Law, Continuity, Bernoulli, Hagen-Poiseuille, Boyle's Law, Euler's Buckling).

The major sub-domains of design calculation in Module 4 are:

1. **Cylinder Design** – Bore, rod diameter, wall thickness, buckling check.
2. **Pump / Motor Sizing** – Displacement, flow rate, volumetric & overall efficiency, power.
3. **Pipeline / Conductor Sizing** – Velocity, Reynolds number, pressure drop.
4. **Reservoir Sizing** – Capacity for thermal expansion, contamination, and de-aeration.
5. **Heat / Thermal Calculations** – Power loss converted to heat, heat-exchanger duty.
6. **Accumulator Sizing** – Stored fluid volume, pre-charge pressure, usable flow.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Building a Water Supply City":** Imagine a city needs to deliver 50,000 litres/hour of water to the 10th floor of a building at a rate of 1 m/s. The civil engineer must compute the **pipe diameter** (so the water flows fast enough but not so fast it leaks), the **pump horsepower** (to lift the water against gravity), the **overhead tank size** (so supply is steady even when pumps switch off), and the **pipe wall thickness** (so it doesn't burst). **Hydraulic Design Calculations are exactly this**—but instead of water for a city, we are sizing the *hydraulic oil* circuit for a press, a crane, or an injection-moulding machine. The "10th floor" is the working pressure (in bar), the "tap on the 10th floor" is the cylinder doing useful mechanical work, and the "overhead tank" is the accumulator.

### 1.3 Governing Physical Constants
| Constant / Property | Symbol | Typical Value (Mineral Oil) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density of hydraulic oil | $\rho$ | **850 – 900** | kg/m³ |
| Bulk modulus | $\beta$ | **1.4 – 1.7 × 10⁹** | Pa |
| Kinematic viscosity (at 40 °C) | $\nu$ | **30 – 68** | cSt (mm²/s) |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Atmospheric pressure | $p_{atm}$ | **1.013 × 10⁵** | Pa |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Cylinder Design Calculations

The hydraulic cylinder converts fluid energy into linear mechanical work. Designing it requires sizing the **piston (bore) diameter** to deliver the required thrust, the **rod diameter** to resist buckling and tension, and the **barrel wall thickness** to contain the burst pressure.

**Step 1 – Piston (Bore) Diameter for Linear Force**

Pascal's law gives the thrust delivered by a piston of effective area $A$ at pressure $p$:

$$F = p \cdot A = p \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2}$$

Solving for the bore diameter $D$:

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4F}{\pi \cdot p}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works:** The fluid transmits pressure equally in all directions. The *force* the cylinder can push is therefore simply *pressure × piston area*. To get more force at the same pressure, we must make the piston larger.

**Step 2 – Piston-Rod Diameter**

The rod is designed using a *tie-rod ratio* (also called *piston area ratio*). Common industrial ratios are:
- **Tension / pull applications (cranes, winches):** $\phi = 0.5$ → $d = D \sqrt{0.5} = 0.707 \, D$
- **Compression / push applications (presses, jacks):** $\phi = 0.3$ → $d = D \sqrt{0.3} \approx 0.55 \, D$

The effective area during **retraction** is reduced by the rod area:

$$A_{ann} = \frac{\pi}{4}\,(D^{2} - d^{2})$$

**Step 3 – Cylinder Barrel Wall Thickness**

Derived from thick-walled cylinder theory (Lamé's equation) and simplified for $t \ll D$:

$$t = \frac{p \cdot D}{2 \sigma_{allow}}$$

For a more accurate (thick-wall) form using a factor of safety $n$:

$$t = \frac{D}{2}\left[\sqrt{\frac{\sigma_{t} + n \, p}{\sigma_{t} - n \, p}} - 1\right]$$

**Step 4 – Euler Buckling Check of the Rod**

If the cylinder is mounted vertically or in a long-stroke configuration, the rod may fail by *buckling* before yielding. Euler's critical load is:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^{2} \, E \, I}{(K \, L)^{2}}$$

where $E$ is Young's modulus, $I = \pi d^{4}/64$ is the second moment of area, $L$ is the stroke length, and $K$ is the effective-length factor (1.0 for both ends pinned, 0.5 for fixed–free, etc.).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Forgetting to check Euler buckling is the most common *full-mark killer*. Examiners almost always award a sub-mark for explicitly stating the buckling load and comparing it to the applied load with a factor of safety ≥ **3**.

### 2.2 Pump Sizing Calculations

A pump converts mechanical (shaft) power into hydraulic (fluid) power. The design must match the actuator's *demand* (in L/min) and the system's *resistance* (in bar).

**Step 1 – Pump Flow Rate**

For an actuator moving at velocity $v$ with effective area $A$:

$$Q = A \cdot v = \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} \cdot v \quad \text{(extend stroke)}$$

For a fixed-displacement pump of volumetric displacement $V_D$ running at $N$ rpm:

$$Q_{ideal} = V_D \cdot N$$

Accounting for volumetric efficiency $\eta_{vol}$:

$$Q_{actual} = V_D \cdot N \cdot \eta_{vol}$$

**Step 2 – Pump Power**

The theoretical hydraulic power delivered is:

$$P_{hyd} = p \cdot Q$$

Converted to the mechanical input power required at the pump shaft (using overall efficiency $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{vol} \cdot \eta_{mech}$):

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{p \cdot Q}{600 \cdot \eta_{overall}} \quad \text{(kW, when p in bar, Q in L/min)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Shortcut Form:** When pressure is in *bar* and flow in *litres per minute (LPM)*, the power in kW is $\dfrac{p \cdot Q}{600 \, \eta}$ — this is the form expected in KTU numerical problems.

### 2.3 Pipeline / Conductor Sizing

**Step 1 – Recommended Oil Velocity**

| Line Type | Recommended Velocity (m/s) |
|---|---|
| Pressure line (suction) | 0.5 – 1.5 |
| Pressure line (return) | 1.5 – 3.0 |
| Suction line (at pump inlet) | 0.3 – 1.0 (max 1.5) |
| High-pressure line to actuator | 3.0 – 6.0 (short), 2.0 – 4.0 (long) |

**Step 2 – Pipe Internal Diameter**

From continuity $Q = A \cdot v$:

$$d = \sqrt{\frac{4Q}{\pi \cdot v}}$$

**Step 3 – Reynolds Number & Pressure Drop**

$$\text{Re} = \frac{v \cdot d}{\nu}$$

- If $\text{Re} < 2000$ → **Laminar** flow.
- If $\text{Re} > 4000$ → **Turbulent** flow.

Laminar pressure drop (Hagen-Poiseuille):

$$\Delta p = \frac{32 \, \mu \, L \, v}{d^{2}} = \frac{128 \, \mu \, L \, Q}{\pi \, d^{4}}$$

Turbulent pressure drop (Darcy-Weisbach):

$$\Delta p = f \cdot \frac{L}{d} \cdot \frac{\rho \, v^{2}}{2}$$

where $f$ is the Darcy friction factor (≈ 0.04 for smooth steel pipes at typical Reynolds numbers).

### 2.4 Reservoir Sizing

$$V_{res} \geq 2 \text{ to } 3 \times Q_{pump} \,(\text{LPM}) \quad \text{→ in litres}$$

The reservoir must also accommodate the **return oil from the cylinder's rod-side volume** when retracted, the **thermal expansion volume** of the entire circuit, and provide a **3–5 minute** residence time for air to separate.

### 2.5 Heat Dissipation Calculation

Every watt of mechanical input that does *not* become useful hydraulic work is converted to heat in the oil. The power lost to heat is:

$$P_{loss} = P_{input} - P_{useful} = P_{input}\,(1 - \eta_{overall})$$

The required surface area of a naturally cooled reservoir (no fan) is:

$$A_{req} = \frac{P_{loss}}{h \cdot \Delta T}$$

where $h \approx 11.5 \text{ W/m}^{2}\text{K}$ is the natural convection coefficient and $\Delta T$ is the allowable temperature rise (typically **30 °C** above ambient → $\Delta T = 30$ K).

For a rectangular reservoir of length $L$, width $W$, and height $H$ (with the top covered and not radiating):

$$A_{surface} = 2(LW + LH + WH) - LW = LW + 2H(L + W)$$

If $A_{req} > A_{surface}$ → **water-cooled heat exchanger required**.

### 2.6 Accumulator Sizing (Boyle's Law)

For a gas-charged (nitrogen) bladder or piston accumulator operating between maximum working pressure $p_1$ and minimum working pressure $p_2$ with a pre-charge pressure $p_0$ (rule of thumb: $p_0 = 0.9 \times p_2$ for isothermal, $p_0 = 0.6 \times p_1$ for adiabatic):

$$p_0 \, V_0 = p_2 \, V_2 = p_1 \, V_1 \quad \text{(isothermal)}$$

The **effective (usable) fluid volume** $\Delta V$ discharged between $p_1$ and $p_2$ is:

$$\Delta V = V_0 \, p_0 \left(\frac{1}{p_2} - \frac{1}{p_1}\right)$$

Solving for the required total gas-bottle volume $V_0$:

$$V_0 = \frac{\Delta V \, p_1}{p_0 \, (p_1 - p_2)}$$

### 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | Boundary / Typical |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cylinder thrust | $F = p \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$ | N | p in Pa, D in m |
| 2 | Cylinder bore | $D = \sqrt{4F/(\pi p)}$ | m | Force-limited design |
| 3 | Rod diameter | $d = D\sqrt{\phi}$ | m | $\phi = 0.3$ push, $0.5$ pull |
| 4 | Barrel thickness | $t = pD/(2\sigma_t)$ | m | Thin-wall assumption |
| 5 | Euler buckling | $P_{cr} = \pi^{2} E I / (KL)^{2}$ | N | E in Pa, I in m⁴ |
| 6 | Pump flow (ideal) | $Q = V_D N$ | m³/s = L/min | $V_D$ in m³/rev |
| 7 | Pump input power | $P_{shaft} = pQ / (600\eta)$ | kW | p in bar, Q in LPM |
| 8 | Pipe diameter | $d = \sqrt{4Q/(\pi v)}$ | m | $v = 0.5-6$ m/s |
| 9 | Reynolds number | $\text{Re} = v d / \nu$ | – | $\text{Re}<2000$ laminar |
| 10 | Laminar Δp | $\Delta p = 128 \mu L Q / (\pi d^{4})$ | Pa | Hagen–Poiseuille |
| 11 | Reservoir volume | $V \geq 2$–$3 \times Q$ | L | Q in LPM |
| 12 | Heat loss | $P_{loss} = P_{in}(1-\eta)$ | W | – |
| 13 | Reservoir area | $A = P_{loss}/(h\,\Delta T)$ | m² | h ≈ 11.5 W/m²K |
| 14 | Accumulator ΔV | $\Delta V = V_0 p_0 (1/p_2 - 1/p_1)$ | m³ | Isothermal |
| 15 | Accumulator $V_0$ | $V_0 = \Delta V p_1 / [p_0 (p_1 - p_2)]$ | m³ | $p_0 = 0.9 p_2$ |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Press Tools & Injection Moulding Machines:** Cylinder bore and tonnage calculations are central to machine specification.
- **Mobile Hydraulics (Excavators, Tippers):** Pump flow and pipe sizing determine cycle time and energy efficiency.
- **Aerospace & Marine:** Accumulators are critical for emergency power and pulsation damping.
- **Steel & Rolling Mills:** Massive reservoir and heat-exchanger systems are required to keep oil temperature below 55 °C in continuous duty.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Full Cylinder Sizing

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
A hydraulic press is required to deliver a **maximum thrust of 250 kN** at a working pressure of **160 bar**. The stroke length is **600 mm**, the cylinder is **pinned at both ends** (effective length factor $K = 1.0$), the piston rod is in *compression* (push duty, $\phi = 0.3$), the cylinder barrel material is **St 52 steel** with allowable tensile stress $\sigma_t = 240 \text{ MPa}$, and the rod material has $E = 207 \text{ GPa}$. Determine the bore diameter $D$, rod diameter $d$, barrel wall thickness $t$, and verify Euler buckling with a safety factor ≥ 3.

**Given:**
- $F = 250 \text{ kN} = 250{,}000 \text{ N}$
- $p = 160 \text{ bar} = 160 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 16 \text{ MPa}$
- $L = 600 \text{ mm} = 0.6 \text{ m}$
- $K = 1.0$
- $\phi = 0.3$
- $\sigma_t = 240 \text{ MPa} = 240 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}$
- $E = 207 \text{ GPa} = 207 \times 10^{9} \text{ Pa}$

**Step 1 — Bore Diameter**

We use Pascal's law to relate force, pressure, and area:

$$F = p \cdot A = p \cdot \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$$

Rearrange to isolate $D$:

$$\frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{F}{p}$$

$$D^{2} = \frac{4F}{\pi \, p}$$

Substitute numerical values (using SI units throughout):

$$D^{2} = \frac{4 \times 250{,}000}{\pi \times 16 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$D^{2} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{50.265 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$D^{2} = 1.9894 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^{2}$$

Take the square root:

$$D = \sqrt{1.9894 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.14105 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{D \approx 141 \text{ mm} \quad (\text{standardise to } 150 \text{ mm})}$$

**Step 2 — Rod Diameter**

For a *push* duty the tie-rod ratio is $\phi = 0.3$:

$$d = D \sqrt{\phi} = 0.14105 \times \sqrt{0.3}$$

$$d = 0.14105 \times 0.54772$$

$$d = 0.07726 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{d \approx 77 \text{ mm} \quad (\text{standardise to } 80 \text{ mm})}$$

**Step 3 — Barrel Wall Thickness**

Use the thin-wall approximation (valid because $t \ll D$ for this pressure):

$$t = \frac{p \cdot D}{2 \sigma_{t}}$$

$$t = \frac{16 \times 10^{6} \times 0.14105}{2 \times 240 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$t = \frac{2.2568 \times 10^{6}}{4.80 \times 10^{8}}$$

$$t = 4.70 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 4.7 \text{ mm} \quad (\text{standardise to } 6 \text{ mm with corrosion allowance})}$$

**Step 4 — Euler Buckling Check**

Moment of inertia of solid circular rod:

$$I = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{64}$$

With $d = 0.08 \text{ m}$ (standardised):

$$I = \frac{\pi \times (0.08)^{4}}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 4.096 \times 10^{-5}}{64}$$

$$I = 2.01 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{4}$$

Euler critical load:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^{2} E I}{(K L)^{2}}$$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^{2} \times 207 \times 10^{9} \times 2.01 \times 10^{-6}}{(1.0 \times 0.6)^{2}}$$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{9.8696 \times 207 \times 10^{9} \times 2.01 \times 10^{-6}}{0.36}$$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{4.103 \times 10^{6}}{0.36}$$

$$P_{cr} = 1.1398 \times 10^{7} \text{ N} \approx 11{,}398 \text{ kN}$$

Factor of safety:

$$n = \frac{P_{cr}}{F} = \frac{11{,}398}{250} \approx 45.6$$

$$\boxed{n \approx 45.6 \gg 3 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Design is safe against buckling.}}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Pump and Motor Sizing

**Problem Statement:**
A hydraulic cylinder ($D = 100$ mm, $d = 60$ mm) extends a load of $F = 60$ kN at a velocity of $v = 0.5$ m/s. The pump has overall efficiency $\eta = 0.85$ and the system pressure drop in the lines is 10 bar. Calculate the required pump flow, the required pump delivery pressure, and the input power of the electric motor driving the pump.

**Given:**
- $D = 0.1$ m, $d = 0.06$ m, $F = 60{,}000$ N
- $v = 0.5$ m/s
- $\eta_{overall} = 0.85$
- Line drop = $10$ bar

**Step 1 — Cylinder Area on the Piston (Cap-End) Side**

$$A_{cap} = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.1)^{2}}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Required Working Pressure**

Force balance on the piston:

$$F = p_{cyl} \cdot A_{cap}$$

$$p_{cyl} = \frac{F}{A_{cap}} = \frac{60{,}000}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 7.639 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} \approx 76.4 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 3 — Pump Delivery Pressure**

The pump must overcome the cylinder pressure *and* the line losses:

$$p_{pump} = p_{cyl} + \Delta p_{line} = 76.4 + 10 = 86.4 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 4 — Required Pump Flow Rate**

$$Q = A_{cap} \cdot v = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5 = 3.927 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

Convert to LPM (1 m³/s = 60,000 LPM):

$$Q = 3.927 \times 10^{-3} \times 60{,}000 = 235.6 \text{ LPM}$$

**Step 5 — Electric Motor Input Power**

Using the KTU shortcut formula (kW = bar × LPM / 600η):

$$P_{motor} = \frac{p_{pump} \cdot Q}{600 \cdot \eta_{overall}} = \frac{86.4 \times 235.6}{600 \times 0.85}$$

$$P_{motor} = \frac{20{,}355.84}{510} \approx 39.9 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{P_{motor} \approx 40 \text{ kW}}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — Accumulator Sizing

**Problem Statement:**
An accumulator must supply a flow of $\Delta V = 5$ L between pressures $p_1 = 200$ bar (max) and $p_2 = 150$ bar (min). Compute the required nitrogen pre-charge pressure $p_0$ and the total gas volume $V_0$.

**Given:**
- $\Delta V = 5 \text{ L} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3}$
- $p_1 = 200 \text{ bar} = 200 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa}$
- $p_2 = 150 \text{ bar} = 150 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa}$

**Step 1 — Pre-Charge Pressure (Isothermal Rule)**

$$p_0 = 0.9 \times p_2 = 0.9 \times 150 = 135 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 2 — Total Gas Volume**

Using Boyle's law (isothermal) and the volume discharged:

$$\Delta V = V_0 \, p_0 \left(\frac{1}{p_2} - \frac{1}{p_1}\right)$$

Rearrange:

$$V_0 = \frac{\Delta V}{p_0 \left(\dfrac{1}{p_2} - \dfrac{1}{p_1}\right)}$$

Compute the bracket term:

$$\frac{1}{p_2} - \frac{1}{p_1} = \frac{1}{150 \times 10^{5}} - \frac{1}{200 \times 10^{5}}$$

$$= \frac{1}{10^{5}} \left(\frac{1}{150} - \frac{1}{200}\right) = \frac{1}{10^{5}} \left(\frac{4-3}{600}\right) = \frac{1}{6 \times 10^{7}} \text{ Pa}^{-1}$$

Now substitute:

$$V_0 = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{135 \times 10^{5} \times \dfrac{1}{6 \times 10^{7}}}$$

$$V_0 = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3} \times 6 \times 10^{7}}{135 \times 10^{5}}$$

$$V_0 = \frac{3 \times 10^{5}}{1.35 \times 10^{7}}$$

$$V_0 = 0.02222 \text{ m}^{3} = 22.22 \text{ L}$$

$$\boxed{V_0 \approx 22.2 \text{ L}}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Comparison Table for the Examiner

| Design Step | Required Input | Computed Output | Governing Equation | Cross-Check |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bore sizing | Force, Pressure | $D$ | $D = \sqrt{4F/(\pi p)}$ | $F \le pA$ |
| Rod sizing | $D$, duty | $d$ | $d = D\sqrt{\phi}$ | $\phi = 0.3$ push / $0.5$ pull |
| Wall thickness | $p$, $D$, $\sigma_t$ | $t$ | $t = pD/(2\sigma_t)$ | $t \le 0.1 D$ (thin wall) |
| Buckling | $E$, $I$, $L$, $K$ | $P_{cr}$ | $P_{cr} = \pi^{2}EI/(KL)^{2}$ | $P_{cr}/F \ge 3$ |
| Pump flow | $A$, $v$ | $Q$ | $Q = Av$ | $Q = V_D N \eta_v$ |
| Motor power | $p$, $Q$, $\eta$ | $P$ | $P = pQ/(600\eta)$ | $P > P_{hyd}/\eta$ |
| Pipe diameter | $Q$, $v$ | $d$ | $d = \sqrt{4Q/(\pi v)}$ | $v_{suction} \le 1.5$ m/s |
| Heat dissipation | $P_{loss}$, $\Delta T$ | $A$ | $A = P_{loss}/(h\Delta T)$ | $A_{actual} \ge A$ |
| Accumulator $V_0$ | $\Delta V$, $p_1$, $p_2$ | $V_0$ | $V_0 = \Delta V p_1/[p_0(p_1-p_2)]$ | $p_0 = 0.9 p_2$ |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Hydraulic System Design

The design of a hydraulic system proceeds in a strictly hierarchical manner — the *output* of one stage becomes the *input* of the next. The following Mermaid block maps the entire decision flow used in Module 4:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Load Specification] --> A1[Required Force F in N]
    A0 --> A2[Required Velocity v in m per s]
    A0 --> A3[Stroke Length L in m]
    A0 --> A4[Duty Cycle and Duty Type]

    A1 --> B1[Working Pressure p selection]
    B1 --> C1[Compute Cylinder Bore D]
    C1 --> C2[Compute Rod Diameter d]
    C2 --> C3[Compute Barrel Wall t]
    C3 --> C4[Euler Buckling Safety Check]
    C4 --> C5{Pass with FoS greater than 3}
    C5 -- No --> C1
    C5 -- Yes --> D1[Accept Cylinder Geometry]

    A2 --> E1[Compute Pump Flow Q]
    E1 --> E2[Add Line and Valve Losses]
    E2 --> E3[Compute Pump Delivery Pressure]
    E3 --> E4[Compute Motor Input Power]
    E4 --> E5[Select Standard Pump and Motor]

    E1 --> F1[Pipe Diameter from Q and v]
    F1 --> F2[Compute Reynolds Number]
    F2 --> F3{Regime}
    F3 -- Laminar --> F4[Hagen Poiseuille Delta p]
    F3 -- Turbulent --> F5[Darcy Weisbach Delta p]
    F4 --> F6[Check against Allowable Loss]
    F5 --> F6
    F6 -- Fail --> F1
    F6 -- Pass --> F7[Accept Pipe Diameter]

    E2 --> G1[Reservoir Volume from Q]
    G1 --> G2[Reservoir Surface Area]
    G2 --> G3[Heat Dissipation Check]
    G3 --> G4{Area Sufficient}
    G4 -- No --> G5[Add Heat Exchanger]
    G4 -- Yes --> G6[Accept Reservoir]

    E2 --> H1[Identify Pulsation and Emergency Need]
    H1 -- Yes --> H2[Accumulator Sizing using Boyle]
    H2 --> H3[Compute V0 and p0]
    H1 -- No --> H4[No Accumulator Required]

    D1 --> Z1[Final Bill of Materials and Schematic]
    E5 --> Z1
    F7 --> Z1
    G6 --> Z1
    H3 --> Z1
    H4 --> Z1
```

### 4.2 Cylinder Force-Velocity-Pressure Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PUMP[Pump p Q] --> PRESS[Pressure p]
    PRESS --> CAP[Cap End Area A cap]
    CAP --> THRUST[Thrust F push]
    ROD[Rod End Area A ann] --> THRUST2[Thrust F pull]
    CAP --> VEL[Velocity v = Q over A]
    VEL --> TIME[Cycle Time = L over v]
    PUMP --> POWER[Power P = pQ]
    POWER --> LOSS[Heat Loss P times 1 minus eta]
    LOSS --> RES[Reservoir Area]
```

### 4.3 Design Calculation Worksheet (Graphical Mind-Map)

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Hydraulic Design Calculations))
    Cylinder
      Bore from Force and Pressure
      Rod from Tie Ratio
      Wall Thickness from Hoop Stress
      Buckling via Euler
    Pump
      Flow from Area and Velocity
      Pressure from Load and Losses
      Power in kW equals pQ over 600 eta
    Conductor
      Diameter from Q and v
      Reynolds Number
      Pressure Drop Hagen Poiseuille or Darcy
    Reservoir
      Capacity two to three Q
      Area from Heat Loss
    Accumulator
      Boyle Law pV equals constant
      Pre Charge 0.9 times p2
      V0 from Delta V and pressures
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *Define the term "Design Calculation" as applied to a hydraulic power system. List any four design parameters that must be computed.*

**Model Answer:**
Design Calculation is the analytical procedure by which all functional dimensions and ratings of a hydraulic system — such as cylinder bore, rod diameter, pump displacement, pipe diameter, reservoir capacity, and accumulator volume — are mathematically determined from the load specification, fluid properties, and governing physical laws (Pascal's Law, Continuity, Bernoulli, Boyle's Law).

The four key design parameters are:
1. **Cylinder bore diameter** $D$ — from required force and working pressure.
2. **Pump flow rate** $Q$ — from cylinder area and required velocity.
3. **Pipe internal diameter** $d$ — from flow rate and recommended velocity.
4. **Reservoir capacity** $V$ — from pump flow and thermal expansion allowance.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> *State Euler's formula for buckling of a long piston rod. Why is the check mandatory in cylinder design?*

**Model Answer:**
Euler's critical buckling load for a slender column is:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^{2} \, E \, I}{(K L)^{2}}$$

where $E$ is the Young's modulus, $I$ is the second moment of area of the rod cross-section, $L$ is the unsupported length, and $K$ is the effective length factor based on end conditions.

This check is mandatory because a rod loaded in compression can fail by *elastic instability (buckling)* at a load **far lower** than its yield strength. Especially in long-stroke cylinders (e.g., $L/D > 10$), buckling governs the design and the factor of safety must be **at least 3** as per KTU convention.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> **[MODULE 4 – INTERNAL CHOICE — Pick ONE of the following two]**

---

#### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

> **(a)** A hydraulic cylinder is required to push a load of $F = 180$ kN at a working pressure of $p = 140$ bar. The stroke is 500 mm. The rod is in *push* duty ($\phi = 0.3$), barrel material is St 52 with $\sigma_t = 220$ MPa, and the rod material has $E = 210$ GPa. The cylinder is fixed-free at the ends ($K = 2.0$). Determine:
>   1. The bore diameter $D$
>   2. The rod diameter $d$
>   3. The barrel wall thickness $t$
>   4. Whether the design is safe against Euler buckling (FoS ≥ 3)  **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** A fixed-displacement pump with $V_D = 50$ cc/rev runs at 1450 rpm and supplies the cylinder in part (a). The overall efficiency of the pump is 0.88. Compute:
>   1. The theoretical and actual flow rates in LPM
>   2. The motor input power in kW
>   3. The maximum velocity of the piston in m/s  **(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (a):**

**Step 1 – Bore diameter** from $F = p \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$:

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4F}{\pi p}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 180{,}000}{\pi \times 140 \times 10^{5}}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{7.2 \times 10^{5}}{4.398 \times 10^{7}}} = \sqrt{1.637 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.1279 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{D \approx 128 \text{ mm} \quad \text{standardise to 130 mm}}$$

**Step 2 – Rod diameter** for push duty ($\phi = 0.3$):

$$d = D \sqrt{0.3} = 0.128 \times 0.5477 = 0.0701 \text{ m} \approx 70 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{d = 70 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 3 – Wall thickness** (thin-wall):

$$t = \frac{p D}{2 \sigma_t} = \frac{140 \times 10^{5} \times 0.130}{2 \times 220 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{1.82 \times 10^{7}}{4.4 \times 10^{8}} = 0.0414 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 41 \text{ mm}}$$

(Note: this is > 0.1 D, so thick-wall Lamé equation should ideally be used; we keep thin-wall as upper-bound estimate and recommend 50 mm with forging allowance.)

**Step 4 – Euler buckling**:

$$I = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{64} = \frac{\pi (0.07)^{4}}{64} = 1.18 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{4}$$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^{2} E I}{(K L)^{2}} = \frac{\pi^{2} \times 210 \times 10^{9} \times 1.18 \times 10^{-6}}{(2.0 \times 0.5)^{2}}$$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{2.448 \times 10^{6}}{1.0} = 2.448 \times 10^{6} \text{ N} = 2448 \text{ kN}$$

FoS:

$$n = \frac{P_{cr}}{F} = \frac{2448}{180} = 13.6 \gg 3$$

$$\boxed{\text{Design is safe against buckling.}}$$

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (b):**

**Step 1 – Flow rates**:

$$Q_{ideal} = V_D \times N = 50 \text{ cc/rev} \times 1450 \text{ rpm} = 72{,}500 \text{ cc/min} = 72.5 \text{ LPM}$$

$$Q_{actual} = Q_{ideal} \times \eta_{vol} \quad \text{(using overall } \eta \text{ as proxy)} = 72.5 \times 0.88 = 63.8 \text{ LPM}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{ideal} = 72.5 \text{ LPM}, \quad Q_{actual} = 63.8 \text{ LPM}}$$

**Step 2 – Motor input power**:

$$P_{motor} = \frac{p \cdot Q_{ideal}}{600 \cdot \eta} = \frac{140 \times 72.5}{600 \times 0.88} = \frac{10{,}150}{528} = 19.22 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{P_{motor} \approx 19.2 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 3 – Piston velocity**:

Using actual flow and standard bore $D = 130$ mm:

$$A = \frac{\pi (0.13)^{2}}{4} = 1.327 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$v = \frac{Q_{actual}}{A} = \frac{63.8 \times 10^{-3}/60}{1.327 \times 10^{-2}} = \frac{1.063 \times 10^{-3}}{1.327 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.0801 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v \approx 0.08 \text{ m/s} = 80 \text{ mm/s}}$$

---

#### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]`

> **(a)** A hydraulic circuit must deliver a flow of $Q = 60$ LPM over a 30-metre long pipe. The recommended oil velocity is $v = 3$ m/s and the kinematic viscosity at operating temperature is $\nu = 46$ cSt. The oil density is $\rho = 870$ kg/m³. Determine:
>   1. The pipe internal diameter $d$
>   2. The Reynolds number and flow regime
>   3. The pressure drop using Hagen-Poiseuille *or* Darcy-Weisbach as appropriate  **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** A reservoir of dimensions $1.2$ m × $0.8$ m × $0.6$ m (L × W × H) receives heat from a pump delivering 35 LPM at 200 bar with overall efficiency 0.85. Ambient temperature is 32 °C and maximum allowable oil temperature is 60 °C. Determine:
>   1. The power lost as heat
>   2. The required heat-dissipation area
>   3. Whether a heat exchanger is required (natural convection $h = 11.5$ W/m²K)  **(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (a):**

**Step 1 – Pipe diameter** from $Q = Av$:

$$d = \sqrt{\frac{4Q}{\pi v}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times (60 \times 10^{-3}/60)}{\pi \times 3}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 10^{-3}}{9.4248}}$$

$$d = \sqrt{4.244 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.0206 \text{ m} \approx 20.6 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{d \approx 21 \text{ mm (standard ½" pipe)}}$$

**Step 2 – Reynolds number**:

$$\text{Re} = \frac{v \cdot d}{\nu} = \frac{3 \times 0.0206}{46 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.0618}{4.6 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 1344$$

$$\boxed{\text{Re} = 1344 < 2000 \Rightarrow \text{LAMINAR flow}}$$

**Step 3 – Pressure drop** (Hagen-Poiseuille, valid for laminar):

Dynamic viscosity:

$$\mu = \rho \nu = 870 \times 46 \times 10^{-6} = 0.04002 \text{ Pa·s}$$

$$\Delta p = \frac{32 \mu L v}{d^{2}} = \frac{32 \times 0.04002 \times 30 \times 3}{(0.0206)^{2}}$$

$$\Delta p = \frac{115.26}{4.2436 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.716 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} \approx 2.72 \text{ bar}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta p \approx 2.72 \text{ bar}}$$

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (b):**

**Step 1 – Power lost as heat**:

$$P_{hyd} = \frac{p \cdot Q}{600} = \frac{200 \times 60}{600} = 20 \text{ kW} = 20{,}000 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{input} = \frac{P_{hyd}}{\eta} = \frac{20}{0.85} = 23.53 \text{ kW}$$

$$P_{loss} = P_{input} - P_{hyd} = 23{,}529 - 20{,}000 = 3529 \text{ W} \approx 3.53 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{P_{loss} \approx 3.53 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 2 – Required heat-dissipation area**:

Allowable temperature rise:

$$\Delta T = 60 - 32 = 28 \text{ °C} = 28 \text{ K}$$

$$A_{req} = \frac{P_{loss}}{h \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{3529}{11.5 \times 28} = \frac{3529}{322} = 10.96 \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$\boxed{A_{req} \approx 11.0 \text{ m}^{2}}$$

**Step 3 – Check against actual reservoir surface area**:

$$A_{actual} = LW + 2H(L + W) = (1.2 \times 0.8) + 2 \times 0.6 \times (1.2 + 0.8)$$

$$A_{actual} = 0.96 + 1.2 \times 2.0 = 0.96 + 2.40 = 3.36 \text{ m}^{2}$$

Since $A_{actual} = 3.36 \text{ m}^{2} \ll A_{req} = 10.96 \text{ m}^{2}$:

$$\boxed{\text{A heat exchanger is REQUIRED.}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Lose-Mark Hot-Spots):**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder:** Mixing *bar* and *Pa* in the same equation. **Always convert to SI (Pa)** before plugging into $F = pA$, then convert the *answer* back to the requested bar / kW form.
> 2. **Forgetting Line Losses:** The pump must supply the *cylinder pressure* + *valve* + *pipe* pressure drops. Missing the line-loss term costs **2 full marks** in a 14-mark question.
> 3. **Tie-Rod Ratio Mismatch:** Using $\phi = 0.5$ for a *push* application or $\phi = 0.3$ for a *pull* application — examiners deduct one mark per sub-question.
> 4. **Ignoring Buckling:** For a long stroke ($L > 5D$), you **must** show the Euler calculation and confirm $P_{cr}/F \ge 3$. Skipping this is the single biggest *mark-killer* on a Module 4 paper.
> 5. **Reservoir Sizing Error:** Students often write $V = Q \times 3$ literally; KTU expects the answer in **litres** with $Q$ in **LPM**, and the multiplier is the *rule-of-thumb* (2–3), not a constant. State the assumption explicitly.
> 6. **Pump Power Formula Units:** Use $p$ (bar) × $Q$ (LPM) ÷ 600 only when *both* units are in those forms. The 600 absorbs the conversion. Using SI units gives the same answer in watts, but students frequently double-convert.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Cylinder bore** is sized from *force ÷ pressure*: $D = \sqrt{4F/(\pi p)}$. Always verify that $F$ is the *worst-case load*, including dynamic and friction multipliers (typically 1.1–1.2×).
- ✅ **Rod diameter** uses the *tie-rod ratio*: 0.3 for push, 0.5 for pull. Memorise the values 0.55 D (push) and 0.71 D (pull) for quick substitution.
- ✅ **Barrel wall thickness** is derived from **hoop stress** in thin-wall assumption: $t = pD/(2\sigma_t)$. Cross-check that $t < 0.1D$; if not, use the **Lamé** thick-wall equation.
- ✅ **Euler buckling** is non-negotiable for long strokes. Factor of safety ≥ **3** is the KTU convention.
- ✅ **Pump flow** = piston area × velocity; account for **volumetric efficiency** when actual flow is needed.
- ✅ **Motor input power** (kW) = $\dfrac{p \text{(bar)} \times Q \text{(LPM)}}{600 \, \eta}$. This is the *single most important formula* in the entire module — revise it daily.
- ✅ **Pipe sizing** uses the *recommended-velocity* table (suction ≤ 1.5 m/s, return ≤ 3 m/s, pressure ≤ 6 m/s). Calculate $d = \sqrt{4Q/(\pi v)}$.
- ✅ **Reynolds number** distinguishes laminar from turbulent. **Hagen–Poiseuille** for $\text{Re}<2000$, **Darcy–Weisbach** for $\text{Re}>4000$.
